exercises.solution: Because while frameworks (Spark, Kafka, cloud warehouses) **implement** the hard mechanics (consensus, replication, recovery) so you don't build them from scratch, you still must **reason about and operate** systems built on these principles — and the principles **leak through** in performance, failures, and configuration. Without understanding them, you **can't**: explain **why a Spark job is slow** (it's the **shuffle** — data movement) or fix it (broadcast, partition); choose a **good shard/partition key** (avoid **hot spots**); decide a NoSQL store's **consistency level** (CAP/quorum trade-off for your use case); design for **failures** (idempotency, retries, replication) that **will** happen at scale; or avoid **cloud egress** costs (keep compute near data). You'd be left **cargo-culting configs** and **surprised by failures and bad performance**. Understanding **partitioning, replication, consistency, fault tolerance, and the network bottleneck** lets you **predict behavior, debug issues, make trade-offs deliberately, and design systems that scale and survive** — the difference between using distributed tools effectively and being mystified by them. These basics underpin **essentially every track**, so they're foundational literacy for the role.

lessons.diagram_caption: Scale by partitioning and replicating across machines; the network is the bottleneck, so minimize data movement.

lessons.title: Data quality — the six dimensions
lessons.summary: What makes data trustworthy: the six dimensions of quality, how to measure them, and why quality is foundational to every data decision.
lessons.concept:
**Data quality** — whether data is **fit for use** — is foundational: every dashboard, model, and decision is only as good as the data behind it. Bad data silently misleads, so quality must be **defined, measured, and protected**.

### The six dimensions
Data quality is assessed across six standard dimensions — every concrete check maps to one:

- **Accuracy** — does the data **match reality**? (a customer's real address)
- **Completeness** — are there **no missing** values/rows? (no null required fields, no dropped records)
- **Consistency** — does it **agree across systems**? (the same customer's data matches in CRM and billing)
- **Timeliness** — is it **up to date**? (fresh within its SLA)
- **Validity** — does it **follow the rules/format**? (a valid email, amount ≥ 0, allowed status)
- **Uniqueness** — are there **no duplicates**? (one row per real entity)

@@diagram:data-quality

### Measuring quality
Turn each dimension into **automated checks**: not-null (completeness), unique (uniqueness), accepted-values/ranges (validity), referential integrity (consistency), freshness (timeliness), and comparison-to-truth (accuracy). Run them with **dbt tests / great_expectations / Soda** in **CI** and at **runtime**, and track them as **metrics**.

### Why it matters
**Trust** is the entire value of a data platform — one wrong number erodes it. Quality issues are **insidious** because they're often **silent** (a job succeeds while emitting bad data). Systematic quality — **defined dimensions, automated checks at boundaries, quarantine + alerting, and observability** — turns trust from a hope into an **engineered guarantee**. This is the foundational view; the governance track goes deeper into validation, quarantine, and remediation.
keypoints: Data quality = fitness for use; every dashboard/model/decision is only as good as the data behind it.
Six dimensions: accuracy (matches reality), completeness (no missing), consistency (agrees across systems), timeliness (up to date), validity (follows rules), uniqueness (no duplicates).
Every concrete check maps to a dimension: not-null→completeness, unique→uniqueness, ranges→validity, referential integrity→consistency, freshness→timeliness, vs-truth→accuracy.
Measure with automated checks (dbt/great_expectations/Soda) in CI and at runtime; track them as metrics.
Quality issues are insidious because they're often silent — a job succeeds while emitting bad data.
Systematic quality (dimensions + automated boundary checks + quarantine/alert + observability) turns trust from hope into an engineered guarantee.

quiz.q: What are the six dimensions of data quality?
options: Accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness, validity, uniqueness
quiz.explain: Quality is fitness-for-use across these six dimensions; every concrete check (not-null, unique, ranges, freshness, etc.) maps to one of them.

quiz.q: Why are data-quality issues especially insidious?
options: They're often silent — a pipeline succeeds while producing bad data, so no error fires and bad data reaches consumers
quiz.explain: A job can technically succeed while emitting wrong data (0 rows, dropped records, drift), so 'task succeeded' never alerts — bad data flows to dashboards undetected without quality checks.

quiz.q: Which check maps to the 'validity' dimension?
options: accepted-values / ranges (e.g. amount ≥ 0, valid email format)
quiz.explain: Validity is about following rules/format — accepted-values and range checks enforce it. not_null→completeness, unique→uniqueness, freshness→timeliness.

exercises.prompt: Map a concrete check to each of the six dimensions.
exercises.solution: **Accuracy** → compare to a **source of truth** (does the stored address match the verified/real one). **Completeness** → **not_null** on required columns and **row-count** vs expected (no missing values/records). **Consistency** → **referential integrity** (every FK matches a parent) and cross-system reconciliation (the same customer matches across CRM/billing). **Timeliness** → **freshness** check (table updated within its SLA). **Validity** → **accepted-values/range/format** (status in an allowed set, amount ≥ 0, valid email pattern). **Uniqueness** → **unique** on the key (one row per real entity, no duplicates). Every practical data-quality assertion you write **maps to one of these six dimensions**, which both **ensures coverage** (you're checking all aspects of fitness-for-use, not just one) and **communicates meaning** (a failing check tells you *which* quality property broke). Together these automated checks, run in CI and at runtime, operationalize the six dimensions.

exercises.prompt: Why is trust the 'entire value' of a data platform, and how does data quality protect it?
exercises.hint: One wrong number erodes trust in everything.
exercises.solution: Because a data platform exists to **inform decisions**, and people will only act on data they **trust**. The moment a stakeholder catches **one wrong number** — a revenue figure that doesn't reconcile, a dashboard that's stale, a metric that's double-counted — they **stop trusting the platform**, often broadly ('if *that* was wrong, what else is?'). Then they **revert to spreadsheets, ask for manual checks, or ignore the data** — destroying the platform's value regardless of how much engineering went into it. **Trust is fragile and the whole point.** **Data quality protects it** by making the data **demonstrably trustworthy**: defined **dimensions** (so 'good' is concrete), **automated checks** at boundaries (catching bad data **before** it reaches consumers), **quarantine + alerting** (isolating problems without corrupting or silently shipping), and **observability** (continuously monitoring freshness/volume/quality and tracing issues). This turns quality from a **hope** ('we think the data's fine') into an **engineered guarantee** ('the data passes defined checks continuously, and we catch problems first'). Sustained quality is what **earns and keeps** the trust that makes the platform valuable — which is why it's foundational, not optional.

exercises.prompt: Where should data-quality checks run, and why both places?
exercises.hint: At boundaries / in CI and at runtime.
exercises.solution: Run checks **at data boundaries** (as data enters the system or moves between layers — bronze→silver→gold), **in CI (pre-deploy)**, and **at runtime (each load) in production** — and you need **all** because they catch different failures. **At boundaries** so bad data is caught **at the door before it spreads** to many downstream consumers (cheaper and safer than fixing after). **In CI** so a **code/transform change** that would break quality **can't ship** — tests on sample/staging data fail the PR (shift left, catching logic bugs early). **At runtime** because **CI can't foresee production data problems** — a source returns bad/empty data, late/skewed records, schema drift, anomalies that only appear with **live data**; runtime checks validate the **actual data flowing through** and **quarantine/alert** on failures. So: **boundaries** localize where to check, **CI** prevents shipping broken logic, and **runtime/observability** catches bad data in production. Relying on one place leaves gaps (CI misses live-data issues; runtime alone lets preventable bugs ship). Running quality checks across **CI and runtime, at boundaries**, gives **end-to-end** coverage — the same complementary relationship as testing and observability.

exercises.prompt: How does the 'silent failure' nature of bad data argue for systematic, automated quality checks?
exercises.hint: No error fires, so you must actively check the data.
exercises.solution: Because bad data **doesn't announce itself** — a pipeline can **technically succeed** (no exception, 'task green') while **producing wrong data** (a source returned 0 rows, a join dropped records, values drifted, duplicates crept in). Since **no error fires**, you **cannot rely on failures** to tell you something's wrong; the bad data **silently flows to dashboards and decisions** and is often discovered **late** (an angry stakeholder, a wrong report) and **hard to trace**. This **insidious silence** means quality must be **actively, systematically checked** rather than assumed: you **define the dimensions** (what 'good' means), **encode automated checks** (not-null, unique, ranges, freshness, referential integrity) that **examine the data itself**, **run them continuously** (CI + runtime), and **alert/quarantine** on violations. Manual or occasional checking can't keep up with the volume and would miss most silent issues. So the silent-failure property is precisely why quality can't be left to hope or human vigilance — it demands **engineered, automated, always-on checks** (and observability) that **proactively detect** bad data **before consumers do**, turning an invisible risk into a **monitored, caught, and handled** one. Systematic automation is the only defense against failures that never raise an error.

lessons.diagram_caption: The six data-quality dimensions: accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness, validity, uniqueness — every check maps to one.

lessons.title: Foundations interview prep & cheat sheet
lessons.summary: Consolidated rapid-review for the whole Foundations track: a master cheat sheet, every high-frequency interview question with model answers, and a mock-interview warm-up.
lessons.concept:
This is the **rapid-review page** for the entire Foundations track — pull it up the night before an interview. The companion **Go deeper** guide has every high-frequency question with full model answers and an 8-question mock interview; here's the master cheat sheet to anchor it.

### The whole track in one screen
- **DE role** — builds/operates the systems that move, store & transform data so it's usable (analyst answers questions, DE builds the kitchen).
- **Data shapes** — structured (table) / semi (JSON) / unstructured (text, image); the **shape dictates the store**.
- **File formats** — columnar **Parquet** beats row **CSV**: column pruning + compression + min/max data skipping.
- **Bits & encoding** — **UTF-8 everywhere**; mojibake = write one encoding, read another.
- **OLTP vs OLAP** — row/normalized/ACID (run the business) vs column/denormalized (understand it); the pipeline connects them.
- **Lifecycle** — source → ingest → store → transform → serve → analyze, wrapped by orchestration/quality/governance/DataOps.
- **Compression** — ratio vs speed; Snappy/Zstd default; **gzip is not splittable**.
- **Serialization** — text (JSON) vs binary+schema (Avro/Protobuf); Parquet = columnar storage.
- **Distributed** — partition (scale) · replicate (availability) · **network = bottleneck**.
- **Data quality** — six dimensions; automated checks at boundaries; failures are **silent**.

### Three lists to memorize
- **Six quality dimensions:** accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness, validity, uniqueness.
- **Lifecycle wrappers:** orchestration, quality/observability, governance, DataOps.
- **Five distributed ideas:** partitioning, replication, consistency (CAP), fault tolerance, minimize data movement.

**Sizing warm-up:** 50M events/day × 500 bytes ≈ 25 GB/day → ~9 TB/year raw (then factor compression, replication, retention). Practice moving fluently between bytes ↔ GB ↔ TB out loud.
keypoints: Foundations interviews test clarity of mental models, not trivia — be able to draw the lifecycle and explain row-vs-column.
Highest-frequency questions: DE-vs-analyst, why Parquet>CSV, why not query prod DB, gzip-not-splittable, JSON-vs-Avro, data skew, ensure quality.
Memorize the three lists: six quality dimensions, four lifecycle wrappers, five distributed ideas.
Be able to do back-of-envelope sizing (bytes↔GB↔TB) out loud for system-design rounds.
Each topic's full guide has its own cheat sheet and expanded, company-tagged answers — drill weak spots there.

quiz.q: In one line, how is a data engineer different from a data analyst?
options: The analyst answers business questions from data; the engineer builds the platform/pipelines that produce and guarantee that data
quiz.explain: Analyst = consumer of data (questions/dashboards); engineer = builder of the reliable, clean data platform they depend on.

quiz.q: Why might a 100-node Spark job processing one large .gz file run as if it had a single node?
options: gzip is not splittable, so the whole stream is read sequentially by one task
quiz.explain: Non-splittable gzip can't be divided across workers; use Parquet + Snappy/Zstd for parallel block reads.

quiz.q: Name the six data-quality dimensions.
options: Speed, size, cost, color, CPU, RAM
Accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness, validity, uniqueness
quiz.explain: Every concrete check (not-null, unique, ranges, freshness, referential integrity, vs-truth) maps to one of these six dimensions.

exercises.prompt: Do the 8-question mock interview (in the Go-deeper guide) out loud, no notes, timing each answer to 60–90 seconds.
exercises.hint: DE-vs-analyst, end-to-end pipeline, Parquet>CSV, prod-DB, gzip-splittability, JSON-vs-Avro, data skew, ensure quality.
exercises.solution: Self-assessment: if any answer felt shaky or ran long, reread that topic's deep-dive guide (each has a cheat sheet and expanded answers) and retry until you can deliver each crisply in 60–90 seconds. Fluency out loud — not recognition — is what passes screens.

exercises.prompt: Back-of-envelope: 120M events/day at 450 bytes each — daily and yearly raw storage?
exercises.hint: multiply, then convert bytes→GB→TB; state assumptions.
exercises.solution: 120,000,000 × 450 = 54,000,000,000 bytes ≈ 54 GB/day → ~19.7 TB/year raw. Then add judgment aloud: columnar + compression cuts it several-fold; multiply by replication (×2–3) for stored footprint; apply retention to bound it.

tracks.summary: The bedrock every data engineer needs: the role itself, data types & file formats, encodings & compression, OLTP vs OLAP, the data lifecycle, serialization, distributed-systems basics, and data quality — the concepts everything else builds on.

tracks.title: Database Systems (DBMS)
tracks.blurb: How database management systems actually work — types, architecture, transactions/ACID, concurrency, recovery, storage, and query processing. The CS foundations under every data tool.

lessons.title: What is a DBMS (and why not just files)?
lessons.summary: A DBMS manages data for many users and apps with integrity, concurrency, and querying — solving everything raw files can't.
lessons.concept:
A **Database Management System (DBMS)** is software that stores, manages, and serves data to many users and applications — safely and efficiently. Before DBMSs, apps stored data in **files**, which caused real pain:

- **Duplication & inconsistency** — each app kept its own files; the same customer lived in many places and drifted out of sync.
- **No integrity** — nothing stopped a bad or orphaned value.
- **No concurrency** — two programs writing the same file corrupted it.
- **No querying** — every app re-implemented search/scan by hand.
- **No recovery/security** — a crash mid-write lost or corrupted data.

A DBMS fixes all of these: **one shared, governed store** with no duplication, **integrity constraints** enforced automatically, **transactions + concurrency control** so many users work safely at once, a **query language** (SQL) so you ask for data declaratively, plus **security, backup, and recovery**. In short: a DBMS turns a pile of files into a reliable, queryable, multi-user system of record. Every tool in this course — warehouses, lakehouses, even NoSQL stores — is a kind of DBMS.
lessons.example:
# the file-system problem the DBMS solves
App A -> customers_a.csv     App B -> customers_b.csv   (same customer, two copies, drift)
# DBMS
App A, App B -> ONE DBMS -> customers table  (one copy, integrity, concurrency, SQL, backup)
keypoints: A DBMS = software to store/manage/serve shared data safely and efficiently.
Raw files cause duplication, inconsistency, no integrity/concurrency/querying/recovery.
A DBMS gives: one shared store, integrity constraints, transactions+concurrency, a query language, security, backup/recovery.
It turns files into a reliable, queryable, multi-user system of record.
Warehouses, lakehouses, and NoSQL stores are all kinds of DBMS.
lessons.diagram_caption: File system (duplicated, no integrity) vs DBMS (one shared store, integrity, concurrency, SQL).

quiz.q: What core problem does a DBMS solve over raw files?
quiz.explain: A DBMS provides a shared, governed, queryable, multi-user store with integrity and recovery.

quiz.q: Which is NOT something a DBMS provides over files?
quiz.explain: DBMSs add integrity, concurrency, querying, security, recovery — not free hardware.

quiz.q: Two programs writing the same flat file at once risk:
quiz.explain: Files lack concurrency control; a DBMS coordinates concurrent access via transactions/locks.

exercises.prompt: List three problems with storing app data in plain files.
exercises.solution: Data duplication/inconsistency across apps, no integrity enforcement, no safe concurrency, no query language, no recovery/security.

exercises.prompt: Name four services a DBMS adds.
exercises.solution: Integrity constraints, transactions + concurrency control, a query language (SQL), security, and backup/recovery.

exercises.prompt: Why is a warehouse considered a DBMS?
exercises.solution: It stores/manages/serves shared data with querying, integrity, and concurrency — the defining DBMS responsibilities.

lessons.title: Types of DBMS
lessons.summary: Relational, document, key-value, column-family, graph, hierarchical/network, NewSQL, time-series/vector — what each is and where it fits.
lessons.concept:
DBMSs come in families, each shaped for different data and access patterns:

- **Relational (RDBMS)** — data in **tables** with SQL, ACID, joins (Postgres, MySQL, Oracle, SQL Server). The workhorse for structured data, OLTP, and analytics. *(Its own track follows.)*
- **Document** — flexible **JSON documents** (MongoDB). Good for semi-structured, app-shaped data.
- **Key-Value** — a giant dictionary, **key → value** (Redis, DynamoDB). Blazing fast lookups/caching.
- **Column-family / wide-column** — rows with flexible columns (Cassandra, HBase). High write throughput at scale.
- **Graph** — **nodes + edges** (Neo4j). Relationship-heavy data (fraud rings, social).
- **Hierarchical / Network** — tree/graph models (IMS, IDMS) — legacy, predate relational.
- **NewSQL** — SQL + ACID **at horizontal scale** (CockroachDB, Spanner).
- **Time-series / Vector** — metrics over time (InfluxDB) / embeddings for AI (pgvector, Pinecone).

The big split is **relational (SQL)** vs **NoSQL** (everything flexible/scale-out). Relational dominates where you need structure, integrity, and joins; the others trade some of those for scale, flexibility, or a specialized shape. Choose by your data's shape and access pattern, not by hype.
lessons.example:
key-value   GET user:123        -> {..}          (Redis)
document    db.users.find({age:{$gt:30}})         (MongoDB)
relational  SELECT * FROM users WHERE age>30      (Postgres)
graph       MATCH (a)-[:FRIEND]->(b) RETURN b      (Neo4j)
keypoints: Major types: relational, document, key-value, column-family, graph, hierarchical/network, NewSQL, time-series/vector.
Relational (RDBMS): tables + SQL + ACID + joins — structured data, OLTP, analytics.
NoSQL families trade strict relational guarantees for flexibility, scale, or a specialized shape.
Key-value = fast lookups; document = semi-structured; column-family = high write scale; graph = relationships.
NewSQL = SQL + ACID at horizontal scale; choose by data shape & access pattern.
lessons.diagram_caption: Relational, document, key-value, column-family, graph, hierarchical, NewSQL, time-series/vector.

quiz.q: Which DBMS type best fits relationship-heavy data like fraud rings?
quiz.explain: Graph DBMSs model nodes+edges, ideal for traversing relationships.

quiz.q: The defining traits of a relational DBMS are:
quiz.explain: RDBMS stores data in tables queried with SQL, with ACID transactions and joins.

quiz.q: NewSQL aims to provide:
quiz.explain: NewSQL keeps SQL and ACID while scaling out horizontally (e.g. CockroachDB, Spanner).

exercises.prompt: Match: caching, semi-structured app data, relationships.
exercises.solution: Caching → key-value (Redis); semi-structured app data → document (MongoDB); relationships → graph (Neo4j).

exercises.prompt: What's the big split among DBMS types?
exercises.solution: Relational (SQL, structured, ACID, joins) vs NoSQL (flexible/scale-out: document, key-value, column-family, graph).

exercises.prompt: How do you choose a DBMS type?
exercises.solution: By the data's shape and access pattern (structure, scale, relationships, latency) — not by hype.

lessons.title: DBMS architecture & components
lessons.summary: Inside a DBMS: the query processor, storage engine, transaction manager, buffer pool, and catalog — and how a query flows through them.
lessons.concept:
A DBMS is built from a few cooperating components. Knowing them demystifies performance and reliability:

- **Query processor** — turns SQL into results: a **parser** (syntax + name resolution), an **optimizer** (chooses the cheapest execution plan using statistics & indexes), and an **executor** (runs the plan).
- **Storage engine** — manages data on disk: **access methods** (B-tree indexes, heap scans), and the **buffer pool / cache** (keeps hot pages in memory so reads/writes avoid disk).
- **Transaction manager** — enforces **ACID**: coordinates commits/rollbacks, concurrency control (locks/MVCC), and works with the log.
- **Recovery/log manager** — the **write-ahead log (WAL)** for durability and crash recovery.
- **Catalog (data dictionary)** — metadata: tables, columns, types, indexes, constraints, stats — the DBMS's self-description that the optimizer and parser rely on.

**The flow:** a client sends SQL → parser → optimizer (plan) → executor → storage engine (via the buffer pool over data/index files), all under the transaction manager with the log ensuring durability. Most tuning is really about helping the **optimizer** pick good plans and keeping the **buffer pool** effective.
lessons.example:
client SQL
   -> parser (validate, resolve names via CATALOG)
   -> optimizer (pick plan using stats + indexes)
   -> executor -> storage engine (buffer pool over data/index files)
   under TRANSACTION MANAGER + WAL (ACID, recovery)
keypoints: Query processor = parser + optimizer + executor (SQL → plan → run).
Storage engine = access methods (B-tree/heap) + buffer pool (cache hot pages).
Transaction manager enforces ACID (concurrency control + commit/rollback).
Write-ahead log (WAL) provides durability & crash recovery.
Catalog/data dictionary holds metadata the optimizer & parser depend on.
Tuning ≈ helping the optimizer choose good plans + keeping the buffer pool hot.
lessons.diagram_caption: Apps/SQL → query processor (parse/optimize/execute) → storage engine; txn manager + catalog alongside.

quiz.q: Which component chooses the execution plan?
quiz.explain: The optimizer selects the cheapest plan using statistics and available indexes.

quiz.q: What does the buffer pool do?
options: caches hot data pages in memory to avoid disk I/O
quiz.explain: The buffer pool keeps frequently-used pages in memory so the engine avoids slow disk access.

quiz.q: The catalog (data dictionary) holds:
options: metadata: tables, columns, types, indexes, constraints, stats
quiz.explain: The catalog is the DBMS's self-description used by the parser and optimizer.

exercises.prompt: Name the three parts of the query processor.
exercises.solution: Parser (validate + resolve names), optimizer (choose plan), executor (run the plan).

exercises.prompt: What two things does the storage engine manage?
exercises.solution: Access methods (indexes/scans like B-trees and heaps) and the buffer pool/cache of hot pages.

exercises.prompt: Trace a query through the components.
exercises.solution: Client SQL → parser → optimizer (plan via catalog stats + indexes) → executor → storage engine/buffer pool over files, under the transaction manager with WAL for durability.

lessons.title: Three-schema architecture & data independence
lessons.summary: External, conceptual, and internal levels — and why separating them lets you change storage or logical design without breaking apps.
lessons.concept:
The **three-schema (ANSI-SPARC) architecture** separates a database into three levels so changes at one level don't ripple to others:

- **External level (views)** — what each user/app sees: a tailored **subset** of the data, possibly renamed or combined. Many external schemas over one database.
- **Conceptual (logical) level** — the **whole logical design**: all tables, columns, relationships, and constraints, independent of how it's stored.
- **Internal (physical) level** — **how** it's stored: files, pages, indexes, compression on disk.

The payoff is **data independence**:
- **Logical data independence** — change the conceptual schema (add a column, split a table) without breaking external views/apps.
- **Physical data independence** — change storage (add an index, repartition, change the file layout) without changing the logical schema or apps.

This separation is why you can add an index or reorganize storage with zero app changes, and why a view can shield apps from a table refactor. It's the architectural reason databases are maintainable over decades.
lessons.example:
External:   v_active_customers (a view: subset + renamed columns)
Conceptual: customers(id, name, region, status, ...)  -- full logical design
Internal:   B-tree on (region), heap file, page size 8KB  -- physical storage
# add an index (internal) -> apps unaffected = physical data independence
keypoints: Three levels: external (user views), conceptual (logical schema), internal (physical storage).
Many external views can sit over one conceptual schema over one internal layout.
Logical data independence: change the logical schema without breaking apps/views.
Physical data independence: change storage (indexes, layout) without changing the logical schema.
This separation is why you can tune/refactor storage with zero app changes.
lessons.diagram_caption: External views → conceptual (logical) schema → internal (physical) storage, with data independence.

quiz.q: Adding an index without changing any app relies on:
quiz.explain: Changing physical storage (indexes/layout) without affecting the logical schema is physical data independence.

quiz.q: Which level is the complete logical design (all tables/relationships)?
quiz.explain: The conceptual level is the full logical schema, independent of storage.

quiz.q: External schemas are:
quiz.explain: External schemas are tailored views each user/app sees over the conceptual schema.

exercises.prompt: Name the three schema levels.
exercises.solution: External (user views), conceptual (logical schema), internal (physical storage).

exercises.prompt: Define logical vs physical data independence.
exercises.solution: Logical: change the conceptual schema without breaking external views/apps. Physical: change storage without changing the logical schema.

exercises.prompt: Why does a view help when refactoring a table?
exercises.solution: An external view can keep its shape while the underlying conceptual schema changes — apps using the view are insulated (logical data independence).

lessons.title: Transactions & ACID
lessons.summary: A transaction is an all-or-nothing unit of work; ACID (atomicity, consistency, isolation, durability) is what makes it safe.
lessons.concept:
A **transaction** is a unit of work that must happen **completely or not at all** — the classic example is a money transfer: debit one account **and** credit another. If only half happens, money vanishes. **ACID** is the set of guarantees that make transactions safe:

- **Atomicity** — all steps commit, or all roll back. No partial transactions. (Implemented via the log + rollback.)
- **Consistency** — a transaction moves the database from one **valid** state to another, respecting all constraints. (Bad transactions are rejected/rolled back.)
- **Isolation** — concurrent transactions don't corrupt each other; the result is as if they ran in some serial order. (Implemented via locks or MVCC; tunable via isolation levels.)
- **Durability** — once **committed**, the change survives crashes and power loss. (Implemented via the write-ahead log on durable storage.)

You wrap work in `BEGIN … COMMIT` (or `ROLLBACK`). ACID is the reason a bank, an order system, or any system of record can be trusted. Many NoSQL stores relax some of these (BASE: eventually consistent) to scale — a deliberate trade-off, not a free lunch.
lessons.example:
BEGIN;
  UPDATE accounts SET bal = bal - 100 WHERE id = 1;  -- debit
  UPDATE accounts SET bal = bal + 100 WHERE id = 2;  -- credit
COMMIT;   -- both happen (atomicity) and persist (durability), or neither
keypoints: A transaction is an all-or-nothing unit of work (BEGIN…COMMIT/ROLLBACK).
Atomicity: all steps or none (via log + rollback).
Consistency: only valid states; constraint-violating txns are rejected.
Isolation: concurrent txns don't corrupt each other (locks/MVCC; isolation levels).
Durability: committed changes survive crashes (write-ahead log).
NoSQL often relaxes ACID to BASE (eventual consistency) to scale — a trade-off.
lessons.diagram_caption: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability — the guarantees a transaction provides.

quiz.q: A money transfer debiting one account but not crediting the other violates:
quiz.explain: Atomicity requires all steps to happen or none — a partial transfer breaks it.

quiz.q: Which property ensures committed data survives a crash?
quiz.explain: Durability guarantees committed changes persist, via the write-ahead log.

quiz.q: BASE (eventual consistency) is:
options: a relaxation of ACID some NoSQL stores use to scale
quiz.explain: BASE trades strict consistency/isolation for availability and horizontal scale.

exercises.prompt: Spell out ACID.
exercises.solution: Atomicity (all-or-nothing), Consistency (valid states only), Isolation (concurrent safety), Durability (survives crashes).

exercises.prompt: Why wrap a transfer in a transaction?
exercises.solution: So the debit and credit BOTH commit or BOTH roll back (atomicity) and, once committed, persist through a crash (durability).

exercises.prompt: What do you give up with BASE, and why?
exercises.solution: Strict consistency/isolation (you get eventual consistency) in exchange for higher availability and horizontal scale.

lessons.title: Concurrency control & isolation levels
lessons.summary: How a DBMS lets many transactions run at once without corruption — locks vs MVCC, the read anomalies, and the isolation levels that trade safety for speed.
lessons.concept:
When many transactions run **at once**, they can interfere. Concurrency control prevents corruption while allowing parallelism.

### The anomalies (what can go wrong)
- **Dirty read** — reading another transaction's **uncommitted** change.
- **Non-repeatable read** — re-reading a row gives a **different value** (someone updated+committed in between).
- **Phantom read** — re-running a range query returns **new rows** (someone inserted matching rows).

### Isolation levels (the trade-off)
The SQL standard defines levels that prevent more anomalies as they get stricter — at the cost of concurrency:
- **Read Uncommitted** — fastest, allows all anomalies.
- **Read Committed** — no dirty reads (a common default).
- **Repeatable Read** — no dirty or non-repeatable reads.
- **Serializable** — as if transactions ran one at a time; prevents all anomalies, lowest concurrency.

### How it's enforced
- **Locking** — transactions take shared/exclusive locks (two-phase locking); can cause **deadlocks** (the DBMS detects and aborts one).
- **MVCC (multi-version concurrency control)** — readers see a **consistent snapshot** without blocking writers (Postgres, Oracle). Usually better concurrency than pure locking.

Pick the **lowest isolation level that's correct** for your workload — stricter is safer but slower.
lessons.example:
-- two sessions; isolation level decides what session 2 sees
SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL READ COMMITTED;  -- no dirty reads
-- under MVCC, a reader sees a snapshot as of its start; writers don't block it
keypoints: Concurrency anomalies: dirty read, non-repeatable read, phantom read.
Isolation levels (Read Uncommitted → Committed → Repeatable Read → Serializable) prevent more anomalies, allow less concurrency.
Read Committed is a common default; Serializable is safest but slowest.
Locking (2PL) can deadlock — the DBMS detects and aborts a victim.
MVCC gives readers a consistent snapshot without blocking writers (better concurrency).
Choose the lowest isolation level that's still correct for your workload.
lessons.diagram_caption: Isolation levels vs the anomalies they prevent; enforced by locks or MVCC.

quiz.q: Reading another transaction's uncommitted change is a:
quiz.explain: A dirty read sees uncommitted data that might be rolled back.

quiz.q: Which isolation level prevents ALL anomalies?
quiz.explain: Serializable makes transactions behave as if run one at a time, preventing every anomaly.

quiz.q: MVCC's main advantage over pure locking is:
options: readers get a consistent snapshot without blocking writers
quiz.explain: MVCC keeps row versions so reads don't block writes (and vice versa), improving concurrency.

exercises.prompt: Name the three classic read anomalies.
exercises.solution: Dirty read (uncommitted data), non-repeatable read (changed value on re-read), phantom read (new rows on re-query).

exercises.prompt: Higher isolation = ? trade-off.
exercises.solution: Higher isolation prevents more anomalies but reduces concurrency/throughput; choose the lowest level that's correct.

exercises.prompt: Locking vs MVCC in one line each.
exercises.solution: Locking: take shared/exclusive locks (can deadlock). MVCC: keep row versions so readers see a snapshot without blocking writers.

lessons.title: Recovery & write-ahead logging
lessons.summary: How a DBMS survives crashes: the write-ahead log, REDO/UNDO, and checkpoints — the mechanism behind durability and atomicity.
lessons.concept:
Crashes happen mid-write. **Recovery** is how a DBMS guarantees that, after any failure, the database comes back in a **consistent** state — committed work preserved, uncommitted work undone.

### Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) — the core rule
**Write the change to the log *before* the data.** The log is an append-only, sequential, durable record of every change. Because the log is written first (and flushed on commit), the DBMS can always reconstruct what happened.

### Recovery after a crash
On restart the DBMS replays the log:
- **REDO** — re-apply changes of **committed** transactions that may not have reached the data files yet.
- **UNDO** — roll back changes of transactions that were **not committed** at crash time.

This is exactly how **durability** (committed survives) and **atomicity** (uncommitted is undone) are implemented.

### Checkpoints
Periodically the DBMS writes a **checkpoint** (flushes dirty pages, records a log position) so recovery doesn't have to replay the entire history — only from the last checkpoint. WAL also enables **point-in-time recovery** and **replication** (ship the log to a replica). The log is the unsung hero behind a trustworthy database.
lessons.example:
1) change a row  ->  2) append to WAL (durable, sequential)  ->  3) update data page (later, buffered)
CRASH -> on restart: REDO committed changes, UNDO uncommitted ones, starting from last CHECKPOINT
keypoints: WAL rule: write the change to the durable log BEFORE the data pages.
Recovery replays the log: REDO committed work, UNDO uncommitted work.
This implements durability (committed survives) and atomicity (uncommitted undone).
Checkpoints bound recovery time (replay only from the last checkpoint).
The WAL also powers point-in-time recovery and replication (ship the log).
lessons.diagram_caption: Change → write log first → data pages later; on crash, REDO committed + UNDO uncommitted.

quiz.q: The write-ahead logging rule is:
options: write the change to the log BEFORE the data
quiz.explain: WAL requires the log record to be durable before the data page changes, enabling recovery.

quiz.q: On crash recovery, the DBMS:
options: REDOs committed and UNDOs uncommitted transactions
quiz.explain: It re-applies committed changes (REDO) and rolls back uncommitted ones (UNDO).

quiz.q: Checkpoints exist to:
options: bound recovery time so replay starts from the last checkpoint, not the beginning
quiz.explain: Checkpoints let recovery replay only recent log, not the entire history.

exercises.prompt: State the WAL rule and why it works.
exercises.solution: Write the change to the durable log before the data; since the log is first and flushed on commit, you can always reconstruct a consistent state.

exercises.prompt: What do REDO and UNDO do?
exercises.solution: REDO re-applies committed changes that may not have hit data files; UNDO rolls back changes from uncommitted transactions.

exercises.prompt: Why are checkpoints needed?
exercises.solution: Without them, recovery would replay the entire log; a checkpoint marks a safe position so replay starts recent.

lessons.title: Storage & indexing
lessons.summary: Pages, the buffer pool, and indexes (B-tree, hash) — how a DBMS lays out data and finds rows fast.
lessons.concept:
A DBMS stores tables as **pages** (fixed-size blocks, e.g. 8KB) in files on disk, and caches hot pages in the **buffer pool** in memory. Reading from memory is orders of magnitude faster than disk, so a healthy buffer-pool hit rate is central to performance.

### Indexes — finding rows without scanning everything
Without an index, finding rows means a **full table scan** (read every page). An **index** is a separate structure that lets the DBMS jump straight to matching rows:
- **B-tree (the default)** — a balanced tree keeping keys **sorted**; supports equality **and range** queries in O(log n), and ordered scans. Most indexes are B-trees.
- **Hash index** — O(1) **equality** lookups only (no ranges).
- Specialized: **bitmap** (low-cardinality analytics), **GiST/GIN** (full-text, JSON, geo).

### The trade-off
Indexes make **reads** fast but **slow writes** (every insert/update must maintain them) and use storage. So index the columns you **filter/join/sort** on, not everything. The optimizer uses available indexes (and the catalog's statistics) to choose plans — which is why the right index can turn a slow scan into an instant lookup.
keypoints: Tables are stored as fixed-size pages; the buffer pool caches hot pages in memory.
No index → full table scan; an index lets the DBMS jump to matching rows.
B-tree (default): sorted keys, equality + range + ordered scans, O(log n).
Hash index: O(1) equality only (no ranges).
Indexes speed reads but slow writes and cost storage — index filter/join/sort columns.
The optimizer uses indexes + statistics to pick plans.
lessons.diagram_caption: B-tree index: sorted keys enabling fast equality and range lookups vs a full table scan.

quiz.q: Why is a B-tree the default index type?
options: it supports both equality and range queries (sorted) in O(log n)
quiz.explain: B-trees keep keys sorted, so they handle equality, ranges, and ordered scans efficiently.

quiz.q: A hash index supports:
quiz.explain: Hash indexes give O(1) equality lookups but cannot do range queries.

quiz.q: The main downside of adding indexes is:
options: slower writes + extra storage (indexes must be maintained)
quiz.explain: Every write must update the indexes, and they consume storage — so index selectively.

exercises.prompt: What does an index save you from?
exercises.solution: A full table scan — it lets the DBMS jump directly to matching rows instead of reading every page.

exercises.prompt: B-tree vs hash index?
exercises.solution: B-tree: sorted, supports equality + range + ordered scans (default). Hash: O(1) equality only, no ranges.

exercises.prompt: Which columns should you index?
exercises.solution: Columns you frequently filter, join, or sort on — not every column, since indexes slow writes and cost storage.

lessons.title: Query processing & optimization
lessons.summary: How SQL becomes a fast execution plan: parsing, the optimizer's cost-based plan choice, and why the same query has many possible plans.
lessons.concept:
SQL is **declarative** — you say *what* you want, not *how*. The DBMS figures out the *how*:

1. **Parse** — check syntax and resolve table/column names against the **catalog**, producing a query tree.
2. **Optimize** — the **query optimizer** considers many equivalent **execution plans** (different join orders, join algorithms, index vs scan) and uses **cost estimates** from table **statistics** (row counts, value distributions) to pick the cheapest.
3. **Execute** — the executor runs the chosen plan against the storage engine (buffer pool over data/index files).

### Why it matters
The **same SQL** can run in milliseconds or minutes depending on the plan. Key optimizer decisions:
- **Access path** — index seek vs full scan.
- **Join algorithm** — nested-loop (small), hash join (large unsorted), merge join (sorted).
- **Join order** — filter/aggregate early to shrink intermediate results.

### What you can do
Read the plan with **`EXPLAIN`**; keep **statistics fresh** (so estimates are accurate); add the right **indexes**; and write sargable predicates. Most SQL tuning is really about helping the optimizer find a good plan — you rarely write the plan yourself.
lessons.example:
EXPLAIN SELECT c.name, sum(o.amount)
FROM customers c JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id
WHERE c.region = 'EU' GROUP BY c.name;
-- optimizer picks: index seek on region? hash vs merge join? join order? -> a plan
keypoints: SQL is declarative; the DBMS decides HOW via parse → optimize → execute.
The optimizer compares many equivalent plans using cost estimates from statistics.
Key choices: access path (index vs scan), join algorithm (nested-loop/hash/merge), join order.
The same SQL can be fast or slow depending on the plan chosen.
Use EXPLAIN to read plans; keep statistics fresh; add the right indexes.
Tuning ≈ helping the optimizer find a good plan, not writing the plan yourself.
lessons.diagram_caption: SQL → parser → optimizer (cost-based plan via stats+indexes) → executor → rows.

quiz.q: Why can the same SQL run fast or slow?
options: the optimizer may choose different execution plans (join order/algorithm, index vs scan)
quiz.explain: Performance depends on the plan the optimizer picks for the declarative query.

quiz.q: The optimizer estimates cost using:
options: table statistics (row counts, distributions) + available indexes
quiz.explain: Cost-based optimization relies on statistics and indexes to compare plans.

quiz.q: To investigate a slow query you'd first:
options: run EXPLAIN to see the plan, check stats and indexes
quiz.explain: EXPLAIN reveals the chosen plan; stale stats or missing indexes are common culprits.

exercises.prompt: Name the three stages of query processing.
exercises.solution: Parse (syntax + name resolution), optimize (choose cheapest plan from statistics), execute (run the plan).

exercises.prompt: Three big decisions the optimizer makes?
exercises.solution: Access path (index seek vs full scan), join algorithm (nested-loop/hash/merge), and join order.

exercises.prompt: How do you help the optimizer?
exercises.solution: Keep statistics fresh, add the right indexes, write sargable predicates, and inspect plans with EXPLAIN.

lessons.title: Users, security & the DBA
lessons.summary: Authentication, authorization (GRANT/roles), the catalog, backups, and what a database administrator actually does.
lessons.concept:
A DBMS is multi-user and holds an organization's most sensitive data, so **security and administration** are first-class.

### Security
- **Authentication** — who are you (login, SSO, certificates).
- **Authorization** — what may you do: privileges (`SELECT`, `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`) granted to **users/roles** with **`GRANT`/`REVOKE`**; follow **least privilege**.
- **Roles & RBAC** — bundle privileges into roles (e.g. `analyst`, `app_rw`) and assign to users — far more manageable than per-user grants.
- **Encryption** — at rest and in transit; **row/column-level security** and **masking** for sensitive fields (PII).
- **Auditing** — log who did what.

### Administration (the DBA)
The **DBA** keeps the database healthy: provisioning, **backups & restore testing**, **recovery** planning, performance tuning (indexes, stats, slow queries), capacity planning, upgrades/patching, replication/HA, and managing users/security. The **catalog (data dictionary)** is their map — it describes every object and is queryable (e.g. `information_schema`).

Even on managed cloud databases, these responsibilities don't vanish — they shift; a data engineer must understand grants, backups, and least privilege to build safe systems.
keypoints: Authentication = who you are; authorization = what you may do (GRANT/REVOKE privileges).
Use roles (RBAC) + least privilege instead of per-user grants.
Protect data: encryption at rest/in transit, row/column security, masking PII, auditing.
The DBA handles backups/restore, recovery, tuning, capacity, upgrades, HA/replication, users.
The catalog/data dictionary (e.g. information_schema) describes every object and is queryable.
On managed cloud DBs these duties shift, not disappear — engineers still need grants/backups/least privilege.
lessons.diagram_caption: Authentication proves who you are; authorization (roles + GRANT/REVOKE, least privilege) decides what you may touch; auditing and encryption protect the data.

quiz.q: Granting privileges via roles instead of per user is an example of:
quiz.explain: Role-based access control bundles privileges into roles for manageable, least-privilege access.

quiz.q: Which is a core DBA responsibility?
quiz.explain: DBAs keep the database healthy: backups/restore, recovery, performance, security, availability.

quiz.q: The principle of least privilege means:
options: grant only the privileges a user/role actually needs
quiz.explain: Least privilege limits each user/role to the minimum access required, reducing risk.

exercises.prompt: Authentication vs authorization?
exercises.solution: Authentication verifies who you are (login); authorization controls what you may do (privileges via GRANT/roles).

exercises.prompt: Why use roles (RBAC)?
exercises.solution: Bundling privileges into roles and assigning roles to users is far more manageable and consistent than per-user grants, and supports least privilege.

exercises.prompt: List three DBA duties.
exercises.solution: Backups/restore testing, recovery/HA planning, performance tuning (indexes/stats), plus security/user management, capacity, and upgrades.

lessons.title: DBMS interview prep & cheat sheet
lessons.summary: The DBMS fundamentals interviewers test — ACID, isolation levels, indexing, normalization basics, architecture — on one page.
lessons.concept:
DBMS fundamentals are asked at every level (they're CS foundations). This is your review sheet.

**Architecture:** DBMS vs files (integrity/concurrency/querying/recovery); components = query processor (parser→optimizer→executor) + storage engine (buffer pool + B-tree) + transaction manager + WAL + catalog; three-schema (external/conceptual/internal) → logical & physical **data independence**.

**Transactions:** **ACID** = Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability. Atomicity/durability via the **WAL** (REDO committed, UNDO uncommitted). 

**Concurrency:** anomalies = dirty / non-repeatable / phantom; isolation levels Read Uncommitted → Committed → Repeatable Read → Serializable (stricter = safer, less concurrent); enforced by **locking (2PL, deadlocks)** or **MVCC (snapshots, no read-write blocking)**.

**Storage & queries:** pages + buffer pool; **B-tree** (equality + range) vs **hash** (equality only); index reads-fast/writes-slow; the **optimizer** picks plans from **statistics** + indexes (use `EXPLAIN`).

**Types:** relational vs NoSQL (document/KV/column/graph) vs NewSQL; choose by data shape & access pattern.

**Top questions:** 'Explain ACID.' 'What's a dirty read / which isolation level prevents it?' 'B-tree vs hash index?' 'How does WAL give durability?' 'Why is the same query sometimes slow?' 'DBMS vs file system?'
lessons.example:
# 30-second recall
ACID -> Atomicity/Consistency/Isolation/Durability
Anomalies -> dirty / non-repeatable / phantom -> isolation levels
Index -> B-tree (range) vs hash (equality); reads fast, writes slow
Recovery -> WAL: REDO committed, UNDO uncommitted
Independence -> logical (schema) + physical (storage)
keypoints: DBMS vs files: integrity, concurrency, querying, recovery, security.
ACID + WAL (REDO committed / UNDO uncommitted) = atomicity & durability.
Anomalies (dirty/non-repeatable/phantom) → isolation levels; locks vs MVCC.
B-tree (equality+range) vs hash (equality); indexes trade write speed for read speed.
Optimizer chooses plans from statistics + indexes; use EXPLAIN.
Three-schema architecture → logical & physical data independence.

quiz.q: Best one-line answer to 'what is ACID'?
options: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability — the guarantees of a transaction
quiz.explain: ACID is the four transaction guarantees that make a database trustworthy.

quiz.q: Which prevents a dirty read at minimum?
quiz.explain: Read Committed (and above) prevents dirty reads by only reading committed data.

quiz.q: Why might the same query be slow one day?
options: the optimizer chose a worse plan (stale stats / missing index)
quiz.explain: Plan choice depends on statistics and indexes; stale stats or a missing index degrade it.

exercises.prompt: ACID in four words.
exercises.solution: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability.

exercises.prompt: B-tree vs hash index, one line.
exercises.solution: B-tree: sorted, equality + range; hash: O(1) equality only.

exercises.prompt: How does WAL guarantee durability?
exercises.solution: The change is written to the durable log before commit returns, so on crash the committed change is REDOne from the log.

tracks.title: RDBMS & the Relational Model
tracks.blurb: The relational model in depth — relations, keys, integrity, relational algebra, ER design, normalization, and how real RDBMSs implement ACID. The theory that makes SQL make sense.

lessons.title: The relational model — relations, tuples, domains
lessons.summary: A table is a relation: a set of tuples over typed attributes. The simple math that underlies every SQL database.
lessons.concept:
Edgar Codd's **relational model** (1970) is the theory behind every SQL database. Its beauty is simplicity: data is just **relations** (tables).

### The vocabulary (and the SQL word for each)
- **Relation** = a **table**.
- **Tuple** = a **row** — one record.
- **Attribute** = a **column** — a named field.
- **Domain** = the **type / set of allowed values** for an attribute (e.g. `country ∈ {NA, EU, APAC, ...}`, `age` is a non-negative integer).
- **Degree** = number of attributes (columns); **cardinality** = number of tuples (rows).

### The rules that make it 'relational'
- **Atomic values** — each cell holds **one** value (first normal form); no lists inside a cell.
- **Unordered, unique rows** — a relation is a **set** of tuples; order doesn't matter and there are no duplicate rows (a key enforces uniqueness).
- **Unordered columns** — you reference columns by name, not position.

Because relations are just sets, you can manipulate them with **relational algebra** (next lessons) and a clean declarative language — **SQL**. This mathematical foundation is *why* relational databases are so powerful and predictable: every query is an operation on sets of tuples.
lessons.example:
STUDENT (a relation)
  attributes/columns →  student_id | name | country     (each with a DOMAIN)
  tuple/row →           S1        | Ana  | NA
                        S2        | Beck | EU
# rows are unordered & unique; every cell holds one atomic value
keypoints: The relational model represents data as relations (tables) — sets of tuples.
Relation=table, tuple=row, attribute=column, domain=allowed values for a column.
Degree = #columns; cardinality = #rows.
Rules: atomic cell values, unordered & unique rows, columns referenced by name.
Because relations are sets, SQL + relational algebra operate on them cleanly — the model's power.
lessons.diagram_caption: A table is a relation: tuples (rows) over attributes (columns), each attribute drawn from a domain.

quiz.q: In relational terms, a row is a:
quiz.explain: A tuple is a single row/record in a relation.

quiz.q: A domain is:
options: the set of allowed values (type) for an attribute
quiz.explain: The domain constrains what values a column may hold.

quiz.q: Which is a rule of the relational model?
options: each cell holds one atomic value; rows are unordered & unique
quiz.explain: Relations are sets of tuples with atomic values, unordered and unique.

exercises.prompt: Map relation/tuple/attribute/domain to SQL words.
exercises.solution: Relation=table, tuple=row, attribute=column, domain=the column's type/allowed values.

exercises.prompt: Why are rows considered a 'set'?
exercises.solution: A relation is a set of tuples — order is irrelevant and duplicate rows aren't allowed (enforced by a key).

exercises.prompt: What does 'atomic values' (1NF) mean?
exercises.solution: Each cell holds a single indivisible value — no lists/arrays/repeating groups inside a cell.

lessons.title: Keys — identifying rows & linking tables
lessons.summary: Super, candidate, primary, composite, and foreign keys — how relations identify rows and connect to each other.
lessons.concept:
Keys are how the relational model **identifies rows** and **links tables**.

### Key types (within a table)
- **Super key** — any set of columns that **uniquely** identifies a row (may have extra columns).
- **Candidate key** — a **minimal** super key (no column can be removed and still be unique). A table can have several.
- **Primary key (PK)** — the candidate key you **choose** as *the* identifier. Must be **unique and NOT NULL** (entity integrity).
- **Alternate key** — a candidate key not chosen as PK.
- **Composite key** — a key made of **two or more columns** (e.g. `(order_id, line_no)`).

Nesting: **super ⊇ candidate ⊇ primary**.

### Foreign keys (between tables)
A **foreign key (FK)** is a column (or set) in one table that **references the primary key** of another, creating the relationship. The DBMS enforces **referential integrity**: an FK value must match an existing PK (or be NULL) — no orphan rows.

```
STUDENT(student_id PK, email, ...)
ENROLLMENT(course_id, student_id FK → STUDENT.student_id)
```

### Natural vs surrogate
A **natural key** is real-world (email, SSN); a **surrogate key** is a meaningless generated integer. Warehouses favor surrogates (see the Data Modeling track); OLTP often uses natural or surrogate PKs. Keys are the backbone of both integrity and joins.
lessons.example:
STUDENT(student_id PK, email)        -- email is a candidate (alternate) key
ENROLLMENT(course_id, student_id FK)  -- FK must match a STUDENT.student_id
-- super ⊇ candidate ⊇ primary ; composite key = (course_id, student_id)
keypoints: Super key: any uniquely-identifying column set; candidate key: a minimal super key.
Primary key: the chosen candidate — unique + NOT NULL (entity integrity).
Composite key: 2+ columns together; alternate key: an unchosen candidate.
Foreign key references another table's PK → referential integrity (no orphans).
Natural (real-world) vs surrogate (generated integer) keys; warehouses favor surrogates.
Keys power both row identity and joins between tables.
lessons.diagram_caption: Super ⊇ candidate ⊇ primary; a foreign key references another table's PK to link them.

quiz.q: A candidate key is:
options: a MINIMAL super key (no removable column)
quiz.explain: A candidate key uniquely identifies rows with no unnecessary columns; the PK is a chosen candidate.

quiz.q: A primary key must be:
quiz.explain: Entity integrity requires the PK to be unique and non-null so every row is identifiable.

quiz.q: A foreign key enforces:
options: referential integrity (must match a PK or be NULL)
quiz.explain: FKs ensure references point to existing PK values — no orphan rows.

exercises.prompt: Order these by generality: primary, super, candidate.
exercises.solution: super ⊇ candidate ⊇ primary (super is broadest; primary is one chosen minimal candidate).

exercises.prompt: What two properties must a primary key have?
exercises.solution: Unique and NOT NULL.

exercises.prompt: What does a foreign key do and guarantee?
exercises.solution: It references another table's primary key to model a relationship, and enforces referential integrity (the value must match an existing PK or be NULL).

lessons.title: Integrity constraints
lessons.summary: Entity, referential, and domain integrity — the rules an RDBMS enforces so data stays correct automatically.
lessons.concept:
**Integrity constraints** are rules the RDBMS enforces on every write, so bad data is **rejected automatically** — correctness is built in, not left to application code.

### The three core constraints
- **Entity integrity** — the **primary key** is **unique** and **NOT NULL**, so every row is identifiable. (No two rows share a PK; no row has a missing PK.)
- **Referential integrity** — a **foreign key** must match an **existing primary key** in the referenced table (or be NULL). This prevents **orphan** rows (e.g. an order pointing to a non-existent customer). You also choose **ON DELETE/UPDATE** behavior: `RESTRICT`, `CASCADE`, or `SET NULL`.
- **Domain integrity** — every value fits its column's **type, range, and rules**: data types, `NOT NULL`, `CHECK` (e.g. `age >= 0`), `UNIQUE`, and `DEFAULT`.

### Why it matters
Constraints make the **database** the guardian of correctness — if app code has a bug, the DBMS still refuses invalid data. This is far safer than relying on every application to validate, and it's exactly the property that makes relational databases trustworthy systems of record. (It's also why the bank capstone's referential-integrity check catches orphan accounts.)
keypoints: Constraints are enforced by the DBMS on every write → bad data rejected automatically.
Entity integrity: PK unique + NOT NULL (every row identifiable).
Referential integrity: FK matches an existing PK (or NULL) → no orphans; ON DELETE CASCADE/RESTRICT/SET NULL.
Domain integrity: types, NOT NULL, CHECK, UNIQUE, DEFAULT — valid values only.
Database-enforced integrity is safer than relying on every app to validate.
lessons.diagram_caption: Entity (PK unique+not null), referential (FK→PK), and domain (valid values) integrity.

quiz.q: Entity integrity requires the primary key to be:
quiz.explain: So every row is uniquely identifiable, the PK must be unique and non-null.

quiz.q: Referential integrity prevents:
options: orphan rows (FK pointing to a non-existent PK)
quiz.explain: An FK must match an existing PK (or be NULL), so no row references something that doesn't exist.

quiz.q: A CHECK (age >= 0) constraint enforces:
quiz.explain: Domain integrity restricts values to a valid range/rule for the column.

exercises.prompt: Name the three integrity constraint types.
exercises.solution: Entity integrity (PK unique+not null), referential integrity (FK→PK), domain integrity (valid types/values).

exercises.prompt: What does ON DELETE CASCADE do?
exercises.solution: When a referenced PK row is deleted, its referencing FK rows are automatically deleted too (vs RESTRICT which blocks, or SET NULL).

exercises.prompt: Why enforce integrity in the DB, not just the app?
exercises.solution: The database rejects invalid data even if application code has a bug — a single, reliable guardian of correctness.

lessons.title: Relational algebra — the operators behind SQL
lessons.summary: Select, project, join, union, intersect, difference, product, rename — the operations SQL compiles down to.
lessons.concept:
**Relational algebra** is the formal set of operations on relations. SQL is a friendly language **over** this algebra — knowing the operators makes SQL (and query plans) click.

### The operators
- **σ Select (sigma)** — keep **rows** matching a condition → SQL `WHERE`.
- **π Project (pi)** — keep certain **columns** (and dedupe) → SQL `SELECT col, ...`.
- **⋈ Join** — combine two relations on matching attributes → SQL `JOIN`. (Natural/theta/equi-joins.)
- **× Cartesian product** — every combination of rows from A and B → SQL `CROSS JOIN`.
- **∪ Union / ∩ Intersect / − Difference** — set operations on **union-compatible** relations → SQL `UNION` / `INTERSECT` / `EXCEPT`.
- **ρ Rename** — alias a relation/attribute → SQL `AS`.

### Why it matters
Every SQL query maps to a tree of these operators, and the **optimizer rewrites** that tree into an equivalent, cheaper one (e.g. **push selections down** so you filter before joining — far less data to join). That's why `WHERE` before a big `JOIN` matters, and why understanding the algebra helps you read `EXPLAIN` plans and write efficient SQL. A join is just `σ` (the condition) over a `×` (the product), which the engine implements smartly with hash/merge joins.
lessons.example:
SQL:  SELECT name FROM students WHERE country='EU'
Algebra:  π_name( σ_country='EU' (students) )
# optimizer pushes σ (filter) below ⋈ (join) so less data is joined
keypoints: Relational algebra = the formal operators on relations; SQL is a language over it.
σ select (rows/WHERE), π project (columns/SELECT), ⋈ join, × product (CROSS JOIN).
∪ union, ∩ intersect, − difference (on union-compatible relations); ρ rename (AS).
Every SQL query maps to an operator tree the optimizer rewrites to an equivalent cheaper one.
Pushing selection (σ) below join (⋈) = filter before joining = much less work.
A join is conceptually σ over × — implemented as hash/merge/nested-loop joins.
lessons.diagram_caption: σ select · π project · ⋈ join · × product · ∪ ∩ − set ops · ρ rename — SQL compiles to these.

quiz.q: Which operator keeps only certain columns?
quiz.explain: Projection (π) selects columns (and dedupes); selection (σ) filters rows.

quiz.q: 'Push selection down' means:
options: filter (σ) before the join so less data is joined
quiz.explain: Filtering early shrinks inputs to the join — a key optimizer rewrite.

quiz.q: UNION/INTERSECT/EXCEPT require the relations to be:
quiz.explain: Set operations need union-compatible relations (matching column structure/types).

exercises.prompt: Map σ and π to SQL.
exercises.solution: σ (select) → WHERE (filter rows); π (project) → SELECT columns (and dedupe).

exercises.prompt: Express SELECT name FROM s WHERE country='EU' in algebra.
exercises.solution: π_name( σ_country='EU' (s) ).

exercises.prompt: Why push selection below join?
exercises.solution: Filtering before joining reduces the rows fed into the join, so the join does far less work — a standard optimizer rewrite.

lessons.title: ER modeling → relational mapping
lessons.summary: Entity-Relationship diagrams (entities, relationships, attributes, cardinality) and how to turn them into tables with keys.
lessons.concept:
Before tables, you model the business with an **Entity-Relationship (ER) diagram** — a picture of *things* and how they relate. Then you mechanically **map it to relations**.

### ER building blocks
- **Entity** — a thing (Customer, Order) → drawn as a **rectangle** → becomes a **table**.
- **Attribute** — a property (name, amount) → **oval** → becomes a **column**. A **key attribute** is underlined → PK.
- **Relationship** — how entities relate (Customer *places* Order) → **diamond**.
- **Cardinality** — how many: **1:1, 1:N, M:N**.

### Mapping ER → tables
- **Entity** → a table; its key attribute → the **primary key**.
- **1:N relationship** → put a **foreign key** on the **'many'** side (Order gets `customer_id`).
- **M:N relationship** → create a **junction (bridge) table** with FKs to both sides (Student ↔ Course → `enrollment(student_id, course_id)`).
- **1:1** → FK on either side (often merged).
- **Multi-valued attribute** → its own table.

### Why
ER modeling forces you to get the **business logic** right (what are the entities, how do they relate, what's unique) before committing to SQL — and the mapping rules make the translation to a correct, normalized schema almost mechanical. It's the bridge from requirements to a relational design.
lessons.example:
ER:  CUSTOMER  --< places >--  ORDER     (1:N)
Map: customers(id PK, name)
     orders(id PK, amount, customer_id FK -> customers.id)   -- FK on the 'many' side
# M:N example: students <-> courses  =>  enrollment(student_id FK, course_id FK)
keypoints: ER diagram models entities (rectangles), relationships (diamonds), attributes (ovals), cardinality (1:1/1:N/M:N).
Map: entity→table, key attribute→PK, attribute→column.
1:N → foreign key on the 'many' side.
M:N → a junction/bridge table with FKs to both entities.
ER modeling nails the business logic first; mapping to a normalized schema is then mechanical.
lessons.diagram_caption: Entities (rectangles), relationship (diamond), attributes (ovals), 1:N cardinality → tables with a foreign key.

quiz.q: A 1:N relationship maps to:
options: a foreign key on the 'many' side
quiz.explain: Put the FK on the many side (e.g. orders.customer_id) to represent one customer → many orders.

quiz.q: An M:N relationship requires:
options: a junction/bridge table with FKs to both entities
quiz.explain: Many-to-many needs a junction table (e.g. enrollment) holding FKs to both sides.

quiz.q: In an ER diagram, a diamond represents a:
quiz.explain: Diamonds are relationships; rectangles are entities; ovals are attributes.

exercises.prompt: Map the ER shapes to relational pieces.
exercises.solution: Entity→table, key attribute→PK, attribute→column, 1:N→FK on the many side, M:N→junction table.

exercises.prompt: How do you model students enrolling in many courses (and vice versa)?
exercises.solution: A junction table enrollment(student_id FK, course_id FK), with the pair as a composite key.

exercises.prompt: Why model with ER before writing SQL?
exercises.solution: It captures the business entities, relationships, and uniqueness correctly first, making the translation to a normalized schema almost mechanical.

lessons.title: Normalization — 1NF to BCNF
lessons.summary: Removing redundancy and anomalies step by step: 1NF, 2NF, 3NF, BCNF — what each fixes, with examples.
lessons.concept:
**Normalization** organizes columns into tables to eliminate **redundancy** and the **update anomalies** it causes. You apply normal forms in order; each fixes a specific problem.

### The anomalies it prevents
If you cram everything into one table, you get **update** anomalies (change a value in many rows), **insert** anomalies (can't add a course with no students), and **delete** anomalies (deleting the last enrollment loses the course).

### The forms
- **1NF** — **atomic values**, no repeating groups (one value per cell; no comma-lists or repeated `phone1, phone2` columns).
- **2NF** — 1NF **+ no partial dependency**: every non-key column depends on the **whole** composite key, not just part of it. (Split out columns that depend on only part of the key.)
- **3NF** — 2NF **+ no transitive dependency**: non-key columns depend **only on the key**, not on another non-key column. (e.g. `zip → city` doesn't belong in an orders table.)
- **BCNF** — a stricter 3NF: **every determinant is a candidate key**. Handles edge cases 3NF misses.

Mnemonic: *every non-key column depends on **the key, the whole key, and nothing but the key**.*

### The trade-off
Normalize **OLTP** (aim for **3NF/BCNF**) for integrity and small safe writes. **Denormalize** for analytics (star schemas, OBT) for read speed — see the Data Modeling track. Knowing both directions is the skill.
lessons.example:
Unnormalized: orders(order_id, items='A,B', cust, cust_city)
1NF: split items to one row each (atomic)
2NF: move cust attributes out of a (order_id,item) composite-key table
3NF: cust_city depends on cust, not the order key -> move to a customer table
keypoints: Normalization removes redundancy and update/insert/delete anomalies.
1NF: atomic values, no repeating groups.
2NF: 1NF + no partial dependency on part of a composite key.
3NF: 2NF + no transitive dependency (non-keys depend only on the key).
BCNF: stricter 3NF — every determinant is a candidate key.
Normalize OLTP (3NF/BCNF); denormalize analytics for read speed — know both directions.
lessons.diagram_caption: 1NF → 2NF → 3NF → BCNF: each removes a kind of redundancy/anomaly.

quiz.q: 1NF requires:
quiz.explain: First normal form means each cell holds one atomic value with no repeating groups.

quiz.q: 3NF removes:
quiz.explain: 3NF eliminates transitive dependencies so non-key columns depend only on the key.

quiz.q: Which form do OLTP schemas typically target?
quiz.explain: OLTP normalizes to 3NF/BCNF for integrity; analytics denormalizes for read speed.

exercises.prompt: Give the 3NF mnemonic.
exercises.solution: Every non-key column depends on the key, the whole key, and nothing but the key.

exercises.prompt: What problem does each form remove? (1NF/2NF/3NF).
exercises.solution: 1NF: non-atomic/repeating groups; 2NF: partial dependency on part of a composite key; 3NF: transitive dependency (non-key → non-key).

exercises.prompt: When do you intentionally denormalize?
exercises.solution: For read-heavy analytics (star schemas, OBT) where avoiding joins matters more than write-time integrity — the data is rebuilt from a normalized source.

lessons.title: How an RDBMS implements ACID
lessons.summary: Inside a real RDBMS: transactions, isolation levels, MVCC vs locking, and the WAL — how the theory becomes a working engine.
lessons.concept:
ACID is a promise; here's how real RDBMSs (Postgres, MySQL/InnoDB, Oracle, SQL Server) **keep** it.

### Atomicity & Durability — the log
Every change is written to a **write-ahead log (WAL)** *before* the data pages. On `COMMIT`, the log is flushed to durable storage → **durability**. On crash, recovery **REDOes** committed and **UNDOes** uncommitted transactions → **atomicity**.

### Isolation — MVCC vs locking
- **MVCC (Postgres, Oracle, InnoDB)** — each write creates a **new row version**; readers see a **consistent snapshot** as of their start, so **readers never block writers** and vice versa. Old versions are cleaned up later (Postgres `VACUUM`).
- **Locking (2PL)** — shared/exclusive locks serialize conflicting access; simpler but more contention and possible **deadlocks** (detected and aborted).

### Isolation levels (the dial)
Read Committed (common default) → Repeatable Read → Serializable trade concurrency for fewer anomalies (dirty/non-repeatable/phantom). Postgres implements Serializable via **SSI** (serializable snapshot isolation).

### Consistency
Upheld by **constraints** (PK/FK/CHECK) plus the above — a transaction that would violate a constraint is rolled back.

Understanding this explains real behavior: why `VACUUM`/bloat exists, why long transactions hurt, why isolation level affects both correctness and throughput.
lessons.example:
BEGIN;                          -- start txn
  UPDATE accounts ...;          -- new row version (MVCC) + WAL record
COMMIT;                          -- flush WAL -> durable (durability); versions visible
-- crash? recovery: REDO committed, UNDO uncommitted (atomicity)
keypoints: Atomicity+durability via WAL: log before data; commit flushes log; recovery REDO/UNDO.
MVCC: writes create row versions; readers see a snapshot → no read/write blocking (Postgres/Oracle/InnoDB).
Locking (2PL): serialize conflicts; simpler but contention + deadlocks.
Isolation levels (Read Committed→Repeatable Read→Serializable) dial concurrency vs anomalies.
Consistency = constraints (PK/FK/CHECK) + rollback of violating transactions.
This explains VACUUM/bloat, why long transactions hurt, and isolation's effect on throughput.
lessons.diagram_caption: RDBMS isolation levels & anomalies, enforced by MVCC (snapshots) or locking (2PL), with WAL for durability.

quiz.q: How does an RDBMS guarantee durability on commit?
options: flush the write-ahead log to durable storage before returning
quiz.explain: WAL is flushed on commit, so committed changes survive a crash.

quiz.q: MVCC's key benefit is:
options: readers see a snapshot and don't block writers (and vice versa)
quiz.explain: Multi-version concurrency control avoids read/write blocking by versioning rows.

quiz.q: Postgres VACUUM exists because MVCC:
options: leaves old row versions that must be cleaned up
quiz.explain: MVCC creates new versions on update/delete; VACUUM reclaims the dead ones.

exercises.prompt: How are atomicity and durability implemented?
exercises.solution: Write-ahead log: changes logged before data; commit flushes the log (durability); crash recovery REDOes committed and UNDOes uncommitted (atomicity).

exercises.prompt: MVCC vs locking, one line each.
exercises.solution: MVCC: version rows so readers see a snapshot without blocking writers. Locking (2PL): take shared/exclusive locks to serialize conflicts (can deadlock).

exercises.prompt: Why do long-running transactions hurt under MVCC?
exercises.solution: They hold an old snapshot, preventing cleanup (VACUUM) of dead row versions, causing bloat and degraded performance.

lessons.title: Indexes & join algorithms
lessons.summary: How RDBMSs find rows fast (B-tree indexes) and combine tables (nested-loop, hash, merge joins).
lessons.concept:
Two engine mechanics decide most RDBMS query performance: **indexes** (finding rows) and **join algorithms** (combining tables).

### Indexes
Without one, a predicate means a **full table scan**. A **B-tree index** keeps keys sorted for **O(log n)** equality *and* range lookups, plus ordered scans — the default. **Hash** indexes do O(1) equality only. Indexes speed reads but slow writes and cost storage, so index the columns you **filter/join/order** on. The optimizer also loves a **covering index** (one that contains all columns a query needs, so it never touches the table).

### Join algorithms (how the optimizer combines tables)
- **Nested-loop join** — for each row of A, look up matches in B (great when one side is small / indexed).
- **Hash join** — build a hash table on the smaller side, probe with the larger; best for **large, unsorted, equality** joins.
- **Merge join** — both inputs **sorted** on the key, then merged; great when inputs are already sorted (or indexed) — good for range/sorted output.

The optimizer picks the algorithm and **join order** from statistics. Knowing these explains `EXPLAIN` output and why the right index (or up-to-date stats) flips a slow hash-of-everything into a fast indexed nested-loop. (This is the single-node cousin of Spark's broadcast/sort-merge joins.)
keypoints: No index → full table scan; B-tree (default) gives O(log n) equality + range + ordered scans.
Hash index: O(1) equality only; covering index: contains all needed columns (skips the table).
Index the columns you filter/join/order on; indexes slow writes + cost storage.
Nested-loop join: small/indexed side; hash join: large unsorted equality; merge join: sorted inputs.
The optimizer picks join algorithm + order from statistics (read it with EXPLAIN).
Same idea as Spark joins (broadcast≈nested-loop on small, sort-merge≈merge) but single-node.
lessons.diagram_caption: B-tree index (sorted keys) for fast lookups; optimizer joins via nested-loop / hash / merge.

quiz.q: Which join is best for two large, unsorted tables on an equality key?
quiz.explain: Hash join builds a hash on the smaller side and probes — ideal for large unsorted equality joins.

quiz.q: A covering index is one that:
options: contains all columns a query needs, so the table isn't read
quiz.explain: If the index has every column the query needs, the engine answers from the index alone.

quiz.q: Merge join works best when inputs are:
options: already sorted on the join key
quiz.explain: Merge join merges two sorted inputs, so it shines when data is sorted/indexed on the key.

exercises.prompt: B-tree vs hash index?
exercises.solution: B-tree: sorted, equality + range + ordered scans (default). Hash: O(1) equality only, no ranges.

exercises.prompt: Name the three join algorithms and when each wins.
exercises.solution: Nested-loop (one side small/indexed), hash join (large unsorted equality), merge join (inputs sorted on the key).

exercises.prompt: How do RDBMS joins relate to Spark joins?
exercises.solution: Same concepts: broadcast≈nested-loop on a small side, sort-merge≈merge join — RDBMS is single-node, Spark distributes them.

lessons.title: RDBMS vs NoSQL — choosing the right store
lessons.summary: When the relational model is the right choice and when NoSQL/NewSQL fits — by data shape, consistency, scale, and access pattern.
lessons.concept:
The relational model is powerful but not universal. Knowing when to use an RDBMS vs NoSQL is a core senior judgment.

### RDBMS strengths
- **Structured data + strong schema/typing** and **integrity constraints**.
- **ACID transactions** — correctness for money, orders, systems of record.
- **Powerful joins + SQL** — flexible ad-hoc querying.
- Scales **up** (and, with read replicas/partitioning, somewhat out).
- Best for: OLTP, BI/analytics, anything needing integrity and complex queries.

### NoSQL strengths (document / key-value / column / graph)
- **Flexible / schema-light** — store varied, evolving shapes.
- **Horizontal scale** — built to shard across many nodes for huge volume/throughput.
