The choice follows the workload: writes→normalize (no redundancy), reads→denormalize (avoid joins).
Warehouse redundancy is acceptable because it's read-mostly and rebuilt by pipelines, not edited live.
Data engineers transform normalized OLTP data into denormalized analytical models (ETL/ELT).
Neither paradigm is universally 'correct' — each fits its side of the workload.
lessons.diagram_caption: OLTP normalizes (each fact once); analytics denormalizes the same data into flat, join-light tables.

quiz.q: Why do OLTP systems normalize?
options: For write integrity — each fact stored once, no update anomalies
quiz.explain: Write-heavy operational systems normalize so data is stored once and stays consistent under many concurrent updates — integrity over read convenience.

quiz.q: Why do analytical warehouses denormalize?
options: Read-heavy queries run faster with few joins; redundancy is acceptable in a rebuilt, read-mostly store
quiz.explain: Analytics scans and aggregates; flat denormalized models minimize joins for speed, and the read-mostly warehouse tolerates the redundancy.

quiz.q: What's a core part of the data engineer's job here?
options: Transform normalized OLTP data into denormalized analytical models
quiz.explain: Engineers build pipelines that reshape the app's normalized tables into facts/dimensions (or wide tables) optimized for analytical reads.

exercises.prompt: An app DB is in 3NF. A dashboard joining 6 tables is slow. Explain the standard warehouse fix and why redundancy is acceptable there.
exercises.hint: Denormalize into a star/wide table; read-mostly.
exercises.solution: Build a **denormalized analytical model** — a star schema or wide table — via a pipeline that pre-joins those 6 tables into a `fact` plus flat dimensions, so the dashboard reads with **one or two joins** instead of six. Redundancy (e.g. region duplicated across customer rows) is **acceptable** because the warehouse is **read-mostly and rebuilt by the pipeline**, not updated row-by-row by users — so there are no update anomalies to fear, and the read speed is worth it.

exercises.prompt: Why is normalization the right choice for the operational app but the wrong default for the warehouse?
exercises.solution: The app is **write-heavy and integrity-critical**: normalization stores each fact once so concurrent updates can't create inconsistencies (anomalies). The warehouse is **read-heavy and speed-critical**: there, normalization forces many joins that slow analytical scans, so denormalization (flat dimensions/wide tables) is preferred. The 'right' model follows the **workload**, which differs between the two systems.

exercises.prompt: True or false: denormalization means your data modeling is sloppy. Justify.
exercises.solution: **False.** Denormalization in a warehouse is a **deliberate, principled trade-off** — duplicating data to eliminate joins and accelerate read-heavy analytics — not a failure to normalize. It's appropriate precisely because the warehouse is read-mostly and rebuilt by pipelines. Sloppy modeling would be mixing grains or undocumented duplication; intentional denormalization for query speed is sound dimensional design.

lessons.title: Star schemas — the analytics workhorse
lessons.summary: Why a central fact surrounded by denormalized dimensions is the default analytics design: simple joins, fast queries, intuitive for BI — plus the conformed-dimension bus.
lessons.concept:
A **star schema** places one **fact table** in the center, joined directly to a ring of **dimension tables** — drawn out, it looks like a star. It's the default model for analytics, and for good reasons.

### The shape
```
        dim_date
           |
dim_customer — FACT_SALES — dim_product
           |
        dim_store
```
Every dimension is **one join away** from the fact (a single level), and dimensions are **denormalized** — each holds all its descriptive attributes in one flat table (e.g. `dim_product` carries product, category, **and** department together, not split into sub-tables).

### Why star schemas win for analytics
- **Simple queries** — answer most questions by joining the fact to a few dimensions; no deep join chains.
- **Fast** — few, predictable joins on small dimensions and a big fact; engines optimize this pattern (star-join optimization, broadcast small dims).
- **Intuitive** — the structure mirrors how the business thinks ('sales by product by month'), so BI tools and analysts navigate it easily.

This deliberately **denormalizes** (vs OLTP's normalized 3NF): some redundancy in dimensions is the price of join-free simplicity and speed for read-heavy analytics.

### Conformed dimensions — the bus
A **conformed dimension** is shared across multiple fact tables with the **same meaning** (one `dim_date`, one `dim_customer` used by sales, returns, and support). Conforming dimensions lets you compare facts consistently ('sales vs returns by the same customer/date') — Kimball's **bus architecture**: build star schemas that share conformed dimensions so the warehouse integrates over time.

### Star vs snowflake
A **snowflake** schema normalizes dimensions into sub-tables (more joins). Star is usually preferred for simplicity and speed; snowflake only when a dimension is huge or shared (next lessons).
lessons.example: `SELECT p.category, d.year, SUM(f.amount) FROM fact_sales f JOIN dim_product p ON … JOIN dim_date d ON … GROUP BY p.category, d.year;` — a textbook star query: fact in the middle, two small dimensions one join away, grouped by their attributes.
keypoints: Star schema = central fact + denormalized dimensions each one join away (looks like a star).
Dimensions are flat/denormalized (all attributes in one table) for join-free simplicity.
Wins for analytics: simple queries, fast predictable joins, intuitive for BI/analysts.
It deliberately denormalizes vs OLTP 3NF — redundancy traded for read speed.
Conformed dimensions are shared across facts with identical meaning (one dim_date/dim_customer).
Kimball's bus architecture: many star schemas sharing conformed dimensions integrate the warehouse.
lessons.diagram_caption: A central fact joined to denormalized dimensions — the default, fast, intuitive analytics shape.

quiz.q: In a star schema, how far is each dimension from the fact?
quiz.explain: Star dimensions are denormalized into single flat tables, so each is one join from the fact — simple, fast queries. Snowflaking would add more join levels.

quiz.q: What is a conformed dimension?
options: A dimension shared across multiple fact tables with the same meaning (e.g. one dim_date)
quiz.explain: Conformed dimensions are reused identically across facts, enabling consistent cross-process comparison — the basis of Kimball's bus architecture.

quiz.q: Why does a star schema denormalize dimensions?
options: To trade some redundancy for simple, fast, join-light analytics queries
quiz.explain: Read-heavy analytics favors flat dimensions (all attributes together) so queries need few joins and run fast — the opposite priority to OLTP's normalized design.

exercises.prompt: Sketch the tables (fact + dimensions) for a star schema answering 'revenue by product category by month by region'.
exercises.hint: One fact + date, product, region dimensions.
exercises.solution: **fact_sales** (grain: order line) with `amount`, plus FKs `date_key`, `product_key`, `region_key`. **dim_date** (date_key, year, quarter, month), **dim_product** (product_key, product, **category**, department), **dim_region** (region_key, region, country). The question is answered by `SUM(amount)` joined to the three dimensions, grouped by `category`, `month`, `region` — each dimension one join away.

exercises.prompt: Why are conformed dimensions important when you have separate sales and returns fact tables?
exercises.solution: If both `fact_sales` and `fact_returns` use the **same** `dim_product`, `dim_date`, and `dim_customer`, you can compare them consistently — e.g. 'return rate by category by month' — because the keys and attribute meanings match exactly. Without conformed dimensions, each fact would define 'product' or 'month' slightly differently, and cross-fact analysis would be unreliable. Conformed dimensions are what let the warehouse integrate multiple processes.

exercises.prompt: An analyst finds a query joins the fact through product → category → department sub-tables (three levels). Is this star or snowflake, and what's the trade-off?
exercises.solution: That's a **snowflake** schema — the product dimension is **normalized** into category and department sub-tables, adding join levels. Trade-off: it **reduces redundancy/storage** and can ease maintenance of shared hierarchies, but at the cost of **more joins and complexity**, usually slower and less intuitive for BI. A **star** (flat `dim_product` holding product, category, department together) is generally preferred unless the dimension is very large or the hierarchy is widely reused.

lessons.title: Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCD)
lessons.summary: How dimensions change over time without losing history: SCD Type 1 (overwrite), Type 2 (versioned rows — the key one), Type 3 (previous-value column), and how to query them.
lessons.concept:
Dimension attributes change — a customer moves city, a product is recategorized. **Slowly Changing Dimension (SCD)** techniques decide **what happens to history** when they do. The choice changes your analysis profoundly.

### Type 1 — overwrite (no history)
Just **update** the attribute in place. Simple, but you **lose the past**: after the customer moves, *all* their historical sales now appear under the new city — as if they always lived there. Use Type 1 for corrections (fixing a typo) where the old value was simply wrong.

### Type 2 — add a new row (full history) ← the important one
Keep the old row and **insert a new version** with the changed attribute, tracking validity:

```
cust_key  customer_id  city     valid_from   valid_to     is_current
101       42           Boston   2023-01-01   2025-04-30   false
205       42           Denver   2025-05-01   9999-12-31   true
```
Note `customer_id` (the natural/business key) stays 42, but each version gets a **new surrogate key** (`cust_key`). Facts point at the surrogate key **in effect at the time**, so old sales stay tied to Boston and new ones to Denver — **history is preserved**. This is the default for analytics that must reflect 'as it was then'.

### Type 3 — previous-value column (limited history)
Add a `previous_city` column alongside `current_city`. Keeps only **one** prior value — useful for 'before/after' analysis of a single change, not full history.

### Querying SCD2
- **Current view:** `WHERE is_current = true`.
- **As-of a date:** `WHERE event_date BETWEEN valid_from AND valid_to` — point each fact at the version valid then.

Building SCD2 is the canonical use of the **dedup-to-latest / merge** pattern: detect changed rows, expire the old version (set `valid_to`/`is_current=false`), insert the new one.
lessons.example: Customer 42 moves Boston → Denver. **Type 1** would make all their past orders look like Denver orders. **Type 2** inserts a new versioned row (new surrogate key, `valid_from=2025-05-01`), so past orders remain attributed to Boston and future ones to Denver — accurate history.
keypoints: SCD techniques decide what happens to history when a dimension attribute changes.
Type 1: overwrite in place — simple but loses history (use for corrections).
Type 2: insert a new versioned row with valid_from/valid_to/is_current — preserves full history (the key one).
In SCD2 the natural key stays the same but each version gets a new surrogate key; facts point at the version in effect.
Type 3: a previous-value column keeps just one prior value (before/after analysis).
Query SCD2 with is_current=true (now) or valid_from/valid_to (as-of a date); build it via merge/expire-and-insert.
lessons.diagram_caption: SCD2 expires the old row and inserts a new versioned row, so facts keep their as-of-then context.

quiz.q: A customer moves city. Under SCD Type 2, what happens?
options: A new versioned row is inserted (new surrogate key, valid_from); the old row is expired — history preserved
quiz.explain: Type 2 keeps the old row (expired) and adds a new version, so historical facts stay tied to the old city and new facts to the new one.

quiz.q: When is SCD Type 1 (overwrite) appropriate?
options: For corrections — fixing a wrong value where the old one was simply an error
quiz.explain: Type 1 discards history, which is fine for fixing typos/errors but wrong when the past value was legitimately true and must be retained.

quiz.q: In SCD2, what stays constant across a customer's versions and what changes?
options: The natural/business key (customer_id) stays; each version gets a new surrogate key
The surrogate key stays the same
quiz.explain: The business key identifies the same real customer across versions; each version row gets its own surrogate key so facts can point at the correct time-valid version.

exercises.prompt: A product's category is reclassified. The business wants past sales to keep the OLD category and new sales the NEW one. Which SCD type, and what rows result?
exercises.solution: **SCD Type 2.** Expire the existing product-dimension row (set `valid_to` / `is_current=false`) and **insert a new version** with the new category, a new surrogate key, and `valid_from` = the change date / `is_current=true`. Past fact rows still reference the old surrogate key (old category); new facts reference the new one — so historical reports keep the old classification and current reports use the new, exactly as asked.

exercises.prompt: Write the WHERE clauses to (a) get the current version of each dimension row, and (b) get the version valid on a given event_date.
exercises.solution: (a) Current: `WHERE is_current = true` (or `valid_to = '9999-12-31'`). (b) As-of: join the fact on the business key and filter `WHERE f.event_date >= d.valid_from AND f.event_date < d.valid_to` (or `BETWEEN valid_from AND valid_to`). The range version reconstructs 'what the dimension looked like **then**', which is the whole point of SCD2.

exercises.prompt: Why does SCD Type 1 distort historical analysis after a customer moves city?
exercises.hint: Where do old sales get attributed?
exercises.solution: Type 1 **overwrites** the city in place, so the single customer row now shows only the new city. Every historical sale joins to that one row, so **all past sales are re-attributed to the new city** — 'Boston' revenue retroactively becomes 'Denver' revenue. History is silently rewritten. SCD2 avoids this by versioning the row so each sale keeps the city that was true at the time.

lessons.title: Warehouse vs Lake vs Lakehouse
lessons.summary: The three analytical storage paradigms: the structured warehouse (schema-on-write), the flexible lake (schema-on-read), and the lakehouse that combines them.
lessons.concept:
Where does analytical data live? Three paradigms evolved, each fixing the previous one's limits.

### Data warehouse — structured, schema-on-write
A **warehouse** (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift) stores **clean, structured, modeled** data (star schemas) with a schema enforced **on write**. Strengths: fast SQL, governance, reliability, BI-friendly. Limits: you must structure data up front (rigid), it's costly for huge raw/semi-structured or unstructured data, and historically it coupled storage with compute.

### Data lake — raw, schema-on-read
A **lake** is cheap **object storage** (S3/GCS/ADLS) holding **any** data — raw, semi-structured, unstructured — in files (often Parquet/JSON). Schema is applied **on read**. Strengths: cheap, flexible, stores everything, decoupled storage/compute. Limits: with no structure or transactions it can become a **'data swamp'** — no schema enforcement, no ACID, hard to trust or query reliably.

### Lakehouse — the combination
A **lakehouse** adds a **transactional table layer** (Delta Lake, Apache Iceberg, Hudi) **on top of the lake**, bringing warehouse-like features to cheap object storage: **ACID transactions, schema enforcement/evolution, time travel, and fast SQL** — directly on open file formats. You get the lake's cost/flexibility **and** the warehouse's reliability/performance, without copying data into a separate warehouse. It's the modern default for new platforms.

### Choosing / the medallion idea
Many architectures layer quality on the lakehouse: **bronze** (raw) → **silver** (cleaned/conformed) → **gold** (modeled star schemas / aggregates for BI). The point: you don't always pick one — the lakehouse lets raw and modeled data coexist, with structure added progressively.
lessons.example: Raw clickstream JSON lands cheaply in the **lake** (bronze). A lakehouse table format (Delta) adds ACID + schema so a job cleans it to **silver**, then models **gold** star schemas — all on the same object storage, queried with fast SQL, no separate warehouse copy.
keypoints: Warehouse: structured, modeled, schema-on-write — fast SQL & governance, but rigid and costly for raw/unstructured data.
Lake: cheap object storage for any raw/semi/unstructured data, schema-on-read — flexible but can become an ungoverned 'swamp'.
Lakehouse: a transactional table layer (Delta/Iceberg/Hudi) over the lake adds ACID, schema, time travel + fast SQL.
Lakehouse combines the lake's cost/flexibility with the warehouse's reliability/performance on open formats.
Medallion: bronze (raw) → silver (cleaned) → gold (modeled) layers quality on the lakehouse.
Often it's not either/or — the lakehouse lets raw and modeled data coexist with structure added progressively.
lessons.diagram_caption: Warehouse (schema-on-write) vs lake (schema-on-read) vs lakehouse (table layer giving both).

quiz.q: What's the key difference between a warehouse and a lake?
options: Warehouse enforces schema on write (structured); lake stores any raw data with schema on read
quiz.explain: Warehouses require structured, modeled data up front (schema-on-write); lakes store raw/any data cheaply and apply schema when read (schema-on-read).

quiz.q: What does a lakehouse add on top of a data lake?
options: A transactional table layer (Delta/Iceberg/Hudi) giving ACID, schema enforcement, time travel, fast SQL
quiz.explain: Lakehouse table formats add warehouse-grade reliability (ACID, schema, time travel) and performance directly on cheap object storage.

quiz.q: A 'data swamp' is…
options: An ungoverned lake with no schema/structure/quality, so data is hard to trust or use
quiz.explain: Without schema enforcement, cataloging, and quality, a lake degrades into a swamp of untrusted, unusable files — a key motivation for the lakehouse.

exercises.prompt: You must store raw, varied clickstream JSON cheaply now and model it for BI later. Which paradigm and how do you progress it?
exercises.solution: Land the raw JSON cheaply in a **lake / lakehouse bronze** layer (object storage). Use a **lakehouse table format** (Delta/Iceberg) so you get ACID + schema, then progress through the **medallion** layers: **silver** (clean, conform, type the events) and **gold** (model star schemas / aggregates for BI). This keeps cost and flexibility up front while adding warehouse-grade structure and reliability where it's queried — without copying into a separate warehouse.

exercises.prompt: Why did the lakehouse emerge — what limitation of each predecessor does it address?
exercises.hint: Warehouse rigidity/cost vs lake's lack of structure.
exercises.solution: The **warehouse** is reliable and fast but **rigid and costly** for huge raw/semi/unstructured data and historically coupled storage+compute. The **lake** is cheap and flexible but lacks schema enforcement, transactions, and governance — risking a **swamp**. The **lakehouse** adds a transactional table layer (ACID, schema, time travel, fast SQL) onto cheap object storage, so you get the **lake's cost/flexibility and the warehouse's reliability/performance together**, eliminating the need to copy data between two systems.

exercises.prompt: Explain schema-on-write vs schema-on-read with an example.
exercises.solution: **Schema-on-write** (warehouse): you define the table's columns/types **before loading**, and writes that don't conform are rejected — data is always structured and trustworthy, but ingestion is rigid (e.g. you must model the events first). **Schema-on-read** (lake): you dump files **as-is** and apply a schema only **when querying** — flexible for varied/evolving raw data (e.g. land arbitrary JSON now), but nothing guarantees structure until read, so quality must be managed. The lakehouse lets you start schema-on-read (bronze) and enforce schema-on-write for curated layers (gold).

lessons.title: MPP & Greenplum — parallel analytical databases
lessons.summary: How massively parallel (MPP) shared-nothing warehouses like Greenplum spread data across segments to run one query on many machines at once — and how to model for them.
lessons.concept:
Before cloud warehouses, the way to query multi-terabyte data fast was **MPP (Massively Parallel Processing)**: a **shared-nothing** cluster where many nodes each own a slice of the data and process it **in parallel**. **Greenplum** (Postgres-derived, still common in banks and enterprises) is the classic example.

### The architecture
- **Coordinator (master)** — receives the SQL, parses, builds a parallel plan, dispatches it to segments, and gathers results. It holds **no user data**.
- **Segments** — the workers. Each is its own Postgres instance with its **own CPU, memory, and disk** (shared-nothing), holding a **distinct slice** of every table. A query runs on **all segments simultaneously**.
- **Interconnect** — the network layer segments use to shuffle data between each other (for joins/aggregations).

### The one decision that makes or breaks performance: distribution
You choose how each table is spread with **`DISTRIBUTED BY (key)`** (hash) or `DISTRIBUTED RANDOMLY`. Get it right and work spreads evenly; get it wrong and you get **data skew** (one segment does most of the work — the whole cluster waits for it) or expensive **redistribution** on joins.
- **Distribute on a high-cardinality, evenly-spread column** (often a join key) so co-located joins avoid moving data.
- **Avoid** distributing on low-cardinality or heavily-null columns (skew).

### Storage for analytics
Greenplum supports **append-optimized, column-oriented** tables with compression — ideal for OLAP bulk read/write (scan few columns over many rows), just like cloud columnar warehouses.

### MPP vs cloud warehouses (2026)
MPP pioneered parallel analytics, but it **couples storage and compute** on fixed hardware. Cloud warehouses (Snowflake/BigQuery/Databricks) **separate** them for elasticity, which is why many enterprises are **migrating Greenplum → cloud**. The concepts transfer directly: distribution key → clustering/partitioning, segments → cloud compute, columnar/compression → the same. Knowing MPP makes you fluent in *both* the on-prem warehouses banks still run and their cloud successors.
lessons.example:
-- Greenplum: distribution decides parallelism & join cost
CREATE TABLE fact_txn (txn_id bigint, account_id bigint, amount numeric, txn_ts timestamp)
  WITH (appendoptimized=true, orientation=column, compresstype=zstd)
  DISTRIBUTED BY (account_id);     -- co-locate with dim_account for fast joins, spread evenly

CREATE TABLE dim_account (account_id bigint, ...) DISTRIBUTED BY (account_id);
-- join on account_id is now LOCAL on each segment (no redistribution)
keypoints: MPP = shared-nothing cluster: many nodes each own a data slice and process queries in parallel.
Greenplum: a coordinator (plans/dispatches/gathers, no data) + segments (own CPU/mem/disk, hold slices).
DISTRIBUTED BY (key) sets how data spreads — the #1 performance lever; bad keys cause skew or join redistribution.
Distribute on a high-cardinality, evenly-spread join key; co-located joins avoid moving data over the interconnect.
Append-optimized, column-oriented + compression = OLAP-friendly storage (scan fewer columns).
MPP couples storage+compute on fixed HW; cloud warehouses separate them — many banks are migrating Greenplum → cloud.
lessons.diagram_caption: Coordinator dispatches one query to all segments; each processes its DISTRIBUTED-BY slice in parallel.

quiz.q: In Greenplum, what does the coordinator (master) hold?
options: No user data — it parses, plans, dispatches to segments, and gathers results
quiz.explain: The coordinator orchestrates; the segments store and process the actual data slices in parallel.

quiz.q: What is the single biggest performance lever in Greenplum?
options: The distribution key (DISTRIBUTED BY) — it controls even spread and whether joins are local
quiz.explain: A good distribution key spreads work evenly and co-locates joins; a bad one causes skew or costly redistribution.

quiz.q: Why are many enterprises migrating Greenplum to cloud warehouses?
options: Greenplum couples storage and compute on fixed hardware; cloud separates them for elasticity and managed scale
quiz.explain: Separation of storage and compute (Snowflake/BigQuery/Databricks) brings elasticity and lower ops than fixed MPP clusters.

exercises.prompt: Explain shared-nothing MPP in one sentence.
exercises.solution: A cluster where each node has its own CPU/memory/disk and owns a slice of the data, so one query runs on all nodes in parallel.

exercises.prompt: How do you pick a Greenplum distribution key?
exercises.solution: Choose a high-cardinality, evenly-distributed column (ideally the common join key) so data spreads without skew and joins stay local to each segment.

exercises.prompt: Map three Greenplum concepts to their cloud-warehouse equivalents.
exercises.solution: Distribution key → clustering/partitioning; segments → elastic cloud compute; append-optimized columnar+compression → the same columnar storage. Main difference: cloud separates storage from compute.

lessons.title: Facts, dimensions & grain
lessons.summary: The heart of dimensional modeling: fact tables (measurements) vs dimension tables (context), declaring the grain first, additive measures, and the three fact-table types.
lessons.concept:
**Dimensional modeling** (Kimball) organizes analytics data into two kinds of table so business questions are easy and fast to answer.

### Fact tables — the measurements
A **fact table** holds **events/measurements**: each row is one occurrence (a sale, a click, a shipment) with **numeric measures** (`amount`, `quantity`) and **foreign keys** to dimensions. Facts are tall and narrow — billions of rows, few columns — and they're what you aggregate (`SUM`, `COUNT`, `AVG`).

### Dimension tables — the context
A **dimension table** holds the **descriptive context** — the *who, what, when, where*: `dim_customer`, `dim_product`, `dim_date`, `dim_store`. They're wide (many descriptive attributes) but short, and you use them to **filter and group** ("sales **by** product category **in** Q1"). The attributes become your report's rows, columns, and filters.

### Grain — declare it first (most important step)
The **grain** is *what one fact row represents*. Decide it before anything else: "one row per **order line item**" or "one row per **daily account balance**". A clear, consistent grain prevents double-counting and makes every later choice obvious. Mixing grains in one fact table is a classic, painful mistake.

### Additivity of measures
- **Additive** — sums across *all* dimensions (`sales_amount`). The easiest and best.
- **Semi-additive** — sums across some but not time (an account **balance**: you can't sum balances across days; you take the latest).
- **Non-additive** — can't be summed at all (a **ratio/percentage**); store the components and compute the ratio after aggregating.

### Three fact-table types
- **Transaction** — one row per event (most common).
- **Periodic snapshot** — one row per entity per period (daily inventory level).
- **Accumulating snapshot** — one row per process instance, updated as it moves through milestones (order placed → shipped → delivered).
lessons.example: `fact_sales` at the grain 'one row per order line': measures `quantity`, `amount`; foreign keys to `dim_date`, `dim_product`, `dim_customer`, `dim_store`. 'Revenue by category last quarter' = `SUM(amount)` joined to `dim_product` (category) and `dim_date` (quarter) — a star query.
keypoints: Fact tables store events/measurements (numeric measures + dimension foreign keys); you aggregate them.
Dimension tables store descriptive context (who/what/when/where); you filter and group by them.
Declare the grain (what one fact row means) FIRST — it prevents double-counting and guides everything.
Measures: additive (sum anywhere), semi-additive (not over time, e.g. balances), non-additive (ratios).
Never mix grains in one fact table — a classic, costly mistake.
Fact types: transaction (per event), periodic snapshot (per period), accumulating snapshot (per process).
lessons.diagram_caption: A fact row = measures + foreign keys at a declared grain; dimensions add descriptive context.

quiz.q: What's the single most important decision when designing a fact table?
options: Declaring the grain — what one row represents
quiz.explain: The grain (one row per …) governs measures, joins, and aggregation correctness. Decide it first and keep it consistent; mixing grains causes double-counting.

quiz.q: An account balance measure is…
options: Semi-additive — you can sum across accounts but not across time (take the latest)
quiz.explain: Balances sum across accounts at a point in time but not across time periods, so they're semi-additive — usually you take the period-end value.

quiz.q: Which is a dimension table?
quiz.explain: Dimensions hold descriptive attributes (name, category, brand) used to filter and group; facts hold the numeric measurements you aggregate.

exercises.prompt: You're modeling e-commerce orders. State a clear grain for the sales fact and list 3 dimensions and 2 measures.
exercises.hint: One row per … line item; dims = date/product/customer; measures = qty/amount.
exercises.solution: **Grain:** one row per **order line item**. **Dimensions:** `dim_date`, `dim_product`, `dim_customer` (and `dim_store`/`dim_promotion` if relevant). **Measures:** `quantity` and `extended_amount` (price×qty) — both **additive**. This grain lets you roll up to order, customer, day, or category by `SUM`ming the measures and grouping by dimension attributes.

exercises.prompt: Why is mixing grains (e.g. order-header rows and line-item rows in one fact table) a problem?
exercises.solution: With mixed grains, a `SUM(amount)` **double-counts** — the order total appears both on the header row and across the line rows, so aggregates are wrong and there's no single safe way to filter. Each grain belongs in its **own** fact table (e.g. `fact_order_line` and a separate `fact_order` snapshot if needed), each internally consistent, so every aggregation is unambiguous.

exercises.prompt: Classify each measure's additivity: (a) units_sold, (b) inventory_on_hand at day end, (c) profit_margin_pct.
exercises.hint: Sum anywhere / not over time / not at all.
exercises.solution: (a) **units_sold — additive:** sum across any dimension including time. (b) **inventory_on_hand — semi-additive:** sum across products/stores at a point in time, but **not across days** (you'd take the latest/period-end). (c) **profit_margin_pct — non-additive:** you can't sum percentages; store the components (profit, revenue) additively and compute the ratio **after** aggregating.

lessons.title: Surrogate vs natural keys
lessons.summary: Why warehouses key dimensions on meaningless surrogate integers rather than business keys — stability, SCD2, performance — and how the two keys coexist.
lessons.concept:
Every dimension needs a **primary key** that facts reference. In analytics you almost always use a **surrogate key**, not the business's natural key — and understanding why is a common interview topic.

### Natural (business) key vs surrogate key
- A **natural key** is real-world data: a `customer_id` from the source app, an email, an SKU.
- A **surrogate key** is a **meaningless auto-generated integer** (`customer_key = 1, 2, 3…`) the warehouse assigns, with no business meaning.

### Why surrogate keys in the warehouse
1. **SCD2 needs them.** With Type-2 history, one business customer has **many versioned rows** — they can't all share the same key. Each version gets its own surrogate key, while the natural key (customer 42) repeats. Surrogates are what make versioned history possible.
2. **Stability / source independence.** Business keys change, get reformatted, or collide across merged source systems (two apps both have `id=1`). A surrogate insulates the warehouse from all that.
3. **Performance.** A narrow 4-byte integer key joins and indexes faster and uses less space in a billion-row fact than a wide string business key.
4. **Handles late/unknown data.** A dedicated surrogate (e.g. `-1` 'Unknown') gives facts a key to point at when the dimension isn't known yet.

### How they coexist
The dimension stores **both**: the **surrogate key** (PK that facts join on) **and** the **natural key** (to match incoming source data during loads). The ETL looks up the natural key to find/create the right surrogate, then writes the surrogate into the fact. Facts carry **only surrogate keys**, keeping them narrow and decoupled from source systems.
lessons.example: `dim_customer(customer_key SERIAL PK, customer_id /* natural */, name, city, valid_from, valid_to)`. `fact_sales` stores `customer_key` (the surrogate). When customer 42 moves city, a new row with a new `customer_key` is added (same `customer_id=42`) — only possible because the PK is a surrogate.
keypoints: Natural key = real business data (id/email/SKU); surrogate key = meaningless warehouse-assigned integer.
SCD2 requires surrogates: each versioned row of one business entity needs its own key.
Surrogates give stability/source-independence — business keys change, reformat, or collide across systems.
Narrow integer surrogates join/index faster and smaller than wide string business keys in huge facts.
A reserved surrogate (e.g. -1 'Unknown') handles late/missing dimension data.
Dimensions store both keys; facts carry only the surrogate (looked up from the natural key in ETL).

quiz.q: Why can't SCD Type 2 use the natural key as the dimension's primary key?
options: One business entity has many versioned rows, so they need distinct surrogate keys
quiz.explain: SCD2 creates multiple rows per business entity (one per change); a single natural key can't be the PK of many rows, so each version gets its own surrogate key.

quiz.q: A surrogate key is…
options: A meaningless warehouse-generated integer with no business meaning
quiz.explain: Surrogate keys are arbitrary integers assigned by the warehouse, decoupled from any business value, which keeps the model stable and fast.

quiz.q: Where is the natural (business) key still stored?
options: In the dimension table alongside the surrogate, to match incoming source data during loads
quiz.explain: The dimension keeps the natural key so ETL can look up which surrogate an incoming source row maps to; facts then store only the surrogate.

exercises.prompt: List three concrete reasons a warehouse uses surrogate keys instead of the source system's customer_id.
exercises.solution: (1) **SCD2 history** — one customer can have many versioned dimension rows, which need distinct keys. (2) **Stability/source-independence** — source `customer_id`s change, get reformatted, or **collide** when merging multiple systems; a surrogate insulates the warehouse. (3) **Performance/size** — a narrow integer surrogate joins and indexes faster and takes less space in a billion-row fact than a wide string/composite business key. (Bonus: a reserved surrogate handles 'Unknown'/late-arriving dimension data.)

exercises.prompt: During ETL, how does a fact row end up with the correct surrogate key?
exercises.hint: Look up the natural key in the dimension.
exercises.solution: The ETL takes the incoming record's **natural key** (e.g. `customer_id=42`), **looks it up in the dimension** to find the matching surrogate key for the version in effect (for SCD2, the currently-valid row), creating a new dimension row if needed. It then writes **that surrogate** into the fact. So the fact never carries the natural key — only the resolved surrogate, keeping it narrow and decoupled from the source.

exercises.prompt: Two source systems both number customers starting at 1. Why does this break natural keys, and how do surrogates fix it?
exercises.solution: Both systems have a `customer_id = 1` for **different real customers**, so using the natural key as the warehouse PK would **collide** (or force an awkward composite key). **Surrogate keys** fix it: the warehouse assigns its own unique integer to each distinct customer regardless of source, and stores the (source_system, natural_id) to disambiguate during loads. Facts join on the conflict-free surrogate, integrating both systems cleanly.

lessons.diagram_caption: Use a surrogate key (a stable, meaningless integer) as a dimension's primary key; keep the natural/business key as an attribute. Surrogates enable SCD2 history and decouple from source systems.

lessons.title: Denormalization — breaking the rules on purpose
lessons.summary: When and how to duplicate data deliberately for read speed: pre-joining, wide tables, the costs (storage, update complexity), and keeping it consistent.
lessons.concept:
**Denormalization** is intentionally introducing **redundancy** — storing the same data in multiple places, or pre-joining tables — to make **reads faster**. In analytics it's not a smell; it's the design.

### Why denormalize
Joins cost time. If a query repeatedly joins fact → product → category → department, **baking those attributes flat into the dimension** (or pre-joining into a wide table) removes the joins, so the query scans one table and flies. Read-heavy systems trade storage and write-simplicity for **read speed**.

### Common denormalization moves
- **Flat dimensions** — put category and department *inside* `dim_product` instead of separate tables (star vs snowflake).
- **Pre-joined wide tables / OBT** — a single wide table with facts and all needed attributes already joined, so dashboards read with **zero joins**.
- **Pre-aggregated summary tables** — store daily/store-level rollups so common reports skip re-aggregating raw data.
- **Duplicated reference data** — copy a small lookup value into a big table to avoid a join.

### The costs (manage them)
- **Storage** — redundancy uses more space (usually cheap and worth it).
- **Update complexity** — the same value lives in many places, so a change must update them all. In a warehouse this is fine because pipelines **rebuild** the denormalized tables; in a write-heavy OLTP system it would risk **inconsistency** (the reason OLTP normalizes).
- **Freshness** — pre-aggregates/wide tables are only as current as their last refresh.

### The rule
**Normalize for writes, denormalize for reads.** Denormalize **deliberately**, driven by real query patterns, and keep the denormalized copies consistent by **rebuilding them in your pipeline** rather than editing in place. It's a tool, not a shortcut.
lessons.example: A dashboard joins `fact_sales → dim_product → dim_category → dim_department` on every load. Denormalizing department and category **into** `dim_product` (a flat star dimension) removes two joins, so the dashboard query is simpler and faster — the redundancy is refreshed each pipeline run.
keypoints: Denormalization = deliberate redundancy / pre-joining to make reads faster (the analytics default).
Moves: flat dimensions (star), pre-joined wide tables (OBT), pre-aggregated summaries, duplicated lookups.
It removes joins so read-heavy queries scan fewer tables and run faster.
Costs: more storage, update complexity (same value in many places), and staleness of pre-aggregates.
Safe in warehouses because pipelines REBUILD denormalized tables — no live row-by-row edits.
Rule: normalize for writes, denormalize for reads — deliberately, driven by query patterns.
lessons.diagram_caption: Denormalizing (flat dims / wide tables) removes joins for read speed, at the cost of redundancy.

quiz.q: What is the main benefit of denormalization in analytics?
options: Faster reads — fewer joins because data is pre-joined/duplicated
quiz.explain: Denormalization removes joins by duplicating/pre-joining data, so read-heavy analytical queries scan fewer tables and run faster — trading storage and write simplicity.

quiz.q: Why is denormalization safe in a warehouse but risky in an OLTP app?
options: The warehouse rebuilds denormalized tables via pipelines (no live edits); OLTP's many in-place updates risk inconsistency
quiz.explain: Redundancy is dangerous when many users update in place (OLTP anomalies). A warehouse regenerates its denormalized tables in batch, so the copies stay consistent.

quiz.q: Which is a denormalization technique?
quiz.explain: Pre-aggregated summary tables (like flat dimensions and wide OBT) duplicate/precompute data to speed reads — classic denormalization.

exercises.prompt: A report re-aggregates a billion-row fact on every load and is slow. Give a denormalization fix and its trade-off.
exercises.solution: Build a **pre-aggregated summary table** (e.g. daily revenue by category) that the pipeline refreshes incrementally, so the report reads a small precomputed table in milliseconds instead of scanning the billion-row fact each load. **Trade-off:** the summary is only as **fresh** as its last refresh (slight staleness) and uses extra storage — both acceptable for analytics, and you match the refresh cadence to how current the report must be.

exercises.prompt: You denormalize category into dim_product (star). What maintenance concern does this introduce and how is it handled in a warehouse?
exercises.solution: The category now **repeats across many product rows**, so if a category is renamed it must change in **every** affected row — an update-complexity/consistency concern. In a warehouse this is handled by the **pipeline rebuilding the dimension** (or doing a controlled SCD update) rather than ad-hoc edits, so all copies update together. (In an OLTP system the same duplication would risk anomalies, which is why OLTP normalizes.)

exercises.prompt: Why should denormalization be driven by real query patterns rather than applied everywhere?
exercises.solution: Each denormalization adds **storage and maintenance cost** (and potential staleness), justified only by the **read speedups it actually delivers**. Denormalizing for joins/aggregations that real queries perform pays off; denormalizing speculatively bloats tables and complicates pipelines for no benefit. So you observe the **actual query patterns** (which joins/aggregations are hot) and denormalize those — a targeted optimization, not a blanket rule.

lessons.title: Inmon vs Kimball — two warehouse philosophies
lessons.summary: The two classic approaches to building a warehouse: Inmon's top-down normalized enterprise hub feeding marts, vs Kimball's bottom-up dimensional marts unified by conformed dimensions.
lessons.concept:
There are two foundational philosophies for *how to build* a data warehouse. Knowing both — and that modern stacks blend them — is a frequent interview topic.

### Inmon — top-down (the enterprise data warehouse)
Bill Inmon's approach builds a **normalized (3NF) enterprise data warehouse (EDW)** first — a single, integrated, non-redundant 'source of truth' for the whole organization — and **then** derives subject-specific **dimensional data marts** (star schemas) from it for analysis.

- **Pros:** highly integrated and consistent, minimal redundancy, a true enterprise-wide model.
- **Cons:** large up-front effort and modeling before any value is delivered; slower to first insight.

### Kimball — bottom-up (dimensional marts first)
Ralph Kimball's approach builds **dimensional data marts (star schemas) directly** for each business process, and integrates them through **conformed dimensions** (the **bus architecture**) — shared `dim_date`, `dim_customer`, etc. used identically across marts.

- **Pros:** fast time-to-value, business-friendly and intuitive, deliver one process at a time.
- **Cons:** integration depends on disciplined conformed dimensions; can feel less unified early on.

### The practical difference
Inmon = *integrate first, then dimensionalize*. Kimball = *dimensionalize per process, integrate via conformed dimensions*. Inmon optimizes for enterprise consistency; Kimball for speed and usability.

### Modern practice — usually a blend
Today the line blurs. A common pattern: **ELT** raw data into a cleaned/normalized **staging** layer (Inmon-ish single source) in a lakehouse, then build **dimensional / wide marts** (Kimball-ish) on top with **dbt** — getting integration *and* fast, business-facing models. The **medallion** (bronze→silver→gold) architecture is essentially this hybrid. So learn both; you'll use ideas from each.
lessons.example: For a retailer: **Inmon** would first model a normalized enterprise warehouse of customers/products/orders, then build a sales star from it. **Kimball** would build the sales star directly and a returns star next, sharing a conformed `dim_customer`/`dim_date` so the two compare cleanly.
keypoints: Inmon (top-down): build a normalized 3NF enterprise warehouse first, then derive dimensional marts.
Kimball (bottom-up): build dimensional star-schema marts directly, integrated via conformed dimensions (bus).
Inmon optimizes enterprise integration/consistency but is slower up front; Kimball optimizes speed/usability.
Conformed dimensions are how Kimball integrates marts without a central normalized model.
Modern stacks blend them: ELT into a cleaned staging layer, then dimensional marts (dbt / medallion).
Learn both — you'll borrow integration from Inmon and dimensional usability from Kimball.
lessons.diagram_caption: Inmon integrates into a normalized hub first; Kimball builds marts directly, unified by conformed dimensions.

quiz.q: Inmon's approach builds what first?
options: A normalized (3NF) enterprise data warehouse, then derives marts from it
quiz.explain: Inmon is top-down: a single integrated normalized EDW is built first as the source of truth, with dimensional marts derived afterward.

quiz.q: How does Kimball integrate separate data marts?
options: Conformed dimensions shared identically across marts (the bus architecture)
quiz.explain: Kimball builds star marts directly and ties them together with conformed dimensions, so facts can be compared consistently across processes.

quiz.q: What's a common modern blend of the two?
options: ELT into a cleaned/normalized staging layer, then build dimensional marts on top (e.g. dbt / medallion)
quiz.explain: Medallion-style architectures land/clean data (Inmon-ish single source) then model dimensional gold marts (Kimball), getting integration plus fast business models.

exercises.prompt: A startup needs its first sales dashboard live in two weeks. Inmon or Kimball, and why?
exercises.solution: **Kimball.** Build the **sales star schema directly** for that one business process — fast time-to-value, intuitive for the business, deliverable in the timeframe. Inmon's top-down enterprise warehouse would require modeling the whole normalized EDW first, delaying any insight for months. You can still keep future integration in mind by defining **conformed dimensions** (dim_date, dim_customer) so later marts (returns, support) line up.

exercises.prompt: A large bank with strict consistency and many source systems is choosing an approach. Argue for Inmon.
exercises.hint: Enterprise integration / single source of truth.
exercises.solution: **Inmon** fits: a **normalized enterprise data warehouse** provides a single, integrated, non-redundant source of truth across many systems — critical when consistency and reconciliation across departments are paramount (and often for regulatory reporting). Marts are then derived from that governed core, so every department's numbers tie back to the same model. The higher up-front cost is justified by the integration and consistency the bank requires. (In practice they'd still expose Kimball-style marts for analysts on top.)

exercises.prompt: What role do conformed dimensions play, and which philosophy depends on them most?
exercises.solution: **Conformed dimensions** are dimensions shared **identically** across multiple fact tables/marts (same keys and attribute meanings), so facts from different processes can be filtered and compared consistently. **Kimball** depends on them most: since it builds marts independently rather than from a central normalized model, conformed dimensions are *the* integration mechanism (the bus architecture). Without them, Kimball marts would drift apart; with them, they integrate into a coherent warehouse.

exercises.prompt: Explain how a medallion (bronze→silver→gold) lakehouse blends Inmon and Kimball.
exercises.solution: **Bronze** lands raw data; **silver** cleans, conforms, and integrates it into a trustworthy, lightly-normalized core — an **Inmon-like single source of truth**. **Gold** then builds **Kimball-style dimensional models** (star schemas, wide marts, aggregates) for business consumption. So you integrate first (Inmon) *and* deliver fast, intuitive dimensional marts (Kimball) — typically orchestrated with dbt — capturing the strengths of both philosophies in one pipeline.

lessons.title: Data Vault — modeling for change & auditability
lessons.summary: An agile, audit-focused modeling style built from Hubs (business keys), Links (relationships) and Satellites (history) — designed to absorb change and load in parallel.
lessons.concept:
**Data Vault** is a modeling methodology optimized for **auditability, agility, and change** in enterprise warehouses with many evolving source systems. Instead of stars, it uses three building blocks.

### The three constructs
- **Hub** — a list of unique **business keys** for an entity (e.g. all `customer_id`s), plus load metadata. The stable backbone — business keys rarely change.
- **Link** — a **relationship** between hubs (e.g. a customer–order association). All relationships are links, modeled as many-to-many, which makes the model flexible to change.
- **Satellite** — the **descriptive attributes and their history**, hanging off a hub or link, time-stamped. All context and change-over-time lives here (like SCD2 history, separated out).

### What it's designed for
- **Auditability** — you **load everything, insert-only, never update or delete**; every record is timestamped and traceable to its source. Ideal for regulated industries.
- **Agility** — adding a new source or attribute means adding a **new satellite or link**, **without redesigning** existing structures. Sources can be loaded independently.
- **Parallel loading** — hubs, links, and satellites have few dependencies, so loads parallelize well.

### The cost
A Data Vault is **not query-friendly** — answering a question touches many hubs/links/satellites with lots of joins. So you **build dimensional marts (star schemas) on top** of the vault for consumption. It also has **more tables** than a star schema.

### When to use it
Large enterprises with **many changing source systems**, strong **audit/compliance** needs, and a requirement to **integrate incrementally** over years. For a small/simple warehouse it's overkill — a Kimball star is simpler. Think of Data Vault as the **integration/history layer**, with Kimball marts as the **presentation layer** on top.
lessons.example: A bank model: a **Hub_Customer** (business keys), a **Link_Customer_Account** (the relationship), and **Sat_Customer** (name/address history, timestamped, insert-only). New regulation needs a new attribute → add a **new satellite**, no change to the hub or existing loads, full history retained.
keypoints: Data Vault models for auditability, agility, and change across many evolving sources.
Hubs = unique business keys; Links = relationships (many-to-many); Satellites = descriptive attributes + history.
Insert-only, timestamped, source-traceable loads → full auditability (regulated industries).
Agile: add a source/attribute via a new satellite/link without redesigning existing structures; loads parallelize.
Not query-friendly (many joins) — build Kimball dimensional marts on top for consumption.
Use for large, change-heavy, audit-driven enterprises; overkill for small/simple warehouses.
lessons.diagram_caption: Hubs (business keys) + Links (relationships) + Satellites (history) — an agile, auditable core.

quiz.q: What do the three Data Vault constructs hold?
options: Hub = business keys, Link = relationships, Satellite = descriptive attributes + history
quiz.explain: Hubs store unique business keys, Links store relationships between hubs, and Satellites store the time-stamped descriptive attributes and their history.

quiz.q: What is Data Vault primarily optimized for?
options: Auditability, agility, and absorbing change across many sources (insert-only, timestamped)
quiz.explain: Data Vault's insert-only, source-traceable, satellite-based design targets audit/compliance and incremental integration of changing sources — not query speed.

quiz.q: Because a Data Vault is join-heavy, what do you typically build on top?
quiz.explain: The vault is the integration/history layer; you build Kimball-style star marts on top so analysts get a query-friendly presentation layer.

exercises.prompt: A new regulation requires capturing a customer attribute you don't store. How does Data Vault absorb this with minimal disruption?
exercises.solution: Add a **new Satellite** hanging off the existing `Hub_Customer` containing the new attribute(s), loaded insert-only and timestamped. The **hub, links, and existing satellites are untouched**, and existing loads keep running — you've extended the model without redesign or downtime, and you capture the new attribute's full history from now on. This agility (extend via new satellites/links) is a core Data Vault advantage for change-heavy, regulated environments.

exercises.prompt: Why is a Data Vault not used directly for BI dashboards, and what's the fix?
exercises.solution: Answering a business question in a vault touches **many hubs, links, and satellites with numerous joins**, so it's slow and unintuitive for analysts. The fix is to build **dimensional marts (star schemas)** on top of the vault as a **presentation/consumption layer** — the vault remains the auditable, agile integration/history core, while the marts give fast, business-friendly access. Vault for integration, Kimball for consumption.

exercises.prompt: Contrast where SCD2 history lives in a Kimball model vs a Data Vault.
exercises.hint: In the dimension vs in satellites.
exercises.solution: In **Kimball**, history lives **inside the dimension** as SCD2 versioned rows (valid_from/valid_to). In **Data Vault**, descriptive attributes and their change-over-time are isolated in **Satellites** (timestamped, insert-only) hanging off the hub — separating the stable business key (hub) from its changing context (satellite). Both preserve full history; Data Vault just **structurally separates** keys, relationships, and history, which is what gives it its auditability and agility (and its extra joins).

exercises.prompt: When is Data Vault overkill?
exercises.solution: For a **small or simple warehouse** with few stable sources and modest audit needs, Data Vault's extra structure (many hubs/links/satellites, more loads, marts required on top) adds **complexity without payoff** — a straightforward **Kimball star schema** is simpler to build, load, and query. Data Vault earns its keep only when you have **many evolving sources, strong audit/compliance requirements, and a need to integrate incrementally** at enterprise scale.

lessons.title: Advanced dimension techniques
lessons.summary: The dimensional patterns beyond the basics: role-playing, junk, degenerate, bridge, and mini-dimensions — each solving a specific real-world modeling problem.
lessons.concept:
Once you know facts and dimensions, these techniques handle the messy real-world cases. Each has a specific job.

### Role-playing dimensions
One physical dimension used in **multiple roles** in the same fact. An order has `order_date`, `ship_date`, `delivery_date` — all `dim_date`. You reuse the single `dim_date` table via **views/aliases** (one per role) rather than building three date tables. One conformed dimension, several roles.

### Junk dimensions
A fact often has several **low-cardinality flags/indicators** (is_gift, payment_type, order_channel). Rather than many tiny dimensions or flags cluttering the fact, combine them into **one 'junk' dimension** holding the distinct combinations. Tidies the fact and groups related flags.

### Degenerate dimensions
A dimension **key with no attributes of its own** — e.g. the **order number** or **invoice number**. It's a real business identifier you want to filter/group by, but it has no descriptive table, so you store it **directly in the fact** (no separate dimension). Common for transaction identifiers.

### Bridge tables
For a **many-to-many** relationship a single foreign key can't express — e.g. a bank account with **multiple** owners, or a product in **multiple** categories. A **bridge table** sits between the fact/dimension and the multi-valued dimension, often with a weighting factor to allocate measures and avoid double-counting.

### Mini-dimensions
When a big dimension has a few **rapidly changing** attributes (e.g. customer credit score, age band), SCD2 on the whole dimension would explode row counts. Split those volatile attributes into a small **mini-dimension** referenced separately by the fact, keeping the main dimension stable.

Each pattern is a tool for a specific shape of problem — recognizing which one applies is the skill.
lessons.example: An orders fact references `dim_date` three times — as `order_date_key`, `ship_date_key`, `delivery_date_key` (role-playing, via three views of one `dim_date`) — and stores the **order number** directly in the fact as a **degenerate dimension** (a key with no attribute table).
keypoints: Role-playing: one dimension (e.g. dim_date) used in multiple roles via views/aliases (order/ship/delivery date).
Junk dimension: combine several low-cardinality flags into one dimension of their distinct combinations.
Degenerate dimension: a business key with no attributes (order/invoice number) stored directly in the fact.
Bridge table: resolves many-to-many relationships (multi-owner account, multi-category product), often with weights.
Mini-dimension: split rapidly-changing attributes out of a big dimension to avoid SCD2 row explosion.
Each technique solves a specific real-world shape — recognizing which applies is the modeling skill.

quiz.q: An order fact has order_date, ship_date, and delivery_date. What technique handles this?
options: Role-playing dimensions — one dim_date used in three roles via views/aliases
quiz.explain: Role-playing reuses a single conformed dim_date in multiple roles (one view/alias per date), instead of duplicating the date dimension three times.

quiz.q: What is a degenerate dimension?
options: A business key with no descriptive attributes (e.g. order number) stored directly in the fact
A dimension with too many rows
quiz.explain: A degenerate dimension is an identifier you filter/group by but which has no attribute table, so it lives in the fact itself — common for transaction numbers.

quiz.q: A product can belong to several categories at once. Which technique resolves this many-to-many?
options: Bridge table (often with a weighting factor)
quiz.explain: A bridge table sits between the fact/dimension and the multi-valued dimension to express many-to-many, with weights to allocate measures and avoid double-counting.

exercises.prompt: Your orders fact has five boolean/low-cardinality flags cluttering it (is_gift, is_first_order, channel, payment_type, is_promo). Which technique tidies this, and how?
exercises.solution: Use a **junk dimension**: create one dimension table holding the **distinct combinations** of those flags (each row a unique combo, with a surrogate key), and replace the five fact columns with a single `junk_key`. This declutters the fact, groups related indicators logically, and keeps the fact narrow — while still letting you filter/group by any flag via the junk dimension. (If a flag is high-cardinality it belongs in its own dimension instead.)

exercises.prompt: A bank account can have multiple owners. Why can't a single owner_key work, and what do you use?
exercises.solution: A single `owner_key` foreign key models only **one** owner per account — it can't represent an account with several owners (a **many-to-many** relationship). Use a **bridge table** (`account_key`, `owner_key`, plus an optional **allocation weight**) between the account and customer dimensions. The weight lets you **allocate measures** (e.g. split a balance across owners) so aggregations don't **double-count**. Bridge tables are the standard tool for multi-valued dimension relationships.

exercises.prompt: A customer dimension has stable attributes (name, city) but a credit_score that changes monthly. Why is full SCD2 a problem, and the fix?
exercises.solution: Applying **SCD2 to the whole dimension** for a monthly-changing `credit_score` would create a **new full customer row every month** — exploding the dimension's size for a single volatile attribute. The fix is a **mini-dimension**: move the rapidly-changing attributes (credit_score band, age band) into a small separate dimension that the **fact references directly**, leaving the main customer dimension stable. The fact captures which credit band applied at event time, without bloating the customer dimension.

exercises.prompt: Give a one-line description of when you'd use each: role-playing, junk, degenerate, bridge, mini-dimension.
exercises.solution: **Role-playing** — one dimension used in multiple roles (order/ship/delivery date). **Junk** — bundle several low-cardinality flags into one dimension. **Degenerate** — a business key with no attributes (order number) stored in the fact. **Bridge** — resolve a many-to-many (multi-owner account, multi-category product), often weighted. **Mini-dimension** — split out rapidly-changing attributes to avoid SCD2 row explosion. Each maps to a specific structural problem in the data.

lessons.diagram_caption: Advanced techniques keep the star clean while handling real complexity: role-playing, junk, degenerate, conformed, bridge, and mini/outrigger dimensions.

lessons.title: The semantic / metrics layer
lessons.summary: Define business metrics once, centrally, so every tool computes them identically — ending the 'every dashboard defines revenue differently' problem.
lessons.concept:
A **semantic layer** (a.k.a. **metrics layer**) is a central place that defines your **business metrics and entities once**, mapping friendly business terms to the physical tables and the exact logic to compute them — so **every** query and BI tool returns the **same number**.

### The problem it solves
Without it, 'revenue', 'active user', or 'churn' gets re-implemented in every dashboard, notebook, and query — each slightly differently (does revenue include tax? refunds? which date?). Result: **three dashboards, three different revenue numbers**, and no one trusts the data. The semantic layer makes the definition **single-sourced**.

### What it defines
- **Metrics** — `revenue = SUM(amount) WHERE status='completed'`, `active_users = COUNT(DISTINCT user) in last 30 days`, with their exact filters and grain.
- **Entities & dimensions** — the joins and attributes you can slice by, named in business terms.
- **Governed access** — consistent definitions and often permissions in one place.

Tools query the layer ("give me **revenue** by **region** by **month**") and it generates the correct SQL — the BI tool no longer hand-writes the metric logic.

### Tools
**dbt Semantic Layer / MetricFlow**, **LookML** (Looker), and **Cube** are common implementations. They sit **between** the warehouse/marts and the BI tools.

### Why it matters for data engineers
It's the bridge from your modeled tables to trustworthy, consistent business reporting. You model facts/dimensions in the warehouse, then express the **metrics** on top once — so the organization shares **one definition of truth** instead of contradictory numbers. It complements (doesn't replace) good dimensional modeling underneath.
lessons.example: Define `revenue` once in the semantic layer as `SUM(amount)` over completed, non-refunded orders by order date. Finance's dashboard, the exec notebook, and the ad-hoc query all reference that metric and get **identical** revenue by region/month — no more reconciliation meetings over whose number is right.
keypoints: A semantic/metrics layer defines business metrics and entities once, centrally, mapping terms to physical tables.
It solves the 'every dashboard defines revenue differently' problem — single-sourced definitions everyone shares.
Defines metrics (with exact filters/grain), the dimensions to slice by, and governed access.
Tools query the layer in business terms and it generates the correct, consistent SQL.
Implementations: dbt Semantic Layer/MetricFlow, LookML, Cube — sitting between marts and BI tools.
It complements dimensional modeling: model tables in the warehouse, express metrics once on top.
lessons.diagram_caption: Metrics defined once in the semantic layer flow consistently to every BI tool and query.

quiz.q: What core problem does a semantic layer solve?
options: Inconsistent metric definitions — every tool computing 'revenue' differently
quiz.explain: It single-sources metric definitions so every dashboard/query returns the same number, ending contradictory 'revenue'/'active user' figures.

quiz.q: Where does a semantic layer sit?
options: Between the warehouse/marts and the BI tools, translating business terms to SQL
quiz.explain: It's a middle layer: BI tools ask for metrics in business terms and it generates consistent SQL against the modeled warehouse tables.

quiz.q: A semantic layer is best described as…
options: A central, single-sourced definition of metrics/entities on top of modeled tables
quiz.explain: It complements dimensional modeling by defining metrics once on top of the warehouse, not replacing the underlying facts/dimensions.

exercises.prompt: Three dashboards show three different revenue numbers. Explain the likely cause and how a semantic layer fixes it.
exercises.solution: Each dashboard **re-implements 'revenue'** with slightly different logic — one includes tax, another excludes refunds, a third uses ship date instead of order date — so the numbers diverge and trust erodes. A **semantic layer** defines `revenue` **once** (exact filters, grain, date), and all three dashboards reference that single definition, so they return **identical** figures. The fix is single-sourcing the metric, not auditing three separate SQL queries forever.

exercises.prompt: Why does a semantic layer complement rather than replace dimensional modeling?
exercises.hint: Tables underneath vs metrics on top.
exercises.solution: Dimensional modeling structures the **data** — facts, conformed dimensions, correct grain — so the underlying tables are clean, joinable, and performant. The semantic layer sits **on top** and defines the **metrics and business terms** computed from those tables. You still need well-modeled facts/dimensions for the metrics to be correct and fast; the semantic layer adds a consistent, governed definition layer above them. Remove the modeling and the metrics have nothing trustworthy to compute from.

exercises.prompt: Name two benefits beyond consistency that a central metrics layer provides.
exercises.solution: (1) **Maintainability / single point of change** — update a metric's logic in one place and every consumer reflects it, instead of editing dozens of dashboards. (2) **Governance & access control** — definitions (and often permissions/row-level security) are centralized and auditable, so the organization can trust and control how metrics are computed and who sees what. (Also: self-service — analysts request metrics by name without re-deriving SQL.)

exercises.prompt: Name two tools that implement a semantic/metrics layer.
exercises.solution: **dbt Semantic Layer / MetricFlow**, **LookML** (Looker's modeling language), and **Cube** are common implementations. They sit between the warehouse/marts and BI tools, letting consumers query metrics in business terms while the layer generates consistent SQL.

lessons.title: The serving layer — semantic/metrics layer & BI
lessons.summary: How gold marts reach decision-makers: a governed semantic/metrics layer that defines each metric once, served to Power BI, Looker, Tableau and APIs so every dashboard shows the same number.
lessons.concept:
Building gold marts isn't the finish line — the data has to **reach decision-makers** consistently. That's the **serving layer**, and its heart is the **semantic (metrics) layer**.

### The problem it solves: metric drift
Without a shared definition, every team re-implements 'revenue' or 'active customers' slightly differently in their own dashboard — and the numbers disagree in the board meeting. The semantic layer fixes this by **defining each metric once**, in one governed place, so **every** tool computes it the same way.

### What a semantic/metrics layer is
A layer (dbt's MetricFlow/semantic models, Looker's LookML, Cube, AtScale, or a set of governed SQL 'metric views') that declares:
- **dimensions** (how you slice: region, segment, day),
- **measures/metrics** (what you count: `net_movement`, `fraud_alert_rate`, `total_exposure`),
- the **joins/grain** they're valid at.
Consumers query *metrics*, not raw tables, so the definition — and the number — is consistent everywhere.

### The serving options (how BI consumes it)
- **BI tools** — Power BI, Looker, Tableau connect to the warehouse/semantic layer for dashboards.
- **Direct SQL / extracts** — analysts query the marts; or scheduled extracts feed reports.
- **APIs / embedded** — a metrics API or embedded analytics powers apps and product dashboards.
- **Caching/aggregates** — pre-aggregated gold + BI extracts keep dashboards fast and cheap.

### Why it matters (who cares)
- **Executives/regulators** get one trusted number (critical in a bank — see the reconciliation/regulatory marts).
- **Analysts** self-serve without re-deriving metrics.
- **Engineers** change a definition once and it propagates everywhere.

**Rule of thumb:** model clean data in silver, build business marts in gold, **define metrics once in the semantic layer**, and let every BI tool read from it. That's how a number stays the same number from the pipeline to the boardroom.
lessons.example:
# define a metric ONCE (dbt semantic layer style):
metrics:
  - name: net_movement
    type: simple
    type_params: {measure: amount_sum}   # SUM(amount) on fact_transactions
# Power BI / Looker / Tableau / a metrics API all read THIS -> same number everywhere
keypoints: The serving layer gets gold marts to decision-makers; its core is the semantic/metrics layer.
Semantic layer defines each metric ONCE (dimensions + measures + grain) → no metric drift across tools.
Options: dbt MetricFlow, LookML, Cube, AtScale, or governed metric views.
Serving paths: BI tools (Power BI/Looker/Tableau), direct SQL/extracts, metrics APIs/embedded, cached aggregates.
Consumers query metrics, not raw tables → consistent numbers from pipeline to boardroom.
Critical in banks: executives & regulators get one trusted number over the reconciled golden source.
lessons.diagram_caption: Gold marts → semantic/metrics layer (define once) → Power BI / Looker / Tableau / API — same number everywhere.

quiz.q: What problem does a semantic/metrics layer primarily solve?
options: Metric drift — every tool computing 'revenue' differently; it defines each metric once for consistency
quiz.explain: Defining metrics once in a governed layer means every dashboard shows the same number.

quiz.q: In the serving layer, consumers should query…
options: the semantic layer's metrics/governed marts, not raw tables
quiz.explain: Querying governed metrics (not raw tables) keeps definitions and numbers consistent across BI tools.

quiz.q: Why is a single trusted metric definition especially important in a bank?
options: Executives and regulators must get one consistent, reconciled number — drift is a compliance risk
quiz.explain: Regulators expect a reconciled golden source; conflicting numbers across reports are a serious problem.

exercises.prompt: What does a semantic layer declare?
exercises.solution: Dimensions (how to slice), measures/metrics (what to compute), and the joins/grain they're valid at — so consumers query metrics consistently.

exercises.prompt: Name three ways BI consumes the serving layer.
exercises.solution: BI tools (Power BI/Looker/Tableau) connecting to the warehouse/semantic layer; direct SQL or scheduled extracts; and metrics APIs / embedded analytics.

exercises.prompt: How does a semantic layer prevent metric drift?
exercises.solution: Each metric is defined once in a governed place; all tools read that definition, so they all compute the same number instead of each re-implementing it.

lessons.title: Data Vault 2.0 — why it exists
lessons.summary: The enterprise-warehouse problem Data Vault solves — integrating many changing sources with full auditability and agility — and the layered architecture that delivers it, versus Kimball and Inmon.
lessons.concept:
An enterprise data warehouse has to **integrate many source systems**, **absorb constant change**, keep a **full audit history**, and **scale** — without re-engineering the model every time a source changes. The two classic approaches each strain under that:
- **Kimball (dimensional)** is excellent for **serving** analytics (star schemas), but integrating *many* changing sources into conformed dimensions is brittle — a source change ripples through marts.
- **Inmon (3NF EDW)** is integrated and normalized, but **slow to change** and load.

### What Data Vault is
**Data Vault 2.0** (Dan Linstedt) is a modeling method + architecture for the **integration layer** of an EDW, built for four properties:
- **Auditable** — everything is **insert-only**; every row carries a **load_date** and **record_source**, so you can reconstruct exactly what each source said at any time (vital in banking, insurance, healthcare).
- **Agile** — adding a source or attribute means **adding** hubs/links/satellites, never redesigning existing structures. Models grow by **extension**.
- **Parallel-loadable** — loads are **key-driven** (hash keys), so there is **no required load order**; every table can load independently and in parallel.
- **Scalable** — patterns repeat, so it automates well across thousands of tables.

### The layered architecture
- **Staging** — land raw data, compute hash keys, hashdiff, load_date, record_source.
- **Raw Vault** — Hubs, Links, Satellites holding the data **exactly as received** (the auditable system of record).
- **Business Vault** — derived structures (computed satellites, PITs, bridges) that apply **soft business rules** and speed up queries.
- **Information Marts** — **dimensional** star schemas / views built on top, which is what BI tools and analysts actually query.

### The key idea
Data Vault **separates the auditable integration layer from the presentation layer**. You don't query the vault directly — you build Kimball-style **marts on top**. Many organisations run a **DV raw vault for integration + Kimball marts for serving** — the best of both.
lessons.example: A bank integrates 14 source systems (core banking, cards, CRM, KYC, …). With Kimball alone, every new source forces painful changes to conformed dimensions and a regulator can't see 'what did system X say on this date'. With Data Vault, each source plugs in as new hubs/links/satellites without touching what exists; every row is insert-only with a record_source and load_date, so the full audit trail is intact — and dimensional marts are built on top for the analysts.
keypoints: Data Vault is an integration-layer pattern for EDWs that must combine many changing sources with full audit history.
Four goals: auditable (insert-only + load_date/record_source), agile (extend, never redesign), parallel-loadable, scalable.
Layers: staging → raw vault (source-faithful Hubs/Links/Satellites) → business vault (logic/PITs/bridges) → information marts.
It separates the auditable system of record (vault) from presentation (dimensional marts) — you don't query the vault directly.
vs Kimball: great for serving but brittle to integrate many sources; vs Inmon: integrated but slow to change.
Common pattern: DV raw vault for integration + Kimball marts on top for serving — best of both.
lessons.diagram_caption: Sources → staging (add hash keys/hashdiff) → raw vault (auditable Hubs/Links/Satellites) → business vault (logic, PITs, bridges) → information marts (star schemas for BI) — each layer loads independently.

quiz.q: What is Data Vault primarily designed to be?
options: A fast star schema for dashboards
An auditable, agile integration layer for combining many changing sources in an enterprise warehouse
quiz.explain: Data Vault is an integration-layer method built for auditability, agility, and parallel loading when many sources must be combined — not a serving/presentation model (marts sit on top).

quiz.q: Which property makes Data Vault auditable?
options: Everything is insert-only, with a load_date and record_source on every row
quiz.explain: Insert-only loads plus load_date and record_source mean you can reconstruct exactly what each source said at any point in time — the audit trail.

quiz.q: Where do BI tools query in a Data Vault architecture?
options: Directly against the raw vault hubs and satellites
Against information marts (dimensional star schemas) built on top of the vault
quiz.explain: The vault is the auditable integration layer; consumers query dimensional marts built on top, because querying time-versioned satellites directly is slow and complex.

exercises.prompt: Your company has 12 source systems, strict audit requirements, and sources that change schema often. Why might Data Vault fit better than pure Kimball here?
exercises.solution: With **12 changing sources and strict audit needs**, pure Kimball struggles: integrating many sources into conformed dimensions is **brittle** (a source change ripples through marts), and dimensional models don't naturally keep a **full insert-only audit trail of every source's exact values over time**. **Data Vault** fits because (1) each source plugs in as **new hubs/links/satellites without redesigning** existing structures — it absorbs schema change by extension; (2) **insert-only** loads with **record_source + load_date** give the regulator-grade audit trail; (3) **hash-key, parallel loads** scale to many sources. You'd still build **Kimball marts on top** for the analysts — so you get auditable integration *and* friendly serving. Kimball alone is better only when there's essentially one source and no heavy audit/integration burden.

exercises.prompt: Explain the role of each layer: staging, raw vault, business vault, information marts.
