quiz.explain: NoSQL starts from the queries and shapes data so each read is a fast single lookup (often duplicating data), whereas relational normalizes entities and joins at query time.

quiz.q: Why is duplicating data acceptable (even encouraged) in NoSQL?
options: Storing data multiple ways serves multiple queries as single-key reads; storage is cheap, joins at scale are not
quiz.explain: Since most NoSQL can't join, you duplicate data keyed for each access pattern so every query is a fast single read — trading cheap storage/write cost for read speed/scale.

quiz.q: In a document store, when should you embed vs reference?
options: Embed data you read together (one fetch); reference data read separately or that's large/shared
quiz.explain: Embed for one-read access of together-read data; reference to avoid bloated documents and excessive duplication for separately-read or large/shared data.

exercises.prompt: You need to query orders both 'by customer' and 'by order id' in Cassandra. How do you model it and why?
exercises.solution: Create **two tables**, each modeled for one query: **`orders_by_customer`** partitioned by `customer_id` (clustered by `order_date` desc) for 'a customer's orders', and **`orders_by_id`** partitioned by `order_id` for 'one order by id'. On each new order, **write it to both tables** (duplicate the data). Now **each query is a single-partition read** — fast and scalable. You do this because Cassandra **can't join or query arbitrary columns** efficiently; the **only** way to make both access patterns fast is to **store the data keyed for each**. The cost is **writing twice** and keeping the copies consistent on updates — accepted in exchange for fast, scalable, join-free reads. This is **query-first, duplicate-as-needed** modeling in action.

exercises.prompt: What's the most common NoSQL modeling mistake, and how do you avoid it?
exercises.solution: The classic mistake is **modeling entities first (relational-style) and only afterward discovering you can't run the queries you need** — because NoSQL stores **can't join** and read efficiently **only by key/partition**, a clean normalized model often **can't answer** common access patterns without slow scans or impossible joins. **Avoid it by modeling query-first:** **list all access patterns up front**, then design **keys/partitions and table/document structure to serve each one** as a single efficient operation, **duplicating/embedding** data as needed. Validate that **every** required query maps to a single-key/partition read (or a supported index). In NoSQL, the queries drive the schema — if you design the data shape before knowing the queries, you'll likely have to **remodel** when the queries appear.

exercises.prompt: Explain the embed-vs-reference trade-off in a document store with an example.
exercises.solution: **Embed** data you **read together** so a common read is **one document fetch, no join** — e.g. a **product** document embedding its **specs and a few recent reviews**, so the product page loads in one read. **Reference** (store an id) data you read **separately**, or that's **large or shared** — e.g. a product's **full review history** (thousands, exceeding document size limits) or a **shared category** record referenced by many products, kept in their own collection and fetched when needed. **Trade-off:** **embedding** gives fast single-read access but **duplicates** data (a shared value copied into many documents → update many places, possible staleness) and risks **document bloat/size limits**; **referencing** avoids duplication (normalized) but needs a **second lookup**. So embed the **bounded, read-together, mostly-static** data, reference the **large, shared, separately-read** data — balancing read speed against duplication and size.

exercises.prompt: Why does query-first modeling suit read-heavy high-scale workloads but cost you on writes?
exercises.hint: Optimize reads by duplicating; updates touch all copies.
exercises.solution: Query-first modeling **optimizes reads** by storing data **pre-shaped for each access pattern** — duplicated/embedded so every query is a **single fast lookup with no joins**, which **scales horizontally** (each read hits one partition). For **read-heavy, high-scale** systems (where reads vastly outnumber writes and must be fast at massive volume), that's exactly the right trade. The **cost lands on writes**: because the same data is **stored in multiple places** (multiple tables, embedded copies), an **update must modify every copy**, adding **write complexity** and risking **divergence** if not all copies are updated atomically (often handled in app logic or accepted as eventual consistency). So you **pay more at write time and in storage** to make reads cheap and scalable — worthwhile when reads dominate, but a poor trade for write-heavy or strongly-consistent transactional workloads (where relational fits better).

lessons.title: NoSQL interview prep & cheat sheet
lessons.summary: Consolidated rapid-review for the NoSQL track: high-frequency answers and a 10-question mock interview.
lessons.concept:
Rapid-review for the NoSQL & Unstructured Data track. The **Go deeper** guide has the full mock; here are the anchors.

### High-frequency answers
- **Four families** → key-value / document / wide-column / graph; pick by access pattern + scale.
- **CAP** → partition → Consistency or Availability; PACELC adds latency-vs-consistency normally.
- **Quorum R+W>N** → overlapping read/write sets → strong consistency.
- **Shard key** → high-cardinality, even spread (hash); bad key → hot partitions.
- **B-tree vs LSM** → read-friendly in-place vs write-buffered (fast writes, compaction).
- **Cache-aside** → check cache → miss → DB → populate; invalidation is hard.
- **Query-first modeling** → design around queries, denormalize/duplicate, partition key is king.

Every NoSQL lesson also has its own '💼 Interview questions' panel and a cheat-sheet button.
keypoints: Four families: key-value, document, wide-column, graph — pick by access pattern and scale.
CAP: partition → C or A; PACELC adds the normal-operation latency-vs-consistency trade-off.
Quorum R+W>N gives strong consistency; tune R/W for the consistency-latency trade-off.
Shard/partition key is the key decision — high cardinality, even distribution, matches the query.
Storage: B-tree (read-heavy) vs LSM (write-heavy, needs compaction); model query-first and denormalize.

quiz.q: In CAP, what must a system give up during a network partition?
options: Either consistency or availability — not both
quiz.explain: During a partition you choose C or A; PACELC adds the latency-vs-consistency trade-off when there's no partition.

quiz.q: What does R + W > N guarantee?
options: The read set overlaps the write set → strong consistency (you read the latest write)
quiz.explain: Overlap means at least one replica in the read responded to the latest write.

quiz.q: What is the danger of a bad shard/partition key?
options: Hot partitions / skew — one node gets most of the load while others idle
quiz.explain: Low-cardinality or time-based keys cluster load; use a high-cardinality, evenly-distributed key (often a hash).

exercises.prompt: Do the 10-question NoSQL mock interview (Go-deeper guide) out loud, no notes.
exercises.hint: families, CAP, quorum, sharding, B-tree vs LSM, Cassandra writes, vector/ANN, cache-aside, query-first modeling, partition key.
exercises.solution: Self-assess: any shaky answer → reread that lesson's concept/diagram and its interview panel, then retry until crisp.

tracks.summary: Beyond the relational table: key-value, document, wide-column and graph stores, CAP & tunable consistency, sharding/replication, LSM storage engines, time-series, search & vector DBs, and query-first modeling — choosing the right store for the access pattern.

tracks.title: Big Data & Apache Spark
tracks.blurb: Process data too big for one machine. (Hands-on Spark coding lives in the SparkQuest app.)

lessons.title: The evolution of Big Data (the story)
lessons.summary: How we got here in four eras — and why each new technology existed to fix the previous era's limits.
lessons.concept:
Big Data tools make far more sense when you know the **story** of why each was invented. It unfolds in four eras, each solving the last one's wall.

**1. Pre-Big-Data (—2003).** Data lived in **relational databases and data warehouses** on a single (ever-bigger) server — you **scaled up**. It worked until data outgrew what one machine could store or process: scaling up gets exponentially expensive and eventually impossible.

**2. The Hadoop era (2003-2012).** Google published the **GFS** (2003) and **MapReduce** (2004) papers, showing how to store and process data across **clusters of cheap commodity machines** — **scale out**. Doug Cutting turned these ideas into **Hadoop** (2006; top-level Apache 2008): **HDFS** (distributed storage) + **MapReduce** (distributed batch compute), surrounded by an ecosystem (Hive, Pig, HBase, YARN). Big, cheap, fault-tolerant — but **batch-only and disk-heavy** (slow).

**3. Spark, NoSQL & streaming (2009-2016).** **Spark** (Berkeley 2009; Apache 2014) kept data **in memory** and ran 10-100× faster than MapReduce for iterative work. **NoSQL** (Dynamo paper 2007 → Cassandra, MongoDB) scaled flexible, non-relational data. **Kafka** (LinkedIn 2011) + **Flink** (2014) brought **real-time streaming**, ending the batch-only world.

**4. Cloud & lakehouse (2014-2026).** The cloud **separated storage from compute** (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift) — scale and pay for each independently. Cheap data lakes risked becoming **swamps**, so **table formats** (Hudi 2016, Delta 2017, **Iceberg** 2017) added ACID + reliability → the **lakehouse**. Today (2026): **Iceberg won**, real-time OLAP is mainstream, and the **AI/agentic** era is reshaping the stack again.

The through-line: each era raised **scale**, then **speed**, then **reliability + openness**. Open **Go deeper** for the dated timeline.
lessons.example:
# the one-line story
RDBMS (scale UP)                      hits a wall
  -> Hadoop: HDFS + MapReduce (scale OUT, batch, disk)         2006
  -> Spark (in-memory) + Kafka/Flink (streaming) + NoSQL       2009-2016
  -> Cloud (storage/compute split) + Lakehouse (Iceberg/Delta) 2014-2026
  -> today: Iceberg wins, real-time OLAP, AI/agentic
keypoints: Four eras: pre-big-data (RDBMS) → Hadoop → Spark/NoSQL/streaming → cloud/lakehouse.
Scale UP (one big box) hit a wall → scale OUT on commodity clusters (Hadoop).
Google's GFS (2003) + MapReduce (2004) papers inspired Hadoop (2006).
Spark (in-memory, 2009/2014) fixed MapReduce's slow, disk-heavy batch; Kafka/Flink added streaming.
Cloud separated storage from compute (Snowflake/BigQuery); table formats made the lakehouse.
Each era answered the previous one's limits — more scale, then speed, then reliability + openness.

quiz.q: What core shift defined the move from RDBMS to Hadoop?
options: scaling up to a bigger server
scaling out across clusters of commodity machines
quiz.explain: Hadoop's breakthrough was distributing storage/compute across many cheap machines (scale out) instead of one big server.

quiz.q: Which papers inspired Hadoop?
quiz.explain: Hadoop implemented the ideas in Google's GFS and MapReduce papers.

quiz.q: Why did Spark largely displace MapReduce?
options: it kept data in memory and ran far faster, especially for iterative work
quiz.explain: Spark's in-memory DAG execution is 10-100× faster than MapReduce's disk-based two-stage model.

exercises.prompt: Name the four eras of Big Data in order.
exercises.solution: Pre-Big-Data (RDBMS/warehouses) → Hadoop era → Spark/NoSQL/streaming → cloud & lakehouse.

exercises.prompt: What limit did each era fix?
exercises.solution: Scale-up hit a wall → Hadoop scaled out (batch); MapReduce was slow → Spark added speed + streaming added real-time; lakes became swamps → lakehouse added reliability; on-prem coupling → cloud separated storage/compute.

exercises.prompt: What's the state of Big Data in 2026?
exercises.solution: Cloud lakehouse is the default; Iceberg won the table-format war; real-time OLAP is mainstream; the AI/agentic era is reshaping the stack.

lessons.diagram_caption: Four eras: pre-big-data → Hadoop → Spark/NoSQL/streaming → cloud & lakehouse, each fixing the last's limits.

lessons.title: The Hadoop era — GFS, MapReduce & the ecosystem
lessons.summary: How Google's papers became Hadoop, what HDFS + MapReduce + YARN actually did, and why the ecosystem grew around them.
lessons.concept:
The Hadoop era is where 'Big Data' became real. It started with three **Google papers**: **GFS** (2003, distributed storage), **MapReduce** (2004, distributed batch compute), and **Bigtable** (2006, wide-column store). Doug Cutting and Mike Cafarella implemented the first two as **Hadoop** (0.1.0 in 2006; Apache top-level in 2008), and Yahoo scaled it to thousands of nodes.

**The two pillars:**
- **HDFS** — a distributed file system that splits a huge file into **blocks** replicated across many commodity machines, so it survives disk/node failure and scales to petabytes cheaply.
- **MapReduce** — a programming model: **map** (process each split in parallel) → **shuffle** (group by key) → **reduce** (aggregate). It moves *compute to the data* and tolerates failures by re-running tasks.
- **YARN** (Hadoop 2.0, 2013) — separated **resource management** from MapReduce, so other engines (like Spark) could run on the same cluster.

**The ecosystem grew** because raw MapReduce was tedious to write:
- **Hive** — SQL (HiveQL) over Hadoop, compiled to MapReduce; plus the **Hive metastore** (still used today).
- **Pig** — a dataflow scripting language.
- **HBase** — a Bigtable-style NoSQL store on HDFS for random reads/writes.
- **Sqoop** (DB import/export), **Oozie** (workflow), **ZooKeeper** (coordination).

**Why it eventually faded for compute:** MapReduce writes intermediate results **to disk** between every stage — slow, especially for iterative/interactive work — and it's verbose. Spark fixed the speed; the cloud + object storage fixed HDFS's operational burden. But the legacy lives on: **HDFS concepts → object storage**, **Hive metastore → modern catalogs**, **MapReduce → Spark's model**. Open **Go deeper** for the MapReduce word-count and the ecosystem map.
lessons.example:
# MapReduce word count (the canonical example), in pseudo-code
def map(doc):        # runs in parallel on each split
    for word in doc.split(): emit(word, 1)
def reduce(word, counts):     # after shuffle groups by word
    emit(word, sum(counts))
# HDFS stores the input/output across the cluster; YARN schedules the tasks
keypoints: Google papers: GFS (2003 storage), MapReduce (2004 compute), Bigtable (2006 NoSQL).
Hadoop (2006) = HDFS (distributed, replicated storage) + MapReduce (distributed batch compute).
MapReduce: map (parallel) → shuffle (group by key) → reduce (aggregate); moves compute to data.
YARN (2013) split resource management from MapReduce, letting Spark etc. share the cluster.
Ecosystem: Hive (SQL + metastore), Pig, HBase, Sqoop, Oozie, ZooKeeper.
Faded for compute (disk-heavy, verbose) but its ideas live on: object storage, catalogs, Spark.

quiz.q: HDFS provides:
options: distributed, replicated block storage across commodity machines
quiz.explain: HDFS splits files into replicated blocks across nodes for cheap, fault-tolerant petabyte-scale storage.

quiz.q: The MapReduce stages are:
quiz.explain: Map processes splits in parallel, shuffle groups by key, reduce aggregates.

quiz.q: What did YARN enable?
options: separating resource management from MapReduce so other engines (Spark) share the cluster
quiz.explain: YARN (Hadoop 2.0) generalized cluster resource management beyond MapReduce.

exercises.prompt: Map each Google paper to what it inspired.
exercises.solution: GFS → HDFS (storage); MapReduce → Hadoop MapReduce (compute); Bigtable → HBase (wide-column NoSQL).

exercises.prompt: Why was Hive created on top of Hadoop?
exercises.solution: Raw MapReduce was tedious to write; Hive let analysts use SQL (HiveQL) compiled to MapReduce, and added the metastore for schemas.

exercises.prompt: Name two Hadoop-era ideas that survive in the modern stack.
exercises.solution: The Hive metastore concept → modern catalogs; HDFS block/replication ideas → cloud object storage; MapReduce's model → Spark.

lessons.diagram_caption: The Hadoop stack: HDFS (storage) → YARN (resources) → engines (MapReduce/Tez/Spark) → tools (Hive, Pig, HBase, Sqoop), with ZooKeeper/Oozie coordinating.

lessons.title: Hadoop — HDFS, YARN, MapReduce & the ecosystem
lessons.summary: Hadoop in depth: HDFS internals (NameNode/DataNode, the write & read paths, rack-aware replication, HA), YARN internals (ResourceManager/NodeManager/ApplicationMaster, the job-submission flow, schedulers), the ecosystem, and the cloud shift — and why the ideas persist.
lessons.concept:
**Hadoop** is the framework big data was built on. Understand its two architectural halves — HDFS (storage) and YARN (compute scheduling) — in depth, because their ideas live on everywhere.

### HDFS architecture
- **NameNode (master)** — holds the filesystem **metadata** (directory tree, file→block mapping, block locations) **in memory** for speed, persisted via an **fsimage** + **edit log** (journal of changes). It's the brain; historically a single point of failure.
- **DataNodes (workers)** — store the actual **blocks** on local disk, send periodic **heartbeats** and **block reports** to the NameNode.
- **Blocks** — files split into large blocks (default 128 MB) so a huge file spans many DataNodes; large blocks minimize metadata and seek overhead.
- **Replication & rack awareness** — each block replicated (default **3×**); the placement policy puts one replica on the local rack and others on a **different rack**, balancing fault tolerance against cross-rack bandwidth.

### HDFS write & read paths
- **Write:** client asks the NameNode where to put a block; the NameNode returns a list of DataNodes; the client streams the block to the first DataNode, which **pipelines** it to the second, then the third (replication pipeline); acks flow back. Metadata is updated on the NameNode.
- **Read:** client asks the NameNode for the block locations, then reads each block **directly from the nearest DataNode** (data locality). The NameNode never carries the data itself — only metadata.

### High availability
Modern HDFS runs an **active + standby NameNode** sharing the edit log via **JournalNodes**, with **ZooKeeper** automatic failover — removing the single-point-of-failure. The old **Secondary NameNode** is *not* a backup; it merely **checkpoints** the fsimage+edits periodically.

### YARN architecture
- **ResourceManager (master)** — global scheduler; its **Scheduler** allocates resources, its **ApplicationsManager** accepts jobs and launches their masters.
- **NodeManager (per node)** — manages **containers** (CPU/memory slices) and reports node health.
- **ApplicationMaster (per job)** — negotiates containers from the RM and coordinates that job's tasks (so the RM isn't a bottleneck — each app manages itself).
- **Container** — the unit of allocated resources where a task runs.

### Job submission flow
Client → RM; RM launches an **ApplicationMaster** in a container; the AM **requests containers** from the RM; the RM tells NodeManagers to launch them; tasks run; the AM monitors/retries and reports completion. **Schedulers** (FIFO, **Capacity**, **Fair**) decide how competing apps share the cluster.

### Ecosystem & the cloud shift
Around these sat HBase (wide-column store), Hive (SQL + metastore), Pig, Sqoop, Oozie, ZooKeeper. The cloud then **decoupled storage and compute** — object storage (S3/GCS/ADLS) replaced HDFS, Kubernetes/serverless replaced YARN, Spark/Trino replaced MapReduce — but **blocks/replication, data locality, a resource scheduler, and the Hive metastore** all carried over. Hadoop literacy is how you reason about and debug modern stacks.
lessons.example: A 1 GB file in HDFS becomes ~8 blocks of 128 MB, each replicated to 3 DataNodes. Lose a node and every block still has 2 copies; Spark-on-YARN schedules each task on a node that already holds its block, avoiding network reads.
keypoints: HDFS NameNode holds metadata in memory (fsimage + edit log); DataNodes store blocks and send heartbeats/block reports.
Files split into large blocks (128 MB) replicated 3× with rack-aware placement (fault tolerance vs cross-rack bandwidth).
Write path pipelines a block across DataNodes; read path fetches blocks directly from the nearest DataNode (locality).
HA = active/standby NameNodes via JournalNodes + ZooKeeper failover; Secondary NameNode only checkpoints (not a backup).
YARN: ResourceManager (scheduler) + NodeManagers (containers) + a per-job ApplicationMaster that negotiates resources.
Job flow: client→RM→AM in a container→AM requests containers→NMs launch tasks→AM monitors/reports.
Schedulers (FIFO/Capacity/Fair) arbitrate how apps share the cluster.
Cloud decoupled storage/compute (object storage, K8s, Spark/Trino) but kept Hadoop's ideas + the Hive metastore.
lessons.diagram_caption: HDFS spreads replicated blocks across DataNodes; YARN schedules compute containers near the data.

quiz.q: How does HDFS achieve fault tolerance?
options: By splitting files into blocks replicated (~3×) across DataNodes
quiz.explain: HDFS replicates each block across multiple DataNodes, so losing a node still leaves other copies; the NameNode tracks where blocks live.

quiz.q: What is YARN's role?
options: Schedules cluster resources (containers) so frameworks like Spark get executors
quiz.explain: YARN is the resource negotiator/scheduler; it allocates CPU/memory containers to applications such as Spark, letting many frameworks share a cluster.

quiz.q: What Hadoop-era component still underpins modern engines like Spark and Trino?
options: The Hive Metastore (table schemas and partitions)
quiz.explain: The Hive Metastore catalog of table definitions and partitions is still used by Spark, Trino, and cloud catalogs to locate and describe tables.

exercises.prompt: Walk through what happens on the NameNode and DataNodes when a client writes a 300 MB file (block size 128 MB, replication 3).
exercises.solution: The file becomes **3 blocks** (128+128+44 MB). For **each block** the client asks the **NameNode**, which picks 3 DataNodes (rack-aware) and returns them. The client streams the block to **DataNode-1**, which **pipelines** it to DataNode-2, which pipelines to DataNode-3; acknowledgments flow back up the pipeline. The **NameNode records** the file→block mapping and block→DataNode locations in its in-memory metadata (and edit log). The NameNode never touches the block data itself — only coordinates and stores metadata.

exercises.prompt: Why is the NameNode historically a single point of failure, and how does HDFS HA solve it?
exercises.solution: All filesystem **metadata lives on the one NameNode** (in memory, backed by fsimage+edits); if it dies, no one can locate any block — the whole filesystem is unavailable, even though the data on DataNodes is intact. **HA** runs an **active and a standby NameNode** that share the edit log through **JournalNodes**; **ZooKeeper** detects the active's failure and **automatically promotes the standby**. The standby has the up-to-date metadata, so failover is fast and the single point of failure is removed.

exercises.prompt: Explain rack awareness in replica placement and the trade-off it balances.
exercises.solution: The default policy places the **first replica on the writer's local rack**, and the **other replicas on a different rack** (often two on a second rack). This balances two goals: keeping **one copy rack-local** (fast writes, locality for reads) while ensuring **at least one copy survives a whole-rack failure** (power/switch loss). It avoids putting all replicas on one rack (no rack-failure protection) or spreading every replica across racks (excessive cross-rack bandwidth, the scarce resource).

exercises.prompt: Describe each YARN component's role by tracing a Spark job from submit to finish.
exercises.solution: The client submits to the **ResourceManager**, whose ApplicationsManager launches the job's **ApplicationMaster** in a **container** on some node (via that node's **NodeManager**). The AM (Spark's driver-side coordinator) **requests executor containers** from the RM's Scheduler; the RM grants them and the relevant **NodeManagers launch the containers** (executors). Tasks run in those containers; the **AM monitors** them, re-requests on failure, and reports progress; on completion the AM releases resources and exits. The RM only allocates — each AM manages its own job, so the RM doesn't bottleneck.

exercises.prompt: What's the difference between the Secondary NameNode and a standby NameNode? (Common misconception.)
exercises.solution: The **Secondary NameNode is NOT a backup/failover node**. Its only job is **checkpointing**: periodically merging the NameNode's **fsimage + edit log** into a new compact fsimage so the edit log doesn't grow unbounded and restarts are faster. If the NameNode dies, the Secondary cannot take over. A **standby NameNode** (HA) *is* a hot replica with current metadata (via JournalNodes) that **automatically takes over** on failure. People confuse the two because of the name — only HA provides failover.

exercises.prompt: Map each Hadoop component to its modern cloud replacement and one idea that carried over.
exercises.solution: **HDFS → object storage** (S3/GCS/ADLS) — kept **block storage with replication/durability**. **YARN → Kubernetes / serverless schedulers** — kept the idea of a **resource scheduler granting containers**. **MapReduce → Spark/Flink/Trino** — kept **map→shuffle→reduce** and **data locality / shuffle cost**. **Hive Metastore** persists almost unchanged as the lake catalog. The cloud replaced the *implementations* (and decoupled storage from compute) while keeping the *concepts*.

lessons.title: MapReduce — the programming model
lessons.summary: The full MapReduce pipeline: InputSplits & RecordReaders, the Mapper lifecycle, partitioner, combiner, the shuffle/sort internals, the Reducer phases, joins, counters, and exactly why it's slow — the model that still underlies every distributed shuffle.
lessons.concept:
MapReduce is the model that made distributed processing accessible. You write two functions; the framework handles parallelism, data movement, and failure. Here is the **full pipeline**, end to end.

### 1. Input: splits & record readers
The job's input is divided into **InputSplits** (logically ~one HDFS block, ~128 MB each) — this sets the **number of map tasks** (one per split). A **RecordReader** turns a split's bytes into **key–value records** for the mapper (for text, key = byte offset, value = line). `InputFormat` (TextInputFormat, SequenceFileInputFormat…) defines splitting + the RecordReader.

### 2. Map phase
Each **Mapper** runs `map(key, value)` per record **in parallel** on the node holding the split (data locality), emitting intermediate `(key, value)` pairs via `context.write`. The Mapper has a lifecycle: `setup()` → `map()` per record → `cleanup()`.

### 3. Partitioner — which reducer gets which key
Each emitted key is assigned to a **reducer** by the **Partitioner** — by default `hash(key) % numReducers`. A custom partitioner controls distribution (and is how you fix **skew** or keep related keys together). The number of reducers is set by you (`numReduceTasks`).

### 4. Combiner — local pre-aggregation
An optional **Combiner** is a 'mini-reduce' run **on the map node** before data is shuffled — e.g. it sums each map's `(word,1)` pairs locally so you ship `(word, 1000)` once. Safe only for **associative & commutative** ops (sum, count, max); **unsafe for average** unless you carry (sum, count). It can hugely cut shuffle volume.

### 5. Shuffle & sort — the expensive heart
Map output is buffered in memory and **spills to disk** when full, sorted by key within each spill; spills are then **merge-sorted**. Reducers **fetch** their partitions from every mapper over the network (the *shuffle*), and merge-sort the fetched data so each reducer sees keys **sorted** with all their values grouped. This network + disk + sort is what dominates MapReduce runtime.

### 6. Reduce phase
Each **Reducer** runs three sub-phases — **shuffle** (fetch), **sort** (merge), **reduce** — then `reduce(key, Iterable<values>)` aggregates each key's values and writes output via the **OutputFormat** to HDFS (one file per reducer, `part-r-00000`…).

### Joins, counters, fault tolerance
- **Reduce-side join:** map both datasets to the join key, tag the source, join in the reducer (general but shuffles everything). **Map-side join:** if one side is small, load it into each mapper (no shuffle) — the MapReduce ancestor of Spark's broadcast join.
- **Counters** track job-wide metrics (records read, bad rows).
- **Fault tolerance:** failed tasks are **re-run** (intermediate output is on disk); **speculative execution** launches a duplicate of a straggler task and keeps whichever finishes first.

### Why it's slow (and why Spark won)
Every job **persists output to disk**, and each task pays **JVM startup**. A multi-step pipeline becomes a chain of disk-bound jobs; iterative algorithms re-read from disk each pass. Spark keeps intermediates **in memory** across a DAG — but its wide ops are still **map → shuffle → reduce** underneath, so this model is the key to understanding shuffles everywhere.
lessons.example: Counting words across a 1 TB corpus: each node **maps** its split to `(word, 1)` pairs, a **combiner** sums them locally to shrink the shuffle, the **shuffle** groups each word's partials onto one reducer, and **reduce** sums them into the final count — fully parallel and fault-tolerant.
keypoints: InputSplits (≈one block) set the number of map tasks; a RecordReader yields key/value records (InputFormat defines both).
Mapper lifecycle setup→map(per record)→cleanup emits intermediate pairs in parallel with data locality.
Partitioner (default hash(key)%R) routes keys to reducers; a custom one fixes skew/co-location.
Combiner = local pre-aggregation before shuffle (safe for associative ops; carry sum+count for average).
Shuffle/sort: map output spills+merge-sorts to disk; reducers fetch their partitions and merge-sort — the cost center.
Reducer phases shuffle→sort→reduce(key, values) then OutputFormat writes one file per reducer.
Map-side join (small side in each mapper, no shuffle) vs reduce-side join (shuffle both, join in reducer).
Fault tolerance: re-run failed tasks (intermediates on disk) + speculative execution for stragglers.
Slow because every job hits disk and pays JVM startup; Spark stays in memory but is map→shuffle→reduce underneath.
lessons.diagram_caption: Map emits key/value pairs in parallel; shuffle groups them by key; reduce aggregates each key.

quiz.q: What happens in the shuffle/sort phase of MapReduce?
options: Records are mapped to key/value pairs
All values for each key are grouped together, moving data across the network to reducers
quiz.explain: Shuffle & sort regroups map output so every value for a given key arrives at the same reducer — the network-bound step that Spark's wide operations also perform.

quiz.q: What does a combiner do?
options: Pre-aggregates map output locally before the shuffle, reducing network data
quiz.explain: A combiner is a local 'mini-reduce' on each node that shrinks map output (e.g. partial sums) before it's shuffled — Spark's map-side aggregation is the same idea.

quiz.q: Why does understanding MapReduce still help with Spark?
options: Spark's wide operations (groupBy/reduceByKey/join) are map→shuffle→reduce under the hood
quiz.explain: Spark's shuffles, map-side combine, and partitioning mirror MapReduce; the model explains why wide ops are costly and how to optimize them.

exercises.prompt: Trace word count through input split → map → combiner → partition → shuffle/sort → reduce for 'big data','big spark'.
exercises.solution: **Split/RecordReader:** two records `(0,'big data')`, `(9,'big spark')`. **Map:** `('big',1)('data',1)` and `('big',1)('spark',1)`. **Combiner (per map):** map1→`('big',1)('data',1)`, map2→`('big',1)('spark',1)` (no local dupes here). **Partition:** `hash(key)%R` routes each key. **Shuffle/sort:** reducer sees sorted, grouped `big:[1,1] data:[1] spark:[1]`. **Reduce:** `big:2 data:1 spark:1`, written as `part-r-00000`.

exercises.prompt: What determines the number of map tasks and the number of reduce tasks, and why does it matter?
exercises.solution: **Map tasks = number of InputSplits** (≈ one per HDFS block), so input size/block size fixes it — more splits = more parallelism but more overhead; tiny files create too many maps (the small-files problem). **Reduce tasks = `numReduceTasks`** (you set it). Too few reducers → each does too much / skew; too many → lots of small output files and scheduling overhead. Right-sizing both balances parallelism against overhead.

exercises.prompt: Why is a combiner safe for SUM but not for AVERAGE, and how do you compute average correctly?
exercises.solution: A combiner runs partial reductions locally, so the op must be **associative & commutative** — `sum` and `count` are (partial sums combine correctly). **Average is not**: `avg(avg(a,b), c) ≠ avg(a,b,c)`. To average correctly, have map emit `(key, (value, 1))`, the combiner/reducer **sum the values and sum the counts** separately, and divide `total_sum/total_count` only at the **end**. You carry the additive components, not the ratio.

exercises.prompt: Contrast map-side and reduce-side joins. When is each appropriate, and which Spark feature mirrors map-side?
exercises.hint: Small side in mapper vs shuffle both.
exercises.solution: **Reduce-side join:** map both datasets to the join key (tagging source), shuffle, and join in the reducer — works for any sizes but **shuffles everything** (expensive). **Map-side join:** if one dataset is **small enough to fit in memory**, load it into every mapper and join locally — **no shuffle**, much faster, but only for small×large. Spark's **broadcast join** is the direct descendant of the map-side join.

exercises.prompt: An MR job has one reducer running 10× longer than the others. Diagnose and give two fixes.
exercises.solution: **Skew**: the default `hash(key)%R` partitioner sent a **hot key** (or many keys) to one reducer, overloading it. Fixes: (1) a **custom partitioner** that spreads the hot key (or **salt** the key — append a random suffix, reduce, then re-combine); (2) add a **combiner** to shrink the hot key's data before shuffle; (3) increase reducers if the imbalance is general. Skew on one key can't be solved by more reducers alone — you must redistribute that key.

exercises.prompt: Explain how MapReduce tolerates a node dying mid-job, and what 'speculative execution' adds.
exercises.solution: Because each task's **output is written to disk** and the framework tracks task state, a failed task (or one on a dead node) is simply **re-scheduled and re-run** elsewhere — completed tasks aren't lost, so the job still finishes. **Speculative execution** addresses *slow* (not failed) tasks: if a task lags far behind its peers (bad disk, busy node), the framework launches a **duplicate** of it on another node and **uses whichever finishes first**, killing the other — preventing one straggler from delaying the whole job.

lessons.title: Spark, NoSQL & the streaming revolution
lessons.summary: Why Spark beat MapReduce, how NoSQL scaled flexible data, and how Kafka + Flink ended the batch-only world.
lessons.concept:
The second wave fixed the Hadoop era's two big limits: **speed** and **batch-only**.

**Spark (in-memory) beat MapReduce.** Born at Berkeley's AMPLab (2009; Apache top-level 2014), Spark keeps intermediate data **in memory** and builds a **DAG** of work with lazy evaluation — 10-100× faster than MapReduce's disk-based, two-stage model, especially for **iterative** (ML) and **interactive** queries. Its **RDD → DataFrame/Spark SQL** API is far easier than raw MapReduce, and it runs batch, SQL, streaming, and ML in one engine.

**NoSQL scaled flexible data.** Amazon's **Dynamo** paper (2007) inspired **Cassandra** (2008) and others; **MongoDB** (2009) popularized document stores. These traded strict relational guarantees for **horizontal scale, flexible schemas, and high write throughput** (the AP side of CAP) — fitting web-scale, semi-structured data the relational model strained on.

**Streaming ended batch-only.** **Kafka** (LinkedIn, open-sourced 2011) became the **durable event log / backbone** for moving data in real time. Processing engines followed: **Spark Streaming**, **Storm**, **Samza**, and **Flink** (Apache 2014) — a true **event-at-a-time** streaming engine with event-time and state. Architectures emerged to combine both: **Lambda** (batch + speed layers, ~2011) and later **Kappa** (stream-only).

**The net effect:** data engineering moved from 'run a big batch job overnight' to 'process data continuously, fast, in many shapes.' These are the tools you still use daily — and the foundation for the cloud/lakehouse era next. Open **Go deeper** for the Spark-vs-MapReduce detail and CAP.
lessons.example:
# same word count — Spark vs the MapReduce version (concise + in-memory)
spark.read.text("s3://logs/") \
     .selectExpr("explode(split(value,' ')) as word") \
     .groupBy("word").count() \
     .orderBy("count", ascending=False).show()
# DAG + in-memory shuffle → far faster than disk-based MapReduce
keypoints: Spark (2009/2014): in-memory DAG → 10-100× faster than MapReduce; one engine for batch/SQL/stream/ML.
RDD → DataFrame/Spark SQL is far easier than raw MapReduce.
NoSQL (Dynamo paper 2007 → Cassandra/Mongo): horizontal scale, flexible schema, high write throughput.
Kafka (2011) = durable real-time event backbone; Flink (2014) = true event-at-a-time streaming.
Lambda (batch+speed) and Kappa (stream-only) architectures combined the paradigms.
The shift: from overnight batch to continuous, fast processing of many data shapes.

quiz.q: Spark's main speed advantage over MapReduce is:
options: keeping intermediate data in memory with a DAG (vs disk between stages)
quiz.explain: In-memory DAG execution avoids MapReduce's disk writes between every stage, hugely speeding iterative/interactive work.

quiz.q: NoSQL stores like Cassandra primarily traded strict relational guarantees for:
options: horizontal scale, flexible schemas, and high write throughput
quiz.explain: NoSQL favors scale and flexibility (often AP in CAP) over strict relational consistency/joins.

quiz.q: What role does Kafka play in the streaming era?
options: a durable, real-time event log/backbone that moves data between systems
quiz.explain: Kafka is the durable publish-subscribe event backbone; engines like Flink process those streams.

exercises.prompt: Give two reasons Spark replaced MapReduce for most compute.
exercises.solution: In-memory DAG = far faster (esp. iterative/interactive), and DataFrame/SQL APIs are far easier than raw MapReduce.

exercises.prompt: When would you reach for a NoSQL store over an RDBMS?
exercises.solution: For web-scale, high-write, flexible/semi-structured data needing horizontal scale and availability over strict relational joins/consistency.

exercises.prompt: Kafka vs Flink — what does each do?
exercises.solution: Kafka is the durable real-time event log/backbone; Flink is a stream-processing engine that computes over those events (event-time, state, exactly-once).

lessons.diagram_caption: The shifts: scale-up→out, disk→memory, batch→streaming, coupled→separated, lake→lakehouse.

lessons.title: Cloud, the lakehouse & today (2026)
lessons.summary: How the cloud separated storage from compute, why table formats created the lakehouse, and where Big Data stands in 2026.
lessons.concept:
The newest era is about **economics and reliability** on the cloud.

**Cloud separated storage from compute.** On-prem Hadoop coupled them on fixed hardware. The cloud put data cheaply on **object storage** (S3/GCS/ADLS) and spun compute up/down on demand. **Snowflake** (founded 2012), **BigQuery** (Dremel paper 2010), and **Redshift** (2013) made **separation of storage and compute** mainstream — scale and pay for each independently, isolate workloads, and go serverless.

**Data lakes risked becoming swamps.** Dumping raw files in object storage is cheap but chaotic: no transactions, no schema enforcement, no way to update or undo. A failed write left partial files; concurrent writers corrupted each other.

**Table formats created the lakehouse.** A metadata/transaction layer over Parquet fixed this: **Apache Hudi** (Uber 2016 — first with row-level upserts/deletes), **Delta Lake** (Databricks 2017/2019), and **Apache Iceberg** (Netflix 2017 → Apache, top-level 2020). They add **ACID, time travel, schema evolution, and performance** directly on the lake — warehouse reliability on lake economics = the **lakehouse**.

**The modern data stack** grew alongside: **dbt** for SQL transformations (ELT), **Airflow** (Airbnb 2014) for orchestration, and managed everything.

**Today (2026):** the lakehouse is the **default architecture**; **Iceberg won** the table-format war (standard **REST catalog**, multi-engine); **real-time OLAP** (ClickHouse/Pinot) is mainstream; and the **AI/agentic** era is the next wave — pipelines feeding (and increasingly built by) AI. You've now seen the whole arc; the rest of this track teaches the engine at its center — **Spark**. Open **Go deeper** for the table-format comparison.
lessons.example:
# the lakehouse in one picture
object storage (Parquet, cheap)            <- storage
  + table format (Iceberg/Delta/Hudi)      <- ACID, time travel, schema evolution
  + REST catalog (Polaris/Gravitino)       <- source of truth, multi-engine
  + any engine (Spark/Trino/Flink/DuckDB)  <- compute, scaled independently
= warehouse reliability on data-lake economics
keypoints: Cloud separated storage from compute (Snowflake/BigQuery/Redshift): scale & pay independently.
Raw data lakes risk becoming swamps: no ACID, no schema enforcement, no updates/undo.
Table formats fix this: Hudi (2016), Delta (2017), Iceberg (2017) → ACID + time travel + schema evolution.
Lakehouse = warehouse reliability on cheap object storage.
Modern stack: dbt (ELT transforms) + Airflow (orchestration) + managed cloud.
2026: lakehouse is default, Iceberg won (REST catalog, multi-engine), real-time OLAP + AI/agentic next.

quiz.q: The key economic shift the cloud brought to Big Data is:
options: separating storage from compute so each scales and is paid for independently
quiz.explain: Cloud warehouses/lakes decouple storage and compute, enabling elasticity, workload isolation, and pay-per-use.

quiz.q: Table formats (Iceberg/Delta/Hudi) turn a data lake into a lakehouse by adding:
options: ACID transactions, time travel, and schema evolution over Parquet
quiz.explain: They add a metadata/transaction layer giving warehouse-grade reliability on the lake.

quiz.q: In 2026 the table-format question is:
options: settled — Iceberg won, with a standard REST catalog and multi-engine support
quiz.explain: Iceberg became the de-facto standard with broad multi-engine and REST-catalog adoption.

exercises.prompt: Why separate storage and compute in the cloud?
exercises.solution: So you can scale and pay for each independently, isolate workloads (BI vs ETL), and run transient/serverless compute over cheap durable storage.

exercises.prompt: What problem do table formats solve on a data lake?
exercises.solution: Plain files have no transactions/schema/undo; formats add ACID, time travel, and schema evolution, preventing the lake from becoming a swamp.

exercises.prompt: Name the three major table formats and their origins.
exercises.solution: Apache Hudi (Uber 2016), Delta Lake (Databricks 2017), Apache Iceberg (Netflix 2017) — Iceberg is the 2026 standard.

lessons.diagram_caption: Cloud: object storage + table format + catalog + any engine = the lakehouse (warehouse reliability on lake economics).

lessons.title: What makes data 'big'? Scale & distribution
lessons.summary: When data outgrows one machine: the Vs (volume, velocity, variety), scale-up vs scale-out, and why distributed processing (parallelism + fault tolerance on commodity hardware) is the answer.
lessons.concept:
'Big data' isn't a row count — it's the point where data **outgrows a single machine**, so you must spread storage and computation across many machines.

### The Vs
- **Volume** — too much to fit on one box's disk/RAM (terabytes to petabytes).
- **Velocity** — arriving too fast to process serially (millions of events/sec).
- **Variety** — structured, semi-structured (JSON), and unstructured (text, images) together.
- (often added) **Veracity** — uncertain/dirty data, and **Value** — the point of it all.

### Scale-up vs scale-out
- **Scale up (vertical)** — buy a bigger machine. Simple, but there's a ceiling and cost grows super-linearly.
- **Scale out (horizontal)** — add more commodity machines and split the work. Effectively unlimited, cheaper per unit — but now you have a **distributed system** with its own problems.

### Why distributed processing
Splitting data into **partitions** across a cluster buys three things:

1. **Parallelism** — N machines process N partitions at once, so a job that took hours takes minutes.
2. **Fault tolerance** — machines fail at scale; the framework re-runs the lost piece elsewhere so the job still finishes.
3. **Cost** — many cheap commodity nodes beat one exotic supercomputer.

### The catch (why frameworks exist)
Distributed computing is hard: moving data between machines (the network) is slow, machines fail, and results must stay correct. **Frameworks like Spark hide that complexity** — you write transformations, and Spark handles partitioning, parallel scheduling, network shuffles, and recovery. The skill is writing code that **minimizes data movement**, because the network — not the CPU — is usually the bottleneck at scale.
lessons.example: A 5 TB log dataset won't fit on one server. Split into ~40,000 partitions of ~128 MB across a 100-node cluster, each node filters and aggregates its slice in parallel, and Spark combines the results — minutes instead of days, and if a node dies its partitions are recomputed elsewhere.
keypoints: Big data = data that outgrows one machine, forcing distribution of storage and compute.
The Vs: Volume, Velocity, Variety (plus Veracity, Value).
Scale-out (many commodity nodes) beats scale-up (one big box) for cost and ceiling.
Distribution buys parallelism, fault tolerance, and cheaper hardware.
The network is the bottleneck — frameworks hide complexity; you minimize data movement.
Partitioning data across the cluster is what enables parallel processing.
lessons.diagram_caption: Past one machine's limits, split data into partitions across a cluster and process in parallel.

quiz.q: What best defines 'big data'?
options: Data that outgrows a single machine, requiring distributed storage/compute
quiz.explain: It's about outgrowing one machine — when you must spread data and work across many nodes — not a fixed row count.

quiz.q: Why is scale-out usually preferred over scale-up for big data?
options: Many commodity nodes are cheaper and have no single-machine ceiling
quiz.explain: Adding commodity machines scales effectively without limit and costs less per unit than ever-bigger single servers — at the price of distributed-system complexity.

quiz.q: At scale, the most common performance bottleneck is…
options: Moving data between machines over the network
quiz.explain: Network data movement (shuffles) dominates; good distributed code minimizes how much data crosses the network.

exercises.prompt: A 2 TB dataset must be processed nightly but the job on one big server takes 14 hours and barely fits in memory. Explain the scale-out fix and one new problem it introduces.
exercises.hint: Split across nodes for parallelism; what about failures and data movement?
exercises.solution: **Scale out:** split the 2 TB into partitions across a cluster (e.g. 50 nodes) so each processes ~40 GB in parallel — the wall-clock time drops roughly N-fold, and no single machine must hold it all. The **new problem** is distributed-system complexity: machines can fail mid-job (needs fault tolerance / re-computation) and steps that regroup data incur **network shuffles**, which a framework like Spark manages for you but which you must design around (minimize data movement).

exercises.prompt: Classify each as Volume, Velocity, or Variety: (a) 500 TB of historical clickstream, (b) 2 million IoT readings per second, (c) a mix of JSON logs, images, and relational tables.
exercises.solution: (a) **Volume** — sheer amount of stored data. (b) **Velocity** — rate of arrival, demanding fast/streaming ingestion. (c) **Variety** — multiple data shapes/formats together. Real systems usually face several Vs at once, which is what makes them 'big data' problems.

exercises.prompt: Why does distributing data also give fault tolerance, and how does that differ from a single big server?
exercises.hint: What happens when one node of many dies vs the one server dies?
exercises.solution: With data split across many nodes, the framework tracks how each partition was produced, so if one node dies its partitions are **recomputed (or read from a replica) elsewhere** and the job still completes. On a single big server, a failure takes down the **entire** job with no recourse. Distribution turns inevitable hardware failures from fatal into routine, recoverable events.

lessons.title: Spark's big picture: cluster, DataFrames, lazy work & the shuffle
lessons.summary: The mental model that ties everything together: driver + executors + cluster manager, data in partitions, the DataFrame API optimized by Catalyst, lazy evaluation, and the shuffle as the cost center.
lessons.concept:
Four ideas explain almost everything about Spark. Hold them together and the rest of the track clicks.

### 1. The cluster: driver + executors
Your program runs in the **driver**, which plans the work and coordinates. A **cluster manager** (YARN, Kubernetes, Spark standalone) grants **executors** — worker processes across the cluster that actually run tasks and hold data in memory. The driver splits work into **tasks** (one per partition) and sends them to executors.

### 2. Data lives in partitions
A DataFrame is split into **partitions** spread across executors — the unit of parallelism. With 200 partitions and 50 cores, ~50 run at once. Partitioning is why Spark scales, and the shuffle (below) is about *re-partitioning*.

### 3. The DataFrame API, optimized by Catalyst
You write high-level **DataFrame**/Spark SQL code (`select`, `filter`, `join`, `groupBy`). The **Catalyst optimizer** turns it into an efficient physical plan (pushing filters down, pruning columns, choosing join strategies), and **Tungsten** runs it with compact memory and codegen. You declare *what*; Spark decides *how* — like SQL.

### 4. Lazy evaluation + the shuffle
**Transformations are lazy** — `filter`/`select`/`join` just build a plan (a DAG). Nothing runs until an **action** (`count`, `write`, `show`) triggers it. This lets Catalyst optimize the whole graph before executing. The expensive operation is the **shuffle**: wide operations (`groupBy`, `join`, `distinct`, `orderBy`) move data across the network by key. Most Spark tuning is **shuffle reduction**.

Put together: *the driver plans a lazy DAG over partitioned data; Catalyst optimizes it; executors run tasks in parallel; and the shuffle is where time goes.*
lessons.example: `spark.read.parquet('sales').filter("region='US'").groupBy('product').sum('amount').write.parquet('out')` — the read/filter/groupBy build a lazy DAG; `write` (the action) triggers it; Catalyst pushes the filter into the scan and the `groupBy` causes one shuffle. That single shuffle is the main cost.
keypoints: Driver plans & coordinates; a cluster manager grants executors that run tasks and hold data.
Data is split into partitions across executors — the unit of parallelism.
You write DataFrame/SQL; Catalyst optimizes the plan and Tungsten executes it efficiently.
Transformations are lazy (build a DAG); actions trigger execution, enabling whole-plan optimization.
Wide ops (groupBy/join/distinct/orderBy) cause a shuffle — data moves across the network by key.
Most Spark performance work is reducing/​balancing the shuffle.
lessons.diagram_caption: Driver plans a lazy DAG; the cluster manager grants executors that run tasks over partitions.

quiz.q: What runs your program and coordinates the job?
quiz.explain: The driver hosts your program, builds the plan, and schedules tasks. Executors run those tasks; the cluster manager allocates the executors.

quiz.q: Why does Spark's lazy evaluation help performance?
options: It lets Catalyst see and optimize the whole DAG before any execution
quiz.explain: Because nothing runs until an action, the optimizer can rearrange/prune the entire plan (pushdown, pruning, join choice) before executing.

quiz.q: Which operation type is the main cost center to minimize?
options: The shuffle (wide ops: groupBy, join, distinct, orderBy)
quiz.explain: Shuffles move data across the network by key and dominate runtime; reducing and balancing them is the core of Spark tuning.

exercises.prompt: In one sentence each, state the role of the driver, an executor, and the cluster manager.
exercises.hint: Plan/coordinate, run tasks/hold data, allocate resources.
exercises.solution: The **driver** runs your program, builds the optimized plan, and schedules/coordinates tasks. An **executor** is a worker process that runs tasks on partitions and holds data in memory/cache. The **cluster manager** (YARN/K8s/standalone) allocates executors (CPU/memory) to your application.

exercises.prompt: Given `df.filter(...).select(...).groupBy('k').count()` followed by `.show()`, which parts are lazy, what triggers execution, and where's the shuffle?
exercises.solution: `filter`, `select`, and `groupBy().count()` are **lazy transformations** that only build the DAG. **`.show()` is the action** that triggers execution. The **`groupBy`** is a wide transformation, so it causes the **shuffle** (data regrouped by `k` across the network); `filter`/`select` are narrow and pipeline without moving data.

exercises.prompt: Why is the DataFrame API generally preferred over hand-tuned low-level code in Spark?
exercises.solution: DataFrame/SQL code is **declarative**, so the **Catalyst optimizer** can push filters down, prune unused columns, reorder/choose joins, and **Tungsten** executes with compact memory and generated code. You get strong performance without manual tuning, and the same logic stays portable — whereas hand-written low-level (RDD) code bypasses these optimizations.

lessons.title: Transformations vs actions & lazy evaluation
lessons.summary: Why nothing runs until you ask: lazy transformations build a DAG, actions trigger it, the optimizer exploits the whole plan — and each action recomputes unless you cache.
lessons.concept:
Spark splits every operation into two kinds, and the distinction governs *when* work happens.

### Transformations are lazy
**Transformations** (`select`, `filter`, `withColumn`, `join`, `groupBy`, `map`) **don't run** — they return a new DataFrame describing *what* to do, extending a **DAG** (plan). You can chain dozens and Spark still hasn't touched the data.

### Actions trigger execution
An **action** (`count`, `collect`, `show`, `take`, `write`, `foreach`) forces Spark to actually run the DAG and produce a result or side effect. *Only then* does data move.

### Why laziness is powerful
Because Spark sees the **entire plan** before running, the Catalyst optimizer can:

- **push filters down** to the scan (read fewer rows),
- **prune columns** never used,
- **pipeline** consecutive narrow ops into one pass,
- choose the best **join strategy**.

An eager system would execute each step blindly; laziness lets Spark optimize globally.

### Narrow vs wide (recap)
Narrow transformations (`filter`, `select`) need no data movement; wide ones (`groupBy`, `join`, `distinct`, `orderBy`) trigger a **shuffle**. The DAG breaks into **stages** at each shuffle.

### The big gotcha: actions recompute
Each action re-runs the **whole lineage** from the source. If you call two actions on the same derived DataFrame, the expensive chain runs **twice**:

```python
clean = raw.filter(...).join(dim, 'k')
clean.count()       # computes the chain
clean.write...      # computes it AGAIN
```
Fix by **caching** the reused result: `clean.cache()` (then the first action materializes it and later actions reuse it). And never `collect()` a huge DataFrame to the driver — it pulls all rows into one machine's memory and OOMs.
lessons.example: `df.filter("amount>100").select('id','amount')` returns instantly (lazy — nothing ran). Calling `.count()` triggers the job; Catalyst pushes the filter into the Parquet scan and reads only the two columns, so it scans a fraction of the data.
keypoints: Transformations (select/filter/join/groupBy) are lazy — they build a DAG, nothing runs.
Actions (count/collect/show/write) trigger execution of the DAG.
Laziness lets Catalyst optimize the whole plan: pushdown, column pruning, pipelining, join choice.
Narrow transforms pipeline with no shuffle; wide transforms shuffle and split the DAG into stages.
Each action recomputes the full lineage — cache() a DataFrame reused across actions.
Never collect() a large DataFrame to the driver — it OOMs the single driver machine.
lessons.diagram_caption: Transformations stack into a lazy DAG; the action triggers it and the optimizer runs the whole plan.

quiz.q: Which of these triggers actual execution?
quiz.explain: count is an action — it forces the DAG to run. filter/select/withColumn are lazy transformations that only build the plan.

quiz.q: Why does lazy evaluation improve performance?
options: Spark sees the whole plan and can optimize it (pushdown, pruning, pipelining) before running
quiz.explain: Deferring execution until an action lets the optimizer rearrange and prune the entire DAG, rather than executing each step blindly.

quiz.q: You call .count() then .write() on the same filtered+joined DataFrame and it's slow twice. Why, and the fix?
options: Each action recomputes the whole lineage; cache() the DataFrame so it's computed once
quiz.explain: Actions recompute from source each time. Caching the reused DataFrame materializes it once so subsequent actions reuse it.

exercises.prompt: Mark each as transformation (lazy) or action (triggers): select, write, groupBy, show, join, take.
exercises.hint: Does it return a new DataFrame or a result/side effect?
exercises.solution: **Transformations (lazy):** select, groupBy, join — they return a new DataFrame and build the DAG. **Actions (trigger):** write, show, take — they produce a result or side effect and force execution. Chaining transformations does no work until one of the actions runs.

exercises.prompt:
Rewrite this to avoid computing the expensive chain twice:
```python
clean = raw.filter('amount>0').join(dim,'k')
n = clean.count()
clean.write.parquet('out')
```
exercises.hint: Cache the reused DataFrame before the first action.
exercises.solution:
```python
clean = raw.filter('amount>0').join(dim,'k').cache()
n = clean.count()        # computes AND caches
clean.write.parquet('out')  # reuses the cache — no recompute
```
Without `cache()`, both `count()` and `write` re-run the filter+join from source. Caching materializes `clean` once so the second action reuses it. (Unpersist when done to free memory.)

exercises.prompt: Why is `df.collect()` dangerous on a large DataFrame, and what should you do instead to inspect it?
exercises.solution: `collect()` pulls **every row to the driver's memory** (one machine), so on a large DataFrame it OOMs or crashes the driver. To inspect, use `df.show(20)` or `df.take(n)` (a small sample), aggregate first, or `write` the full result to storage. Reserve `collect()` for genuinely small results.

lessons.title: Partitions & the shuffle
lessons.summary: Parallelism's unit and its biggest cost: how partitions drive parallel tasks, what triggers a shuffle, right-sizing partitions, repartition vs coalesce, and skew.
lessons.concept:
### Partitions = the unit of parallelism
A DataFrame is split into **partitions**; Spark runs **one task per partition**, so with 200 partitions and 50 cores ~50 tasks run at once. Too few partitions → idle cores; too many tiny ones → scheduling overhead dominates. Aim for partitions of roughly **128–256 MB**.

### The shuffle — the expensive operation
**Narrow** ops (`filter`, `select`, `withColumn`) act within a partition — no movement. **Wide** ops (`groupBy`, `join`, `distinct`, `orderBy`) need rows with the same key together, so Spark **shuffles**: it redistributes data across the network by key, **writing to disk** on the way. Shuffle = network + disk + serialization, and it **dominates runtime**. The DAG splits into **stages** at each shuffle.

### Reduce the shuffle
- **Filter and select early** so less data is shuffled.
- **Broadcast** a small table in a join to avoid shuffling the big one (next lesson).
- Avoid needless re-grouping and repeated `distinct`/`orderBy`.

### Right-size partitions
- `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` (default 200) sets the post-shuffle partition count — tune it to your data size (too high → tiny partitions; too low → huge ones).
- `repartition(n)` does a **full shuffle** to increase/rebalance partitions; `coalesce(n)` **reduces** partitions **without** a full shuffle (great before writing fewer output files).

### Skew — the silent killer
A shuffle sends all rows for a key to one task. If one key is huge (a 'null' or a mega-customer), that **one task** does most of the work while others finish — 199 tasks done in seconds, 1 runs for an hour. Fixes: **salting** the hot key, or **Adaptive Query Execution** (AQE) which detects and splits skewed partitions automatically.

Diagnose with the **Spark UI**: a stage with one long-running task = skew; lots of shuffle spill = undersized memory or oversized partitions.
lessons.example: `big.join(other, 'customer_id')` shuffles both sides by `customer_id`. If one customer has 40% of the rows, that key's task runs far longer than the rest — classic skew. Salting (append a random suffix to the key, aggregate, then combine) spreads it across tasks.
keypoints: Partitions are the unit of parallelism — one task per partition; aim ~128–256 MB each.
Narrow ops stay in-partition; wide ops (groupBy/join/distinct/orderBy) trigger a shuffle (network+disk).
The shuffle dominates runtime and splits the DAG into stages — reduce it by filtering early & broadcasting.
Tune spark.sql.shuffle.partitions to data size; repartition() full-shuffles, coalesce() reduces without one.
Skew: one hot key overloads a single task — fix with salting or AQE skew handling.
Diagnose with the Spark UI: one long task = skew; heavy spill = memory/partition sizing.
lessons.diagram_caption: Narrow ops stay within a partition; a wide op shuffles data across the network by key.

quiz.q: What triggers a shuffle?
quiz.explain: Wide operations need same-key rows together, so they redistribute data across the network — the shuffle. Narrow ops (filter/select) don't move data.

quiz.q: What's the difference between repartition(n) and coalesce(n)?
options: repartition does a full shuffle to change/rebalance partitions; coalesce reduces partitions without a full shuffle
quiz.explain: repartition(n) fully shuffles (can increase or rebalance); coalesce(n) merges partitions to reduce them cheaply, e.g. before writing fewer files.

quiz.q: 199 tasks finish in seconds but 1 runs for an hour. What's wrong?
options: Data skew — one key has most of the rows, overloading its task
quiz.explain: Skew: a shuffle sends all rows for a key to one task, so a giant key makes one task do most of the work. Fix with salting or AQE skew splitting.

exercises.prompt: You're about to write a DataFrame that currently has 2,000 partitions but should be ~10 output files. Which operation, and why not the other?
exercises.hint: Reduce partitions cheaply vs full shuffle.
exercises.solution: Use **`coalesce(10)`**: it merges existing partitions down to 10 **without a full shuffle**, cheaply producing ~10 output files. `repartition(10)` would also give 10 files but triggers a **full shuffle** (moving all data across the network) — unnecessary cost when you're only reducing partitions. (Use repartition when you need to *increase* partitions or *rebalance* skewed ones.)

exercises.prompt: A join on customer_id is slow with one task running far longer than the rest. Diagnose and give two fixes.
exercises.solution: It's **data skew**: one `customer_id` holds a huge share of rows, so its shuffle task does most of the work. Fixes: (1) **Salting** — append a random suffix to the hot key on both sides, aggregate the partials, then combine, spreading the key across tasks; (2) enable **AQE skew handling** (`spark.sql.adaptive.enabled` + skewJoin), which detects oversized partitions and splits them automatically. Filtering/broadcasting can also help if one side is small.

exercises.prompt: Explain why filtering before a groupBy is faster than after, in terms of the shuffle.
exercises.solution: `groupBy` triggers a **shuffle** that moves data across the network. Filtering **before** it means fewer rows are shuffled, so less network/disk I/O and smaller post-shuffle work. Filtering **after** would shuffle all the rows first and discard many afterward — wasted movement. Spark's optimizer pushes filters down for this reason; structuring code the same way reinforces it.

lessons.title: DataFrames & Spark SQL
lessons.summary: The high-level API you'll actually use: typed columns and functions, groupBy/agg, joins, the DataFrame↔SQL equivalence, and how Catalyst makes both fast.
lessons.concept:
A **DataFrame** is a distributed table with a **schema** (named, typed columns). It's the API you'll use 99% of the time, and it comes in two interchangeable flavors.

### Two equivalent APIs
```python
# DataFrame API
df.filter(F.col('amount') > 100) \
  .groupBy('product').agg(F.sum('amount').alias('rev'))

# Spark SQL (same result)
df.createOrReplaceTempView('sales')
spark.sql("SELECT product, SUM(amount) AS rev FROM sales WHERE amount>100 GROUP BY product")
```
Both compile to the **same Catalyst plan** — choose by readability. SQL is great for set logic; the DataFrame API composes well in code.

### Columns & functions
Reference columns with `F.col('x')` or `df['x']`; build expressions with the `pyspark.sql.functions` library (`F.when`, `F.coalesce`, `F.to_date`, `F.regexp_replace`, …). Prefer these **built-ins** over Python UDFs — they're optimized and run in the JVM (a UDF serializes every row to Python and blocks optimization).

### Core operations
`select`, `filter/where`, `withColumn` (add/replace a column), `groupBy().agg()`, `join`, `orderBy`, `dropDuplicates`, and window functions (`Window.partitionBy(...).orderBy(...)`). They mirror SQL closely.

### Why DataFrames are fast
Because they carry a **schema**, the **Catalyst optimizer** can push filters into the scan, prune unused columns, reorder joins, and **Tungsten** runs the plan with compact off-heap memory and generated code. You write *what*; Spark figures out an efficient *how* — the same declarative win as SQL.

### Reading & writing
`spark.read.parquet(...)` / `.csv(...)` / `.json(...)`; `df.write.mode('overwrite').partitionBy('date').parquet(...)`. Prefer **columnar formats (Parquet)** so the optimizer reads only needed columns. Writing **partitioned by date** enables partition pruning on later reads.
lessons.example: `spark.read.parquet('events').where("country='US'").groupBy('page').count().orderBy(F.desc('count'))` reads only the needed columns (Parquet + pruning), pushes the country filter into the scan, and shuffles once for the groupBy — concise DataFrame code that Catalyst runs efficiently.
keypoints: A DataFrame is a distributed, schema'd table — the default API.
The DataFrame API and Spark SQL are equivalent (same Catalyst plan) — pick by readability.
Use F.* built-in functions over Python UDFs (built-ins are optimized and run in the JVM).
Core ops mirror SQL: select, filter, withColumn, groupBy/agg, join, window functions.
Schema + Catalyst + Tungsten = pushdown, pruning, codegen → fast without manual tuning.
Read/write columnar Parquet and partition by date for pruning on later reads.
lessons.diagram_caption: DataFrames add a schema over RDDs so Catalyst can optimize — the API you actually write.

quiz.q: How do the DataFrame API and Spark SQL relate?
options: They're equivalent — both compile to the same Catalyst plan; choose by readability
quiz.explain: They're two front-ends to the same optimizer/engine, producing identical plans. Mix and match by what reads clearest.

quiz.q: Why prefer F.* built-in functions over a Python UDF?
options: Built-ins are optimized and run in the JVM; a Python UDF serializes every row to Python and blocks optimization
quiz.explain: Python UDFs round-trip each row between the JVM and a Python process and are opaque to Catalyst, often 10×+ slower than equivalent built-ins.

quiz.q: Why read Parquet and partition output by date?
options: Columnar Parquet lets the optimizer read only needed columns; date partitioning enables pruning on later reads
quiz.explain: Parquet's columnar layout supports column pruning and predicate pushdown; partitioning by date means later queries scan only relevant partitions.

exercises.prompt: Write the same query two ways — DataFrame API and Spark SQL — for: total `amount` per `product` where `amount > 100`.
exercises.hint: groupBy().agg(F.sum) vs a temp view + SELECT … GROUP BY.
exercises.solution:
```python
# DataFrame API
(df.filter(F.col('amount') > 100)
   .groupBy('product')
   .agg(F.sum('amount').alias('rev')))

# Spark SQL
df.createOrReplaceTempView('sales')
spark.sql('''SELECT product, SUM(amount) AS rev
             FROM sales WHERE amount > 100
             GROUP BY product''')
```
Both produce the same Catalyst plan and result — the choice is purely readability.

exercises.prompt:
Rewrite this slow UDF using a built-in function:
```python
upper = F.udf(lambda s: s.upper())
df.withColumn('u', upper('name'))
```
exercises.solution:
```python
df.withColumn('u', F.upper('name'))
```
`F.upper` is a built-in that runs in the JVM and is visible to Catalyst, so it's far faster than a Python UDF that serializes every row to a Python process. Always check `pyspark.sql.functions` before writing a UDF.

exercises.prompt: You read a 50-column Parquet table but only need 3 columns and one country. What two optimizations does Catalyst apply, and how do you help it?
exercises.solution: Catalyst applies **column pruning** (reads only the 3 selected columns from the columnar Parquet) and **predicate pushdown** (pushes `country='US'` into the scan so non-matching row groups are skipped). You help it by **selecting only the needed columns** and **filtering early** (and by storing data partitioned/clustered so the filter prunes files) — so the job scans a fraction of the data.

lessons.title: Why Spark beat MapReduce
lessons.summary: The leap from Hadoop MapReduce to Spark: in-memory processing vs disk-between-steps, a richer DAG of operations, and one unified engine for batch, SQL, streaming and ML.
lessons.concept:
**Hadoop MapReduce** (2000s) made big data processing mainstream: split work into a **map** step (transform each record) and a **reduce** step (aggregate by key), run across a cluster with automatic fault tolerance. It worked, but it was slow and clunky for real workloads.

### MapReduce's core weakness: disk between every step
MapReduce **writes intermediate results to disk** (HDFS) after every map and reduce. A multi-step job (filter → join → aggregate) becomes a chain of MR jobs, each reading from and writing to disk. For **iterative** work (ML, graph algorithms) that re-reads data many times, this disk I/O dominates — jobs crawl.

### Spark's leap: keep data in memory
Spark keeps working data **in memory** across steps (spilling to disk only when needed). For multi-step and iterative jobs this is **10–100× faster**, because it skips the constant disk round-trips. Lineage (the recipe to rebuild a partition) preserves fault tolerance **without** writing every intermediate result.

### A richer model than map+reduce
Spark expresses a whole **DAG** of operations (`filter`, `join`, `groupBy`, `window`, …), not just two rigid phases, and its scheduler optimizes the entire graph (pipelining, predicate pushdown). You write high-level DataFrame/SQL code instead of verbose Java map/reduce classes.

### One unified engine
Spark handles **batch, SQL, streaming, and ML** in a single framework with one API — instead of stitching together separate tools. That unification, plus the speed, is why Spark became the default big-data engine.

MapReduce concepts still matter (Spark's wide operations are essentially map+shuffle+reduce under the hood, and HDFS/YARN often host Spark), but you rarely write raw MapReduce today.
lessons.example: A logistic-regression training loop reads the dataset 20 times. In MapReduce each pass re-reads from disk → painfully slow. In Spark you `cache()` the data in memory once and the 20 iterations reuse it — often two orders of magnitude faster.
keypoints: MapReduce = map (per-record) + reduce (per-key) with automatic fault tolerance — it made big data mainstream.
Its weakness: writes intermediate results to disk after every step, killing multi-step/iterative speed.
Spark keeps data in memory across steps → 10–100× faster for iterative/multi-step jobs.
Lineage preserves fault tolerance without persisting every intermediate result.
Spark runs a whole optimized DAG (not just two phases) with high-level DataFrame/SQL APIs.
Spark is one unified engine for batch, SQL, streaming, and ML — MapReduce concepts still underlie it.
lessons.diagram_caption: MapReduce round-trips to disk between steps; Spark keeps data in memory across the DAG.

quiz.q: What is MapReduce's main performance weakness?
options: It writes intermediate results to disk after every step, slowing multi-step/iterative jobs
It only runs on one machine
quiz.explain: MapReduce persists intermediate data to disk between steps; iterative jobs that re-read data pay massive disk I/O, which Spark avoids by staying in memory.

quiz.q: How does Spark keep fault tolerance without writing every intermediate to disk?
options: Lineage — it records how to recompute a lost partition from its inputs
quiz.explain: Spark tracks each partition's lineage (the transformations that built it), so a lost partition is recomputed from its source rather than restored from a persisted copy.

quiz.q: Why is Spark especially faster than MapReduce for ML/iterative workloads?
options: Iterations reuse in-memory cached data instead of re-reading from disk each pass
quiz.explain: Iterative algorithms re-read the dataset many times; Spark caches it in memory so each pass is fast, while MapReduce re-reads from disk every iteration.

exercises.prompt: A pipeline is filter → join → group → filter. Explain why this is slow as MapReduce and faster in Spark.
exercises.hint: Count the disk round-trips vs in-memory pipelining.
exercises.solution: As MapReduce it becomes a **chain of jobs**, each writing its output to disk (HDFS) and the next reading it back — several full disk round-trips. Spark builds one **DAG** and keeps intermediates **in memory**, pipelining the narrow steps and only shuffling where required (the join/group). Far less disk I/O, and the optimizer can prune columns and push the filters down — so it runs much faster.

exercises.prompt: Name two things Spark unifies that previously needed separate tools, and why that matters.
exercises.hint: Batch, SQL, streaming, ML in one engine.
exercises.solution: Spark handles, e.g., **batch ETL and SQL** and **streaming and ML** within one engine and API (Structured Streaming reuses the DataFrame API; MLlib runs on the same data). It matters because you avoid stitching together and operating multiple systems, share code and skills across workloads, and keep one fault-tolerance/optimization model instead of several.

exercises.prompt: True or false: you should rewrite all your jobs in raw MapReduce for reliability. Justify.
exercises.hint: Does Spark sacrifice fault tolerance for speed?
exercises.solution: **False.** Spark provides the same automatic fault tolerance (via lineage / recomputation) **and** is far faster and higher-level. There's no reliability reason to drop to raw MapReduce, which is verbose and disk-bound. MapReduce remains useful as a **mental model** (map → shuffle → reduce underlies Spark's wide ops), but Spark (or similar engines) is the practical choice.

lessons.title: Join strategies — broadcast, sort-merge & shuffle-hash
lessons.summary: How Spark physically joins tables and how to pick the fast path: broadcast hash join for small×large, sort-merge for large×large, and handling join skew.
lessons.concept:
A `join` is logically simple but **physically** the costliest thing Spark does, because matching keys must end up on the same executor. Spark chooses among a few strategies.

### Broadcast hash join — the fast path for small × large
If one side is **small**, Spark **broadcasts** a copy of it to every executor, which then joins its local partitions of the big side — **no shuffle of the big table**. Spark auto-broadcasts a side under `spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold` (~10 MB default); force it with `broadcast(small_df)`.

```python
from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast
big.join(broadcast(small_dim), 'key')
```
Small dimension joined to a huge fact → almost always broadcast.

### Sort-merge join — the default for large × large
When both sides are big, Spark **shuffles both** by the join key, **sorts** each partition, and **merges** them. It's robust for huge inputs but pays two shuffles + sorts — the main cost in big joins.

### Shuffle-hash join
Shuffles both sides by key, then builds an in-memory **hash** of the smaller side per partition (no sort). Used in some cases where one side fits in memory after shuffling; less common than the other two.

### Picking (and AQE picking for you)
Rule of thumb: **small × large → broadcast**; **large × large → sort-merge**. **AQE** (Adaptive Query Execution) can **switch a planned sort-merge to a broadcast at runtime** if it discovers a side is actually small, and can split skewed partitions.

### Join skew & the grain trap
A hot key (one customer with 40% of rows) overloads one task — **salt** it or rely on AQE skew handling. And mind the **grain**: joining a non-unique key **multiplies rows** (fan-out), so a later `sum` double-counts — aggregate to the right grain first.
lessons.example: Joining a 2-billion-row `events` fact to a 5,000-row `country_dim`: wrap the dimension in `broadcast(country_dim)` so it's copied to every executor and the giant fact is **never shuffled** — often the single biggest speedup in a Spark job.
keypoints: Joins are costly because matching keys must co-locate on an executor.
Broadcast hash join: ship a small side to all executors, no shuffle of the big side — small×large.
