quiz.explain: Static access keys are a major breach vector; assign roles to compute and never hard-code keys.

quiz.q: Why is data egress a 'hidden' cloud cost?
options: Providers charge for data leaving a region/cloud (ingress is usually free), so cross-region/internet transfer adds up
quiz.explain: Minimize it by colocating compute and storage and using private endpoints.

exercises.prompt: Do the 10-question Cloud mock interview (Go-deeper guide) out loud, no notes.
exercises.hint: object storage, storage/compute separation, compute spectrum, warehouses, FinOps, egress, IAM, encryption, three-cloud map, serverless.
exercises.solution: Self-assess: any shaky answer → reread that lesson's concept/diagram and its interview panel, then retry until crisp.

tracks.summary: Build data systems on the cloud: object storage, warehouses, compute options, IAM/security, networking & egress, cost/FinOps, serverless pipelines, and the AWS/GCP/Azure data service map — the platform every modern data engineer runs on.

tracks.title: Snowflake — complete course
tracks.blurb: Become great at Snowflake for data engineering: architecture, warehouses & cost, loading & continuous ingestion, performance, security & governance, and the modern stack (Snowpark, Iceberg, Dynamic Tables, Cortex).

lessons.title: Snowflake architecture — the three layers
lessons.summary: Snowflake's defining design — fully decoupled storage and compute across three layers (cloud services, virtual warehouses, centralized storage) — and the micro-partitions that make queries fast with no indexes to manage.
lessons.concept:
Snowflake is a **cloud data platform** whose whole design flows from one idea: **decouple storage from compute**. It runs as a managed service (you never see a server) across **three layers**.

### 1. Cloud Services — the brain
A stateless, multi-tenant layer that coordinates everything: the **query optimizer**, the **metadata/catalog** (table definitions, micro-partition statistics), **security & access control**, **transaction management**, and infrastructure. You issue SQL; this layer plans and governs it.

### 2. Compute — Virtual Warehouses
A **virtual warehouse** is an independent **MPP compute cluster**. The key property: **many warehouses run at once, fully isolated**. Your heavy ETL warehouse can't slow the BI warehouse — they share data but not compute. Each warehouse can be **sized, scaled, and suspended independently**.

### 3. Storage — one centralized copy
Data lives **once** in cloud object storage (S3/GCS/Azure Blob), as **micro-partitions**: **immutable**, ~50–500 MB, **compressed columnar** files. Each micro-partition keeps **min/max statistics per column**, so the optimizer **prunes** — it skips partitions that can't match a filter and reads only what's needed. There are **no indexes or partitions to design**; pruning is automatic.

### Why decoupling matters
- **Scale compute** up or down (or to zero) **without moving data**.
- **Many workloads** (ETL, BI, data science) share **one copy** of the data — no copies to sync.
- **Pay separately** for storage (cheap, per-TB) and compute (per-second).

### Editions
**Standard** → **Enterprise** (more time-travel, multi-cluster, materialized views) → **Business Critical** (stronger security/compliance). Higher editions cost more per credit.
lessons.example: A company runs three warehouses on the same tables: `WH_ETL` (Large, loads data at night), `WH_BI` (Medium, multi-cluster, serves 200 analysts by day), `WH_DS` (XL, ad-hoc model training). They never contend for compute — Snowflake gives each its own cluster — yet all read the exact same micro-partitions. Scaling `WH_DS` to 2XL for a big job doesn't touch the others, and suspending `WH_ETL` after the nightly load costs nothing.
keypoints: Snowflake's core design is fully decoupled storage and compute, delivered as a managed service (no servers).
Three layers: cloud services (optimizer/metadata/security/transactions), virtual warehouses (compute), centralized storage.
Virtual warehouses are independent MPP clusters — many run isolated at once, sized/suspended separately.
Storage is one copy as micro-partitions: immutable, ~50–500MB, compressed columnar, with per-column min/max stats.
Pruning uses those stats to skip partitions that can't match — automatic; there are no indexes/partitions to design.
Decoupling lets compute scale without moving data, many workloads share one copy, and you pay for storage/compute separately.
lessons.diagram_caption: Three decoupled layers: cloud services (the brain) coordinates queries; independent virtual warehouses provide isolated compute; one centralized micro-partition storage layer is shared by all.

quiz.q: What is the defining architectural idea of Snowflake?
options: Storing data as rows for fast writes
Decoupling storage from compute, so each scales independently and many warehouses share one data copy
Running on a single big server
quiz.explain: Separating storage and compute lets you scale compute without moving data and run many isolated warehouses against one shared storage layer.

quiz.q: What is a micro-partition?
options: An immutable ~50–500MB compressed columnar file with per-column min/max stats, used for pruning
A type of index you create
quiz.explain: Snowflake stores data as immutable columnar micro-partitions whose min/max stats let the optimizer skip (prune) partitions that can't match a query.

quiz.q: Why don't a heavy ETL job and the BI dashboards slow each other down?
options: They run on separate, isolated virtual warehouses while sharing the same storage
ETL runs on a different database copy
quiz.explain: Each workload gets its own virtual warehouse (isolated compute); they read the same micro-partitions but never contend for CPU/memory.

exercises.prompt: Your nightly ETL and your daytime BI keep contending for resources on one warehouse. Using Snowflake's architecture, how do you fix it without copying data?
exercises.solution: Create **separate virtual warehouses** — e.g. `WH_ETL` and `WH_BI` — pointed at the **same databases/tables**. Because storage and compute are **decoupled**, both warehouses read the **same micro-partitions** (no data copied or synced), but they run on **isolated compute**, so the nightly ETL can run Large/2XL without stealing resources from the BI users, and vice-versa. Size each for its workload (ETL bigger for throughput; BI multi-cluster for concurrency), set **auto-suspend** so the idle one costs nothing, and you've removed contention with **zero data duplication** — exactly what the decoupled architecture is for.

exercises.prompt: Explain how Snowflake answers `WHERE order_date = '2025-03-01'` quickly with no index defined.
exercises.solution: Each **micro-partition** stores **min/max statistics** for every column, including `order_date`. When the optimizer plans the query, it checks each partition's `order_date` min/max range and **prunes** (skips) every partition whose range can't contain `'2025-03-01'` — without reading them. It then scans only the handful of partitions that might match, and because storage is **columnar**, it reads only the `order_date` (and selected) columns within those, not whole rows. So the query touches a tiny fraction of the data with **no index to create or maintain** — pruning + columnar storage is automatic. (For very large tables you can define a **clustering key** so related rows co-locate in partitions, making pruning even tighter.)

exercises.prompt: What does each of the three layers do, and what does putting metadata/optimization in a separate 'cloud services' layer enable?
exercises.solution: **Cloud services** is the brain: it parses/optimizes queries, holds **metadata** (table defs, micro-partition stats), enforces **security**, and manages **transactions** — statelessly and multi-tenant. **Virtual warehouses** are the compute: isolated MPP clusters that execute the query plans. **Storage** holds one columnar micro-partition copy of the data. Keeping metadata/optimization in a **shared services layer** (not inside each warehouse) is what lets **any** warehouse instantly use the **same statistics and catalog** — so a brand-new warehouse prunes effectively from query one, time-travel/cloning work via metadata pointers, and the system can manage transactions and access **consistently across all compute**. It also means **scaling or suspending compute never loses the catalog/stats**, because those live in services, not in the warehouses.

lessons.title: Virtual warehouses — sizing, scaling & cost
lessons.summary: How to size warehouses (T-shirt sizes and credits), the crucial difference between scaling UP and OUT, auto-suspend/resume, per-second billing, and the levers that control a Snowflake bill.
lessons.concept:
Compute is **~80% of a Snowflake bill**, so understanding warehouses is understanding cost.

### Sizing — T-shirt sizes
A warehouse comes in sizes **XS, S, M, L, XL, 2XL … 6XL**. Each step **doubles** the compute (and the **credits per hour**): XS = **1**, S = 2, M = 4, L = 8, XL = 16, … up to 6XL = **512**. A bigger size = more CPU/memory = a single query runs **faster** (and big queries that spill to disk on a small warehouse may run *much* faster, not just 2×).

### Scale UP vs scale OUT — the key distinction
- **Scale UP** (a bigger size) → makes **one query faster** / handles more data per query. Use when individual queries are slow.
- **Scale OUT** (a **multi-cluster warehouse**) → adds **identical clusters automatically** when concurrency rises, so **many users don't queue**. Use when *lots of queries at once* is the problem, not query size. (Multi-cluster is Enterprise+.)
Sizing the warehouse up does **not** fix concurrency; adding clusters does. Adding clusters does **not** make a single query faster; size does.

### Auto-suspend & auto-resume
Set **auto-suspend** (e.g. 60s of idle) and the warehouse **stops billing** when no queries run; the next query **auto-resumes** it in ~1s. This is the single biggest cost lever — idle warehouses should cost **nothing**.

### Billing
**Per-second, 1-minute minimum.** Cost = **size (credits/hr) × time running**. A credit costs ~$2 (Standard) / ~$3 (Enterprise) / ~$4 (Business Critical) on AWS US. **Serverless** features (Snowpipe, automatic clustering, serverless tasks) bill separately with their own multipliers.

### Cost control levers
1. **Right-size** — start small; only scale up if queries are genuinely slow.
2. **Auto-suspend aggressively** (30–60s).
3. **One warehouse per workload** so you can size and monitor each.
4. **Resource monitors** — cap credits per warehouse/account and alert or suspend at a threshold.
5. **Scale out only for concurrency**, and set sensible min/max clusters.
lessons.example: A BI team has 150 analysts hitting dashboards 9–5. They use a **Medium multi-cluster** warehouse (min 1, max 4 clusters): off-peak it runs 1 cluster, at peak it auto-adds up to 4 so nobody queues, then scales back. Auto-suspend is 60s. A separate **Large** `WH_ETL` runs the nightly load then suspends. A **resource monitor** caps the account at 500 credits/day and emails at 80%. Right-sizing + suspend + per-workload warehouses keeps the bill predictable.
keypoints: Compute is ~80% of the bill; warehouse sizing/usage is the main cost lever.
T-shirt sizes XS→6XL each double compute and credits/hr (XS=1, S=2, M=4 … 6XL=512).
Scale UP (bigger size) makes a single query faster; scale OUT (multi-cluster) adds clusters so concurrent users don't queue.
Sizing up does NOT fix concurrency; multi-cluster does NOT speed a single query — match the lever to the problem.
Auto-suspend/auto-resume means idle warehouses cost nothing — the biggest cost win. Billing is per-second, 1-min minimum.
Control cost: right-size, suspend aggressively, one warehouse per workload, resource monitors to cap/alert on credits.
lessons.diagram_caption: Scale UP (a bigger warehouse) makes one query faster; scale OUT (a multi-cluster warehouse) adds clusters so many concurrent users don't queue — and auto-suspend makes idle warehouses free.

quiz.q: 200 analysts run dashboards at 9am and queries start queuing. What do you change?
options: Scale the warehouse UP to a bigger size
Use a multi-cluster warehouse (scale OUT) so clusters auto-add for concurrency
quiz.explain: Queuing is a concurrency problem — scale OUT with multi-cluster to add clusters. A bigger single warehouse speeds one query but doesn't add concurrency.

quiz.q: How does auto-suspend save money?
options: It stops the warehouse (and its billing) when idle; the next query auto-resumes it in ~1s
quiz.explain: An idle warehouse with auto-suspend stops consuming credits; a new query resumes it almost instantly — so you pay only while queries actually run.

quiz.q: A single huge query is slow and spills to disk. Best fix?
options: Scale the warehouse UP to a bigger size (more memory/CPU)
quiz.explain: One slow/spilling query is a per-query resource problem — scale UP gives it more memory/CPU. Multi-cluster only helps when many queries run at once.

exercises.prompt: Design the warehouse setup for: a nightly ETL load, 150 daytime BI users, and occasional ad-hoc data-science jobs. Give size, scaling mode, and suspend for each.
exercises.hint: One warehouse per workload; up vs out.
exercises.solution: **WH_ETL** — a **Large** (or XL) single-cluster warehouse: ETL is throughput-heavy, so **scale up** for speed; **auto-suspend 60s** so it costs nothing after the nightly run. **WH_BI** — a **Medium multi-cluster** warehouse (min 1, max ~4): 150 concurrent users is a **concurrency** problem, so **scale out** — clusters auto-add at peak and scale back off-peak; auto-suspend 60s. **WH_DS** — an **XL** single-cluster warehouse that data scientists **resume on demand**; ad-hoc jobs are big but infrequent, so size up when used and suspend aggressively (e.g. 60s) so it's free when idle. Three **separate** warehouses on the **same data** means each is sized/monitored independently with no contention, and a **resource monitor** caps total daily credits. This matches each lever (up for query speed, out for concurrency, suspend for idle) to its workload.

exercises.prompt: Your bill doubled. List the first four things you'd check, given compute is ~80% of cost.
exercises.solution: (1) **Auto-suspend settings** — is a warehouse left running idle (no/long suspend)? Idle compute is the most common waste; set suspend to 30–60s. (2) **Warehouse sizing** — did someone scale a warehouse **up** (each size doubles credits/hr) or is a warehouse oversized for its queries? Right-size down. (3) **Multi-cluster max** — is a multi-cluster warehouse fanning out to many clusters (or min-clusters set too high), multiplying cost? Cap max and check min. (4) **Serverless/feature usage** — Snowpipe, automatic clustering, serverless tasks, materialized-view maintenance, or **Cortex/AI** functions bill separately with multipliers; a new pipeline or AI workload can spike cost. Use the **ACCOUNT_USAGE** views (warehouse_metering_history, query_history) to attribute credits by warehouse/query, then fix the biggest contributor first. A **resource monitor** prevents the next surprise.

exercises.prompt: Explain why 'just make the warehouse bigger' is the wrong reflex for a slow dashboard used by many people at peak.
exercises.solution: A bigger warehouse (**scale up**) gives **each query** more CPU/memory, so it helps when a **single query** is slow or spilling. But 'slow dashboards at peak for many people' is usually a **concurrency** problem: dozens of queries arrive at once and **queue** behind the warehouse's limited slots — each query might be fast, but they wait for a turn. Scaling up doesn't add **slots/throughput for concurrency**; it just makes each (already-fast) query a bit faster while they still queue, **and doubles your credit rate**. The right lever is **scale out** — a **multi-cluster** warehouse adds **identical clusters** automatically when concurrency rises, so queries run in parallel across clusters instead of queuing, then scales back when the rush passes. Diagnose first (is the *query* slow, or are queries *waiting*?) via query_history's queued time; match up vs out to the actual cause.

lessons.title: Loading & continuous ingestion
lessons.summary: Every way to get data into Snowflake by latency — COPY INTO from stages, Snowpipe auto-ingest, Snowpipe Streaming rows, and Streams + Tasks and Dynamic Tables for CDC and declarative pipelines.
lessons.concept:
Snowflake offers a **spectrum of ingestion** from batch files to real-time rows. Pick by **latency** and **source**.

### Stages — where files live first
A **stage** is a location Snowflake loads from: **internal** (Snowflake-managed) or **external** (your S3/GCS/Azure bucket). You stage files, then load them.

### COPY INTO — bulk batch (the workhorse)
```sql
copy into raw.orders
from @my_stage/orders/
file_format = (type = parquet)
match_by_column_name = case_insensitive;
```
Loads files from a stage into a table. You run it manually or on a schedule (a **Task**). Best for periodic batch loads.

### Snowpipe — auto-ingest (micro-batch, serverless)
Snowpipe runs `COPY` **automatically as files arrive** in a stage (triggered by cloud storage events). Serverless (no warehouse), billed per file processed. Good for files that trickle in continuously, with **minute-ish** latency.

### Snowpipe Streaming — real-time rows (no files)
Applications write **rows directly** into Snowflake tables via the streaming API — **no staging files** — and rows are queryable in **seconds** (up to ~10 GB/s per table). For IoT telemetry, CDC streams, fraud, live dashboards.

### Streams & Tasks — CDC + scheduled SQL
- A **Stream** is a **change-tracking** object on a table: it records the **inserts/updates/deletes** since you last consumed it (CDC).
- A **Task** runs **SQL on a schedule** (or in a dependency tree).
Consume a stream inside a task to build **incremental** pipelines: `insert into target select ... from my_stream`.

### Dynamic Tables — declarative pipelines (the modern way)
Instead of hand-wiring Streams + Tasks, declare a target table as a **SELECT plus a target freshness (lag)**:
```sql
create dynamic table daily_sales
  target_lag = '1 hour' warehouse = wh_etl as
  select date, sum(amount) from orders group by 1;
```
Snowflake **refreshes it incrementally** to meet the lag — no manual orchestration. Dynamic Tables work with **Iceberg** too, and pair with **Snowpipe Streaming** for continuous transform.
lessons.example: A retailer's pipeline: order files land in S3 → **Snowpipe** auto-loads them into `raw.orders` within a minute. Clickstream events stream in via **Snowpipe Streaming** into `raw.events` (queryable in seconds). Then a **Dynamic Table** `mart.daily_sales` (target_lag = 1 hour) keeps a rolled-up sales table fresh automatically — no Airflow, no manual refresh. For a complex CDC merge, a **Stream** on `raw.orders` feeds a **Task** that MERGEs changes into a curated table every 15 minutes.
keypoints: A stage (internal or external S3/GCS/Azure) is where files live before loading.
COPY INTO = bulk batch load from a stage; run manually or via a Task — the workhorse for periodic loads.
Snowpipe = serverless auto-ingest that runs COPY as files arrive (micro-batch, ~minute latency).
Snowpipe Streaming = write rows directly (no files), queryable in seconds, up to ~10GB/s per table — for real-time.
Streams = change-tracking (CDC) on a table; Tasks = scheduled SQL; combine them for incremental pipelines.
Dynamic Tables = declarative: a SELECT + target_lag auto-refreshed incrementally — the modern alternative to hand-wired Streams/Tasks.
lessons.diagram_caption: Ingestion by latency: COPY INTO (batch from a stage) → Snowpipe (files as they arrive) → Snowpipe Streaming (rows in seconds); Streams+Tasks and Dynamic Tables transform continuously.

quiz.q: You need files loaded automatically within ~a minute as they land in S3, with no warehouse to manage. What do you use?
options: Manual COPY INTO once a day
quiz.explain: Snowpipe runs COPY automatically on cloud-storage events, serverless, with minute-ish latency — exactly the 'files as they arrive' case.

quiz.q: What is a Stream in Snowflake?
options: A change-tracking (CDC) object that records the inserts/updates/deletes on a table since last consumed
quiz.explain: A Stream captures row-level changes to a table (CDC); you consume it (often inside a Task) to process only what changed.

quiz.q: What do Dynamic Tables give you over manually wiring Streams + Tasks?
options: A declarative pipeline: define a SELECT + a target freshness and Snowflake refreshes it incrementally for you
quiz.explain: Dynamic Tables are declarative — you state the query and the target lag; Snowflake handles incremental refresh and dependencies, no manual orchestration.

exercises.prompt: Map each to the right Snowflake ingestion method: (a) nightly Parquet dumps in S3, (b) IoT sensor rows needing second-level freshness, (c) files trickling in all day, (d) keeping a rollup table fresh hourly with no orchestrator.
exercises.hint: COPY, Snowpipe Streaming, Snowpipe, Dynamic Table.
exercises.solution: (a) **Nightly Parquet dumps** → **COPY INTO** from the external stage, scheduled by a **Task** (or run by your orchestrator) — classic batch. (b) **IoT rows, second-level freshness** → **Snowpipe Streaming** — write rows directly via the streaming API, no files, queryable in seconds. (c) **Files trickling in all day** → **Snowpipe (auto-ingest)** — serverless COPY triggered by storage events, minute-ish latency, no warehouse to babysit. (d) **Hourly rollup, no orchestrator** → a **Dynamic Table** with `target_lag = '1 hour'` — declare the SELECT and Snowflake keeps it incrementally fresh automatically. Each choice matches the **latency and source shape**: batch files (COPY), continuous files (Snowpipe), continuous rows (Snowpipe Streaming), declarative transform (Dynamic Table).

exercises.prompt: Write a Stream + Task pattern that MERGEs only changed orders from raw.orders into a curated table every 15 minutes.
exercises.hint: create stream; create task ... schedule; consume the stream in a MERGE.
exercises.solution:
Create a **stream** to capture changes, then a **task** that consumes it on a schedule:
```
create stream orders_stream on table raw.orders;

create task merge_orders
  warehouse = wh_etl
  schedule = '15 minute'
  when system$stream_has_data('orders_stream')
as
  merge into curated.orders t
  using orders_stream s on t.order_id = s.order_id
  when matched and s.metadata$action = 'DELETE' then delete
  when matched then update set t.status = s.status, t.amount = s.amount
  when not matched then insert (order_id, status, amount) values (s.order_id, s.status, s.amount);
```
The **stream** tracks inserts/updates/deletes since last read (via `metadata$action`); the **task** runs every 15 min **only when the stream has data** (`when system$stream_has_data` avoids empty runs), and the **MERGE** applies just the changes — incremental, not a full reload. (For many such pipelines, a **Dynamic Table** can replace this hand-wiring entirely.)

exercises.prompt: When would you prefer Snowpipe Streaming over Snowpipe, and what's the architectural difference?
exercises.hint: Rows vs files; seconds vs minutes.
exercises.solution: Prefer **Snowpipe Streaming** when you need **real-time, row-level** ingestion — second-level freshness for IoT telemetry, CDC feeds, fraud detection, or live dashboards — and your producer can write rows via the streaming API. The architectural difference: classic **Snowpipe** is **file-based** — your producer writes **files** to a stage and Snowpipe runs `COPY` when storage events fire, so latency is bounded by file creation + event + load (≈ minutes) and you manage file sizing. **Snowpipe Streaming** is **row-based** — the client SDK writes **rows directly into the table** with **no staging files**, so data is queryable in **seconds** at very high throughput (up to ~10 GB/s per table) and with lower client cost. So: many small files arriving → Snowpipe; a continuous high-rate row stream needing seconds latency → Snowpipe Streaming (often paired with **Dynamic Tables** to transform as it lands).

lessons.title: Time Travel, cloning & micro-partition magic
lessons.summary: The features that fall out of immutable micro-partitions — Time Travel to query/restore the past, zero-copy cloning for instant free environments, Fail-safe, and clustering for big-table pruning.
lessons.concept:
Because micro-partitions are **immutable** (a change writes new partitions, old ones are retained), Snowflake gets several powerful features almost for free.

### Time Travel — query and restore the past
Query a table **as it was** at a past time, within its **retention window** (1 day on Standard, up to **90 days** on Enterprise):
```sql
select * from orders at(offset => -3600);          -- as of 1 hour ago
select * from orders before(statement => '<query_id>');
undrop table orders;                                -- recover a dropped table
```
Use it to recover from a bad `UPDATE`/`DELETE`/`DROP` — restore the prior version with a `CREATE TABLE … CLONE … AT(...)` or `INSERT … SELECT … AT(...)`.

### Zero-copy Cloning — instant, free copies
```sql
create table dev.orders clone prod.orders;
create database dev_sandbox clone prod;            -- clone a whole database!
```
A clone **points at the same micro-partitions** — **no data is copied**, so it's **instant and uses no extra storage**. As you modify the clone, **only changed partitions diverge** (copy-on-write). Perfect for **dev/test environments**, **what-if** analysis, and reproducing a bug on real data safely.

### Fail-safe
After Time Travel expires, **Fail-safe** keeps data for **7 more days** (Snowflake-managed, **not** user-queryable) purely for **disaster recovery** by Snowflake support. It's a safety net, not a feature you query.

### Micro-partition mechanics you should know
- **Pruning** — the optimizer skips partitions whose min/max can't match (automatic, no indexes).
- **Clustering** — for very large tables, define a **clustering key** so related rows co-locate in partitions; **automatic clustering** maintains it (serverless), improving pruning on the columns you filter by.
- **No vacuuming/partitions to manage** — Snowflake handles it.
lessons.example: An analyst runs `delete from orders where region = 'EU'` without a WHERE on date and nukes 2M rows. Recovery is instant and free: `create table orders_restored clone orders at(offset => -600);` (the table as of 10 minutes ago), verify, then swap it back. Separately, the data team spins up a full **dev clone** of the 40 TB production database in seconds — `create database dev clone prod` — to test a migration on real data, costing **no extra storage** until they change something.
keypoints: Immutable micro-partitions retain old versions, which powers Time Travel, cloning, and Fail-safe.
Time Travel queries/restores a table as of a past time within retention (1 day Standard, up to 90 days Enterprise); UNDROP recovers drops.
Zero-copy cloning makes an instant copy that shares micro-partitions — no storage used until the clone is modified (copy-on-write).
Cloning is ideal for dev/test environments, what-if analysis, and reproducing bugs on real data safely.
Fail-safe = 7 extra Snowflake-managed days after Time Travel for disaster recovery — not user-queryable.
Big-table performance: define a clustering key so related rows co-locate; automatic clustering maintains pruning — no manual partitions/indexes.
lessons.diagram_caption: Immutable micro-partitions keep old versions, so Time Travel queries the past and UNDROP recovers drops; a zero-copy clone just points at the same partitions (instant, free, diverges on write); Fail-safe adds 7 recovery days.

quiz.q: Someone ran a bad DELETE 10 minutes ago. How do you recover with Time Travel?
options: Query/clone the table AT an offset before the delete and restore it
quiz.explain: Time Travel lets you read the table as it was before the delete (e.g. AT(offset => -600)) and clone/restore that version — fast and within the retention window.

quiz.q: What makes a zero-copy clone instant and free?
options: It points at the same existing micro-partitions; no data is copied until the clone is modified
It only clones the schema, not data
quiz.explain: A clone is a metadata operation referencing the same immutable partitions; storage is only consumed for partitions that change afterward (copy-on-write).

quiz.q: What is Fail-safe?
options: 7 extra Snowflake-managed days after Time Travel for disaster recovery (not user-queryable)
quiz.explain: Fail-safe is a non-queryable, Snowflake-managed recovery buffer (7 days) after Time Travel expires — a last-resort safety net, not a feature you use directly.

exercises.prompt: Your team needs a full copy of the 30 TB prod database for testing a risky migration, ideally today and cheaply. How does Snowflake make this trivial?
exercises.solution: Run `create database dev_migration clone prod;` — a **zero-copy clone** of the entire database. It completes in **seconds** and uses **no extra storage**, because the clone's tables **point at the same micro-partitions** as prod; nothing is physically copied. Your team then runs the migration against `dev_migration` on **real 30 TB data**, and storage is only consumed for the **partitions they actually change** (copy-on-write). When done, drop the clone. This replaces the old world of provisioning a second 30 TB environment and copying data for days/dollars with an **instant, near-free** real-data sandbox — a direct benefit of immutable micro-partitions. (You can even clone at a Time Travel point: `clone prod at(offset => -3600)`.)

exercises.prompt: A large fact table filters mostly by event_date but pruning is weak because loads interleave dates. What feature helps and how?
exercises.solution: Define a **clustering key** on `event_date` (e.g. `alter table fct_events cluster by (event_date)`). Pruning works by skipping micro-partitions whose **min/max** can't match a filter; if loads **interleave dates**, each partition spans a wide date range, so few can be pruned and queries scan too much. Clustering **co-locates rows with similar `event_date`** into the same partitions, **narrowing each partition's min/max range**, so a `where event_date = …` filter prunes far more aggressively. **Automatic clustering** (serverless) then **maintains** this as new data loads, re-sorting in the background — no manual re-sorting jobs. The trade-off: automatic clustering **consumes credits**, so cluster by the column(s) you actually filter/join on and on tables large enough to justify it; for small tables, default pruning is already fine.

exercises.prompt: Explain why Time Travel, cloning, and Fail-safe are all consequences of one design choice — and what that choice costs.
exercises.hint: Immutable partitions; storage of old versions.
exercises.solution: The one choice is **immutable micro-partitions**: a change never edits a partition in place — it **writes new partitions and retains the old ones** (for the retention window). Once old versions are kept and addressed by metadata, you get all three almost for free: **Time Travel** = read the metadata pointing at the partitions that were active at a past time; **UNDROP** = the dropped object's partitions still exist, just re-point to them; **zero-copy clone** = a new object whose metadata references the **same** partitions, diverging only on write; **Fail-safe** = keep partitions a bit longer (Snowflake-managed) for recovery. The **cost** is **storage of historical/retained partitions** — longer Time-Travel retention and heavily-updated tables keep more old partitions around, increasing storage bytes (and Fail-safe adds 7 days you can't shorten). So you tune **retention per table** to balance recoverability against storage cost — but storage is cheap relative to the safety and agility these features provide.

lessons.title: Snowpark, Iceberg, Dynamic Tables & Cortex AI
lessons.summary: Snowflake as a full modern data platform — Python transformations with Snowpark, open lakehouse tables with Iceberg, declarative pipelines with Dynamic Tables, and LLMs in SQL with Cortex — all in one place.
lessons.concept:
Snowflake in 2026 is far more than a SQL warehouse. Four capabilities make it a complete data-engineering platform.

### Snowpark — Python (and Java/Scala) on the warehouse
Write transformations with a **DataFrame API** in **Python** that compiles to SQL and runs on your warehouse — **no separate Spark cluster**:
```python
from snowflake.snowpark import Session, functions as F
df = session.table('raw.orders').filter(F.col('amount') > 0) \
            .group_by('customer_id').agg(F.sum('amount').alias('ltv'))
df.write.save_as_table('marts.customer_ltv')
```
Plus **Python UDFs** and **stored procedures** that run **inside** Snowflake (bring code to the data, not data to the code). Snowpark-optimized warehouses handle memory-heavy Python.

### Apache Iceberg — the open lakehouse
Snowflake reads and **writes** open **Apache Iceberg** tables (full read/write, partitioned writes, row-level deletes), using an **external catalog** (e.g. an open Polaris catalog). Your data sits in **open format in your own storage**, queryable by Snowflake **and** other engines (Spark, Trino) — Snowflake gives the **compute and governance** without lock-in. This is the **open lakehouse** on Snowflake.

### Dynamic Tables — declarative pipelines
Declare a target table as a SELECT + a `target_lag`; Snowflake refreshes it **incrementally** to meet the freshness — replacing hand-wired Streams/Tasks. Iceberg-compatible, and pairs with Snowpipe Streaming for continuous transform.

### Cortex AI — LLMs in SQL
Run **LLM inference directly in SQL**, with **no data leaving Snowflake** and no external API:
```sql
select review_id,
       snowflake.cortex.sentiment(review_text)            as sentiment,
       snowflake.cortex.summarize(review_text)            as summary,
       snowflake.cortex.complete('mistral-large', prompt) as answer
from reviews;
```
Plus embedding/vector functions for **search and RAG** on your governed data. AI becomes just another SQL function over your tables.
lessons.example: A pipeline that would once have needed Spark + a vector DB + an LLM API now lives entirely in Snowflake: **Snowpark Python** cleans and feature-engineers raw events; the curated data is stored as **Iceberg** tables (also readable by the ML team's Spark); a **Dynamic Table** keeps an aggregated feature table fresh hourly; and **Cortex** runs `sentiment()` and `summarize()` over support tickets and builds embeddings for semantic search — all governed by Snowflake's access controls, with the data never leaving the platform.
keypoints: Snowpark runs Python/Java/Scala DataFrame transforms + UDFs/procs ON the warehouse — no separate Spark cluster.
Snowflake reads AND writes open Apache Iceberg tables via an external catalog — open-format lakehouse, no lock-in, multi-engine.
Dynamic Tables give declarative incremental pipelines (SELECT + target_lag), Iceberg-compatible — the modern Streams/Tasks replacement.
Cortex AI runs LLM inference in SQL (sentiment, summarize, complete, translate, PII redaction) with no data leaving Snowflake.
Cortex also provides embedding/vector functions for search and RAG over governed data.
Together: transformation (Snowpark) + open tables (Iceberg) + declarative pipelines (Dynamic Tables) + AI (Cortex) on one platform.
lessons.diagram_caption: One platform: Snowpark (Python/DataFrames on the warehouse), Iceberg (open lakehouse tables + external catalog), Dynamic Tables (declarative pipelines), and Cortex (LLMs in SQL) — data stays in Snowflake.

quiz.q: What does Snowpark let you do?
options: Write Python/Java/Scala DataFrame transforms and UDFs/procs that run on the Snowflake warehouse (no separate Spark)
Replace the warehouse with a laptop
quiz.explain: Snowpark brings a DataFrame API and code (e.g. Python) to run inside Snowflake on the warehouse — bringing code to the data without a separate Spark cluster.

quiz.q: Why use Iceberg tables in Snowflake?
options: Data stays in open format in your storage, queryable by Snowflake and other engines (no lock-in), with Snowflake compute/governance
quiz.explain: Iceberg gives an open-format lakehouse: your data is portable and multi-engine, while Snowflake provides compute and governance — avoiding lock-in.

quiz.q: What is Cortex AI?
options: LLM/AI functions you call in SQL (sentiment, summarize, complete, embeddings) with data staying in Snowflake
An external API you must call
quiz.explain: Cortex runs LLM inference and embeddings as SQL functions over your governed tables — no data leaves Snowflake and no external API call is needed.

exercises.prompt: Your ML team wants to read the curated tables with Spark, but the analysts use Snowflake SQL. How do Iceberg tables satisfy both without copying data?
exercises.solution: Store the curated tables as **Apache Iceberg** tables (Snowflake can **write** Iceberg) in **your own cloud storage**, registered in an **external/open catalog** (e.g. Polaris). Now the **same physical data, in open format**, is queryable by **both**: analysts use **Snowflake SQL** (Snowflake provides compute + governance), and the ML team points **Spark** (or Trino) at the **same Iceberg tables** via the catalog — **no copy, no export, no second source of truth**. Iceberg's metadata (snapshots, schema, partitions) makes this multi-engine access consistent and transactional. You get the **open lakehouse**: one governed copy in open format, many engines, **no lock-in** — versus the old pattern of exporting Snowflake data into a separate lake for Spark, which duplicates data and drifts.

exercises.prompt: Rewrite this requirement as Snowflake features: 'clean raw events in Python, keep an hourly feature rollup fresh automatically, and tag support tickets by sentiment — without moving data out.'
exercises.solution: **Clean raw events in Python** → **Snowpark**: a Python DataFrame job (`session.table('raw.events').filter(...).group_by(...).agg(...)`) or a Python **stored procedure** that runs **on the warehouse**, writing a curated table — no data leaves Snowflake, no separate Spark. **Hourly feature rollup kept fresh automatically** → a **Dynamic Table** with `target_lag = '1 hour'` whose SELECT builds the feature aggregation; Snowflake **incrementally refreshes** it — no orchestrator. **Tag tickets by sentiment** → **Cortex**: `select ticket_id, snowflake.cortex.sentiment(body) as sentiment from tickets` (and `summarize()`/embeddings for search) — **LLM inference in SQL**, data stays governed in Snowflake. The whole requirement is satisfied **inside one platform**: Snowpark (transform) + Dynamic Table (declarative freshness) + Cortex (AI), with Snowflake's access controls governing all of it.

exercises.prompt: A teammate proposes exporting Snowflake data to a separate lake for Python ML and to an external LLM API for text analysis. Argue for keeping it in-platform with the 2026 features.
exercises.hint: Snowpark + Iceberg + Cortex; governance, copies, egress.
exercises.solution: Exporting creates **duplicate copies** (drift, sync jobs, extra storage), **egress cost and latency**, and a **governance/security gap** (data leaving the controlled platform — a real risk for PII). The 2026 features remove the need: **Snowpark** runs the **Python ML feature engineering on the warehouse** (and Python UDFs/procs in-platform), so no export for Python; if other engines truly need the data, write it as **open Iceberg** tables so Spark/Trino read the **same governed copy** (open lakehouse, still no export-and-drift); and **Cortex** runs **LLM text analysis as SQL functions** (`sentiment`, `summarize`, `complete`, embeddings) with **data never leaving Snowflake** and **no external API key/egress**. Net: **one governed copy**, **no egress**, **consistent access controls and audit**, and **less operational glue** — you keep compute next to the data and avoid the classic 'export to a lake + call an external API' sprawl. Export only when a genuine capability gap exists, and prefer Iceberg over copies when it does.

lessons.title: SQL & semi-structured data (VARIANT/JSON)
lessons.summary: Snowflake queries JSON, Avro, Parquet and XML as first-class SQL using the VARIANT type. Learn schema-on-read path access, PARSE_JSON, OBJECT/ARRAY, and LATERAL FLATTEN to explode nested data into rows.
lessons.concept:
Snowflake handles **semi-structured data** natively — no separate NoSQL store, no rigid schema up front.

### The VARIANT type
Load **JSON / Avro / Parquet / ORC / XML** into a **VARIANT** column (schema-on-read). Snowflake automatically **sub-columnarizes** common paths under the hood, so querying a VARIANT is **columnar-fast**, not a full-document scan.

### Path access & casting
Reach into the document with **`:`** for fields and **`[ ]`** for array elements, then cast with **`::`**:
```sql
select v:user:id::int, v:items[0]:sku::string from events;
```
**`OBJECT`** and **`ARRAY`** are the structured cousins; **`PARSE_JSON`** turns a string into a VARIANT; **`TO_JSON`/`OBJECT_CONSTRUCT`** go the other way.

### FLATTEN — explode arrays into rows
**`LATERAL FLATTEN`** turns each array element into its **own row** — the bridge from nested to relational:
```sql
select e.v:order_id::int as order_id, f.value:sku::string as sku
from orders e, lateral flatten(input => e.v:items) f;
```

### Loading
`COPY INTO` with a **JSON file format** loads files into a VARIANT staging table; you then shred to typed columns/views. This is the standard pattern: **land flexible, model relational**.
keypoints: VARIANT stores JSON/Avro/Parquet/XML with schema-on-read; Snowflake auto-sub-columnarizes paths so queries stay columnar-fast.
Access fields with : and array elements with [ ], then cast with :: (e.g., v:user:id::int).
PARSE_JSON makes a VARIANT from text; OBJECT_CONSTRUCT/TO_JSON go back; OBJECT and ARRAY are the structured types.
LATERAL FLATTEN explodes a nested array into one row per element — the nested→relational bridge.
Standard pattern: COPY raw files into a VARIANT staging table, then shred to typed columns/views (land flexible, model relational).
No separate NoSQL system needed — semi-structured and relational data live and join in one SQL engine.
lessons.diagram_caption: Store flexible JSON in a VARIANT, query it with : and [ ] paths (cast with ::), and LATERAL FLATTEN to turn nested arrays into relational rows — schema-on-read, columnar-fast.

quiz.q: What does LATERAL FLATTEN do?
options: Explodes a nested array (or object) into one row per element, bridging semi-structured data to relational rows
quiz.explain: FLATTEN turns each element of an array/object into its own row, so you can join and aggregate nested data with normal SQL.

quiz.q: Why is querying a VARIANT column still fast in Snowflake?
options: It isn't — VARIANT is always slow
Snowflake automatically sub-columnarizes common paths, so it reads only the needed sub-columns instead of scanning whole documents
Because it runs on a NoSQL engine
quiz.explain: Under the hood Snowflake stores frequently-accessed paths in a columnar form, so path queries prune like normal columns rather than parsing full JSON each time.

exercises.prompt: Raw order events arrive as JSON with a nested items array. Write SQL to produce one row per item with order_id, sku, and qty.
exercises.solution:
Land the JSON in a **VARIANT** (`create table raw_orders(v variant)` then `COPY ... file_format=(type=json)`). Then:
```sql
select v:order_id::int        as order_id,
       f.value:sku::string    as sku,
       f.value:qty::int       as qty
from raw_orders,
     lateral flatten(input => v:items) f;
```
`v:order_id::int` reads and casts the top-level field; **`lateral flatten(input => v:items)`** explodes the **items** array so each element becomes a row, and `f.value:sku::string` / `f.value:qty::int` pull typed columns from each element. You now have a clean relational result you can insert into a typed `fct_order_items` table or expose as a view — the 'land flexible, model relational' pattern.

exercises.prompt: Why might you load JSON into a VARIANT staging table first instead of defining all columns up front?
exercises.solution: Because semi-structured sources **evolve** (new fields appear, optional nesting) and forcing a rigid schema at load time is brittle — a changed payload breaks ingestion. Loading into a **VARIANT** is **schema-on-read**: you ingest whatever arrives without failures, keep the full fidelity of the document, and **shred** to typed columns/views **downstream** where you control the contract. If the source adds a field, ingestion still works and you choose when to surface it. You still get **columnar performance** (auto sub-columnarization), so there's little penalty. It decouples **ingestion stability** from **modeling**, which is exactly what you want for messy, changing JSON.

lessons.title: Security & RBAC (roles, masking, row policies)
lessons.summary: Snowflake security is role-based: privileges go to roles, roles to users, and roles inherit down a hierarchy. Layer on dynamic masking policies, row access policies, network policies, and SSO/MFA for defense in depth.
lessons.concept:
Snowflake access control is **role-based (RBAC)** with a twist: you **grant privileges to roles**, grant **roles to users** (and to other roles), and roles **inherit** down a **hierarchy**.

### The role model
System roles: **ACCOUNTADMIN** (top, use sparingly), **SECURITYADMIN** (manage grants), **USERADMIN** (users/roles), **SYSADMIN** (own databases/warehouses). Best practice: build **custom roles** (e.g., `analyst`, `loader`) under SYSADMIN and grant those to users — never hand out ACCOUNTADMIN. **Future grants** auto-apply privileges to objects created later.
```sql
create role analyst;
grant usage on database analytics to role analyst;
grant select on all tables in schema analytics.marts to role analyst;
grant role analyst to user jdoe;
```

### Dynamic Data Masking (column-level)
A **masking policy** rewrites a column's value based on the querying role:
```sql
create masking policy mask_email as (v string) returns string ->
  case when current_role() in ('PII_READER') then v else '***@***' end;
alter table customers modify column email set masking policy mask_email;
```

### Row Access Policies (row-level)
Restrict **which rows** a role sees (e.g., region/tenant), enforced on every query.

### Network & identity
**Network policies** allow only specific IP ranges; **SSO/SAML**, **OAuth**, and **MFA** secure logins. **Secure views/UDFs** hide logic and underlying data.

Grant least privilege, mask PII, filter rows — defense in depth.
keypoints: RBAC: grant privileges to roles, roles to users/roles; roles inherit down a hierarchy (ACCOUNTADMIN > SECURITYADMIN/SYSADMIN > custom > user).
Best practice: custom roles under SYSADMIN with least privilege; never hand out ACCOUNTADMIN; use future grants for new objects.
Dynamic Data Masking rewrites a column's value based on current_role() — column-level protection for PII.
Row access policies restrict which rows a role sees (region/tenant), enforced on every query.
Network policies allow only specific IPs; SSO/SAML, OAuth and MFA secure authentication; secure views hide logic/data.
Defense in depth = least-privilege roles + column masking + row filtering + network/identity controls.
lessons.diagram_caption: Privileges are granted to roles (not users), and roles inherit down a hierarchy; masking policies (columns), row access policies (rows), and network policies layer fine-grained protection on top.

quiz.q: In Snowflake RBAC, privileges are granted to…
options: Roles, which are then granted to users (and other roles) and inherit down a hierarchy
quiz.explain: You grant privileges to roles and roles to users/roles. This indirection plus inheritance makes access manageable and auditable — you never grant straight to users in good practice.

quiz.q: What does a dynamic masking policy do?
options: Rewrites a column's returned value based on the querying role (e.g., show real email only to a PII_READER role, otherwise masked)
quiz.explain: Masking policies apply at query time per role, so the same column shows full or masked values depending on who's asking — column-level data protection without copying data.

exercises.prompt: Analysts may query customers but must not see real emails, and should only see their own region's rows. Implement this in Snowflake.
exercises.hint: Masking policy + row access policy + roles.
exercises.solution: Create a custom **role** `analyst` (under SYSADMIN) with `SELECT` on the customers table — least privilege, no ACCOUNTADMIN. For **email PII**, attach a **masking policy**: `create masking policy mask_email as (v string) returns string -> case when current_role() in ('PII_READER') then v else '***@***' end;` and `alter table customers modify column email set masking policy mask_email;` — analysts see masked values, only PII_READER sees real ones. For **region rows**, attach a **row access policy** that returns true only when the row's region matches the analyst's mapped region (via a role↔region table), enforced on every query. Now the same table safely serves analysts (masked email, own-region rows) and privileged roles (full access) — no copies, enforced centrally.

exercises.prompt: Why grant privileges to roles rather than directly to users?
exercises.solution: Granting to **roles** (then roles to users) gives a **manageable, auditable** model: access is defined by **job function** (a `analyst` or `loader` role), so onboarding/offboarding is just granting/revoking a role — not re-deriving dozens of object grants per person. It scales (one policy change on a role affects everyone in it), supports **inheritance** (a parent role automatically gets child privileges), and makes audits answer 'what can this role do?' clearly. Direct user grants sprawl, drift, and are easy to forget on departure (a security risk). Role-based grants are the backbone of least-privilege, reviewable access control.

lessons.title: Performance tuning & cost
lessons.summary: Snowflake performance comes from pruning micro-partitions, leaning on three cache layers, and right-sizing warehouses. Learn clustering keys, the Query Profile, materialized views, search optimization, and multi-cluster scaling.
lessons.concept:
Snowflake performance is about **reading fewer micro-partitions**, **using caches**, and **sizing compute** correctly.

### Micro-partitions & pruning
Snowflake automatically stores tables as **micro-partitions** (~16 MB compressed) with min/max metadata. Queries that filter on well-ordered columns **prune** — skip partitions that can't match. On very large tables where the natural order doesn't match your filters, add a **clustering key** so Snowflake co-locates related rows (auto-reclustering maintains it). Check pruning in the **Query Profile** ('partitions scanned' vs 'total').

### The three caches
- **Result cache** — identical query within 24h returns **instantly and free**.
- **Warehouse (local SSD) cache** — hot data stays on the warehouse's local disk across queries.
- **Remote storage** — the durable source of truth.
Keeping a warehouse warm (or right-sized) maximizes cache hits.

### Accelerators
- **Materialized views** — precompute heavy aggregations (auto-maintained).
- **Search Optimization Service** — fast **point lookups** on big tables.
- **Query Acceleration Service** — offloads scan-heavy parts.

### Warehouses & cost
**Virtual warehouses** are compute you size (XS→6XL); bigger = more parallelism per query. **Multi-cluster** warehouses add clusters for **concurrency** (many users), not single-query speed. **Auto-suspend/resume** stops paying when idle. Tune size to the workload; use the **Query Profile** to find spilling/exploding joins.
keypoints: Tables are auto micro-partitioned with min/max metadata; filters prune partitions that can't match.
Add a clustering key on very large tables whose natural order doesn't match common filters; check pruning in the Query Profile.
Three caches: result cache (identical query = instant/free), warehouse local-SSD cache (hot data), remote storage (source of truth).
Materialized views precompute heavy aggregations; Search Optimization speeds point lookups; Query Acceleration offloads big scans.
Virtual warehouses scale single-query power by size; multi-cluster warehouses scale concurrency (many users).
Auto-suspend/resume stops idle cost; use the Query Profile to find spilling, exploding joins, and poor pruning.
lessons.diagram_caption: Snowflake auto-partitions and prunes; a clustering key makes queries read only matching micro-partitions, while the result/warehouse/remote caches and right-sized warehouses cut compute before you scale up.

quiz.q: What does a clustering key improve?
options: It co-locates related rows so queries prune (skip) more micro-partitions on big tables whose natural order doesn't match the filters
quiz.explain: Clustering orders data by chosen columns so min/max pruning skips more partitions for those filters — fewer partitions scanned, faster and cheaper queries on large tables.

quiz.q: What's the difference between a bigger warehouse and a multi-cluster warehouse?
options: A bigger size gives one query more parallel power; multi-cluster adds clusters to handle more concurrent users/queries
Multi-cluster makes a single query faster
quiz.explain: Scale up (size) for heavier individual queries; scale out (multi-cluster) for concurrency. They solve different problems — single-query speed vs many simultaneous users.

exercises.prompt: A dashboard on a 10 TB events table is slow. Walk through how you'd diagnose and fix it in Snowflake.
exercises.solution: Open the **Query Profile**: check **'partitions scanned vs total'** (poor pruning?), and look for **spilling** to local/remote disk or an **exploding join**. If pruning is bad and queries filter by date/region, add a **clustering key** `(event_date, region)` so micro-partitions prune; verify with `system$clustering_information`. For a repeated heavy aggregation, add a **materialized view** so it's precomputed; for frequent **point lookups**, enable **Search Optimization**. Make sure the dashboard reuses the **result cache** (identical queries) and keep the warehouse **warm** for local-SSD cache hits. If many users hit it at once, use a **multi-cluster** warehouse for concurrency; if single queries spill, **size up**. Re-check the Query Profile to confirm fewer partitions scanned and no spilling.

exercises.prompt: Your BI warehouse is expensive. Give three cost levers that don't hurt correctness.
exercises.solution: (1) **Auto-suspend** (e.g., 60s) + auto-resume so you stop paying the moment it's idle — BI is bursty, so this alone saves a lot. (2) **Right-size** the warehouse to the workload (don't run an XL if a MEDIUM serves the dashboards) and use **multi-cluster** only for real concurrency spikes (min 1, max N) rather than one giant always-on warehouse. (3) **Reduce compute per query**: **materialized views** for hot rollups, **clustering** for pruning, and rely on the **result cache** (identical queries are free) — all cut the work the warehouse does. Bonus: separate workloads onto their own warehouses so a heavy load job doesn't force you to oversize the BI warehouse. None of these change results — they cut idle time and wasted scanning.

lessons.title: Data sharing & Marketplace
lessons.summary: Snowflake's Secure Data Sharing gives other accounts live, read-only access to your data with no copying and no pipelines. Learn shares, reader accounts, cross-region/cloud sharing, and the Marketplace.
lessons.concept:
**Secure Data Sharing** is one of Snowflake's signature features: grant another account **live, read-only** access to your data **without copying it**.

### How it works
Because Snowflake separates storage from compute, a **share** is essentially a **metadata grant** — the provider creates a **SHARE**, adds databases/schemas/tables (or **secure views**), and grants it to a **consumer account**. The consumer mounts it as a read-only database and queries it **with their own compute**. There's **no ETL, no file transfer, no duplication** — the consumer always sees the **current** data, and the provider pays only for storage (consumers pay for their own queries).
```sql
create share sales_share;
grant usage on database analytics to share sales_share;
grant select on table analytics.marts.orders to share sales_share;
alter share sales_share add accounts = (consumer_acct);
```

### Reaching everyone
- **Reader accounts** let you share with parties who **aren't Snowflake customers** (you provision a managed account for them).
- **Cross-region / cross-cloud** sharing uses **replication** so consumers in other regions/clouds get the data.
- **Snowflake Marketplace** publishes shares as **discoverable listings** (free, paid, or personalized), and **Data Clean Rooms** allow privacy-preserving joins between parties.

### Why it matters
Share data products **instantly** with partners, customers, or other internal teams — no nightly exports, no stale copies, governed by the same RBAC/masking.
keypoints: Secure Data Sharing grants live, read-only access to your data with no copy, no ETL — a metadata grant enabled by storage/compute separation.
Provider creates a SHARE, adds objects (or secure views), and grants it to consumer accounts; consumers query with their own compute.
Consumers always see current data; provider pays storage, consumers pay for their own queries.
Reader accounts let you share with non-Snowflake parties; cross-region/cloud sharing uses replication.
The Marketplace publishes shares as discoverable listings (free/paid/personalized); Data Clean Rooms enable privacy-preserving joins.
Secure views/masking/RBAC still govern shared data — share governed data products, not raw tables.
lessons.diagram_caption: A share is a live metadata grant: the consumer account queries the provider's same stored data with their own compute — no copy, no pipeline, always current — and the Marketplace turns shares into discoverable listings.

quiz.q: What makes Snowflake data sharing 'zero-copy'?
options: Storage and compute are separate, so a share is a metadata grant to the same stored data — the consumer queries it with their own compute, no duplication or ETL
It replicates the whole database nightly
quiz.explain: Because compute is decoupled from storage, the provider just grants access to the existing data. The consumer reads the same files with their own warehouse — no copying, always current.

quiz.q: How do you share with a party that isn't a Snowflake customer?
options: Provision a reader account (a managed account you create) so they can query the share without their own Snowflake subscription
quiz.explain: Reader accounts let non-Snowflake consumers access a share through an account the provider manages, extending sharing beyond existing Snowflake customers.

exercises.prompt: A partner needs daily access to your orders (only some columns), always current, without you building an export pipeline. How do you deliver this in Snowflake?
exercises.hint: Secure view + share (+ reader account if needed).
exercises.solution: Create a **secure view** exposing only the allowed columns/rows (`orders_shared`), then a **SHARE**: grant `usage` on the database and `select` on the secure view to the share, and `add accounts = (partner_acct)`. The partner **mounts** it as a read-only database and queries **live** data with **their own compute** — no export, no copy, always current, and governed (the secure view limits columns; masking/row policies still apply). If the partner **isn't on Snowflake**, provision a **reader account** for them. If they're in another **region/cloud**, enable **cross-cloud sharing** (replication). You maintain nothing beyond the view — there's no nightly pipeline to break, and they never see stale data.

exercises.prompt: Contrast secure data sharing with the old approach of exporting files to partners.
exercises.solution: **File exports** require a **pipeline** (extract → file → transfer), produce **stale copies** (as fresh as the last run), **duplicate** data (storage + drift), and are **hard to govern** once the file leaves (no row/column control, no revoke). **Secure data sharing** gives **live** access to the **same data** with **no pipeline** to build/break, **no copy** (consumer pays their own compute), and **full governance** (secure views, masking, row policies, instant **revoke**). It's also **discoverable** via the Marketplace and works **cross-region/cloud**. So sharing replaces brittle, stale, ungoverned exports with instant, current, controlled data products — a major reason teams choose Snowflake for data distribution.

lessons.title: Capstone — an end-to-end Snowflake platform
lessons.summary: Assemble the whole Snowflake course into one platform: ingestion (Snowpipe/Streaming/Dynamic Tables), raw→staging→marts modeling with VARIANT + relational, per-workload warehouses, RBAC + policies, performance tuning, sharing, and Snowpark/Cortex.
lessons.concept:
A production Snowflake platform composes everything from this course.

### Ingest
- **COPY** (batch from a stage), **Snowpipe** (auto-ingest files as they land), **Snowpipe Streaming** (low-latency row streams), and **Dynamic Tables** (declarative, incrementally-refreshed transforms — you write the target SELECT, Snowflake maintains it).

### Model
**Databases → schemas**, layered **raw → staging → marts**. Land semi-structured data in **VARIANT**, shred to typed/relational marts. Use **streams + tasks** (or Dynamic Tables) for CDC-style incremental pipelines.

### Compute
**Virtual warehouses** sized per workload (a small one for BI, a bigger one for loads), each isolated so they don't contend; **auto-suspend** to control cost. **Time Travel** and **zero-copy cloning** give instant dev/test copies and recovery.

### Govern, tune, share
- **RBAC** + **masking** + **row access policies** for least-privilege, protected data.
- **Clustering**, **caches**, **materialized views**, **search optimization** for speed; right-size warehouses for cost.
- **Secure Data Sharing / Marketplace** to distribute data products with no copies.

### Build & ML
**Snowpark** (Python/Java/Scala DataFrames + UDFs) runs code **in** Snowflake; **Cortex** offers built-in **LLM/ML** functions. 

The theme: **one governed platform** from ingestion to insight, with **separate compute per workload** and **no data movement**.
keypoints: Ingest with COPY/Snowpipe (files), Snowpipe Streaming (rows), or Dynamic Tables (declarative incremental transforms).
Model raw → staging → marts across databases/schemas; land semi-structured in VARIANT and shred to relational.
Size virtual warehouses per workload and isolate them (BI vs loads); auto-suspend for cost; Time Travel + zero-copy clone for dev/recovery.
Govern with RBAC + masking + row policies; tune with clustering/caches/MVs/search optimization; right-size compute.
Distribute data products via Secure Data Sharing / Marketplace — no copies.
Run code in-platform with Snowpark (DataFrames/UDFs) and ML/LLM with Cortex — one governed platform, ingestion to insight, no data movement.
lessons.diagram_caption: One governed platform end-to-end: ingest via Snowpipe/Streaming/Dynamic Tables, model raw→staging→marts (VARIANT + relational) on per-workload warehouses, then serve BI and Snowpark/Cortex — governed, tuned, and shareable.

quiz.q: What do Dynamic Tables give you?
options: Declarative, incrementally-refreshed transforms — you write the target SELECT and a target lag, and Snowflake maintains the table automatically
quiz.explain: Dynamic Tables let you declare the desired result (and freshness via target_lag); Snowflake incrementally refreshes them, replacing hand-built streams+tasks for many pipelines.

quiz.q: Why run separate virtual warehouses for loading and BI?
options: Workload isolation — a heavy load job won't contend with or slow down interactive BI queries, and each can be sized/scaled independently
quiz.explain: Separate warehouses isolate workloads so loads and dashboards don't fight for compute; you size and auto-suspend each independently for performance and cost.

exercises.prompt: Design an end-to-end Snowflake platform for a retailer: streaming orders + nightly files, BI dashboards, partner data sharing. Name the feature at each layer.
exercises.solution: **Ingest**: nightly files via **Snowpipe** (auto-ingest from a stage), live orders via **Snowpipe Streaming**; land JSON in **VARIANT**. **Model**: **raw → staging → marts** in databases/schemas; use **Dynamic Tables** (or streams+tasks) for incremental `daily_sales`/`fct_orders`; shred VARIANT to typed columns. **Compute**: a **LARGE** `load_wh` for ingestion and a **SMALL** multi-cluster `bi_wh` for dashboards — **isolated**, **auto-suspend** on. **Govern**: custom **RBAC** roles, **masking** on PII (email), **row access** by region. **Tune**: **clustering** on big events, **materialized views** for hot rollups, result/warehouse **caches**. **Recover/dev**: **Time Travel** + **zero-copy clone** for instant dev copies. **Share**: expose a **secure view** to partners via **Secure Data Sharing / Marketplace** (no copies). Optionally **Snowpark/Cortex** for in-platform Python/ML. One governed platform, ingestion to insight.

exercises.prompt: How do Time Travel and zero-copy cloning change dev/test and recovery?
exercises.hint: Instant copies, undo, no storage cost.
exercises.solution: **Zero-copy cloning** creates an **instant** clone of a database/schema/table that **shares the underlying micro-partitions** — so a full dev/test copy costs **no extra storage** until you change data (copy-on-write). Teams spin up realistic **dev environments** in seconds instead of reloading terabytes. **Time Travel** lets you query or restore data **as of** a past timestamp/version within the retention window — so you can **undo** a bad load (`UNDROP`, `CLONE ... AT`), compare before/after, or recover from a mistake **without backups/restores**. Together they make experimentation safe and cheap and turn recovery from an ops project into a SQL statement — a big operational advantage over copy-based systems.

lessons.title: Snowpipe — continuous file ingestion
lessons.summary: Snowpipe is its own discipline: continuous, serverless, micro-batch file loading. This lesson covers auto-ingest vs the REST API, the per-cloud notification wiring, the serverless cost model, file-sizing, dedup/reload semantics, and how to monitor and unstick a pipe in production.
lessons.concept:
**Snowpipe** loads files **continuously** as they land — instead of you scheduling `COPY`. It runs on **Snowflake-managed serverless compute** (not your warehouse), micro-batches new files, and typically lands them within a **minute or two**.

### The pipe object
A **pipe** wraps a `COPY INTO` statement. With `AUTO_INGEST = TRUE`, Snowflake watches a stage and loads new files automatically; otherwise you call the **REST API** (`insertFiles`) to notify it.

### Two trigger modes
- **Auto-ingest** — cloud **event notifications** tell Snowpipe a file arrived: **AWS** S3 → SNS/SQS, **Azure** → Event Grid, **GCS** → Pub/Sub. This is the standard for files already landing in your bucket.
- **REST API** — your own loader calls `insertFiles` with the file list. Use when you control the producer or can't wire notifications.

### What makes it different from COPY
- **Serverless & per-file billed** — no warehouse to size or suspend; you pay a small per-file charge plus compute (see `PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY`).
- **Continuous, not scheduled** — minutes of latency without a cron job.
- **Load-history dedup (14 days)** — Snowpipe won't reload a file it already loaded within the window, so re-notifications are safe.

### File sizing matters more here
Because there's a **per-file overhead**, thousands of tiny files are expensive and slow on Snowpipe. Aim for **100–250 MB compressed**; aggregate tiny files upstream.

### Monitor & operate
`SYSTEM$PIPE_STATUS('pipe')` (is it running? pending files?), `PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY` (credits/files), and `COPY_HISTORY` (per-file load results, errors). When a pipe 'stops loading,' it's almost always notifications not firing, permissions, or a `PAUSED` pipe — all visible in these views.

Snowpipe is for **minutes-latency, file-based** ingestion. For **seconds** and **rows** (no files), use **Snowpipe Streaming**; for **periodic batches**, plain `COPY` on a task.
lessons.example:
```sql
-- 1) secure, key-less access to the bucket
create storage integration s3_int type = external_stage
  storage_provider = 's3' enabled = true
  storage_aws_role_arn = 'arn:aws:iam::123:role/snowflake'
  storage_allowed_locations = ('s3://acme-lake/raw/orders/');

create stage ext_orders url='s3://acme-lake/raw/orders/'
  storage_integration = s3_int file_format = (type=json);

-- 2) the pipe (auto-ingest) — note the SQS ARN you wire into the bucket event
create pipe orders_pipe auto_ingest = true as
  copy into raw.orders from @ext_orders
  file_format = (type=json) on_error = 'skip_file';
show pipes;                          -- copy notification_channel (SQS ARN) into S3 events

-- 3) operate it
select system$pipe_status('orders_pipe');          -- running? pendingFileCount?
select * from table(information_schema.pipe_usage_history(
  date_range_start=>dateadd('day',-1,current_timestamp()), pipe_name=>'ORDERS_PIPE'));
alter pipe orders_pipe refresh;                     -- reload recent files (re-notify)
```
keypoints: Snowpipe = continuous, serverless, micro-batch file loading on Snowflake-managed compute (not your warehouse), ~1–2 min latency.
A pipe wraps a COPY; AUTO_INGEST=TRUE loads on cloud event notifications (S3→SNS/SQS, Azure→Event Grid, GCS→Pub/Sub); else call the REST API insertFiles.
Billed per-file + compute (PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY) — no warehouse to size/suspend.
14-day load-history dedup makes re-notification safe; ALTER PIPE … REFRESH reloads recent files.
File sizing is critical: 100–250 MB compressed; thousands of tiny files are slow and costly due to per-file overhead.
Operate with SYSTEM$PIPE_STATUS (running/pending), PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY (cost), COPY_HISTORY (per-file errors); a 'stuck' pipe is usually notifications/permissions/paused.
lessons.diagram_caption: Files land in cloud storage; an event notification (or a REST insertFiles call) tells Snowpipe, which micro-batches them into the table on serverless compute — minutes of latency, no warehouse, with 14-day dedup and pipe-status monitoring.

quiz.q: How is Snowpipe billed, and why does that change file-sizing advice?
options: On your warehouse's credits — so keep the warehouse small
On Snowflake-managed serverless compute with a per-file charge — so thousands of tiny files get expensive; aim for 100–250 MB files
A flat monthly fee regardless of files
Per query like a normal SELECT
quiz.explain: Snowpipe runs serverless (not your warehouse) and adds a per-file overhead. Many tiny files multiply that overhead, so you batch upstream toward 100–250 MB compressed files.

quiz.q: A pipe suddenly stops loading new files. What's the most likely cause and how do you check?
options: Snowflake is down; nothing to do
Event notifications aren't reaching the pipe (or it's paused / permissions) — check SYSTEM$PIPE_STATUS (running? pendingFileCount) and the bucket's event config
quiz.explain: Auto-ingest depends on cloud notifications; the usual culprits are misconfigured bucket events, IAM permissions, or a PAUSED pipe — all visible via SYSTEM$PIPE_STATUS and the notification setup.

quiz.q: When should you use Snowpipe Streaming instead of Snowpipe?
options: When you need seconds-level latency and are sending rows directly (no files to stage)
When you load one big file nightly
When you want to avoid SQL
quiz.explain: Snowpipe is file-based, minutes latency. Snowpipe Streaming ingests rows via an SDK/Kafka connector for sub-minute latency without staging files.

exercises.prompt: Set up production auto-ingest from S3: the storage integration, stage, pipe, and the one manual wiring step that connects the bucket to the pipe.
exercises.hint: Storage integration → stage → CREATE PIPE auto_ingest → SQS ARN into S3 events.
exercises.solution: Create a **storage integration** (maps to an IAM role, no keys in SQL) scoped to the prefix, then a **stage** using it. Create the **pipe** with `auto_ingest = true` wrapping the `COPY`. Then the manual step: run `SHOW PIPES` to get the pipe's **`notification_channel`** (an **SQS ARN**) and configure the **S3 bucket event notification** (ObjectCreated) to publish to that SQS — that's the wire that makes new files trigger the pipe. (On Azure you'd point an Event Grid subscription at the pipe's notification integration; on GCS, a Pub/Sub topic.) Verify with `SELECT SYSTEM$PIPE_STATUS('orders_pipe')` and drop a test file.

exercises.prompt: A teammate reloaded the same files and worries about duplicates; another complains the pipe is slow with 50,000 tiny files an hour. Explain what protects against the first and how you'd fix the second.
exercises.solution: **Duplicates:** Snowpipe keeps **load history for ~14 days** and **won't reload a file it already loaded** in that window, so re-notifying or re-listing the same files is safe (idempotent). `ALTER PIPE … REFRESH` re-submits recent files but the dedup still applies. **Tiny files:** Snowpipe has a **per-file overhead**, so 50k tiny files/hour means huge overhead and cost. Fix by **aggregating upstream** to ~100–250 MB compressed files (batch in the producer, or land small files and compact before the watched prefix). Check `PIPE_USAGE_HISTORY` to see the file count vs credits and confirm the improvement. If you genuinely need row-level, seconds latency, switch that source to **Snowpipe Streaming**.

exercises.prompt: Compare Snowpipe, scheduled COPY, and Snowpipe Streaming for: (a) a partner drop of 20 large files each night, (b) files trickling in every minute, (c) clickstream rows needing 5-second freshness.
exercises.solution: (a) **Scheduled COPY** (on a task) — a predictable nightly batch of large files doesn't need continuous ingestion; one `COPY` is simplest and cheapest. (b) **Snowpipe** (auto-ingest) — files arriving continuously want event-driven, minutes-latency loading without a cron; per-file billing fits as long as files aren't tiny. (c) **Snowpipe Streaming** — 5-second freshness on **rows** can't wait to accumulate files; the streaming SDK/Kafka connector writes rows directly with sub-minute latency. The deciding axes are **cadence** (batch vs continuous) and **granularity** (files vs rows).

lessons.title: COPY INTO & stages — bulk loading
lessons.summary: COPY is the foundation every other ingestion method builds on. This lesson covers stages and storage integrations, file formats, the full COPY option set (ON_ERROR, VALIDATION_MODE, FORCE, PURGE, transforms), load-metadata dedup, file-sizing, and parallelism.
lessons.concept:
**`COPY INTO`** bulk-loads files from a **stage** into a table, in **parallel** across a warehouse's nodes. It's the workhorse for batch loading and the base that Snowpipe (continuous COPY) and Dynamic Tables sit on top of.

### Stages — where files live
- **User** (`@~`), **Table** (`@%orders`), **Named internal** (`@my_stage` — Snowflake-managed), **External** (`@ext` → S3/GCS/Azure).
- For external stages, a **storage integration** maps to an IAM role / managed identity so **no keys live in SQL**.

### File formats
Define once, reuse: CSV/JSON/Avro/ORC/Parquet/XML with options (`skip_header`, `field_optionally_enclosed_by`, `null_if`, compression…).

### The COPY option set (know these)
- **`ON_ERROR`** — `abort_statement` (default) / `continue` / `skip_file` / `skip_file_5%`.
- **`VALIDATION_MODE = RETURN_ERRORS`** — dry-run; report failures, load nothing.
- **`FORCE = TRUE`** — reload even already-loaded files (bypass load metadata).
- **`PURGE = TRUE`** — delete source files after load.
- **`MATCH_BY_COLUMN_NAME`** — map Parquet/JSON fields to columns by name.
- **`PATTERN`** — regex to select files.

### Transform during load
COPY can run a `SELECT` over staged data (cast, reshape, pick columns) on the way in.

### Load metadata (idempotency)
COPY records loaded files for **~64 days** and won't reload them — re-running is safe. `FORCE` bypasses this (can duplicate).

### Performance
**100–250 MB compressed** files parallelize best; thousands of tiny files or one giant file both hurt. Audit with **`COPY_HISTORY`**.
keypoints: COPY INTO bulk-loads staged files in parallel across the warehouse — the base under Snowpipe and Dynamic Tables.
Stages (user/table/internal/external) point at files; storage integrations give key-less external access.
Know the options: ON_ERROR, VALIDATION_MODE (dry-run), FORCE, PURGE, MATCH_BY_COLUMN_NAME, PATTERN.
COPY can transform via a SELECT during load (cast/reshape/select columns).
Load metadata (~64 days) makes re-runs idempotent; FORCE bypasses it and can duplicate.
File-sizing rules: 100–250 MB compressed; audit loads and errors with COPY_HISTORY.
lessons.diagram_caption: Stages point at files, file formats parse them, and COPY bulk-loads in parallel across the warehouse — with options for error handling and transforms, and load metadata that prevents double-loading.

quiz.q: What protects a COPY from double-loading the same file on a re-run?
options: Load metadata: COPY records loaded files (~64 days) and skips them, unless you set FORCE=TRUE
quiz.explain: COPY tracks which files it has loaded for ~64 days and won't reload them, so re-running is safe. FORCE=TRUE bypasses this and can duplicate data.

quiz.q: You want to see what would fail without loading anything. Which option?
options: VALIDATION_MODE=RETURN_ERRORS (dry-run that reports errors and loads nothing)
quiz.explain: VALIDATION_MODE runs the parse without loading and returns the rows/errors that would occur — the safe pre-flight for a big load.

exercises.prompt: Set up a key-less external stage and a COPY that loads only 2025 CSVs, dry-runs first, skips bad rows, and purges on success.
exercises.hint: storage integration → file format → stage → VALIDATION_MODE → COPY.
exercises.solution: Create a **storage integration** (IAM role, no keys) scoped to the prefix, a **file format** (CSV, skip_header, null_if), and an **external stage** using both. Run `COPY … PATTERN='.*orders_2025.*\.csv' VALIDATION_MODE=RETURN_ERRORS` to **dry-run** and inspect what would fail. Then the real load: `COPY … PATTERN=… ON_ERROR='skip_file' PURGE=TRUE`. Verify with **COPY_HISTORY** (status, row_count, error_count per file). The PATTERN limits files, VALIDATION_MODE pre-flights, ON_ERROR quarantines bad files, PURGE cleans up, and load metadata keeps a re-run safe.

exercises.prompt: Explain why COPY is the foundation for Snowpipe and Dynamic Tables, and one thing COPY does that Snowpipe also relies on.
exercises.hint: Snowpipe wraps a COPY; load metadata.
exercises.solution: A **Snowpipe pipe literally wraps a `COPY INTO`** statement — same stage, file format, transforms, and `ON_ERROR` semantics — just triggered continuously on managed compute instead of on a schedule/warehouse. **Dynamic Tables** transform data that was first **loaded** (often by COPY/Snowpipe). So understanding COPY's stages, file formats, and options *is* understanding the loading layer.
