exercises.prompt: Design partitioning and clustering for a huge events table queried by date range and often filtered by user_id and event_type.
exercises.hint: Partition by date; cluster by user_id, event_type.
exercises.solution: **Partition by `event_date`** (a DATE/TIMESTAMP column) since queries always filter by **date range** — this prunes to the relevant daily partitions, the biggest reduction. **Cluster by `user_id, event_type`** (the high-cardinality columns frequently filtered/joined), ordered by **filtering priority** (user_id first if it's the more common/selective filter) — clustering prunes on a **leftmost prefix**, so order matters. Now a query for 'last 7 days, `user_id=42`, `event_type='click'`' **prunes** to 7 daily partitions and **skips blocks** within them whose user_id/event_type min/max can't match — scanning a tiny fraction. Enable **`require_partition_filter`** so a forgotten date filter can't trigger a full-table scan (cost guardrail). Keep partitions **reasonably sized** (daily is usually fine; watch the per-table partition limit if you'd have too many). BigQuery **re-clusters automatically** as new data loads, so no maintenance. This partition-by-date + cluster-by-filter-columns pattern is the BigQuery standard and cuts bytes scanned (cost + latency) by orders of magnitude versus an unpartitioned table.

exercises.prompt: A team's BigQuery bill is high because analysts run SELECT * with no date filter on a huge table. How do partitioning/clustering plus a guardrail help?
exercises.solution: The bill is high because on-demand BigQuery charges **per TB scanned**, and `SELECT *` with **no date filter** scans **every column of the entire table** every time. Fixes: (1) **Partition by date** and enable **`require_partition_filter`** — now a query **must** filter on the partition column, so it can't scan all history; an unfiltered query **errors** instead of silently scanning terabytes, forcing analysts to add a date range that **prunes** to a small set of partitions. (2) **Cluster** on the commonly-filtered high-cardinality columns so even within the date range, queries that filter on them **skip blocks**. (3) Educate/enforce **column selection** — `SELECT` only needed columns (columnar storage means fewer columns = fewer bytes), never `SELECT *` on wide tables; optionally provide **authorized views** exposing only needed columns. (4) Set a **`maximum_bytes_billed`** limit on queries/jobs as a hard cost cap. Together, **partitioning + require_partition_filter** stop full-history scans, **clustering** reduces within-range bytes, **column selection** cuts width, and **maximum_bytes_billed** caps worst case — slashing the bill while keeping analysts productive. The root cause is **scanning too much**; layout + guardrails fix it.

lessons.title: Pricing & cost control
lessons.summary: BigQuery bills compute two ways — on-demand (per TB scanned) or capacity (reserved/autoscaling slots via editions) — plus storage; control cost with partitioning/clustering, column selection, materialized views, BI Engine, and maximum-bytes-billed guardrails.
lessons.concept:
BigQuery cost has two parts: **storage** (cheap, per GB, with cheaper 'long-term' storage for untouched data) and **compute** (the big variable), billed one of two ways.

### On-demand (per TB scanned)
You pay **per terabyte of data scanned** by queries, with **no commitment** — simplest, pay-per-query, nothing when idle. Because cost = **bytes scanned**, you control it via **layout and query discipline**: **partition/cluster pruning**, **selecting only needed columns** (columnar), avoiding `SELECT *`, and previewing/`--dry_run` to see bytes before running.

### Capacity (slots / editions)
You **reserve compute capacity** as **slots** (with **autoscaling**) via **BigQuery editions** (Standard / Enterprise / Enterprise Plus) and **commitments**. You pay for **slot capacity over time**, not per-TB — giving **predictable cost** for **steady, heavy** workloads and isolating workloads via **reservations**. Autoscaling adds slots under load.

### Choosing
- **On-demand** — variable/unpredictable or low volume; simplest; pay-per-query.
- **Capacity (slots)** — **steady, high** query volume where per-TB would be costly; want predictable spend and workload isolation. Many orgs **mix** (capacity for the steady core, on-demand for spiky/ad-hoc).

### Cost-control levers
- **Partition + cluster** and **`require_partition_filter`** — prune scans.
- **Select only needed columns** — columnar billing.
- **`maximum_bytes_billed`** — hard cap so a runaway query can't scan terabytes.
- **Custom quotas** — per-user/project daily byte limits.
- **Materialized views** — precompute heavy aggregations (scan far less; auto-maintained).
- **BI Engine** — in-memory acceleration for dashboards (faster + can reduce slot use).
- **Cached results** — identical queries return cached results free.
- **Long-term storage** auto-discount for untouched data; **partition expiration** to drop old data.

### Why it matters
BigQuery's serverless power makes it **easy to accidentally scan terabytes**. Knowing the **two pricing models** and the **levers** (pruning, column selection, MVs, guardrails) is what keeps it fast **and** cheap.
lessons.example: A startup with **spiky, ad-hoc** analytics uses **on-demand** (pay-per-query, nothing when idle), relies on **partitioning + clustering** to keep scans small, and sets **`maximum_bytes_billed`** + a per-user **custom quota** so no one accidentally scans the whole lake. As they grow to **steady, heavy** BI, they buy a **slots reservation (Enterprise edition with autoscaling)** for the core workload — **predictable cost** and **workload isolation** — while keeping **on-demand** for occasional ad-hoc. Heavy dashboard aggregations become **materialized views** + **BI Engine** for speed and fewer bytes. Same platform, cost matched to workload shape.
keypoints: Cost = storage (cheap per GB, long-term discount) + compute (the variable), billed on-demand or via capacity.
On-demand: per TB scanned, no commitment — control via pruning, column selection, and dry-run previews.
Capacity: reserve autoscaling slots via editions (Standard/Enterprise/Enterprise Plus) for predictable cost at steady scale.
Choose on-demand for variable/low volume; capacity for steady heavy workloads; many orgs mix both.
Guardrails: maximum_bytes_billed (hard cap), custom quotas (per-user/project), require_partition_filter.
Cut compute with materialized views, BI Engine, cached results, and partition/column pruning.
lessons.diagram_caption: On-demand bills per TB scanned (control via layout); capacity buys autoscaling slots (editions) for steady heavy use — plus MVs, BI Engine, and quota guardrails.

quiz.q: How does on-demand BigQuery pricing work?
options: Per terabyte of data scanned by queries, with no commitment — so reducing bytes scanned reduces cost
quiz.explain: On-demand bills by data scanned; layout/query pruning (partition, cluster, column selection) directly lowers cost.

quiz.q: When would you choose capacity (slots) pricing over on-demand?
options: For steady, heavy query volume where per-TB would be costly — to get predictable cost and workload isolation
quiz.explain: Reserved/autoscaling slots give predictable spend and isolation for steady heavy workloads; on-demand suits variable/low volume.

exercises.prompt: Prevent analysts from accidentally running up huge BigQuery bills while keeping them productive.
exercises.solution: Layer **guardrails + good layout**. (1) **Partition** big tables by date and enable **`require_partition_filter`** so queries **must** filter the partition column — an unfiltered scan **errors** instead of scanning all history, forcing pruning. (2) **Cluster** on common filter columns so even filtered queries scan less. (3) Set **`maximum_bytes_billed`** (per query/job or default) as a **hard cap** — a query that would scan more than N bytes is **rejected**, capping worst-case cost. (4) Apply **custom quotas** (per-user/per-project **daily bytes**) so no single analyst can blow the budget. (5) Teach **column selection** (no `SELECT *`) and **`--dry_run`** to see bytes **before** running, and provide **authorized/materialized views** that pre-aggregate or expose only needed columns. (6) Monitor with **INFORMATION_SCHEMA jobs** / billing exports and alert on heavy scanners. Together: `require_partition_filter` blocks full scans, `maximum_bytes_billed` caps each query, quotas cap each user, and good layout + column discipline keep normal queries cheap by default — productive analysts, bounded cost. For steady heavy teams, also consider a **slots reservation** for predictable spend.

exercises.prompt: A team's on-demand bill grows as steady BI usage increases. Would slots help, and how would you decide?
exercises.hint: Per-TB vs reserved slots; measure utilization.
exercises.solution: Possibly — evaluate by **utilization**. **On-demand** bills **per TB scanned**, which is great for **variable/low** usage (pay nothing when idle) but can become **expensive** as **steady, heavy** query volume grows (you're paying per-TB on a large, constant workload). **Capacity (slots) pricing** buys **reserved/autoscaling slots** for a **fixed rate over time**, which at **high steady utilization** is often **cheaper** than per-TB and gives **predictable cost** plus **workload isolation** (reservations keep ETL from starving BI). **How to decide:** measure the workload — use **INFORMATION_SCHEMA**/billing to see **bytes scanned over time** and estimate the **slot-hours** the workload uses; compare the **on-demand cost** (per-TB) against the **cost of a slots commitment** (e.g. an Enterprise edition reservation with autoscaling) sized to the steady demand. If usage is **consistently high**, a **commitment** typically wins on cost and adds predictability/isolation; keep **on-demand** for the **spiky/ad-hoc** remainder (you can mix). If usage is still **bursty/unpredictable**, stay on-demand (don't pay for idle slots). Also first **reduce bytes** via partitioning/clustering/MVs — sometimes that alone tames an on-demand bill without needing slots. Decision rule: **steady high utilization → slots (predictable/cheaper/isolated)**; **variable/low → on-demand**, validated by measured utilization.

lessons.title: Loading & streaming ingestion
lessons.summary: Batch-load from GCS (Parquet/Avro/CSV/JSON) with free load jobs, stream in real time via the Storage Write API, or query data in place with external/BigLake tables — choosing by latency, cost, and whether data should live in BigQuery.
lessons.concept:
Getting data into (or queryable by) BigQuery has three main paths.

### Batch loading (free)
**Load jobs** import files from **Google Cloud Storage** (or local) into BigQuery managed storage. Supported formats: **Parquet, Avro, ORC** (self-describing, best), **CSV, JSON**. Key point: **batch load jobs are free** (you pay only for storage), so loading from GCS is the cheap default for bulk ingestion. You can **append** or **overwrite** (incl. specific partitions via `WRITE_TRUNCATE` on a partition), auto-detect schema, and load directly into **partitioned/clustered** tables.

### Streaming ingestion (real-time, billed)
For **real-time** data, the **Storage Write API** (the modern, recommended high-throughput streaming API; supersedes the older `tabledata.insertAll` streaming inserts) writes rows that are **immediately queryable**, with **exactly-once** semantics and lower cost/higher throughput than the legacy API. Streaming is **billed** (per byte) — use it when you genuinely need **freshness**; otherwise batch-load (free).

### External / BigLake tables (query in place)
**External tables** let BigQuery **query data in place** in **GCS** (or Bigtable, Sheets, other clouds) **without loading** it — you define a table over the files and query them. **BigLake** tables add **fine-grained governance** (column/row security) and performance (metadata caching) over external data, and support **open formats (Parquet, Iceberg, Delta)** across **GCS and other clouds** — making BigQuery a query engine over the open lakehouse.

### Other paths
- **Dataflow / Dataproc / Datastream** write to BigQuery (pipelines, CDC).
- **BigQuery Data Transfer Service** schedules loads from SaaS/other warehouses.
- **Federated queries** to Cloud SQL/Spanner.

### Choosing
- **Bulk, not real-time** → **batch load from GCS** (free, into partitioned tables).
- **Real-time freshness** → **Storage Write API** (billed).
- **Don't want to load / data lives in the lake or another cloud** → **external/BigLake** tables.

### Why it matters
Picking the right path balances **latency, cost, and data placement**: batch is free and the default for bulk; streaming buys freshness at a cost; external/BigLake query open lake data in place. Loading into **partitioned/clustered** tables sets up the cost/perf wins from earlier lessons.
lessons.example: A pipeline lands hourly **Parquet** in GCS and **batch-loads** it into a **date-partitioned, clustered** BigQuery table with a **free load job** — cheap bulk ingestion, query-optimized layout. A separate real-time use case (live dashboard) writes events via the **Storage Write API** so they're **immediately queryable** (accepting the streaming cost for freshness). And a large historical dataset that also feeds a Spark job stays in **GCS as Iceberg**, exposed as a **BigLake** table so BigQuery queries it **in place** with fine-grained security — no duplicate load. Each path matched latency/cost/placement.
keypoints: Batch load jobs import GCS files (Parquet/Avro/ORC best; CSV/JSON) into managed storage — and are FREE (pay only storage).
Load directly into partitioned/clustered tables; append or overwrite (incl. per-partition WRITE_TRUNCATE), with schema auto-detect.
Streaming via the Storage Write API makes rows immediately queryable with exactly-once — real-time but billed per byte.
External tables query GCS/other sources in place without loading; BigLake adds fine-grained security + open formats (Iceberg/Delta) across clouds.
Other paths: Dataflow/Dataproc/Datastream pipelines, Data Transfer Service, federated queries.
Choose by latency/cost/placement: batch (free, bulk) vs streaming (billed, real-time) vs external/BigLake (query in place).
lessons.diagram_caption: Batch-load GCS files into managed storage (free), stream real-time via the Storage Write API (billed), or query GCS in place with external/BigLake tables.

quiz.q: Which BigQuery ingestion path is free?
options: Batch load jobs from GCS (you pay only for storage)
quiz.explain: Batch load jobs are free; streaming (Storage Write API) is billed per byte, so use batch for bulk and streaming only for freshness.

quiz.q: What do external/BigLake tables let you do?
options: Query data in place in GCS/other sources without loading; BigLake adds fine-grained security and open formats (Iceberg/Delta) across clouds
quiz.explain: External tables query files in place; BigLake adds governance, performance, and open-format/multi-cloud support over lake data.

exercises.prompt: Choose ingestion paths for: (a) hourly bulk Parquet, (b) a live real-time dashboard, (c) a huge GCS dataset also read by Spark. Justify each.
exercises.hint: Batch (free), Storage Write API (billed), BigLake (in place).
exercises.solution: **(a) Hourly bulk Parquet → batch load (free).** Use a **load job** from GCS into a **date-partitioned, clustered** managed table. Batch loads are **free** (pay only storage), Parquet is self-describing/efficient, and loading into a partitioned/clustered layout sets up cheap queries. Hourly isn't real-time, so streaming's cost is unjustified. **(b) Live real-time dashboard → Storage Write API (billed).** The dashboard needs **freshness**, so write events via the **Storage Write API** (modern, high-throughput, **exactly-once**) so rows are **immediately queryable**. Accept the **streaming cost** because freshness is the requirement; you could still micro-batch if seconds-latency suffices to save cost. **(c) Huge GCS dataset also read by Spark → BigLake external table.** It already lives in **GCS** (e.g. Parquet/Iceberg) and is read by **Spark**, so **don't duplicate** it into BigQuery — expose it as a **BigLake** table so BigQuery queries it **in place** with **fine-grained security** and open-format support, while Spark reads the same files. One copy, two engines. **Summary:** batch (free) for bulk, Storage Write API (billed) for real-time, BigLake (in place) for shared lake data — matching **latency, cost, and data placement** for each case.

exercises.prompt: A team streams everything via the Storage Write API and the bill is high, but most data isn't time-sensitive. What's the fix?
exercises.hint: Stream only what needs freshness; batch the rest (free).
exercises.solution: They're **paying for streaming on data that doesn't need it**. Streaming (Storage Write API) is **billed per byte** and is for **real-time freshness**; **batch load jobs are free**. **Fix:** **segment by freshness need.** For the **majority** that isn't time-sensitive, switch to **batch loading** from GCS (e.g. write the data to GCS and run free **load jobs** every few minutes/hourly into partitioned tables) — eliminating the streaming charge for that volume. **Reserve streaming** (Storage Write API) only for the **genuinely real-time** subset (e.g. the live dashboard's events). If near-real-time (a minute or two) is acceptable for some streams, **micro-batch** them to GCS and load frequently rather than streaming row-by-row. This typically **slashes** the ingestion bill because most data moves to the **free** batch path while freshness is preserved where it actually matters. The principle: **stream only what needs to be fresh; batch-load everything else (free).** Also ensure data lands in **partitioned/clustered** tables so downstream queries stay cheap.

lessons.title: Query optimization
lessons.summary: Because BigQuery bills bytes scanned, optimization means scanning less (select needed columns, filter on partition/cluster keys, avoid SELECT *) and shuffling smart for big joins (denormalize/nested data, broadcast small joins, approximate aggregations) — guided by the query execution plan.
lessons.concept:
BigQuery query optimization has two themes: **scan less data** (the cost/IO lever) and **shuffle/compute efficiently** (for big joins/aggregations). Read the **query execution plan** to know which matters.

### Scan less (the big one)
- **Select only needed columns** — columnar storage means projecting 4 of 100 columns reads ~4% of column data. **Never `SELECT *`** on wide tables.
- **Filter on partition and cluster keys** — prune partitions and skip blocks (prior lesson). The dominant reducer of bytes scanned.
- **Avoid scanning then filtering** — push filters down; don't compute over data you'll discard.
- **Materialized views / cached results** — reuse precomputed/identical results (scan far less).

### Shuffle smart (big joins/aggregations)
- **Denormalize with nested & repeated fields** — BigQuery natively supports **STRUCT/ARRAY** (nested, repeated) columns. Storing related data **nested** (instead of a separate table joined at query time) avoids large **shuffles/joins** — a BigQuery-idiomatic optimization (e.g. an order with a repeated `line_items` array).
- **Broadcast small joins** — join a small dimension to a large fact so the small side is broadcast (BigQuery's optimizer often does this; keep dimensions small/filtered).
- **Filter/aggregate before joining** — reduce data entering a join.
- **Avoid data skew** — a skewed join key overloads some workers; mitigate (filter hot keys, restructure).
- **Approximate functions** — `APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT`, `APPROX_QUANTILES` are far cheaper than exact for huge cardinalities when approximate is acceptable.

### Read the execution plan
The **query plan / execution details** show **stages**, **bytes scanned/shuffled**, **slot time**, and **skew** (a stage where one worker took far longer). Use it to find the bottleneck — too much scanned (fix pruning/columns), heavy shuffle (denormalize/broadcast), or skew (restructure).

### Anti-patterns
- `SELECT *`, no partition filter, joining huge normalized tables unnecessarily, exact distinct on billions, self-joins/cross joins by accident.

### Why it matters
In BigQuery there's **no cluster to tune** — optimization is **data layout + SQL discipline**. Scanning less (columns/partitions) cuts cost and latency; smart shuffling (nested data, broadcast, approximate) makes big joins/aggregations fast.
lessons.example: A slow, costly query did `SELECT * FROM events e JOIN users u …` over a huge normalized schema with no date filter. Diagnosed via the **execution plan** (massive bytes scanned + a heavy shuffle stage). Fixes: **select only the needed columns**; add a **partition filter** on `event_date` (+ rely on **clustering**) to prune; **denormalize** frequently-joined user attributes into the events table as **nested fields** (or pre-join into a wide table) to **avoid the shuffle**; and use **`APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT`** for the unique-users metric. Bytes scanned dropped ~100×, the shuffle disappeared, and the query went from minutes to seconds — pure layout + SQL changes, no cluster tuning.
keypoints: BigQuery bills bytes scanned, so the top lever is scanning less: select needed columns and filter on partition/cluster keys.
Never SELECT * on wide tables; columnar storage means projecting few columns reads few bytes.
Denormalize with nested & repeated (STRUCT/ARRAY) fields to avoid large shuffles/joins — a BigQuery-idiomatic optimization.
Broadcast small joins, filter/aggregate before joining, and mitigate join-key skew for big joins.
Use approximate functions (APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT, APPROX_QUANTILES) for cheap huge-cardinality aggregations.
Read the query execution plan (stages, bytes, slot time, skew) to find and fix the actual bottleneck — no cluster to tune.
lessons.diagram_caption: Scan less (select columns, prune partitions/clusters) and shuffle smart (nested data, broadcast small joins, approximate) — guided by the execution plan.

quiz.q: What is the single biggest query optimization in BigQuery, and why?
options: Scanning less data (select needed columns, filter on partition/cluster keys) — because BigQuery bills bytes scanned and reads columnar
quiz.explain: Cost and latency are driven by bytes scanned; column selection + partition/cluster pruning minimize it. There's no cluster to tune.

quiz.q: Why denormalize with nested & repeated (STRUCT/ARRAY) fields in BigQuery?
options: Storing related data nested avoids large shuffles/joins at query time — a BigQuery-idiomatic performance optimization
quiz.explain: BigQuery natively supports nested/repeated columns; keeping related data together avoids expensive join shuffles.

exercises.prompt: A query joins several huge normalized tables, scans far too much, and is slow. Walk through optimizing it in BigQuery.
exercises.hint: Plan → columns, partition filter, denormalize/nested, broadcast, approximate.
exercises.solution: **Diagnose** with the **query execution plan / details**: check **bytes scanned** (too much → scan problem) and look for a **heavy shuffle stage** or **skew** (one worker far slower). **Then fix:** (1) **Select only needed columns** — drop `SELECT *`; columnar storage means fewer columns = fewer bytes. (2) **Filter on partition + cluster keys** (e.g. a date range) to **prune** partitions and **skip blocks** — usually the biggest reduction. (3) **Reduce the joins:** **denormalize** frequently-joined attributes into the fact table using **nested & repeated (STRUCT/ARRAY)** fields or pre-join into a **wide/materialized** table, so the expensive **shuffle/join** at query time disappears — the BigQuery-idiomatic move. (4) For joins you keep, **filter/aggregate before** joining and ensure small dimensions are **broadcast** (keep them small/filtered); mitigate **skewed** join keys. (5) Use **approximate** functions (`APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT`, `APPROX_QUANTILES`) for huge-cardinality metrics where exactness isn't required. (6) Consider a **materialized view** if this heavy aggregation repeats. **Re-check the plan** after each change. The result: bytes scanned drop dramatically (columns + pruning), the shuffle shrinks/disappears (nested/broadcast), and the query speeds up — all via **layout + SQL**, since there's **no cluster to tune** in BigQuery.

exercises.prompt: When and why would you use APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT instead of COUNT(DISTINCT ...) in BigQuery?
exercises.hint: Cost/perf of exact distinct on huge cardinality.
exercises.solution: Use **`APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT`** when counting **distinct values over a very large, high-cardinality** dataset and a **small approximation error is acceptable** (typical for analytics/dashboards — e.g. 'roughly how many unique users today'). **Exact `COUNT(DISTINCT ...)`** requires the engine to **track every distinct value**, which over **billions** of rows/high cardinality is **expensive** in memory and slot time and can cause heavy shuffles/skew, making the query slow and costly. `APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT` uses a **sketch (HyperLogLog++)** that estimates the distinct count with **bounded error** at a **fraction of the cost** and far better scalability — often dramatically faster on huge inputs. So: for **exact** requirements (billing, compliance, exact reconciliation) use exact distinct; for **analytics/dashboards on huge cardinality** where ~1% error is fine, use **`APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT`** (and `APPROX_QUANTILES` for percentiles) to cut cost and latency. It's a standard BigQuery optimization: trade negligible accuracy for big efficiency gains on large-scale distinct/percentile aggregations.

lessons.title: Materialized views, BI Engine, BQML & BigLake
lessons.summary: BigQuery is a platform, not just a query engine: materialized views precompute aggregations, BI Engine accelerates dashboards in memory, BigQuery ML trains and predicts in SQL, and BigLake governs and queries open data across clouds.
lessons.concept:
Beyond running SQL, BigQuery has features that make it a **data + analytics + ML platform**.

### Materialized views (MVs)
**MVs precompute and store** the result of an expensive query (joins/aggregations). Queries hit the **MV** (fast, far less scanned) instead of recomputing from base tables. BigQuery MVs are **automatically refreshed** as base data changes and feature **smart tuning** — the optimizer can **transparently rewrite** a query against base tables to use a matching MV. Ideal for **repeated heavy aggregations** (dashboards, rollups).

### BI Engine
**BI Engine** is an **in-memory analysis accelerator**: it caches hot data in memory so dashboard queries (from Looker, Looker Studio, Tableau, etc.) return in **sub-second** time, and can **reduce slot consumption**. Great for **interactive BI** on BigQuery.

### BigQuery ML (BQML)
**BQML** lets you **train and run ML models in SQL** — `CREATE MODEL`, `ML.PREDICT`, `ML.EVALUATE` — without moving data out of BigQuery. Supports regression, classification, clustering, time-series (ARIMA), matrix factorization, and integration with **Vertex AI** (and even calling LLMs). It brings ML to **SQL users** and keeps it **next to the data** (no export).

### BigLake
**BigLake** unifies governance over **BigQuery managed tables and open-format lake data**: it lets BigQuery (and other engines) query **GCS, Iceberg, Delta, and other clouds (AWS S3, Azure)** as governed tables with **fine-grained (column/row) security** and performance (metadata caching). It makes BigQuery a **governed query engine over the open lakehouse**, not just its own storage.

### Other platform features
- **Search indexes** for point lookups; **vector search** for embeddings/RAG.
- **Data Transfer Service**, **scheduled queries**, **remote functions** (call Cloud Functions/LLMs from SQL).
- **Analytics Hub** for data sharing/exchange.

### Why it matters
BigQuery isn't just 'serverless SQL' — it's a **platform**: precompute (MVs), accelerate (BI Engine), do **ML in SQL** (BQML), and **govern open multi-cloud data** (BigLake). Knowing these turns BigQuery from a query engine into the **center of a GCP data+AI stack**.
lessons.example: A dashboard's expensive daily revenue rollup becomes a **materialized view** (auto-refreshed; the optimizer rewrites base-table queries to use it), and **BI Engine** caches the hot data so the dashboard loads **sub-second**. The data science team trains a churn model with **BQML** (`CREATE MODEL … ; SELECT … FROM ML.PREDICT(...)`) **in SQL, on the data in place** — no export. And a large dataset shared with an AWS-based partner stays in **S3 as Iceberg**, exposed via **BigLake** so BigQuery queries it **in place** with **column-level security**. One platform: precompute, accelerate, ML-in-SQL, and governed multi-cloud data.
keypoints: Materialized views precompute heavy aggregations, auto-refresh, and can transparently rewrite base-table queries to use them.
BI Engine accelerates dashboards with in-memory caching for sub-second queries and reduced slot use.
BigQuery ML trains and predicts in SQL (CREATE MODEL/ML.PREDICT) without moving data, integrating with Vertex AI.
BigLake governs and queries open-format data (Iceberg/Delta/GCS) across clouds (S3/Azure) with fine-grained security.
Other platform features: search/vector indexes, remote functions (call Cloud Functions/LLMs), Analytics Hub sharing.
BigQuery is a data+analytics+ML platform — precompute, accelerate, ML-in-SQL, and govern open multi-cloud data.
lessons.diagram_caption: BigQuery as a platform: materialized views (precompute), BI Engine (in-memory acceleration), BigQuery ML (ML in SQL), and BigLake (govern open multi-cloud data).

quiz.q: What do BigQuery materialized views provide?
options: Precomputed, auto-refreshed aggregation results that queries use (with transparent rewrite) instead of recomputing from base tables
quiz.explain: MVs store precomputed results, auto-refresh, and can be used automatically via query rewrite — cutting repeated heavy computation.

quiz.q: What is BigQuery ML (BQML)?
options: Training and running ML models in SQL (CREATE MODEL/ML.PREDICT) directly on BigQuery data, no export
quiz.explain: BQML brings ML to SQL users, training/predicting on data in place and integrating with Vertex AI — no data movement.

exercises.prompt: A BI dashboard repeatedly runs the same heavy aggregation and is slow/costly. Use BigQuery platform features to fix it.
exercises.solution: Combine **materialized views** and **BI Engine**. (1) Turn the **repeated heavy aggregation** (e.g. daily revenue by region) into a **materialized view** — BigQuery **precomputes and stores** it, **auto-refreshes** it as base data changes, and the optimizer can **transparently rewrite** the dashboard's base-table queries to use the MV, so it scans **far less** and runs much faster/cheaper than recomputing each time. (2) Enable **BI Engine** so the **hot data** (the MV / underlying columns) is **cached in memory**, giving the dashboard **sub-second** response and **reducing slot consumption** for interactive queries. (3) **Result caching** also helps for identical repeated queries (free). Together: the **MV** eliminates recomputation (less scanned, lower cost), **BI Engine** makes it **interactive** (sub-second), and caching covers repeats — turning a slow, costly dashboard into a fast, cheap one **without** changing the dashboard tool. This is the BigQuery-idiomatic way to accelerate repeated BI: **precompute (MV) + accelerate (BI Engine)**.

exercises.prompt: A data science team wants to train a churn model and a partner's data lives in S3 as Iceberg. How do BQML and BigLake help, and what's the benefit of each?
exercises.hint: BQML = ML in SQL on data in place; BigLake = govern/query open multi-cloud data.
exercises.solution: **BQML for the model:** train and serve the **churn model in SQL** with `CREATE MODEL … OPTIONS(model_type='logistic_reg' …) AS SELECT …` then `SELECT … FROM ML.PREDICT(MODEL …, (SELECT …))` — **directly on the BigQuery data, no export**. Benefit: **SQL users** (analysts/engineers) can build/deploy ML **next to the data** (no pipeline to move data to a separate ML platform, no copy), with `ML.EVALUATE` for metrics and **Vertex AI** integration for more advanced needs — fast iteration, governed data, less plumbing. **BigLake for the partner's S3 Iceberg data:** instead of copying it into BigQuery, expose it as a **BigLake table** so BigQuery queries the **Iceberg data in S3 in place** with **fine-grained (column/row) security** and good performance (metadata caching), across clouds. Benefit: **no duplication/egress** of the partner's data, **consistent governance** over open-format multi-cloud data, and the team can **join** the partner's lake data to BigQuery tables in one query — and even **train BQML** on the combined data. Together: **BigLake** brings the **open multi-cloud data** under governed, in-place query, and **BQML** does the **ML in SQL** on it — keeping everything **next to the data** without exports, the BigQuery platform advantage.

lessons.title: The Beam model: unified batch + streaming
lessons.summary: Apache Beam is a unified, portable programming model: a pipeline is a DAG of PCollections (datasets) transformed by PTransforms, and the SAME code runs over bounded (batch) or unbounded (streaming) data on any runner — Dataflow being GCP's serverless runner.
lessons.concept:
**Cloud Dataflow** runs **Apache Beam** pipelines serverlessly. Beam's big idea is a **unified, portable model** for both **batch and streaming** — write the pipeline once, run it anywhere, over bounded or unbounded data.

### Core abstractions
- **Pipeline** — the whole data-processing job (a DAG).
- **PCollection** — a distributed dataset flowing through the pipeline. It's **bounded** (finite — **batch**) or **unbounded** (infinite — **streaming**). Crucially, the **same transforms** work on both.
- **PTransform** — an operation that takes PCollection(s) and produces PCollection(s): `ParDo` (map), `GroupByKey`, `Combine`, `Window`, IO connectors, etc.
- **Runner** — the engine that executes the pipeline: **Dataflow** (GCP, serverless), **Flink**, **Spark**, **Direct** (local). The pipeline code is **portable** across runners.

```python
with beam.Pipeline() as p:
  (p | beam.io.ReadFromPubSub(topic=...)        # source → PCollection
     | beam.Map(parse)                          # PTransform
     | beam.WindowInto(window.FixedWindows(60))
     | beam.CombinePerKey(sum)
     | beam.io.WriteToBigQuery(table=...))       # sink
```

### Unified batch + streaming
The **same pipeline** can read a **bounded** source (GCS file → batch) or an **unbounded** source (Pub/Sub → streaming). Streaming-specific concerns — **windowing, watermarks, triggers** (next lesson) — are part of the model, so you don't switch frameworks to go from batch to streaming. (Batch is just a special case of streaming over bounded data.)

### Portability
Because the model is **runner-agnostic**, a Beam pipeline can run on **Dataflow** today and **Flink/Spark** elsewhere — avoiding lock-in. On GCP you use the **Dataflow runner** (serverless, autoscaling — runner lesson).

### SDKs
Beam has **Java, Python, Go**, and **SQL** (and YAML) SDKs, plus **cross-language** transforms.

### Why it matters
Beam/Dataflow is GCP's answer to **complex and streaming** data processing: **one model** for batch + streaming, **portable** across runners, executed **serverlessly** on Dataflow. Understanding PCollections/PTransforms/runners — and that batch and streaming share code — is the foundation for everything else (windowing, transforms, the runner).
lessons.example: A team writes one Beam pipeline: `ReadFromPubSub → parse → window into 1-minute fixed windows → CombinePerKey(sum) → WriteToBigQuery`. Run against the **Pub/Sub** source it's a **streaming** job (unbounded). To **backfill** from history, they point the **same pipeline** at **GCS files** (bounded) and it runs as a **batch** job — no rewrite, because Beam unifies batch and streaming. It runs on **Dataflow** (serverless) on GCP, but the same code could run on **Flink** elsewhere — portability. PCollections flow through PTransforms; the runner executes the DAG.
keypoints: Dataflow runs Apache Beam: a unified, portable model for batch and streaming data processing.
A pipeline is a DAG; PCollections are datasets (bounded=batch or unbounded=streaming) transformed by PTransforms.
The SAME code runs over bounded or unbounded data — batch is a special case of streaming over finite data.
PTransforms include ParDo (map), GroupByKey, Combine, Window, and IO connectors; they compose into the DAG.
Runners (Dataflow, Flink, Spark, Direct) execute pipelines — Beam code is portable across them (no lock-in).
SDKs: Java, Python, Go, SQL; Dataflow is GCP's serverless, autoscaling runner.
lessons.diagram_caption: Beam is a unified, portable model: a pipeline of PCollections + PTransforms runs the same code over bounded (batch) or unbounded (streaming) data on any runner.

quiz.q: What is the key idea of the Apache Beam model?
options: Separate frameworks for batch and streaming
A unified, portable model where the same pipeline (PCollections + PTransforms) runs over bounded (batch) or unbounded (streaming) data on any runner
quiz.explain: Beam unifies batch and streaming in one portable model; the same code runs on bounded or unbounded data across runners like Dataflow/Flink/Spark.

quiz.q: What is a PCollection?
options: A distributed dataset flowing through a pipeline — bounded (batch) or unbounded (streaming) — transformed by PTransforms
quiz.explain: PCollections are the datasets in a Beam pipeline; the same transforms apply whether they're bounded or unbounded.

exercises.prompt: Your team needs both a real-time pipeline and a backfill of the same logic over history. How does Beam/Dataflow help?
exercises.hint: Same pipeline, unbounded vs bounded source.
exercises.solution: Beam's **unified batch+streaming** model lets you write the **transformation logic once** and run it both ways. Build one **Beam pipeline** (parse → window/aggregate → write to BigQuery). For **real-time**, run it against the **unbounded** source (**Pub/Sub**) as a **streaming** Dataflow job. For the **backfill**, point the **same pipeline** at the **bounded** source (historical files in **GCS**, or a BigQuery export) and run it as a **batch** Dataflow job — **no separate codebase**, because in Beam batch is just streaming over finite data and the transforms are identical. This avoids the classic problem of maintaining **two implementations** (a Spark batch job + a separate streaming job) that drift apart; here the **logic is shared**, so backfills and live processing stay consistent. Dataflow executes both **serverlessly** (autoscaling, no clusters). You'd handle windowing/watermarks for the streaming case (next lesson), but the core pipeline is reused. This 'write once, run batch or streaming' capability is a primary reason to choose Beam/Dataflow for pipelines that need both.

exercises.prompt: Why is Beam's runner portability valuable, and how does it relate to Dataflow?
exercises.hint: Runner-agnostic model; avoid lock-in; Dataflow = GCP serverless runner.
exercises.solution: Beam separates the **pipeline definition** (the portable model — PCollections/PTransforms) from the **execution engine** (the **runner**). The same Beam code can run on the **Dataflow** runner (GCP, serverless), the **Flink** runner, the **Spark** runner, or the **Direct** runner (local testing). **Value:** **portability / no lock-in** — you can develop and test locally (Direct runner), run in production on **Dataflow**, and, if needed, move to **Flink/Spark** (on-prem or another cloud) **without rewriting** the pipeline logic; teams aren't tied to one engine. It also lets you choose the **best runner per environment**. **Relation to Dataflow:** on GCP you typically use the **Dataflow runner**, which executes Beam pipelines **serverlessly** with **autoscaling**, fusion, and managed shuffle/state (runner lesson) — so you get Beam's portable model **plus** Google's fully-managed execution. So Beam gives the **portable programming model**; Dataflow is the **managed GCP runner** for it. You write portable Beam, and Dataflow runs it without infrastructure management.

lessons.title: Windowing, watermarks & triggers
lessons.summary: Beam's streaming power is its event-time model: windows bucket unbounded data by event time, watermarks track event-time progress, and triggers decide when to emit results — giving full control over the completeness-vs-latency trade-off.
lessons.concept:
Unbounded streams need a way to **group data over time** and decide **when results are ready**. Beam's model — **windows, watermarks, triggers, accumulation** — is the most expressive in the field and the same concepts appear in Flink/Spark.

### Windowing
A **window** assigns each element (by its **event time**) to one or more time buckets:
- **Fixed (tumbling)** — non-overlapping (e.g. every 1 minute).
- **Sliding** — overlapping (e.g. 1-minute windows every 30 s).
- **Session** — activity-based, closing after a **gap** of inactivity (per key).
- **Global** — one window (with triggers) for the whole stream.
Aggregations (`GroupByKey`/`Combine`) then operate **per window**.

### Event time vs processing time
Beam aggregates by **event time** (when the event happened), not **processing time** (when it arrived) — so results are **correct** despite out-of-order/late data. (Like Spark/Flink.)

### Watermarks
A **watermark** is the system's estimate of **event-time progress** — 'we've probably seen all events up to time T'. The runner advances it as data flows. When the watermark **passes a window's end**, the window's **on-time** result can fire and its state can be cleared.

### Triggers
**Triggers** decide **when** to emit a window's results:
- **On-time** — when the watermark passes the window end (default).
- **Early (speculative)** — emit partial results **before** the watermark (low latency).
- **Late** — emit **updates** when late data arrives after the watermark.
Combined with **allowed lateness** (how long to keep accepting late data) and **accumulation mode** (**accumulating** = each firing includes prior results; **discarding** = only new data), you get **full control** over the **completeness vs latency** trade-off.

### The trade-off
- Want **low latency**? Fire **early** triggers (partial results, refined later).
- Want **completeness**? Wait for the watermark + **allowed lateness** (handle stragglers).
Beam lets you choose explicitly per pipeline.

### Why it matters
Windowing + watermarks + triggers are **how you do correct streaming aggregation** in Beam/Dataflow — the same event-time concepts as Flink/Spark, but with Beam's especially expressive **trigger/accumulation** controls. This is the heart of streaming on GCP.
lessons.example: A real-time metric counts events per **1-minute fixed window** by **event time**, with a **watermark** for progress, a **10-minute allowed lateness**, and triggers: an **early** trigger emits a speculative count every 10 s (low-latency dashboard), the **on-time** firing emits the count when the watermark passes the window, and **late** firings emit **updates** if stragglers arrive within the 10-minute lateness — in **accumulating** mode so each firing reflects the full count so far. An event stamped 10:00:30 arriving at 10:09 still updates the 10:00 window (within lateness); one arriving at 10:30 is dropped. The pipeline explicitly chose its completeness/latency balance.
keypoints: Windows bucket unbounded data by event time: fixed (tumbling), sliding (overlapping), session (gap-based), global.
Beam aggregates by event time (not arrival), so results are correct despite out-of-order/late data.
A watermark estimates event-time progress; passing a window's end lets its on-time result fire and state clear.
Triggers decide WHEN to emit: on-time (watermark), early (speculative/low-latency), late (updates for stragglers).
Allowed lateness + accumulation mode (accumulating vs discarding) give full control over completeness vs latency.
Same event-time concepts as Flink/Spark, with Beam's especially expressive trigger/accumulation controls.
lessons.diagram_caption: Windows bucket events by event time; watermarks track progress; triggers (on-time/early/late) + allowed lateness/accumulation control completeness vs latency.

quiz.q: What does a watermark represent in Beam?
options: The system's estimate of event-time progress ('seen all events up to time T') — used to fire on-time results and clear state
quiz.explain: The watermark tracks event-time progress; when it passes a window's end, the on-time result fires and the window's state can be released.

quiz.q: What do triggers control?
options: WHEN a window's results are emitted — on-time (watermark), early (speculative), and late (updates) — trading completeness vs latency
quiz.explain: Triggers + allowed lateness + accumulation mode decide when/how window results fire, giving explicit control over latency vs completeness.

exercises.prompt: Design windowing/triggers for a dashboard that wants low-latency partial counts but eventually-correct totals, with events up to ~10 minutes late.
exercises.hint: Fixed windows + early trigger + watermark + allowed lateness + accumulating.
exercises.solution: Use **fixed (tumbling) windows** by **event time** (e.g. 1-minute) for the per-minute counts. Set **allowed lateness = 10 minutes** to accept stragglers. Configure **triggers** to satisfy both needs: an **early trigger** that fires **speculatively** every few seconds (or every N elements) to give the dashboard **low-latency partial counts** before the watermark; the **on-time** firing when the **watermark** passes the window end (the 'complete-so-far' count); and **late** firings that emit **updates** as late events arrive within the 10-minute lateness. Use **accumulating** accumulation mode so **each firing includes all data seen so far** (the dashboard shows a refining total), not just the delta. So: the dashboard updates **fast** (early triggers) and converges to the **correct** total (on-time + late firings within allowed lateness), with the watermark coordinating event-time progress. Events later than 10 minutes are dropped (bounded state). This explicitly trades a little extra state/latency for the **low-latency + eventually-correct** behavior the dashboard needs — exactly what Beam's trigger/accumulation model is for.

exercises.prompt: Why aggregate by event time with watermarks instead of processing time, and what does allowed lateness add?
exercises.hint: Correctness vs arrival; stragglers; bounded state.
exercises.solution: **Event time** is when the event actually **happened** (a timestamp in the data); **processing time** is when it **arrived** at the pipeline. Aggregating by **processing time** makes results depend on **arrival timing** — a backlog, retry, or network delay lumps old events into the current window, and late events land in the wrong bucket, so counts are **wrong and non-reproducible**. Aggregating by **event time** (with **watermarks** tracking event-time progress) puts each event in its **correct** window regardless of when it arrives, giving **correct, reproducible** results. The **watermark** tells the system when a window is 'probably complete' so it can **fire on-time results** and **release state**. **Allowed lateness** adds tolerance for **stragglers**: it keeps a window's state for an extra period **after** the watermark passes, so **late** events still update the result (via late firings) instead of being dropped immediately — and once lateness expires, state is cleared so memory stays **bounded**. So event-time + watermarks = correctness despite out-of-order data; allowed lateness = handle real-world stragglers without unbounded state. This is the foundation of correct streaming aggregation in Beam/Dataflow (and Flink/Spark).

lessons.title: Core transforms: ParDo, GroupByKey, Combine
lessons.summary: Beam pipelines are built from composable transforms: ParDo (per-element processing), GroupByKey/CoGroupByKey (grouping/joins), Combine (aggregations), side inputs (broadcast lookups), and stateful DoFns with timers for advanced streaming.
lessons.concept:
Beam provides a small set of **composable PTransforms** that cover most data processing; you wire them into the pipeline DAG.

### ParDo — the workhorse
**`ParDo`** applies a **`DoFn`** to **each element** (like map/flatMap) — parse, filter, transform, emit zero-or-more outputs (incl. **multiple outputs** / tagged outputs). Most custom logic is a `DoFn`.
```python
class Parse(beam.DoFn):
  def process(self, element):
    yield transform(element)
pcoll | beam.ParDo(Parse())
```

### GroupByKey / CoGroupByKey
- **`GroupByKey`** — groups a keyed PCollection by key (the basis of aggregation; triggers a **shuffle**).
- **`CoGroupByKey`** — **joins** two (or more) keyed PCollections by key.

### Combine
**`Combine`** / **`CombinePerKey`** — **associative/commutative aggregation** (sum, mean, count, custom `CombineFn`). More efficient than GroupByKey+ParDo because the runner can **pre-combine** (partial aggregation) before shuffling — like a Spark reduce/combiner.

### Side inputs
A **side input** broadcasts a **small** PCollection (e.g. a lookup table) into a `ParDo` so each element can be **enriched** against it — efficient for small reference data (like a broadcast join).

### Stateful processing & timers
**Stateful `DoFn`s** keep **per-key state** and set **event-time/processing-time timers** — for **sessionization**, deduplication, custom windowing, and **complex event processing**. The advanced streaming tool.

### Composite transforms
Package a sub-pipeline of transforms as a **reusable `PTransform`** — your own building blocks (e.g. a 'CleanAndDedup' transform).

### IO connectors
`ReadFrom…`/`WriteTo…` for **Pub/Sub, BigQuery, GCS, Kafka, JDBC**, etc.

### Why it matters
**ParDo + GroupByKey + Combine** cover the bulk of ETL; **side inputs** handle enrichment; **stateful DoFns + timers** handle advanced streaming. Composing these (with windowing) is how you build any Dataflow pipeline — and `Combine` over `GroupByKey` is a key **performance** habit (pre-aggregation).
lessons.example: A pipeline: `ParDo(parse)` cleans raw events; a **side input** of a small `country → region` map enriches each event; `WindowInto(fixed 1-min)` then `CombinePerKey(sum)` computes per-key totals (the runner **pre-combines** before the shuffle, far cheaper than `GroupByKey` + manual sum); `CoGroupByKey` joins the result to a sessions stream; and a **stateful DoFn with timers** sessionizes user activity (emit a session after N minutes idle). Each piece is a composable transform; the whole is a DAG the Dataflow runner executes.
keypoints: ParDo applies a DoFn per element (map/flatMap/filter, multiple outputs) — the workhorse for custom logic.
GroupByKey groups a keyed PCollection (shuffle); CoGroupByKey joins keyed PCollections.
Combine/CombinePerKey does associative aggregation and pre-combines before shuffle — more efficient than GroupByKey+ParDo.
Side inputs broadcast a small PCollection into a ParDo for enrichment/lookups (broadcast-join style).
Stateful DoFns with event-time/processing-time timers enable sessionization, dedup, and complex event processing.
Composite transforms package reusable sub-pipelines; IO connectors read/write Pub/Sub, BigQuery, GCS, Kafka, etc.
lessons.diagram_caption: Core Beam transforms — ParDo, GroupByKey/CoGroupByKey, Combine, side inputs, stateful DoFns + timers — compose into the pipeline DAG.

quiz.q: What is ParDo in Beam?
options: A transform that applies a DoFn to each element (map/flatMap/filter, can emit zero-or-more outputs) — the per-element workhorse
quiz.explain: ParDo runs a DoFn per element for custom processing; most pipeline logic is expressed as DoFns.

quiz.q: Why prefer Combine/CombinePerKey over GroupByKey + a manual sum?
options: Combine is associative so the runner can pre-combine (partial aggregation) before shuffling — far more efficient
quiz.explain: Combine pre-aggregates before the shuffle (like a combiner/reducer), moving far less data than GroupByKey + ParDo.

exercises.prompt: Build a Beam pipeline that parses events, enriches them with a small lookup, computes per-key minute totals efficiently, and sessionizes user activity. Which transforms?
exercises.hint: ParDo, side input, Window+CombinePerKey, stateful DoFn+timers.
exercises.solution: Compose the right transforms: (1) **`ParDo(parse)`** — a `DoFn` to clean/parse each raw event (filter bad ones). (2) **Enrich** with a **side input**: load the small lookup (e.g. `country → region`) as a side input and reference it inside a `ParDo` to add the region to each element — efficient broadcast-style enrichment for small reference data. (3) **Per-key minute totals efficiently**: `WindowInto(FixedWindows(60))` then **`CombinePerKey(sum)`** — using **Combine** (not GroupByKey + manual sum) so the runner **pre-combines** partial sums **before the shuffle**, moving far less data (a key performance habit). (4) **Sessionize user activity**: use a **stateful `DoFn` with event-time timers** (or **session windows**) — keep per-user state, append events, and **fire a session** when a timer indicates N minutes of inactivity, then clear state. Wire these into the pipeline DAG (with appropriate windowing/triggers from the prior lesson), reading from Pub/Sub and writing to BigQuery via IO connectors. This uses **ParDo** (parse/enrich), **side input** (lookup), **Combine** (efficient aggregation), and **stateful DoFn + timers** (sessionization) — the core building blocks covering enrichment, aggregation, and advanced stateful streaming.

exercises.prompt: When would you use a stateful DoFn with timers rather than plain windowing/Combine?
exercises.hint: Custom per-key logic beyond standard windows/aggregations.
exercises.solution: Use a **stateful `DoFn` with timers** when the per-key logic goes **beyond standard windowing + associative aggregation** — i.e. you need **custom, per-key state and time-based callbacks**. Examples: **sessionization** with custom rules (emit a session after N minutes idle, with custom merging), **deduplication** (remember seen IDs per key for a bounded time), **complex event processing / pattern detection** (track a sequence/state machine per key and fire when a pattern completes), **rate limiting / alerting** (per-key counters with timeouts), or **custom windowing** semantics not expressible with built-in windows. Plain **windowing + Combine** handles the common case — bucket by time and aggregate associatively — efficiently and simply, so prefer it when your logic is 'aggregate per window'. But when you need **arbitrary per-key state** that evolves over events **and** **timers** (event-time or processing-time) to trigger actions (session close, timeout, pattern emit), the **stateful DoFn** gives you that low-level control. Trade-off: stateful DoFns are more powerful but more complex and require careful state/timer management (and watermark/lateness handling); use them for the advanced streaming cases, and windowing/Combine for standard aggregations.

lessons.title: The Dataflow runner: serverless autoscaling
lessons.summary: Dataflow is the fully-managed, serverless runner for Beam: it provisions and autoscales workers, fuses stages, dynamically rebalances stragglers, and offloads shuffle and streaming state to managed services — you submit a pipeline and it runs, no clusters.
lessons.concept:
**Dataflow** is GCP's **serverless runner** for Beam pipelines. You submit a pipeline; Dataflow **provisions, scales, and manages** the execution — **no clusters** to size or tune.

### What the service does
- **Autoscaling** — adds/removes workers based on the workload (backlog for streaming, work remaining for batch) — no manual sizing.
- **Fusion** — the optimizer **merges adjacent transforms** into single stages to avoid materializing intermediate data (less overhead).
- **Dynamic work rebalancing** — detects **stragglers** (slow workers) and **redistributes** work so a few slow shards don't bottleneck the job — a big advantage over static partitioning.
- **Managed shuffle / Streaming Engine** — **offloads** the shuffle (batch) and **streaming state/timers** to a **managed backend** (Dataflow Shuffle, Streaming Engine), so workers are stateless and scaling is faster/cheaper.
- **Dataflow Prime** — vertical + horizontal autoscaling and resource optimization.

### Execution model
The pipeline DAG is optimized (fusion), workers execute fused stages, shuffles/state go to the managed backend, and the service handles **fault tolerance** (exactly-once for supported sources/sinks via checkpointing) and **autoscaling** throughout.

### Operating it
- **Monitoring** — the Dataflow UI shows the job graph, per-stage throughput, **system lag / data freshness** (streaming), and autoscaling.
- **Cost** — billed by **worker resources** (vCPU/memory/storage) + shuffle/Streaming Engine usage; autoscaling and Prime right-size it. Use **FlexRS** (flexible resource scheduling, spot-like) for non-urgent batch to cut cost.
- **Exactly-once** — Dataflow provides exactly-once processing within the pipeline; combined with idempotent/transactional sinks (e.g. BigQuery Storage Write API) for end-to-end correctness.

### vs cluster-based engines
Unlike **Dataproc/Spark** (you manage a cluster), Dataflow is **serverless** — no cluster lifecycle, autoscaling and stragglers handled for you. The trade-off is less low-level control and the Beam programming model.

### Why it matters
The Dataflow service is what makes Beam **practical at scale**: **serverless autoscaling**, **straggler rebalancing**, and **managed shuffle/state** mean you focus on the **pipeline**, not infrastructure — especially valuable for **streaming**, where Dataflow's autoscaling + Streaming Engine shine.
lessons.example: A streaming pipeline's input spikes 10×; **Dataflow autoscaling** adds workers to keep up with the backlog, then scales back when it subsides — no manual intervention. One key range is hot, creating a **straggler**; **dynamic work rebalancing** redistributes its work so the job isn't bottlenecked. The shuffle and streaming **state** live in the **Streaming Engine** (managed), so workers stay light and scaling is fast. The team watches **data freshness / system lag** in the Dataflow UI to confirm it's keeping up, and runs a non-urgent nightly batch with **FlexRS** to save cost. They never sized a cluster — they submitted a Beam pipeline and Dataflow ran it serverlessly.
keypoints: Dataflow is the serverless Beam runner: submit a pipeline and it provisions/scales/manages execution — no clusters.
Autoscaling adds/removes workers by workload (streaming backlog / batch work remaining).
Fusion merges adjacent transforms into stages; dynamic work rebalancing redistributes straggler work.
Managed shuffle (batch) and Streaming Engine (streaming state/timers) offload work from workers for faster/cheaper scaling.
Provides exactly-once within the pipeline; pair with idempotent/transactional sinks for end-to-end correctness.
Billed by worker resources + shuffle/Streaming Engine; FlexRS/Prime optimize cost; monitor via the job graph and data freshness.
lessons.diagram_caption: The Dataflow service runs Beam serverlessly — autoscaling workers, fusing stages, rebalancing stragglers, and offloading shuffle/state to managed backends.

quiz.q: What does dynamic work rebalancing do in Dataflow?
options: Detects straggler (slow) workers and redistributes their work so a few slow shards don't bottleneck the job
quiz.explain: Rebalancing reassigns work from slow shards to idle workers, avoiding the straggler bottleneck of static partitioning.

quiz.q: How is Dataflow different from Dataproc/Spark for running pipelines?
options: Dataflow is serverless (no cluster to manage; autoscaling, fusion, managed shuffle/state); Dataproc you manage a Spark cluster
quiz.explain: Dataflow runs Beam serverlessly with autoscaling and managed shuffle/state; Dataproc gives you a managed Spark/Hadoop cluster to operate.

exercises.prompt: A streaming Dataflow job's input is spiky and one key is hot, causing lag. How does the Dataflow service handle this, and what would you monitor?
exercises.hint: Autoscaling, dynamic rebalancing, Streaming Engine; data freshness.
exercises.solution: The **Dataflow service** addresses both automatically. For the **spiky input**, **autoscaling** monitors the **backlog** and **adds workers** to catch up, then **scales back** when the spike subsides — no manual resizing. For the **hot key / straggler**, **dynamic work rebalancing** detects the slow shard and **redistributes** work to idle workers so the job isn't bottlenecked by one heavy key (and **Streaming Engine** keeps **state/timers** in a managed backend so workers stay light and scaling is fast). To **monitor**, watch the **Dataflow UI**: **data freshness / system lag** (how far behind event time the pipeline is — the key streaming health metric), **autoscaling** (worker count over time vs backlog), per-stage **throughput**, and any **errors**. If lag persists despite autoscaling, investigate the **hot key** (mitigate skew — e.g. add a sharding key, pre-aggregate) and check sink throughput (e.g. BigQuery write limits). Also confirm **exactly-once** with a transactional sink (Storage Write API). So: autoscaling handles volume, rebalancing handles stragglers, Streaming Engine handles state, and you verify via **data freshness/lag** — the serverless runner doing the heavy lifting while you watch a few key metrics.

exercises.prompt: How would you reduce the cost of a large but non-urgent nightly Dataflow batch job?
exercises.hint: FlexRS, autoscaling, fusion, right resources, Prime.
exercises.solution: Use Dataflow's cost levers for **non-urgent batch**: (1) **FlexRS (Flexible Resource Scheduling)** — schedules the job using **preemptible/spot-like** resources within a time window, **significantly cheaper** for jobs that can tolerate delayed start and aren't time-critical (perfect for a nightly batch). (2) Let **autoscaling** right-size workers to the actual work (don't over-provision a fixed worker count). (3) **Dataflow Prime** for automatic vertical+horizontal scaling and resource optimization. (4) Choose appropriate **machine types** (don't over-allocate memory/CPU) and enable **Dataflow Shuffle** (managed shuffle) so workers are lighter. (5) Optimize the **pipeline** itself — **Combine** (pre-aggregate before shuffle) instead of GroupByKey+manual, filter early, avoid unnecessary reshuffles — since less data processed = lower cost. (6) Ensure **fusion** isn't broken by unnecessary materialization. So: **FlexRS** (cheap scheduling for non-urgent), **autoscaling/Prime** (right-size), managed **Shuffle**, sensible **machine types**, and an **efficient pipeline** (Combine, early filtering) together cut the nightly batch cost substantially without hurting the (non-urgent) SLA. For **urgent/streaming** jobs you wouldn't use FlexRS, but for nightly batch it's the top lever.

lessons.title: Templates & choosing Dataflow
lessons.summary: Dataflow templates package pipelines so they can be launched by parameters (including Google-provided no-code templates), and the key judgment is when to use Dataflow (streaming/complex Beam) vs Dataproc (Spark), BigQuery/Dataform (SQL ELT), or a direct Pub/Sub→BigQuery path.
lessons.concept:
Two practical topics round out Dataflow: **templates** (reuse/operationalize pipelines) and **choosing** Dataflow vs alternatives.

### Templates
A **template** packages a Beam pipeline so it can be **launched by parameters** without rebuilding/deploying code each time:
- **Flex Templates** (modern) — the pipeline is packaged as a container image; launch with parameters via UI/gcloud/API/scheduler.
- **Classic Templates** — older, staged template files.
- **Google-provided templates** — ready-made pipelines for common tasks (**Pub/Sub → BigQuery**, **GCS → BigQuery**, **BigQuery → GCS**, **Pub/Sub → GCS**, format conversions, etc.) — **no code** needed for standard movement.
Templates make pipelines **reusable, parameterized, and easy to launch** (e.g. from Composer/Workflows or by analysts).

### When to use Dataflow
- **Streaming** with **event-time** windowing/watermarks/triggers (its sweet spot).
- **Complex transformations** not well-expressed in SQL (custom logic, enrichment, stateful processing).
- Want **serverless autoscaling** and the **portable Beam** model (and batch+streaming code reuse).
- Cross-language or sophisticated pipelines.

### Dataflow vs alternatives
- **vs Dataproc (Spark/Hadoop)** — use **Dataproc** if you have **existing Spark/Hadoop** code/skills or need specific OSS frameworks; **Dataflow** for serverless Beam and streaming without cluster management.
- **vs BigQuery / Dataform** — if the transformation is **expressible in SQL** and the data is (or will be) in BigQuery, **SQL ELT (BigQuery/Dataform)** is simpler and cheaper to operate — no pipeline engine. Reserve Dataflow for what SQL can't do.
- **vs direct Pub/Sub → BigQuery** — for **simple ingestion** (Pub/Sub straight into BigQuery), the built-in **Pub/Sub BigQuery subscription** or a Google template may suffice — no custom Dataflow.

### Decision rule
**SQL-expressible ELT → BigQuery/Dataform; existing Spark → Dataproc; streaming/complex/portable Beam → Dataflow; trivial ingest → Pub/Sub→BQ.** Match to the workload.

### Why it matters
Dataflow is powerful but not always the simplest tool. **Templates** operationalize it, and knowing **when to choose it vs SQL ELT, Spark, or direct ingestion** keeps your GCP pipelines simple and cost-effective — a common design/interview judgment.
lessons.example: A team needs **Pub/Sub → BigQuery** ingestion: rather than write a custom pipeline, they use the **Google-provided Dataflow template** (or a Pub/Sub BigQuery subscription) — no code. For a **complex streaming** enrichment/sessionization that SQL can't express, they write a **Beam pipeline** and run it on **Dataflow** (serverless, autoscaling, event-time windows), packaging it as a **Flex Template** so it's launchable with parameters from **Cloud Composer**. Their **SQL-expressible** transformations (cleaning, joining, aggregating data already in BigQuery) are done in **Dataform/BigQuery** (cheaper to operate). And an existing **Spark** job is run on **Dataproc**. Each workload went to the right tool — Dataflow only where its streaming/complex-Beam strengths were needed.
keypoints: Templates (Flex/Classic) package pipelines to launch by parameters; Google-provided templates handle common moves with no code.
Use Dataflow for streaming (event-time windowing), complex/stateful transforms, serverless autoscaling, and portable Beam.
vs Dataproc: choose Dataproc for existing Spark/Hadoop; Dataflow for serverless Beam/streaming without clusters.
vs BigQuery/Dataform: if it's SQL-expressible and in BigQuery, SQL ELT is simpler/cheaper than a Dataflow pipeline.
vs direct Pub/Sub→BigQuery: for simple ingestion, a Pub/Sub BigQuery subscription or template may suffice — no custom Dataflow.
Decision: SQL ELT→BigQuery/Dataform; existing Spark→Dataproc; streaming/complex Beam→Dataflow; trivial ingest→Pub/Sub→BQ.
lessons.diagram_caption: Templates package/launch pipelines; choose Dataflow for streaming/complex Beam, Dataproc for Spark, BigQuery/Dataform for SQL ELT, Pub/Sub→BQ for simple ingest.

quiz.q: What do Dataflow templates provide?
options: Packaged pipelines launchable by parameters (incl. Google-provided no-code templates for common moves like Pub/Sub→BigQuery)
quiz.explain: Templates make pipelines reusable/parameterized; Google-provided ones handle standard data movement without writing code.

quiz.q: When should you prefer BigQuery/Dataform over a Dataflow pipeline?
options: When the transformation is SQL-expressible and the data is (or will be) in BigQuery — SQL ELT is simpler and cheaper to operate
For complex streaming with event-time windows
quiz.explain: If SQL can express it on BigQuery data, SQL ELT (Dataform/BigQuery) avoids running a pipeline engine — simpler and cheaper. Reserve Dataflow for what SQL can't do.

exercises.prompt: For each, pick the GCP processing tool: (a) Pub/Sub→BigQuery simple ingest, (b) complex streaming sessionization, (c) SQL cleaning/joining of BigQuery data, (d) an existing Spark job. Justify.
exercises.solution: **(a) Pub/Sub→BigQuery simple ingest → a Pub/Sub BigQuery subscription or Google-provided Dataflow template (no custom code).** It's straightforward movement; use the **built-in** path or a **template** rather than writing/operating a custom pipeline. **(b) Complex streaming sessionization → Dataflow (Beam).** This needs **event-time windowing/watermarks/triggers** and **stateful** processing that SQL can't express well — Dataflow's sweet spot (serverless, autoscaling, Streaming Engine); write a Beam pipeline (stateful DoFn/session windows) and package as a Flex Template. **(c) SQL cleaning/joining of BigQuery data → BigQuery/Dataform (SQL ELT).** It's **SQL-expressible** and the data is **in BigQuery**, so do it as **tested SQL models in Dataform** — no pipeline engine to run, simpler and cheaper to operate than Dataflow. **(d) Existing Spark job → Dataproc.** You already have **Spark** code/skills, so run it on **Dataproc** (managed Spark, ephemeral cluster or serverless) rather than rewriting to Beam. **Theme:** match the workload — **trivial ingest** to built-in/template, **streaming/complex Beam** to Dataflow, **SQL-expressible** to BigQuery/Dataform, **existing Spark** to Dataproc. Don't force everything into Dataflow; use it where its streaming/complex-transform strengths are needed.

exercises.prompt: A team builds custom Dataflow pipelines for everything, including simple SQL transforms on BigQuery data and basic Pub/Sub→BigQuery ingestion. What's the problem and the fix?
exercises.solution: **Problem:** they're **over-using Dataflow** — running and maintaining **custom pipeline code/infrastructure** for tasks that have **simpler, cheaper** native solutions, adding needless development, operational burden, and cost. **Fix — match each workload to the right tool:** (1) **Simple SQL transforms on BigQuery data** → move to **Dataform/BigQuery SQL ELT** (tested SQL models, scheduled queries) — **no pipeline engine** to run, far simpler and cheaper, and the data's already in BigQuery. (2) **Basic Pub/Sub→BigQuery ingestion** → use the built-in **Pub/Sub BigQuery subscription** or a **Google-provided Dataflow template** — **no custom code** to maintain. (3) **Reserve custom Dataflow** for what genuinely needs it: **streaming with event-time windowing/triggers**, **complex/stateful** transforms SQL can't express, or **portable Beam** requirements. (4) For **existing Spark**, use **Dataproc**. This **reduces** the codebase, operational load, and cost — analysts/engineers maintain **SQL** instead of pipelines, standard ingestion is **managed**, and Dataflow effort focuses only on the **hard streaming/complex** cases. The principle: **don't default to Dataflow**; use the **simplest tool that fits** (SQL ELT → BigQuery/Dataform; managed ingest → Pub/Sub→BQ; Spark → Dataproc; streaming/complex → Dataflow).

lessons.title: Topics, subscriptions & fan-out
lessons.summary: Pub/Sub is GCP's global, serverless messaging backbone: publishers send to a topic, and each subscription gets its own copy of every message (fan-out), decoupling producers from consumers with auto-scaling, at-least-once delivery.
lessons.concept:
**Pub/Sub** is Google's **global, serverless** messaging service — the **entry point** for streaming on GCP and the way to **decouple** event producers from consumers. (It's the GCP analog of Kafka/Kinesis, but fully managed and global.)

### The model
- **Publishers** send **messages** to a **topic** (a named channel).
- **Subscriptions** attach to a topic; **each subscription receives a copy of every message** published after it was created.
- **Subscribers** consume from a subscription.

### Fan-out
The key pattern: **one topic → many subscriptions**, and **each subscription gets every message independently**. So multiple consumers (a Dataflow job, a BigQuery subscription, a Cloud Function, an archival job) each get their **own copy** of the stream and process at their **own pace** — true **fan-out** without producers knowing the consumers.

### Decoupling
Producers publish to a **topic** without knowing who consumes; consumers attach **subscriptions** without affecting producers. This **decoupling** is the point of a messaging backbone — add/remove consumers freely, absorb spikes, and isolate failures.

### Global & serverless
- **Global** — a topic is reachable across regions; Pub/Sub handles routing and replication.
- **Serverless & auto-scaling** — no partitions/shards to provision (unlike Kafka/Kinesis); it scales automatically with load. (**Pub/Sub Lite** is a cheaper, zonal, partitioned variant for very high, predictable throughput where you manage capacity.)
- **At-least-once** delivery by default (exactly-once and ordering are opt-in — next lesson).

### Subscription types
- **Pull** — subscriber pulls messages (you control the rate).
- **Push** — Pub/Sub pushes to an HTTPS endpoint.
- **BigQuery subscription** — writes directly to a BigQuery table (no code).
- **Cloud Storage subscription** — writes batches to GCS.

### Why it matters
Pub/Sub is the **streaming backbone** of GCP: ingest events globally, **fan out** to many consumers, and **decouple** the system — all serverless with no capacity to manage. It's where real-time data **enters** the platform before Dataflow/BigQuery process it.
lessons.example: An app publishes click events to a **`clicks`** topic. Three **subscriptions** each get **every** event independently: a **Dataflow** subscription does real-time enrichment → BigQuery; a **BigQuery subscription** lands raw events directly (no code) for ad-hoc analysis; and a **Cloud Function** subscription triggers alerts. The app (producer) doesn't know or care about these consumers — new ones can be added by creating a subscription, and a slow consumer doesn't back up the others (each has its own copy/backlog). Pub/Sub auto-scales through traffic spikes with no shards to manage. That **fan-out + decoupling**, globally and serverlessly, is the Pub/Sub value.
keypoints: Pub/Sub is GCP's global, serverless messaging backbone — the entry point for streaming and decoupling producers/consumers.
Publishers send to a topic; each subscription on that topic gets its own copy of every message (fan-out).
One topic → many subscriptions lets multiple consumers process the same stream independently at their own pace.
Serverless & auto-scaling — no partitions/shards to provision (Pub/Sub Lite is the zonal, partitioned, cheaper variant).
Default delivery is at-least-once; ordering and exactly-once are opt-in (next lesson).
Subscription types: pull, push, BigQuery subscription (direct, no code), Cloud Storage subscription.
lessons.diagram_caption: Publishers send to a topic; each subscription receives every message (fan-out), so many consumers process the same stream independently — global, serverless.

quiz.q: What does each subscription on a Pub/Sub topic receive?
options: Its own copy of every message published to the topic — enabling independent fan-out to multiple consumers
Nothing until the topic is full
quiz.explain: Each subscription gets a full copy of the topic's messages, so multiple consumers can each process the entire stream independently.

quiz.q: How does Pub/Sub differ from Kafka/Kinesis operationally?
options: It's global and serverless with no partitions/shards to provision — it auto-scales (Pub/Sub Lite is the partitioned variant)
quiz.explain: Standard Pub/Sub is fully managed, global, and auto-scaling with no shard management; Pub/Sub Lite is the zonal partitioned option.

exercises.prompt: Design event ingestion where one stream of clicks must feed real-time enrichment, raw landing in BigQuery, and an alerting function — independently. How does Pub/Sub fit?
exercises.solution: Publish all click events to a single **`clicks` topic**, then create **three subscriptions** so each consumer gets its **own copy** of every event (fan-out): (1) a **pull subscription** consumed by a **Dataflow** streaming job for real-time **enrichment** → BigQuery; (2) a **BigQuery subscription** that lands **raw** events **directly** into a table (no code) for ad-hoc analysis; (3) a **push subscription** (or pull) to a **Cloud Function** for **alerting**. Because Pub/Sub **decouples** producers from consumers, the app just publishes to the topic and doesn't know about the consumers — you can **add/remove** consumers by creating/deleting subscriptions without touching the producer, and a **slow** consumer (e.g. the alerting function) **doesn't back up** the others (each subscription has its own backlog/ack state). Pub/Sub **auto-scales** through spikes with **no shards** to manage, and is **global**. This is the canonical GCP streaming-ingestion pattern: **one topic, multiple subscriptions** for independent fan-out, with downstream tools (Dataflow for complex, direct BQ subscription for simple) chosen per consumer.

exercises.prompt: When would you choose Pub/Sub Lite over standard Pub/Sub?
exercises.hint: Cost vs management for very high predictable throughput.
exercises.solution: Choose **Pub/Sub Lite** when you have **very high, predictable throughput** and want **lower cost**, and you're willing to **manage capacity**. The difference: **standard Pub/Sub** is **global, serverless, auto-scaling** (no capacity to provision) and the easiest default — you pay for usage and it handles scale/replication automatically. **Pub/Sub Lite** is **zonal** and **partitioned** (like Kafka), where you **pre-provision throughput/storage capacity** for a partition — this makes it **significantly cheaper per GB** at **high, steady** volumes, but you **manage** the capacity and accept **zonal** (less resilient) deployment and more operational responsibility. So: pick **standard Pub/Sub** for most cases (simplicity, global, auto-scale, variable load); pick **Pub/Sub Lite** when throughput is **large and predictable**, **cost** is the priority, and you can **manage partitions/capacity** and tolerate zonal scope. It's the trade of **operational simplicity/global (standard)** vs **lower cost at the expense of capacity management/zonal (Lite)** — analogous to choosing a fully-managed vs a capacity-provisioned streaming service.

lessons.title: Delivery: pull/push, ack, ordering & exactly-once
lessons.summary: Pub/Sub delivers at-least-once by default — subscribers ack within a deadline or messages are redelivered — with opt-in ordering keys, exactly-once delivery, dead-letter topics for poison messages, and retention/replay (seek) to reprocess.
lessons.concept:
Pub/Sub's delivery semantics are configurable; understanding **ack**, **ordering**, **exactly-once**, **dead-letter**, and **replay** is essential to building correct streaming consumers.

### Pull vs push
- **Pull** — the subscriber **requests** messages (StreamingPull/Pull) and controls the consumption **rate** (backpressure). Best for high throughput and most pipelines.
