- **Push** — Pub/Sub **delivers** messages to an **HTTPS endpoint** (e.g. Cloud Run/Functions). Simpler for low-volume, webhook-style consumers; rate is controlled by Pub/Sub flow control.

### Ack & redelivery (at-least-once)
Default delivery is **at-least-once**: a subscriber must **acknowledge (ack)** each message within the **ack deadline**; if it doesn't (crash, timeout, or **nack**), Pub/Sub **redelivers** it. So consumers can see **duplicates** and must be **idempotent** (or use exactly-once). You can **extend** the ack deadline for long processing.

### Ordering keys
By default, order isn't guaranteed. **Ordering keys** (opt-in) guarantee messages with the **same key** are delivered **in publish order** to a subscription — for per-entity ordering (e.g. all events for one `user_id`). (Trade some throughput for ordering.)

### Exactly-once delivery
**Exactly-once** (opt-in, per subscription) ensures an acked message is **not redelivered** and provides **dedup** within the subscription — so a correctly-acking consumer processes each message **once**. (Combine with idempotent sinks for end-to-end exactly-once; Dataflow integrates with this.)

### Dead-letter topics (DLQ)
A message that **repeatedly fails** (exceeds a max delivery attempts) can be routed to a **dead-letter topic** for inspection/replay — so a **poison message** doesn't block the subscription forever.

### Retention, replay & seek
Pub/Sub **retains** messages (default 7 days, configurable) and supports **seek**: rewind a subscription to a **timestamp** or a **snapshot** to **reprocess** (replay) — invaluable after a bug fix or for backfills.

### Why it matters
Default **at-least-once + ack** is the baseline; **ordering keys**, **exactly-once**, **dead-letter**, and **replay/seek** are the opt-in features that make consumers **correct and operable** under real conditions (duplicates, failures, ordering needs, reprocessing). Choosing them deliberately is core streaming-consumer design.
lessons.example: A payments consumer pulls from a subscription with **exactly-once** enabled and **ordering keys** on `account_id` (so an account's events apply in order). Each message is processed and **acked within the deadline**; a message that fails processing 5 times is routed to a **dead-letter topic** for a human to inspect (instead of blocking the subscription). After fixing a downstream bug, the team **seeks** the subscription back to a **timestamp** and **replays** the last hour to reprocess correctly. Retention (7 days) made the replay possible. The consumer is **idempotent** as a belt-and-suspenders measure. Each delivery feature was chosen to meet a real requirement: ordering, exactly-once, poison-message isolation, and reprocessing.
keypoints: Pull (subscriber-controlled rate, high throughput) vs push (Pub/Sub delivers to an HTTPS endpoint).
Default is at-least-once: ack each message within the deadline or it's redelivered — consumers must be idempotent.
Ordering keys (opt-in) guarantee in-order delivery for messages sharing a key (per-entity ordering).
Exactly-once delivery (opt-in, per subscription) prevents redelivery of acked messages and dedups within the subscription.
Dead-letter topics route repeatedly-failing (poison) messages aside after max attempts so they don't block the subscription.
Retention + seek (to a timestamp/snapshot) let you replay/reprocess messages — for backfills or after a bug fix.
lessons.diagram_caption: Default at-least-once with ack/redelivery; opt-in ordering keys, exactly-once, dead-letter topics, and retention/seek for replay — configurable delivery semantics.

quiz.q: What is Pub/Sub's default delivery guarantee, and what must consumers handle?
options: At-least-once: messages must be acked within a deadline or they're redelivered, so consumers can see duplicates and should be idempotent
quiz.explain: By default Pub/Sub is at-least-once with ack/redelivery, so consumers may get duplicates and should be idempotent (or enable exactly-once).

quiz.q: What problem does a dead-letter topic solve?
options: A repeatedly-failing 'poison' message is routed aside after max attempts so it doesn't block the subscription
quiz.explain: Dead-letter topics capture messages that exceed max delivery attempts for later inspection/replay, preventing a poison message from blocking progress.

exercises.prompt: Design a correct Pub/Sub consumer for payment events needing per-account ordering, no double-processing, poison-message handling, and the ability to reprocess after a bug.
exercises.hint: Ordering keys, exactly-once, DLQ, idempotency, seek/replay.
exercises.solution: Configure delivery deliberately: (1) **Ordering keys** on `account_id` so each account's events are delivered **in publish order** (per-entity ordering needed for correct balances). (2) **Exactly-once delivery** on the subscription so an **acked** message isn't redelivered and is **deduped** within the subscription — preventing double-processing; still make the consumer **idempotent** (key on message/transaction id) as defense in depth and for end-to-end safety with the sink. (3) **Ack discipline** — process then **ack within the deadline**; **extend** the deadline for long processing; **nack**/let it redeliver on transient failure. (4) **Dead-letter topic** with a max-attempts threshold so a **poison message** (always fails) is routed aside for inspection instead of **blocking** the subscription. (5) **Retention + seek** — ensure retention covers your recovery window (e.g. 7 days) so that, after fixing a downstream bug, you can **seek** the subscription to a **timestamp/snapshot** and **replay** the affected period to reprocess correctly. Together: **ordering keys** (order), **exactly-once + idempotency** (no double-apply), **DLQ** (poison isolation), and **retention/seek** (reprocessing) make the consumer correct and operable under real-world duplicates, failures, and bugs — exactly what a payments stream requires.

exercises.prompt: A downstream bug corrupted an hour of processed data. How can Pub/Sub help you recover, and what's the prerequisite?
exercises.hint: Seek to a timestamp / snapshot; retention.
exercises.solution: Use Pub/Sub **seek** to **replay** the affected messages. After fixing the bug, **seek the subscription back to a timestamp** (the start of the bad hour) — or to a **snapshot** you'd taken — which causes Pub/Sub to **redeliver** the messages from that point so your (now-fixed) consumer **reprocesses** them correctly. The **prerequisite** is **retention**: the messages must still be **retained** (default **7 days**, configurable) — seek can only go back as far as retention holds the messages, so your retention window must **cover** the recovery period. Best practice: set retention to comfortably exceed your likely recovery needs, and consider taking **snapshots** before risky changes so you have a known good seek point. Also ensure reprocessing is **idempotent** (so replaying doesn't double-apply to already-correct records) or that you reprocess into a clean target/partition. Combined with **exactly-once** and idempotent sinks, seek-based replay lets you **recover from downstream bugs** by reprocessing the source stream rather than trying to surgically fix corrupted outputs — provided retention preserved the data. The lesson: **retention enables replay**; size it for recovery, and use **seek** to reprocess.

lessons.title: Streaming ingestion patterns
lessons.summary: Pub/Sub is the front door for streaming on GCP: events flow into a topic, then either a Dataflow pipeline (for transforms/windowing) or a direct BigQuery subscription (for simple ingest) lands them for real-time analytics.
lessons.concept:
Pub/Sub rarely stands alone — it's the **ingestion entry point** that feeds processing and analytics. The common GCP streaming patterns:

### Pattern 1: Pub/Sub → Dataflow → BigQuery
The **workhorse** for **non-trivial** streaming: events → **Pub/Sub** → a **Dataflow** (Beam) pipeline that does **windowing, aggregation, enrichment, dedup, stateful** logic → **BigQuery** (or other sinks). Use when you need **event-time** processing or transformations SQL can't express.

### Pattern 2: Pub/Sub → BigQuery subscription (direct)
For **simple ingestion** (land raw events for analysis), a **BigQuery subscription** writes messages **straight into a BigQuery table** — **no Dataflow, no code**. Cheapest/simplest when you just need the data in BigQuery and any transformation can be done later in SQL.

### Pattern 3: Pub/Sub → Cloud Storage subscription
A **Cloud Storage subscription** writes batches of messages to **GCS** (e.g. Avro/text) — for archival or batch processing of the stream.

### Pattern 4: Pub/Sub → Cloud Functions/Run
**Event-driven** processing: a **push** subscription triggers a **Cloud Function/Run** service per message (or batch) for lightweight transforms, routing, or calling other services.

### Pattern 5: ingestion fan-out
Combine: one topic with **multiple subscriptions** — a Dataflow subscription for the real-time pipeline **and** a BigQuery subscription for raw landing **and** a function for alerts — each independent (fan-out lesson).

### Choosing
- **Complex/event-time/stateful** → **Dataflow**.
- **Simple land-in-BigQuery** → **BigQuery subscription** (no code).
- **Archive to lake** → **Cloud Storage subscription**.
- **Lightweight per-event action** → **Cloud Functions/Run** (push).

### Why it matters
Pub/Sub is **where streaming data enters GCP**; the right downstream pattern — **Dataflow** for complex processing vs a **direct BigQuery subscription** for simple ingest — keeps pipelines **simple and cost-effective**. Don't write a Dataflow job when a BigQuery subscription suffices; don't force complex logic into a subscription.
lessons.example: A product collects clickstream events. They publish to a **`clicks` topic**, then use **two patterns** via fan-out: a **Dataflow** subscription runs the **real-time** pipeline (1-minute windowed aggregations, sessionization) → BigQuery for live dashboards; and a **BigQuery subscription** lands the **raw** events directly (no code) for ad-hoc/forensic SQL. A third **push** subscription triggers a **Cloud Function** to fire alerts on specific events. As the product grows, adding a new consumer is just another subscription. They deliberately used the **direct BigQuery subscription** for raw landing (no Dataflow needed) and reserved **Dataflow** for the complex windowed processing — matching each path to its need.
keypoints: Pub/Sub is the streaming entry point; it feeds processing/analytics rather than standing alone.
Pub/Sub → Dataflow → BigQuery: the workhorse for windowing/aggregation/enrichment/stateful streaming (complex).
Pub/Sub → BigQuery subscription: direct, no-code landing of events into BigQuery for simple ingestion.
Pub/Sub → Cloud Storage subscription: batch messages to GCS for archive/batch processing.
Pub/Sub → Cloud Functions/Run (push): lightweight per-event processing/routing.
Choose by need: Dataflow for complex/event-time; direct BQ subscription for simple ingest — don't over-build.
lessons.diagram_caption: Events → Pub/Sub → either Dataflow (for transforms/windowing) or a direct BigQuery subscription (simple ingest) → real-time analytics; fan out as needed.

quiz.q: What is the workhorse pattern for non-trivial streaming on GCP?
options: Pub/Sub → Dataflow → BigQuery — Dataflow does windowing/aggregation/enrichment/stateful logic before landing in BigQuery
quiz.explain: For complex/event-time streaming, events flow Pub/Sub → Dataflow (Beam processing) → BigQuery; simple ingest can skip Dataflow.

quiz.q: When can you skip Dataflow and use a direct BigQuery subscription?
options: For simple ingestion — landing raw events straight into BigQuery (no transformation needed up front; do it later in SQL)
quiz.explain: A BigQuery subscription writes messages directly into a table with no code — ideal when you just need the data landed and can transform later in SQL.

exercises.prompt: Choose downstream patterns for: (a) raw events landed for ad-hoc SQL, (b) 1-minute windowed real-time aggregations, (c) archive the stream to the lake, (d) fire an alert per matching event.
exercises.hint: BQ subscription, Dataflow, GCS subscription, Cloud Function.
exercises.solution: **(a) Raw events for ad-hoc SQL → Pub/Sub BigQuery subscription (direct).** No transformation needed up front, so land messages **straight into BigQuery** with the **no-code** BigQuery subscription; transform later in SQL. Skip Dataflow — it'd be unnecessary cost/complexity. **(b) 1-minute windowed real-time aggregations → Pub/Sub → Dataflow → BigQuery.** This needs **event-time windowing** and aggregation, which is **Dataflow's** sweet spot (Beam windows/triggers); the pipeline writes results to BigQuery for live dashboards. **(c) Archive the stream to the lake → Pub/Sub Cloud Storage subscription.** Use the **Cloud Storage subscription** to write **batches** of messages to **GCS** (e.g. Avro) for cheap archival/batch reprocessing. **(d) Alert per matching event → Pub/Sub → Cloud Functions/Run (push).** A **push** subscription triggers a lightweight **Cloud Function** per event to evaluate the condition and fire the alert. Implement all four via **fan-out** (one topic, four subscriptions), each independent. The theme: **match each consumer to the right pattern** — direct BQ subscription for simple landing, Dataflow for complex windowed processing, GCS subscription for archive, Cloud Function for per-event actions — rather than forcing everything through one heavy pipeline.

exercises.prompt: A team builds a Dataflow job just to copy Pub/Sub messages into BigQuery unchanged. Is there a simpler option?
exercises.solution: Yes — use a **Pub/Sub BigQuery subscription** instead of a custom Dataflow job. If the requirement is to **land messages into BigQuery unchanged** (no transformation, windowing, or enrichment), a **BigQuery subscription** writes the messages **directly** into a BigQuery table **with no code and no pipeline to operate** — far simpler and cheaper than running and maintaining a Dataflow job (which spins up workers, costs more, and is more to monitor). The custom Dataflow job is **over-engineering** for a straight copy. Switch to the **BigQuery subscription** (configure the topic, target table, and schema handling); do any needed **transformations later in SQL** within BigQuery (cheap, no pipeline). **Reserve Dataflow** for when you actually need **event-time windowing, aggregation, enrichment, dedup, or stateful** processing that the direct subscription can't do. So the simpler option is the **direct BigQuery subscription** — and the general principle is to **not default to Dataflow**: use the **simplest managed path** (direct subscription) for simple ingest, and bring in Dataflow only for genuinely complex streaming.

lessons.title: GCS: classes, lifecycle, security & events
lessons.summary: Cloud Storage is GCP's object store and data-lake foundation: one global namespace with strong consistency, storage classes by access frequency (Standard→Nearline→Coldline→Archive), lifecycle/Autoclass automation, IAM-based security, versioning/retention, and event notifications to Pub/Sub.
lessons.concept:
**Google Cloud Storage (GCS)** is GCP's **object storage** — the **data-lake foundation** that BigQuery, Dataflow, and Dataproc all read. It's the GCP analog of S3, with the same core concepts (the S3-in-depth lessons map directly), plus GCP specifics.

### Buckets & objects
Data is **objects** (with a key/name) in **buckets** (globally-unique names). 'Folders' are **prefixes** (no real directories) — so **prefix layout = your partition scheme** (organize as raw/clean/curated zones with `key=value` date prefixes, just like S3). GCS has **strong consistency** (read-after-write globally) and a **single global namespace**.

### Storage classes (by access frequency)
| Class | Access | Min duration |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard** | Hot / frequent | none |
| **Nearline** | ~Monthly | 30 days |
| **Coldline** | ~Quarterly | 90 days |
| **Archive** | Rarely (DR/compliance) | 365 days |
Colder = cheaper storage, higher retrieval cost/min-duration. Pick by **access frequency** (same idea as S3 tiers).

### Lifecycle & Autoclass
- **Lifecycle rules** — automatically **transition** objects to colder classes or **delete** them after N days (and manage versions). Automates cost control.
- **Autoclass** — GCS **automatically moves objects between classes** based on access patterns (no rules, no retrieval-cost surprises) — the GCS analog of S3 Intelligent-Tiering.

### Security
- **IAM** (recommended) at project/bucket/(object) level; **uniform bucket-level access** (disable per-object ACLs) for simpler, consistent permissions.
- **Encryption** at rest by default (Google-managed, or **CMEK** via Cloud KMS, or customer-supplied); **TLS** in transit.
- **VPC Service Controls** to wall off data exfiltration; **signed URLs** for temporary access.
- **Public Access Prevention** (keep buckets private).

### Versioning, retention & holds
- **Object versioning** — keep prior versions (recover from overwrite/delete).
- **Retention policy / bucket lock** — enforce immutability for **compliance** (WORM); **holds** prevent deletion.

### Events & integration
- **Object change notifications → Pub/Sub** (and **Cloud Functions/Eventarc**) so a file landing **triggers** processing (event-driven ingestion) — like S3 events.
- **Performance** — scales massively; **composite uploads/parallel** for large objects; **request rate** auto-scales (avoid hotspotting a single prefix at extreme rates).

### Why it matters
GCS is the **lake foundation** on GCP: organize it well (zones/prefixes, like S3), match **storage classes** to access (lifecycle/Autoclass for cost), secure it (IAM/uniform access/CMEK/Public Access Prevention), and wire **events → Pub/Sub** for reactive pipelines. The object-store fundamentals from the S3 lessons apply directly; this maps them to GCP.
lessons.example: A lake on GCS uses **raw/clean/curated** prefixes with date partitions (`clean/orders/year=2025/month=05/`), stored as **Standard** Parquet for recent data. A **lifecycle rule** transitions raw objects to **Nearline at 30 days**, **Coldline at 90**, and **deletes** at 365 (raw is reproducible); curated marts use **Autoclass**. Security: **uniform bucket-level access** with **IAM**, **CMEK** encryption for sensitive buckets, and **Public Access Prevention** on. **Versioning** guards against accidental overwrites, and a **retention lock** enforces WORM on a compliance bucket. When a file lands in `raw/`, an **object notification → Pub/Sub** triggers a **Cloud Function** that kicks off a Dataflow/BigQuery load — **event-driven** ingestion. BigQuery queries the curated data via external/BigLake tables. The S3 object-store patterns, mapped to GCP.
keypoints: GCS is GCP's object store / lake foundation (analog of S3): buckets + objects, prefixes as the partition scheme, strong consistency, global namespace.
Storage classes by access frequency: Standard (hot) → Nearline (~monthly) → Coldline (~quarterly) → Archive (rare); colder = cheaper storage, higher retrieval/min-duration.
Lifecycle rules transition/delete objects automatically; Autoclass auto-moves objects by access pattern (like S3 Intelligent-Tiering).
Security: IAM + uniform bucket-level access, default + CMEK encryption, Public Access Prevention, VPC Service Controls, signed URLs.
Versioning recovers overwrites/deletes; retention policy / bucket lock enforces WORM immutability for compliance.
Object change notifications → Pub/Sub (Cloud Functions/Eventarc) enable event-driven ingestion when files land.
lessons.diagram_caption: GCS is the lake foundation: storage classes by access frequency, lifecycle/Autoclass automation, IAM/uniform security, versioning/retention, and events → Pub/Sub.

quiz.q: How do you choose a GCS storage class?
options: By access frequency: Standard (hot) → Nearline (~monthly) → Coldline (~quarterly) → Archive (rare); colder is cheaper storage but higher retrieval/min-duration
quiz.explain: Match the class to how often data is accessed; lifecycle rules or Autoclass automate moving data to cheaper classes as it cools.

quiz.q: How does GCS enable event-driven ingestion?
options: Object change notifications publish to Pub/Sub (or trigger Cloud Functions/Eventarc) so a file landing kicks off processing
quiz.explain: GCS object notifications to Pub/Sub/Cloud Functions let a new file automatically trigger a pipeline — like S3 events.

exercises.prompt: Design a cost-optimized, secure, event-driven GCS lake layout (zones, classes/lifecycle, security, events).
exercises.solution: **Layout:** organize buckets/prefixes into **raw / clean / curated** zones with **date-partitioned prefixes** (`clean/<table>/year=/month=/day=/`), storing **columnar Parquet** in clean/curated (so BigQuery/Dataflow/Dataproc query efficiently) — the same zone+prefix pattern as S3. **Cost (classes + lifecycle):** keep **recent/hot** data **Standard**; add **lifecycle rules** to transition **raw** (reproducible) to **Nearline (30d) → Coldline (90d)** and **delete** at your retention limit; use **Autoclass** on buckets with **unpredictable** access so GCS auto-moves objects (no retrieval-cost surprises). **Security:** enable **uniform bucket-level access** + **IAM** (no per-object ACLs), **Public Access Prevention**, default encryption with **CMEK (Cloud KMS)** for sensitive buckets, **VPC Service Controls** to prevent exfiltration, and **signed URLs** for temporary external access; enable **versioning** (recover overwrites) and a **retention/bucket lock** on compliance buckets (WORM). **Events:** configure **object change notifications → Pub/Sub** on `raw/` so a landing file triggers a **Cloud Function** (or Eventarc) that starts a **Dataflow/BigQuery load** — **event-driven** ingestion with no polling. **Integration:** expose curated data to BigQuery via **external/BigLake** tables where appropriate. This gives a **cost-optimized** (classes/lifecycle/Autoclass), **secure** (IAM/CMEK/PAP/VPC-SC), **reactive** (events→Pub/Sub) lake — the GCP realization of the S3 best practices.

exercises.prompt: How do the GCS object-store fundamentals relate to what you learned about S3, and what GCP specifics differ?
exercises.hint: Same concepts; GCP names/features (Autoclass, uniform access, strong consistency, Pub/Sub events).
exercises.solution: The **fundamentals are the same** — GCS, like S3, is a **flat object store** (buckets + objects; **prefixes**, not real folders, are your **partition scheme**), so the **lake layout** (raw/clean/curated zones, date-partitioned prefixes, columnar Parquet, file sizing) and the **principles** (storage classes by access frequency, lifecycle automation, IAM-based security, encryption, event-driven ingestion) **map directly** from the S3-in-depth lessons. **GCP specifics that differ:** (1) **Strong global consistency** (read-after-write everywhere) and a **single global namespace** (bucket names are globally unique) — vs S3's eventual-consistency history. (2) **Storage classes** named **Standard/Nearline/Coldline/Archive** (vs S3 Standard/IA/Glacier tiers), with **Autoclass** as the auto-tiering (analog of S3 Intelligent-Tiering). (3) **Security** via **IAM + uniform bucket-level access** (recommended over ACLs), **Public Access Prevention**, **VPC Service Controls**, and **CMEK** (Cloud KMS) — analogous to S3 IAM/bucket policies/BPA/SSE-KMS. (4) **Events** go to **Pub/Sub** / **Cloud Functions / Eventarc** (vs S3 → SNS/SQS/Lambda/EventBridge). (5) Integration with **BigQuery (external/BigLake)**, **Dataflow**, **Dataproc**. So: **learn the object-store concepts once** (from S3), and apply them on GCP with the **GCS names/features** — the deep S3 lessons largely transfer, and this lesson maps them to GCP.

lessons.title: Dataproc: managed Spark/Hadoop
lessons.summary: Dataproc runs managed Spark/Hadoop on GCP for lift-and-shift of existing OSS code, using ephemeral clusters (spin up, run, delete) with preemptible VMs and GCS storage for low cost — or Dataproc Serverless; choose it over Dataflow when you have existing Spark/skills.
lessons.concept:
**Dataproc** is GCP's managed **Spark/Hadoop** (and Presto, Hive, Flink, etc.) service — the way to run the **open-source big-data ecosystem** on GCP, especially for **lift-and-shift** of existing Spark/Hadoop workloads. Three forms:

### Ephemeral clusters (the GCP-idiomatic pattern)
Unlike traditional always-on Hadoop, the GCP pattern is **ephemeral, job-scoped clusters**: **spin up a cluster, run the job, delete it** (workflow templates / the API automate this). Benefits:
- **No idle clusters** — pay only for the job's duration.
- **Preemptible (Spot) VMs** for **secondary/worker** nodes → big cost savings for fault-tolerant Spark (lost nodes just re-run tasks).
- **Store data in GCS (not HDFS)** so storage is **durable and decoupled** from the cluster — the cluster is disposable, the data persists.
This 'cluster per job, data in GCS' model is far cheaper and simpler than long-running Hadoop.

### Dataproc Serverless
**Submit a Spark job with no cluster to create or size** — Dataproc Serverless **auto-provisions and scales** the runtime, then tears down. The **simplest** way to run Spark on GCP without cluster management — comparable convenience to Dataflow but for **Spark** code.

### Dataproc on GKE
Run **Spark on your Kubernetes (GKE)** cluster — **share k8s capacity** and standardize on containers, for **Kubernetes-centric** orgs.

### Dataproc vs Dataflow (the key decision)
- **Dataproc** — you have **existing Spark/Hadoop** code, libraries, or skills, or need a specific OSS framework. Lift-and-shift with minimal change.
- **Dataflow** — **new** pipelines, **streaming/event-time**, want **serverless Beam** and autoscaling without managing clusters/jobs.
Both read/write **GCS and BigQuery**.

### Cost & ops
- **Ephemeral + preemptible + GCS** is the cost trifecta.
- **Autoscaling**, **workflow templates** (parameterized job pipelines), and **initialization actions** (install libs) operationalize it.
- Output to **GCS/BigQuery**; integrate with the catalog/Dataplex.

### Why it matters
Dataproc is the **migration and OSS-flexibility** path on GCP: run your **existing Spark/Hadoop** cheaply via **ephemeral, preemptible, GCS-backed** clusters (or serverless), reserving **Dataflow** for new serverless/streaming Beam pipelines. Knowing **when each fits** is core GCP judgment.
lessons.example: A team migrating an on-prem **Spark** pipeline to GCP uses **Dataproc**: their Spark code runs **almost unchanged**. Instead of an always-on cluster, they use **ephemeral clusters** via a **workflow template** — spin up, run the nightly job, delete — with **secondary workers on preemptible VMs** (the job is fault-tolerant, so Spark re-runs tasks lost to preemption) and data in **GCS** (durable, decoupled). Cost is a fraction of a long-running Hadoop cluster. A lighter ad-hoc Spark job runs on **Dataproc Serverless** (no cluster). For a **new streaming** pipeline they'd choose **Dataflow** instead. Lift-and-shift on Dataproc, new serverless/streaming on Dataflow — each matched to its workload.
keypoints: Dataproc is managed Spark/Hadoop (and Hive/Presto/Flink) on GCP — for lift-and-shift of existing OSS big-data workloads.
GCP-idiomatic pattern: ephemeral, job-scoped clusters (spin up → run → delete) — no idle clusters, pay per job.
Use preemptible (Spot) VMs for secondary workers and store data in GCS (not HDFS) for cheap, durable, decoupled processing.
Dataproc Serverless submits Spark with no cluster to manage (auto-scales); Dataproc on GKE runs Spark on Kubernetes.
Choose Dataproc for existing Spark/Hadoop code/skills; choose Dataflow for new serverless/streaming Beam pipelines.
Operationalize with workflow templates, autoscaling, and initialization actions; read/write GCS and BigQuery.
lessons.diagram_caption: Dataproc runs Spark/Hadoop in ephemeral (preemptible + GCS), serverless, or GKE forms — for lift-and-shift; Dataflow is for new serverless/streaming Beam.

quiz.q: What is the GCP-idiomatic way to run Dataproc cost-effectively?
options: Ephemeral, job-scoped clusters (spin up, run, delete) with preemptible secondary workers and data in GCS
quiz.explain: Ephemeral clusters + preemptible VMs + GCS storage avoid idle cost and decouple durable data from disposable compute.

quiz.q: When would you choose Dataproc over Dataflow?
options: When you have existing Spark/Hadoop code, libraries, or skills to lift-and-shift, or need a specific OSS framework
quiz.explain: Dataproc suits existing OSS/Spark workloads and skills; Dataflow suits new serverless/streaming Beam pipelines.

exercises.prompt: A company is migrating an existing on-prem Spark/Hadoop pipeline to GCP cost-effectively. Recommend a Dataproc setup and contrast with Dataflow.
exercises.hint: Ephemeral clusters, preemptible, GCS; vs rewriting to Beam.
exercises.solution: Use **Dataproc** for **lift-and-shift** — the existing **Spark/Hadoop** code runs with **minimal changes**, avoiding a costly rewrite. Cost-effective setup: (1) **Ephemeral, job-scoped clusters** via **workflow templates** — spin up for the job, run, **delete** — so there are **no idle clusters** (pay only per run). (2) **Preemptible (Spot) VMs** for **secondary/worker** nodes (keep the primary on standard) — large savings since the job is **fault-tolerant** (Spark re-runs tasks lost to preemption); diversify to reduce simultaneous loss. (3) Store data in **GCS** (not HDFS) so storage is **durable and decoupled** from the disposable cluster, and output to GCS/BigQuery. (4) Use **initialization actions** to install needed libraries, **autoscaling** to right-size, and consider **Dataproc Serverless** for jobs where you don't even want to manage a cluster. **Contrast with Dataflow:** choosing **Dataflow** would require **rewriting** the Spark logic into **Beam** — unnecessary effort for an existing, working Spark pipeline. **Dataflow** is the better choice for **new** pipelines, especially **streaming/event-time**, where serverless Beam and autoscaling shine. So: **existing Spark/Hadoop → Dataproc** (ephemeral + preemptible + GCS, cheap lift-and-shift); **new streaming/serverless → Dataflow**. This migrates the workload quickly and cheaply while reserving Dataflow for where it adds value.

exercises.prompt: Why store data in GCS rather than HDFS on a Dataproc cluster, and what does that enable?
exercises.hint: Decouple durable storage from disposable compute.
exercises.solution: Storing data in **GCS** instead of **HDFS** **decouples durable storage from the (disposable) compute cluster** — which is the foundation of the cost-effective Dataproc pattern. With HDFS, data lives **on the cluster's disks**, so the cluster must stay **alive** to keep the data, forcing **always-on** clusters (idle cost) and making resizing/migration painful. With **GCS** (via the Cloud Storage connector), the data persists **independently** in object storage, so you can run **ephemeral, job-scoped clusters** — spin up, read from GCS, process, write back to GCS, **delete the cluster** — paying only for the job and never for idle storage-bearing clusters. It also enables: **multiple/other engines** (BigQuery external tables, Dataflow, other Dataproc clusters) to read the **same** GCS data; **durability** (GCS is highly durable/replicated, unlike ephemeral HDFS); easy **scaling** (compute and storage scale separately); and **preemptible** workers without data-loss risk (no HDFS data on them). So GCS-as-storage enables the **ephemeral + preemptible + decoupled** model that makes Dataproc cheap and flexible, and lets the lake be **shared** across engines. The principle mirrors BigQuery's separation of storage/compute and the cloud-lakehouse pattern generally: **keep durable data in object storage, make compute disposable.**

lessons.title: Cloud Composer: managed Airflow
lessons.summary: Cloud Composer is managed Apache Airflow on GCP — AWS-of-GCP's MWAA equivalent — for complex, cross-system orchestration via Python DAGs; Cloud Workflows is the lighter serverless option for simple service orchestration.
lessons.concept:
**Cloud Composer** is GCP's **managed Apache Airflow**: Google runs the Airflow **environment** (scheduler, workers, web UI, metadata DB — on GKE), and **you write Python DAGs**. It's GCP's primary **pipeline orchestrator** for complex workflows.

### The Airflow model
A **DAG** in Python defines **tasks** (operators/sensors) with **dependencies**; Airflow **schedules** runs, manages **retries/backfills/SLAs**, and provides a rich **UI**. Strengths: **complex/dynamic DAGs**, **backfills**, **sensors** (wait for conditions), and a **huge operator ecosystem** for GCP **and** external systems.

### Orchestrating GCP
Composer DAGs use **Google Cloud operators** to run **BigQuery** queries, **Dataflow**/**Dataproc** jobs, **GCS** operations, **Dataform** runs, and to coordinate **external** systems (on-prem, other clouds, SaaS). Airflow becomes the **control plane** for the data platform.

### When to use Composer
- You want **Apache Airflow** specifically — **complex/dynamic DAGs**, backfills, rich scheduling.
- **Cross-system** orchestration (GCP + external).
- **Portability** / existing Airflow DAGs and skills.

### Cloud Workflows (the lighter alternative)
**Cloud Workflows** is a **serverless** orchestration service for **sequencing/branching calls to services and APIs** (YAML/JSON), with no environment to run. Lighter and cheaper than Composer for **simpler, service-to-service** orchestration. Also: **Workflows + Cloud Scheduler + EventArc** for event/time-driven flows.

### Choosing
- **Complex/cross-system Airflow DAGs, backfills, ecosystem** → **Composer**.
- **Simple serverless service orchestration** → **Cloud Workflows** (or scheduled queries / Dataform's own scheduling for pure SQL).
(Same trade-off as AWS MWAA vs Step Functions.)

### Why it matters
Composer gives **managed Airflow** for the **complex orchestration** GCP data platforms need (coordinating BigQuery/Dataflow/Dataproc/Dataform and external systems), while **Cloud Workflows** handles **lighter** serverless orchestration — matching the orchestrator to the job, like Step Functions vs MWAA on AWS.
lessons.example: A data platform's nightly pipeline is a **Composer (Airflow) DAG**: a **sensor** waits for source files in GCS → a **Dataproc** job cleans them → a **BigQuery** operator loads/transforms → a **Dataform** run builds the SQL models → a **Dataflow** export job ships results, with **retries**, **SLAs**, and **alerting** — and it also coordinates an **on-prem** step via an operator. Composer fits because it's a **complex, cross-system** DAG needing Airflow's ecosystem and backfills. A separate, **simple** 'call service A then B on a schedule' flow is built with **Cloud Workflows** (serverless, lighter) instead. Each orchestrator matched its workload.
keypoints: Cloud Composer is managed Apache Airflow on GCP (Google runs scheduler/workers/UI); you write Python DAGs.
Airflow excels at complex/dynamic DAGs, dependencies, backfills, sensors, and a huge operator ecosystem.
Composer DAGs orchestrate BigQuery/Dataflow/Dataproc/Dataform/GCS and external systems via Google Cloud operators.
Use Composer for complex/cross-system orchestration, backfills, portability, and existing Airflow skills.
Cloud Workflows is the lighter, serverless option for simple service/API orchestration (no environment to run).
Choose Composer (Airflow) for complex DAGs; Workflows for light serverless orchestration — like MWAA vs Step Functions on AWS.
lessons.diagram_caption: Cloud Composer runs managed Airflow — you write Python DAGs orchestrating BigQuery/Dataflow/Dataproc/GCS and external systems; Cloud Workflows is the lighter option.

quiz.q: What is Cloud Composer?
options: Managed Apache Airflow on GCP — Google runs the Airflow environment while you write Python DAGs to orchestrate pipelines
quiz.explain: Composer is GCP's managed Airflow for orchestrating complex, cross-system data pipelines via Python DAGs.

quiz.q: When would you use Cloud Workflows instead of Composer?
options: For complex dynamic DAGs with backfills
For simpler, serverless service/API orchestration with no Airflow environment to run
quiz.explain: Cloud Workflows is a lighter, serverless orchestrator for sequencing service calls; Composer (Airflow) is for complex DAGs.

exercises.prompt: Design orchestration for a nightly GCP pipeline (wait for files → Dataproc clean → BigQuery load/transform → Dataform models → export), including an on-prem step, with retries and alerting. Composer or Workflows?
exercises.solution: Use **Cloud Composer (managed Airflow)** — this is a **complex, multi-step, cross-system** DAG that benefits from Airflow's strengths. Build a **DAG**: (1) a **GCS sensor** waits for the source files to land; (2) a **Dataproc** operator runs the cleaning Spark job (ephemeral cluster); (3) a **BigQuery** operator loads/transforms; (4) a **Dataform** operator triggers the SQL model build (dependency-ordered, tested); (5) a **Dataflow**/BigQuery-export operator ships results; plus an **operator** for the **on-prem** step (cross-system) — all wired with **dependencies**. Add **retries** (per task, with backoff), **SLAs**, and **failure alerting** (email/Slack), and rely on Airflow's **backfill** and rich **UI** for reruns and observability. **Composer over Workflows** because: it's a **complex DAG** with sensors, branching, backfills, and a **cross-system** (on-prem) step needing Airflow's **operator ecosystem** and portability — Cloud **Workflows** is lighter/serverless and better for **simple service-to-service** sequencing, not a rich multi-system DAG like this. (If parts were trivial 'call A then B on a schedule', Workflows could handle those, and the two can coexist.) This mirrors the AWS MWAA-vs-Step-Functions decision: **complex/cross-system → managed Airflow (Composer)**; **simple serverless → Workflows**.

exercises.prompt: When is Cloud Workflows (not Composer) the better choice, and why?
exercises.solution: **Cloud Workflows** is better when the orchestration is **simple service/API sequencing** and you want a **serverless, low-cost** option with **no environment to run**. Composer runs a **managed Airflow environment** (scheduler/workers/web server on GKE) **continuously**, which is **heavier and costlier** and **overkill** for basic flows. Choose **Workflows** when you need to **call a few services/APIs in sequence or with simple branching** (e.g. trigger a Cloud Function, then a BigQuery job, then send a notification), possibly on a **schedule** (with **Cloud Scheduler**) or in response to an **event** (with **Eventarc**) — it's **serverless** (pay per execution, scales to zero), defined in YAML/JSON, with built-in error handling, and **nothing to operate**. You'd **not** use Workflows for **complex/dynamic DAGs**, **backfills**, rich **sensors**, or a large **operator ecosystem** across many systems — those are Airflow/Composer's domain. So: **simple, serverless, service-to-service orchestration → Cloud Workflows** (lighter, cheaper, no environment); **complex, cross-system, backfill-heavy DAGs → Composer (Airflow)**. Matching the orchestrator's weight to the workload avoids paying for and operating Airflow you don't need — the same principle as Step Functions vs MWAA on AWS.

lessons.title: Dataform: SQL ELT in BigQuery
lessons.summary: Dataform brings dbt-style software engineering to BigQuery SQL: define SQLX models with ref()-based dependencies, incremental builds, assertions (tests), and Git/CI-CD, and Dataform compiles a dependency DAG and runs the SQL in BigQuery — serverless ELT, no data movement.
lessons.concept:
**Dataform** is GCP's managed service for **SQL-based ELT in BigQuery** — essentially **dbt-style** development, built in. It adds **software engineering** (dependencies, testing, version control) to BigQuery SQL transformations.

### The model
You write **SQLX** files — a `SELECT` plus config — for each **model** (a table/view to build). Instead of hardcoding table names, you reference other models with **`ref()`**, so Dataform **infers the dependency DAG** and builds models **in the right order**.
```sql
-- definitions/silver_orders.sqlx
config { type: "incremental" }
SELECT * FROM ${ref("bronze_orders")} WHERE _loaded > (SELECT MAX(_loaded) FROM ${self()})
```

### Key features (dbt-style)
- **Dependency DAG** via `ref()` — correct build order, no manual sequencing.
- **Incremental models** — only process new data each run (not full rebuilds).
- **Assertions (tests)** — declarative data-quality checks (uniqueness, not-null, custom) that fail the run on violation.
- **Version control (Git)** — models live in **Git**; review changes like code.
- **CI/CD & scheduling** — schedule runs (or trigger from Composer), with environments/dev-prod.
- **Reusable JS/macros** for DRY SQL.

### Serverless, in-place
Dataform **compiles** the SQLX into BigQuery SQL and **runs it in BigQuery** — **no separate engine, no data movement** (the data's already in BigQuery). It's the **BigQuery-native** way to build a tested, dependency-ordered, version-controlled **transformation layer**.

### Dataform vs Dataflow/Dataproc
- **Dataform** — **SQL-expressible** transformations on **BigQuery** data (cleaning, joining, aggregating, modeling). The **default** for ELT in a BigQuery-centric stack.
- **Dataflow/Dataproc** — for what SQL **can't** express (streaming/event-time, complex/custom processing, existing Spark).

### Why it matters
Dataform is the **BigQuery-centric ELT** tool: it makes SQL transformations **engineered** (DAG, incremental, tested, Git/CI-CD) and runs them **serverlessly in BigQuery** with no data movement — simpler and cheaper than a pipeline engine for the (large) class of work that's **SQL on BigQuery**. (It's GCP's built-in answer to dbt.)
lessons.example: A team builds their **BigQuery transformation layer** in **Dataform**: SQLX models for bronze → silver → gold, wired by **`ref()`** so Dataform builds them in **dependency order**; **incremental** models process only new data; **assertions** verify `order_id` is unique and not-null (failing the run if violated); everything lives in **Git** with **CI/CD** and dev/prod **environments**, scheduled (or triggered by **Composer**). It all **compiles to SQL and runs in BigQuery** — **no pipeline engine, no data movement**. For a **streaming** enrichment SQL can't express, they use **Dataflow** instead. Dataform handles the (majority) SQL-expressible ELT as **engineered, tested** SQL; Dataflow handles the rest.
keypoints: Dataform is GCP's managed dbt-style SQL ELT for BigQuery — software engineering for SQL transformations.
Write SQLX models; reference others with ref() so Dataform infers the dependency DAG and builds in order.
Features: incremental models, assertions (data tests), Git version control, CI/CD, dev/prod environments, reusable macros.
Compiles to BigQuery SQL and runs in BigQuery — serverless, no separate engine, no data movement.
Use Dataform for SQL-expressible ELT on BigQuery data (the default); Dataflow/Dataproc for what SQL can't express.
It's GCP's built-in answer to dbt — tested, dependency-ordered, version-controlled BigQuery transformations.
lessons.diagram_caption: Dataform compiles SQLX models (ref()-based DAG, incremental, assertions, Git) and runs the SQL in BigQuery — engineered, serverless ELT with no data movement.

quiz.q: What does Dataform bring to BigQuery SQL?
options: dbt-style software engineering: ref()-based dependency DAG, incremental models, assertions (tests), Git/CI-CD — compiled and run in BigQuery
quiz.explain: Dataform adds dependencies, testing, and version control to BigQuery SQL ELT, running serverlessly in BigQuery with no data movement.

quiz.q: When do you use Dataform vs Dataflow?
options: Dataform for streaming, Dataflow for SQL
Dataform for SQL-expressible ELT on BigQuery data (default); Dataflow for what SQL can't express (streaming/complex/custom)
quiz.explain: Dataform handles SQL transformations in BigQuery; Dataflow handles streaming/event-time and complex processing SQL can't do.

exercises.prompt: Build a tested, dependency-ordered BigQuery transformation layer (bronze→silver→gold) with incremental processing and CI/CD. How does Dataform do it?
exercises.hint: SQLX + ref() DAG + incremental + assertions + Git.
exercises.solution: Use **Dataform**. Define each table/view as a **SQLX model** (a `SELECT` + config) — `bronze_*`, `silver_*`, `gold_*`. Wire dependencies with **`ref()`** (e.g. `silver_orders` selects from `${ref("bronze_orders")}`) so Dataform **infers the dependency DAG** and builds models in the **correct order** automatically — no manual sequencing. Mark appropriate models **`type: "incremental"`** so each run processes **only new data** (e.g. rows newer than the current max), avoiding full rebuilds (cheaper/faster). Add **assertions** (declarative tests) — uniqueness/not-null on keys, custom row conditions — that **fail the run** on violation, so bad data is caught as a **gate**. Keep all SQLX in **Git** for review, and set up **CI/CD** with **dev/prod environments** (validate/compile on PR, deploy on merge) plus **scheduled** runs (or trigger from **Composer**). Everything **compiles to BigQuery SQL and runs in BigQuery** — **serverless, no data movement, no pipeline engine**. The result is an **engineered** transformation layer: dependency-ordered (`ref()`), incremental, **tested** (assertions), and **version-controlled** (Git/CI-CD) — dbt-style rigor, native to BigQuery. This is the default, simplest way to build the SQL ELT layer on a BigQuery-centric GCP stack.

exercises.prompt: Why is Dataform (SQL ELT) often preferable to a Dataflow pipeline for transforming BigQuery data?
exercises.hint: Simpler/cheaper; no engine/data movement; SQL skills.
exercises.solution: For transformations that are **SQL-expressible on data already in BigQuery**, **Dataform** is usually **simpler, cheaper, and faster to ship** than a **Dataflow** pipeline. Reasons: (1) **No separate engine / no data movement** — Dataform **compiles to SQL and runs in BigQuery**, where the data already lives; a Dataflow job would **read out, process in workers, write back**, adding cost, latency, and infrastructure. (2) **Less to operate** — no pipeline workers to run/monitor; Dataform is serverless and uses BigQuery's compute. (3) **SQL skills** — analysts/engineers write **SQL** (not Beam/Java/Python), so more of the team can build/maintain it. (4) **Engineered SQL** — Dataform adds the **dependency DAG, incremental models, assertions/tests, and Git/CI-CD** that make SQL ELT robust (the dbt model). (5) **Cost** — running SQL in BigQuery (on-demand or slots) for set-based transforms is typically cheaper than spinning up Dataflow workers for the same logic. **Reserve Dataflow** for what SQL **can't** express: **streaming/event-time** processing, complex **stateful** logic, custom/ML transforms, or non-BigQuery sources. So the rule: **SQL-expressible on BigQuery → Dataform** (simpler/cheaper/engineered); **streaming/complex/custom → Dataflow**. Defaulting SQL ELT to Dataform (not Dataflow) keeps the BigQuery-centric platform lean — the same 'don't over-build with a pipeline engine' principle as choosing a direct BigQuery subscription over a copy-only Dataflow job.

lessons.title: Dataplex: lakehouse governance
lessons.summary: Dataplex unifies governance across GCS and BigQuery: organize data into logical lakes/zones, auto-catalog and discover metadata with lineage, enforce data-quality rules, and centralize security/policy — governing the whole lakehouse from one control plane.
lessons.concept:
As data spreads across **GCS** and **BigQuery** (and projects), you need **unified governance**. **Dataplex** is GCP's **data governance / data-fabric** service — a **control plane** to **organize, discover, secure, and ensure the quality** of data across the lakehouse.

### Organize: lakes & zones
**Dataplex** lets you group distributed data (GCS buckets, BigQuery datasets, across projects) into logical **lakes** and **zones** (e.g. raw vs curated), giving a **domain-oriented** logical structure over physically scattered data — a foundation for a **data mesh**.

### Discover & catalog
- **Automatic cataloging** — Dataplex **discovers** data in registered assets and harvests **metadata** (schemas, tables) into a **catalog**, making data **searchable**.
- **Data Catalog** (now part of Dataplex) — search/discover data assets, with **tags** and business metadata.
- **Lineage** — track how data flows/derives across BigQuery/Dataflow/etc.

### Data quality
**Dataplex data quality** — define **rules** and **profiling** (completeness, uniqueness, validity) that run against data and produce **quality scores/metrics** — so quality is **measured and governed**, not assumed. (AutoDQ / data profiling.)

### Security & policy
Centralize **access policies** and governance across the lake — manage permissions on **lakes/zones/assets**, integrate with IAM, and apply consistent policy (with BigQuery's column/row security and BigLake's fine-grained controls).

### Why it matters
Dataplex provides **central governance** over a **distributed lakehouse**: one place for **discovery** (catalog/search), **lineage**, **data quality**, and **security/policy** spanning **GCS + BigQuery** — so a growing, multi-project data estate stays **findable, trustworthy, and governed**. It's GCP's answer to the governance sprawl that comes with scale (analogous to Lake Formation/Unity Catalog/Purview in spirit).
lessons.example: A company's data spans many **BigQuery datasets** and **GCS buckets** across projects. They use **Dataplex** to define **lakes** (per domain) with **raw/curated zones**, **auto-catalog** all assets so analysts can **search/discover** data and see **lineage**, attach **data-quality rules** (e.g. `order_id` unique, `amount ≥ 0`) that produce **quality scores** surfaced to data owners, and centralize **access policy** across the estate. When a new BigQuery dataset is added to a lake, it's **auto-discovered, cataloged, and governed** by the zone's policies. Instead of governance sprawl across projects, there's **one control plane** for discovery, lineage, quality, and security over the whole **GCS + BigQuery** lakehouse.
keypoints: Dataplex is GCP's unified governance/data-fabric control plane across GCS and BigQuery (and projects).
Organize scattered data into logical lakes and zones (e.g. raw/curated) — domain-oriented, a data-mesh foundation.
Automatic cataloging + discovery (Data Catalog) makes data searchable, with tags and business metadata, plus lineage.
Data quality rules and profiling produce quality scores/metrics — quality is measured and governed, not assumed.
Centralize access policy and security across lakes/zones/assets, integrating with IAM and fine-grained controls.
One control plane for discovery, lineage, quality, and security over the whole lakehouse — governance at scale.
lessons.diagram_caption: Dataplex unifies discovery/catalog, lineage, data quality, and security across GCS + BigQuery — organize into lakes/zones and govern the whole lakehouse centrally.

quiz.q: What does Dataplex provide?
options: Unified governance across GCS and BigQuery — organize into lakes/zones with cataloging/discovery, lineage, data quality, and centralized security
quiz.explain: Dataplex is a governance control plane: organization (lakes/zones), discovery/catalog, lineage, data quality, and security across the lakehouse.

quiz.q: What problem does Dataplex solve as a data estate grows?
options: Governance sprawl — it centralizes discovery, lineage, quality, and security over distributed GCS + BigQuery data, keeping it findable and trustworthy
quiz.explain: As data scatters across projects, Dataplex unifies catalog/discovery, lineage, quality, and policy so the lakehouse stays governed at scale.

exercises.prompt: A company's data is scattered across many BigQuery datasets and GCS buckets in multiple projects, hard to find and govern. How does Dataplex help?
exercises.hint: Lakes/zones, catalog/discovery, lineage, quality, central policy.
exercises.solution: Use **Dataplex** as the **central governance control plane** over the distributed estate. (1) **Organize** — define logical **lakes** (per domain) with **zones** (raw/curated) and **register** the scattered **GCS buckets** and **BigQuery datasets** (across projects) into them, giving a **domain-oriented** structure over physically scattered data (a **data-mesh** foundation). (2) **Discover/catalog** — Dataplex **auto-catalogs** the registered assets (harvesting schemas/metadata) and provides **search/discovery** (Data Catalog) with **tags/business metadata**, so analysts can **find** data instead of hunting across projects, and see **lineage** of how it's derived. (3) **Data quality** — attach **rules/profiling** (completeness, uniqueness, validity) that run and produce **quality scores/metrics** surfaced to owners, so trust is **measured**. (4) **Security/policy** — **centralize access policy** across lakes/zones/assets (integrating IAM and BigQuery/BigLake fine-grained controls), applied consistently as new assets join a zone. The payoff: instead of **governance sprawl** (every project/team governing ad hoc, data hard to find or trust), there's **one control plane** for **discovery, lineage, quality, and security** spanning **GCS + BigQuery** — the estate stays **findable, trustworthy, and governed as it scales**. New datasets added to a lake are **auto-discovered, cataloged, and governed** by the zone's policies, so governance scales with the data.

exercises.prompt: How does Dataplex relate to BigQuery/BigLake security and to the governance tools on other clouds?
exercises.hint: Layered governance; analog of Lake Formation/Unity Catalog/Purview.
exercises.solution: **Within GCP**, Dataplex **complements** BigQuery and BigLake security rather than replacing them: **BigQuery** provides **column/row-level security**, authorized views, and policy tags; **BigLake** extends **fine-grained governance** to open-format data on GCS/other clouds; and **Dataplex** sits **above** as the **unifying control plane** — organizing data into **lakes/zones**, providing **catalog/discovery**, **lineage**, **data quality**, and **centralized policy** across **GCS + BigQuery** and projects. So you get **layered** governance: fine-grained access at the **table/column/row** level (BigQuery/BigLake) plus **estate-wide** discovery, quality, lineage, and organization (Dataplex). **Across clouds**, Dataplex is GCP's answer to the same governance need served by **AWS Lake Formation** (central lake permissions, fine-grained access, sharing), **Databricks Unity Catalog** (catalog, lineage, fine-grained security, governance across the lakehouse), and **Microsoft Purview** (catalog/governance) — all addressing **governance at scale** over a distributed lakehouse: **discovery/catalog, lineage, quality, and centralized security**. The shared theme across clouds: as data spreads, you need a **central governance layer** that makes it **findable, trustworthy, and consistently secured** — Dataplex is GCP's, working **with** BigQuery/BigLake's finer-grained controls.

tracks.title: DataOps & Infrastructure
tracks.blurb: Ship pipelines like software: containers, version control, CI/CD, and monitoring.

lessons.title: Git — version control fundamentals
lessons.summary: Track every change to your code: the staging model, commits, branches, and the core command workflow.
lessons.concept:
**Git** is the version-control system that underpins all modern data work. It records the **full history** of your code, lets you **branch** to develop changes in isolation, and makes every change **reviewable and reversible**. Rule of thumb: *if it isn't in Git, it isn't reproducible* — so pipeline code, **dbt models**, SQL, configs, and **Terraform** all live there (data itself does not).

**The three areas.** A file moves through: the **working directory** (your edits) → the **staging area / index** (changes marked for the next commit) → the **repository** (committed history). This staging step is Git's signature — you choose exactly what goes into each commit.

**The core workflow:**
```bash
git init                 # start a repo  (or: git clone <url>)
git status               # what's changed / staged
git add file.py          # stage a file  (git add -A = everything)
git commit -m "message"  # record staged changes to history
git log --oneline        # view history
git diff                 # unstaged changes;  git diff --staged for staged
```

**Branching** lets you build a change without touching `main`:
```bash
git switch -c feature/clean-orders   # create + switch to a branch
# ...edit, add, commit...
git switch main; git merge feature/clean-orders   # bring it back
git branch -d feature/clean-orders
```

**Remotes** sync with a shared server (GitHub/GitLab):
```bash
git remote add origin <url>
git push -u origin main      # upload commits
git pull                     # fetch + merge others' commits
git fetch                    # download without merging
```

**Undoing things** (knowing these removes the fear):
```bash
git restore file.py          # discard working-dir edits
git restore --staged file.py # unstage
git revert <hash>            # new commit that undoes a commit (safe on shared history)
git reset --hard <hash>      # move branch back (rewrites history — local only!)
git stash                    # shelve edits; git stash pop to restore
```

**`.gitignore`** keeps junk and secrets out (`__pycache__/`, `.env`, `*.parquet`, `data/`). Commit **small and often** with clear messages — that history is what makes data work auditable. **Go deeper** has the full command reference and recovery recipes.
lessons.example:
# a typical change, start to finish
git switch -c feature/add-tax-column      # branch off main
vim transforms/orders.sql                 # make the change
git add transforms/orders.sql
git commit -m "Add tax column to orders model"
git push -u origin feature/add-tax-column # share it (open a PR next)

# oops, committed a secret? undo the last commit but keep the edits:
git reset --soft HEAD~1
echo '.env' >> .gitignore && git add .gitignore
keypoints: Git records full history and makes changes reviewable and reversible — version EVERYTHING as code.
Files flow: working directory → staging area (git add) → repository (git commit).
Core loop: status → add → commit → log/diff; commit small and often with clear messages.
Branch to develop in isolation (git switch -c), then merge back into main.
Sync with remotes: push (upload), pull (fetch+merge), fetch (download only).
Undo safely: restore (discard), revert (undo a commit), reset (move branch, local only); .gitignore excludes junk/secrets.

quiz.q: What does `git add` do?
options: Stages a change for the next commit (working dir → staging area)
quiz.explain: git add moves changes into the staging area; git commit then records the staged changes into history.

quiz.q: You need to undo a commit that's already pushed/shared. Which is safe?
options: git revert <hash> (a new commit that undoes it)
quiz.explain: revert creates a new commit that reverses the change, preserving shared history; reset --hard rewrites history and is unsafe on shared branches.

quiz.q: Why work on a branch instead of committing straight to main?
options: To develop a change in isolation, review it, and merge only when ready — keeping main stable
quiz.explain: Branches isolate work-in-progress so main stays releasable; the change is reviewed via a PR before merging.

exercises.prompt: Stage all changes and commit them with a message.
exercises.solution:
git add -A
git commit -m "Describe the change"

exercises.prompt: Create a feature branch, make a commit, and push it to the remote.
exercises.solution:
git switch -c feature/x
git add -A && git commit -m "..."
git push -u origin feature/x

exercises.prompt: You accidentally staged a secrets file. Unstage it and ignore it.
exercises.solution:
git restore --staged .env
echo '.env' >> .gitignore
git add .gitignore && git commit -m "Ignore .env"

exercises.prompt: Discard all uncommitted edits to a file you messed up.
exercises.solution: git restore path/to/file.py   # working-dir edits gone, last commit restored

lessons.diagram_caption: Working directory → (git add) staging → (git commit) local repo → (git push) remote

lessons.title: GitHub — collaboration, pull requests & Actions
lessons.summary: Collaborate through pull requests and code review, pick a branching strategy, and automate with GitHub Actions.
lessons.concept:
**GitHub** (and GitLab/Bitbucket) hosts your Git repositories in the cloud and adds the **collaboration** layer: pull requests, code review, issues, and CI/CD automation. It's where data teams actually work together.

**Getting a repo:**
```bash
git clone https://github.com/org/repo.git   # your copy of a repo you can push to
# fork on GitHub -> clone your fork (for contributing to repos you can't push to)
```

**The GitHub flow** is the standard collaboration loop:
1. **Branch** off `main` (`git switch -c feature/x`).
2. Commit and **push** the branch.
3. Open a **Pull Request (PR)** — proposes merging your branch into `main`.
4. **Review:** teammates comment, request changes, and approve; CI runs automatically.
5. **Merge** once approved and CI is green; delete the branch.

This means **every change is peer-reviewed and tested before it hits `main`** — the core of safe data engineering.

**Branching strategies:**
- **GitHub Flow / trunk-based** — short-lived feature branches merged to `main` frequently; `main` is always deployable. The modern default.
- **GitFlow** — long-lived `develop`/`release` branches; heavier, for versioned releases.

**Merge conflicts** happen when two branches edit the same lines. Git marks them (`<<<<<<<`, `=======`, `>>>>>>>`); you edit to the desired result, then `git add` and commit. Keeping branches short and rebasing on `main` minimizes them.

**GitHub Actions** automates CI/CD from a YAML file in `.github/workflows/`:
```yaml
name: ci
on: [pull_request]
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      - run: pytest && dbt test    # tests must pass to merge
```
**Protected branches** enforce that PRs pass review + CI before merge. Plus **Issues** and **Projects** track work. **Go deeper** for the full PR + Actions workflow.
lessons.example:
# contribute a change via a pull request
git switch -c fix/null-amounts
# ...edit, commit...
git push -u origin fix/null-amounts
# -> open a PR on GitHub: title, description, reviewers
# -> CI (GitHub Actions) runs pytest + dbt test on the PR
# -> teammate approves -> Merge -> branch deleted

# resolve a conflict after main moved on
git switch fix/null-amounts
git fetch origin && git rebase origin/main   # replay your commits on latest main
# fix conflict markers, then:
git add . && git rebase --continue && git push --force-with-lease
keypoints: GitHub hosts repos and adds collaboration: pull requests, code review, issues, and CI/CD.
GitHub flow: branch → push → open PR → review + CI → merge → delete branch.
Every change is peer-reviewed AND auto-tested before reaching main — the safety core of DataOps.
Prefer short-lived feature branches / trunk-based (main always deployable) over heavy GitFlow.
Merge conflicts: edit the marked lines, git add, commit; short branches + rebase reduce them.
GitHub Actions (.github/workflows/*.yml) runs CI/CD; protected branches enforce review+green CI.

quiz.q: What is a pull request?
options: A proposal to merge a branch into another, with review and CI before it's accepted
quiz.explain: A PR proposes merging your branch; teammates review and CI runs, so changes are vetted before merging to main.

quiz.q: Which automates running tests on every PR in GitHub?
quiz.explain: GitHub Actions runs CI/CD jobs (e.g. pytest, dbt test) triggered by events like pull_request.

quiz.q: What causes a merge conflict?
options: Two branches changing the same lines, so Git can't auto-merge
quiz.explain: When both branches edit the same lines, Git marks the conflict for you to resolve manually, then add+commit.

exercises.prompt: Outline the GitHub flow steps to ship a change.
exercises.solution: 1) git switch -c feature/x  2) commit + push  3) open a PR  4) review + CI pass  5) merge to main, delete branch

exercises.prompt: Write a minimal GitHub Actions workflow that runs pytest on every PR.
exercises.solution:
name: ci
on: [pull_request]
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      - run: pytest

exercises.prompt: Why enable protected branches on main?
exercises.solution: They require PRs to pass code review and green CI before merging, so nothing untested or unreviewed reaches main.

exercises.prompt: Your branch conflicts with main. What's a clean way to update it?
exercises.solution: git fetch origin && git rebase origin/main, resolve conflict markers, git add, git rebase --continue, then push --force-with-lease.

lessons.diagram_caption: GitHub flow: branch → push → Pull Request → review + CI → merge to main

lessons.title: CI/CD for data pipelines
lessons.summary: Automatically test every change and deploy merged changes safely — the assembly line for data code.
lessons.concept:
**CI/CD** automates the path from a code change to production. **CI (Continuous Integration)** tests every proposed change; **CD (Continuous Delivery/Deployment)** ships merged changes in a controlled, repeatable way. For data teams this is what makes a transformation change *safe* instead of *scary*.

**CI — on every pull request,** an automated pipeline:
- **Lints** and compiles the code (ruff/black, `dbt parse`).
- Runs **unit tests** (pytest on transform functions).
- Runs **data tests** (`dbt test`, Great Expectations) against a sample or staging.
- Builds artifacts/images.
A change **can't merge unless CI passes** — so broken SQL or a bad transform is caught *before* production.

**CD — on merge to `main`,** the change is deployed automatically: `dbt run` on prod, the Airflow/Dagster DAG is updated, infra changes are applied (`terraform apply`), a container image is published. No manual copy-paste.

**Environments** stage the risk: **dev → staging → prod**, each with its own data and config (from env vars/secrets). Changes prove themselves in staging before prod.

**Safe deploy patterns:** **idempotent** jobs (re-runnable), **blue-green** (deploy alongside, switch over), **canary** (roll out to a slice first), and one-command **rollback** (revert the PR, redeploy the previous version). 

**A data CI/CD pipeline** in GitHub Actions might: on PR → `dbt build --select state:modified+` against a CI schema + `pytest`; on merge → `dbt run` + `dbt test` on prod, then refresh the dashboards. The result: data work becomes **reproducible, reviewed, tested, and boring to deploy** — exactly what you want. **Go deeper** for full pipeline examples.
lessons.example:
# .github/workflows/data-ci.yml  (sketch)
on:
  pull_request: { branches: [main] }
jobs:
  ci:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      - run: ruff check . && pytest          # lint + unit tests
      - run: dbt deps && dbt build --target ci # build + DATA tests on a CI schema
# on merge to main, a deploy workflow runs: dbt run --target prod, then dbt test
keypoints: CI tests every change on PR; CD deploys merged changes repeatably — the assembly line for data code.
CI steps: lint/compile, unit tests (pytest), data tests (dbt test/Great Expectations), build.
A change can't merge unless CI passes — broken transforms are caught before production.
CD auto-deploys on merge: dbt run on prod, DAG updates, terraform apply, image publish.
Promote through dev → staging → prod environments, each with its own data/config.
Deploy safely with idempotent jobs, blue-green/canary rollout, and one-command rollback.

quiz.q: What must happen before a change can merge in a CI-gated repo?
options: Automated CI (lint + unit + data tests) passes on the PR
quiz.explain: CI runs on the PR and the change can only merge if all checks pass, catching breakage before production.

quiz.q: In data CI/CD, what does 'data tests' usually mean?
options: Assertions on the data itself — dbt test / Great Expectations (not-null, unique, ranges)
quiz.explain: Beyond code unit tests, data tests validate the transformed data (uniqueness, nulls, accepted values) before publishing.

quiz.q: Why deploy through dev → staging → prod environments?
options: To prove a change on staging data/config before it touches production
quiz.explain: Staging mirrors prod so changes are validated safely before the production deploy, reducing risk.

exercises.prompt: Name the CI checks you'd run on a dbt + Python repo's PR.
exercises.solution: Lint (ruff/black), unit tests (pytest), data tests (dbt build/test on a CI schema), and a compile/parse step.

exercises.prompt: What runs on merge to main (CD), versus on the PR (CI)?
exercises.solution: CI (on PR): lint + unit + data tests, no deploy. CD (on merge): deploy — dbt run/test on prod, update DAGs, terraform apply.

exercises.prompt: Give one safe-deploy pattern and what it protects against.
exercises.solution: Blue-green: deploy the new version alongside the old and switch traffic over, so a bad deploy is reverted instantly by switching back — zero-downtime, fast rollback.

exercises.prompt: Why must deploy/load jobs be idempotent in CD?
exercises.solution: Automated deploys and retries may run a job more than once; idempotent jobs produce the same result without duplicating data or side effects.

lessons.diagram_caption: CI (test every PR) → merge → CD (deploy to dev → staging → prod, safely)

lessons.title: Containers (Docker) & reproducibility
lessons.summary: Packaging code with its dependencies into portable, reproducible containers — solving 'works on my machine' and making data jobs run identically everywhere.
lessons.concept:
A recurring pain in data work: a pipeline runs on your laptop but **breaks in production** because of different library versions, OS, or configs ('works on my machine'). **Containers** solve this.

### What a container is
A **container** (Docker) packages your code **with all its dependencies** — libraries, runtime, system tools, configs — into a single **portable, isolated** unit defined by a **Dockerfile** (a recipe) and built into an **image**. Run the image anywhere — laptop, CI, prod, any cloud — and it behaves **identically**, because it carries its whole environment.

```dockerfile
FROM python:3.11-slim
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install -r requirements.txt
COPY . /app
CMD ["python", "pipeline.py"]
```

### Why it matters for data engineering
- **Reproducibility** — the exact same environment in dev, CI, and prod → no version-mismatch surprises; results are reproducible.
- **Portability** — run the same job on any machine/cloud/orchestrator.
- **Isolation** — each job has its own dependencies; two pipelines needing different library versions don't conflict.
- **Efficient** — lighter than VMs (share the host kernel), fast startup, dense packing.

### Containers vs VMs
A **VM** virtualizes a whole machine (its own OS — heavy); a **container** shares the host OS kernel and isolates just the app (lightweight, seconds to start). For deployable data jobs, containers are the modern unit.

### In practice
Pipelines run as containers (Airflow KubernetesExecutor runs each task in a container; Spark on K8s; CI runs in containers). You **build an image in CI**, store it in a **registry**, and run it in any environment — the foundation for Kubernetes (next) and reproducible deployments. **Reproducibility via containers is a DataOps cornerstone.**
keypoints: Containers (Docker) package code + all dependencies into a portable, isolated unit (Dockerfile → image).
Run the image anywhere (laptop/CI/prod/any cloud) and it behaves identically — solves 'works on my machine'.
Benefits: reproducibility (same env everywhere), portability, isolation (no dependency conflicts), efficiency.
Containers share the host kernel (lightweight, fast) vs VMs that virtualize a whole OS (heavy).
Data jobs run as containers (Airflow K8s executor, Spark on K8s, CI); build image in CI, store in a registry.
Reproducibility via containers is a DataOps cornerstone and the foundation for Kubernetes orchestration.

quiz.q: What problem do containers primarily solve for data pipelines?
options: 'Works on my machine' — they package code with all dependencies so it runs identically everywhere (reproducibility)
quiz.explain: A container carries its whole environment (libraries, runtime, config), so a job behaves the same in dev, CI, and prod — eliminating version-mismatch breakage.

quiz.q: How do containers differ from VMs?
options: Containers share the host OS kernel and isolate just the app (lightweight, fast); VMs virtualize a whole OS (heavy)
quiz.explain: Containers are lightweight (share the kernel, start in seconds, pack densely); VMs run a full guest OS, making them heavier — containers are the modern unit for deployable jobs.

quiz.q: What defines a container's environment?
options: A Dockerfile (recipe) built into an image carrying code + dependencies
quiz.explain: A Dockerfile specifies the base image, dependencies, and command; it's built into an image that runs identically anywhere.

exercises.prompt: A pipeline works locally but fails in production with a library error. How do containers prevent this class of bug?
exercises.solution: The failure is an **environment mismatch** — production has different library/OS/runtime versions than your laptop. A **container packages the code together with its exact dependencies** (pinned library versions, runtime, system tools) into an **image**. You build that image **once** and run **the same image** in dev, CI, and prod, so the environment is **identical everywhere** — there's no 'prod has a different pandas version' because prod runs the same packaged environment you tested. This **reproducibility** eliminates the whole class of 'works on my machine' / version-mismatch bugs: if it runs in the container in CI, it runs the same in production.

exercises.prompt: Why are containers better than VMs for running many short data jobs?
exercises.solution: Containers **share the host OS kernel** and isolate only the application, so they're **lightweight** (MBs, not GBs), **start in seconds** (no booting a full OS), and **pack densely** on a host. For **many short jobs** (e.g. per-task pipeline steps, CI runs), this means you can **spin up and tear down** containers quickly and run **many in parallel** efficiently — low overhead per job. **VMs** virtualize a **whole machine with its own OS**, so each is **heavy (GBs, slow boot)** and resource-hungry; running many short jobs as VMs wastes time and resources on OS overhead. Containers give the isolation/reproducibility you need with a fraction of the cost — ideal for ephemeral, parallel data tasks (and why Kubernetes/Spark-on-K8s use them).

exercises.prompt: Sketch the lifecycle: from a Dockerfile to a running pipeline task in production.
exercises.hint: Dockerfile → build image → registry → run anywhere.
exercises.solution: (1) Write a **Dockerfile** specifying the base image, dependencies (`pip install`), and the command to run the pipeline. (2) **CI builds** it into an **image** (and runs tests inside it). (3) The image is **pushed to a container registry** (Docker Hub, ECR/GCR/ACR) with a version tag. (4) The orchestrator/runtime (**Kubernetes**, Airflow KubernetesExecutor, ECS, Spark-on-K8s) **pulls the image** and **runs it as a container** wherever the task is scheduled — in production, identical to how it ran in CI. So the image is built once, versioned, and run anywhere — reproducible, portable, and isolated. Updates are a new image version, enabling clean rollbacks.

exercises.prompt: How do containers enable dependency isolation between two pipelines?
exercises.solution: Each container carries its **own self-contained set of dependencies** (its image), isolated from the host and from other containers. So **Pipeline A** needing `pandas 1.5` and **Pipeline B** needing `pandas 2.1` (or different Python versions, system libraries, etc.) can each run in **their own container with their own versions** — **no conflict**, because neither shares a global environment. Without containers, both would draw on the **same machine's installed libraries**, where one version requirement breaks the other (dependency hell). Containerizing each job means its environment is **encapsulated and independent**, so teams can evolve pipelines' dependencies separately and run incompatible requirements side by side on the same infrastructure.

lessons.diagram_caption: Containers package code + dependencies for portable, reproducible, isolated runs — between VMs and serverless.

lessons.title: Data testing in CI/CD
lessons.summary: Building automated test gates into the pipeline: unit tests for logic, data tests/assertions, schema and data-diff checks, run in CI so bad changes never reach production.
lessons.concept:
Testing is how you **trust** a data pipeline. DataOps wires tests into **CI/CD** so every change is **automatically validated** before it ships — the 'shift left' principle (catch problems early, not in the prod dashboard).

### Test the code AND the data
- **Unit tests** — does the **transformation logic** work? Given known inputs, the function/model returns the expected output. Fast, deterministic, run on every PR.
- **Data tests / assertions** — is the **actual data** correct? `not_null`, `unique`, `accepted_values`, `relationships` (referential integrity), range/volume, freshness. Tools: **dbt tests**, **great_expectations**, **Soda**.

Perfect code can still emit garbage from bad input, so you need **both**.

### Schema & data-diff tests
- **Schema tests** catch **upstream drift** (columns added/removed/retyped) before it breaks downstream.
- **Data diff** compares a **changed** pipeline's output against current production (or a snapshot) to catch **unintended changes** — invaluable when refactoring (e.g. it flags an accidental revenue doubling before merge).

### In the CI/CD pipeline
On each PR, CI **runs unit + data tests** (and lint/compile) against a **staging** environment on realistic data; failing tests **block the merge**. On merge, CD deploys, and post-deploy **monitoring/observability** catches runtime data issues. So testing (pre-deploy) and monitoring (runtime) together give end-to-end confidence.

### Quarantine, not just fail
For data tests at runtime, you can **quarantine** bad records (route to a dead-letter table) instead of failing the whole load — process the good data, isolate the bad for inspection. The goal: **never let untested changes or bad data silently reach consumers**.
keypoints: Test the code (unit tests of transform logic) AND the data (assertions on actual values) — both matter.
Data tests: not_null, unique, accepted_values, relationships, range/volume, freshness (dbt/great_expectations/Soda).
Schema tests catch upstream drift; data diffs compare changed output vs production to catch unintended changes.
