Tools: **MLflow**, **Evidently**/**WhyLabs** (drift), plus your orchestrator. Models aren't shipped once — they're **operated**. Open **Go deeper** for the monitoring + retraining loop.

**Closing the loop in practice.** The hardest part is **labels**: you often learn the truth long after predicting (was that transaction really fraud?). Build the pipeline that joins **delayed labels** back to logged predictions, compute real performance, feed it to the drift/alerting system, and trigger retraining. Without that loop you're flying blind on a model that's quietly decaying.
lessons.example:
# log every prediction with its features for monitoring (a data pipeline)
log_prediction(request_id, features, score, model_version, ts)

# drift check: compare recent feature distribution to the training reference
from evidently.metrics import DataDriftTable
report = run_drift(reference=train_features, current=last_24h_features)
if report.share_drifted > 0.3:
    trigger_retrain()          # CT: retrain when drift crosses a threshold

# performance is measured later, when labels arrive (e.g. did the 'fraud' flag pan out?)
join_labels_when_available(predictions, ground_truth)
keypoints: Models decay as the world changes; MLOps keeps production models healthy (DevOps + DataOps for ML).
ML adds Continuous Training (CT) to CI/CD: retrain automatically on new data, schedule, or trigger.
Monitor data/feature drift (input distribution shifts) with tests like PSI/KS vs the training set.
Concept drift = the input→target relationship changes; the model is wrong even on normal-looking inputs.
Track prediction distribution now and accuracy later, once (often delayed) labels arrive.
DEs build monitoring pipelines (log predictions+features, join labels, compute drift) and retraining automation.

quiz.q: What is data (feature) drift?
options: The input distribution shifts away from what the model trained on, degrading it
quiz.explain: When incoming feature distributions diverge from training, predictions degrade — detected with distribution tests like PSI/KS.

quiz.q: Why is measuring model accuracy in production often delayed?
options: Ground-truth labels arrive later than predictions (you learn if a transaction was fraud days later)
quiz.explain: You can monitor predictions immediately but can only compute accuracy once true labels become available, which often lags.

quiz.q: What does Continuous Training (CT) add to CI/CD for ML?
options: Automatic retraining on new data / schedule / drift triggers to combat model decay
quiz.explain: CT closes the loop by retraining and redeploying models automatically as data changes, keeping them accurate over time.

exercises.prompt: Name two kinds of drift and what each means.
exercises.solution: Data/feature drift: the input distribution changes. Concept drift: the input→target relationship changes (the model is wrong even on normal inputs).

exercises.prompt: How would you detect feature drift in a pipeline?
exercises.solution: Compare the recent feature distribution to the training reference with a test (PSI/KS) and alert when divergence crosses a threshold.

exercises.prompt: Give three triggers for retraining a model.
exercises.solution: On a fixed schedule, when drift exceeds a threshold, or when measured performance drops below an SLA.

exercises.prompt: Why must you log predictions WITH their features in production?
exercises.solution: To compute drift, debug bad predictions, and later join ground-truth labels to measure real performance — the monitoring data pipeline.

lessons.diagram_caption: MLOps loop: monitor (drift + performance) → detect decay → retrain (CT) → validate → promote

lessons.title: Embeddings & vector databases
lessons.summary: Turn text/images into vectors, store them in a vector database, and power semantic search and RAG.
lessons.concept:
The modern AI layer runs on **embeddings** — and building the pipelines around them is where data engineering meets GenAI.

**Embeddings** turn text, images, or other data into **vectors** (lists of numbers) that capture **meaning**: similar items land near each other in vector space. A model produces them (sentence-transformers, OpenAI/Cohere embedding APIs). Unlike keyword matching, embeddings find things that are *semantically* similar ("car" near "automobile").

**Vector databases** (Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant, Milvus, **pgvector**, FAISS) store embeddings and do **similarity search** — given a query vector, return the nearest vectors fast. They use **Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN)** indexes (**HNSW**, IVF) because exact search over millions of high-dimensional vectors is too slow; ANN trades a little accuracy for huge speed.

**The DE owns the data pipeline around them:**
- **Ingest & chunk** — split documents into passages.
- **Embed** — run each chunk through the embedding model (batch + rate-limit).
- **Upsert** — write vectors + metadata to the vector DB and build the index.
- **Retrieve** — embed the query, search top-k, return results (often with metadata filters).
- **Keep fresh** — re-embed on change (an embedding pipeline is an ETL job).

**Use cases:** semantic **search**, **recommendations** (similar items/users), de-duplication, and **RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)** — the dominant LLM pattern: retrieve relevant chunks from the vector DB and feed them to an LLM as context, so it answers from *your* data. The DE builds the **retrieval pipeline** that makes RAG work. Open **Go deeper** for an embedding + RAG pipeline.

**Engineering the retrieval layer.** Good RAG is a data problem: **chunk size/overlap**, which **metadata** to store for filtering, the **embedding model** and its dimensions, the **ANN index** parameters (HNSW M/ef), and **hybrid search** (vector + keyword) all shape result quality. And it's an ETL job — documents change, so re-embedding and upserting on update keeps answers correct. This is squarely DE work, now central to every GenAI product.
lessons.example:
# embedding + RAG retrieval pipeline (the DE's part)
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
emb = SentenceTransformer('all-MiniLM-L6-v2')

# 1) index: chunk -> embed -> upsert to the vector DB
chunks = chunk_documents(docs)
vectors = emb.encode([c.text for c in chunks])
vector_db.upsert([(c.id, v, c.metadata) for c, v in zip(chunks, vectors)])

# 2) retrieve: embed query -> ANN search top-k -> feed to the LLM
qv = emb.encode(question)
context = vector_db.query(qv, top_k=5, filter={'lang': 'en'})
answer = llm(f"Answer using this context:\n{context}\n\nQ: {question}")
keypoints: Embeddings turn text/images into vectors that capture meaning — similar items are near in vector space.
Vector databases (Pinecone/Weaviate/pgvector/FAISS) store embeddings and do fast similarity search.
They use Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) indexes (HNSW/IVF) — a little accuracy for huge speed.
The DE builds the pipeline: ingest → chunk → embed → upsert/index → retrieve → keep fresh.
Use cases: semantic search, recommendations, de-duplication, and RAG for LLMs.
RAG retrieves relevant chunks from the vector DB and feeds them to an LLM so it answers from your data.

quiz.q: What does an embedding represent?
options: A vector capturing the meaning of text/data, so similar items are close in vector space
quiz.explain: Embeddings map data to vectors where semantic similarity = geometric closeness, enabling meaning-based search.

quiz.q: Why do vector databases use Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) search?
options: Exact search over millions of high-dim vectors is too slow; ANN trades slight accuracy for speed
quiz.explain: ANN indexes (HNSW/IVF) return near-exact nearest neighbors far faster than brute-force exact search at scale.

quiz.q: In RAG, what does the data engineer's pipeline provide to the LLM?
options: Relevant retrieved chunks (from the vector DB) as context so the LLM answers from your data
quiz.explain: RAG retrieves top-k relevant passages via vector search and supplies them as context to the LLM — the DE builds that retrieval pipeline.

exercises.prompt: Outline the steps to index documents for semantic search.
exercises.solution: Chunk documents into passages → embed each chunk with an embedding model → upsert vectors + metadata to the vector DB → build the ANN index.

exercises.prompt: How do you retrieve relevant context for a user question in RAG?
exercises.solution: Embed the question into a vector, run an ANN similarity search for the top-k nearest chunks (optionally with metadata filters), and pass them to the LLM as context.

exercises.prompt: Why is keyword search insufficient for 'find similar support tickets'?
exercises.solution: Keyword search misses synonyms/paraphrases; embeddings match on meaning, so semantically similar tickets are found even with different words.

exercises.prompt: What keeps a vector index correct as source data changes?
exercises.solution: An embedding ETL pipeline that re-embeds and upserts changed/new documents (and removes deleted ones) keeps the index fresh.

lessons.diagram_caption: Chunk → embed → vector DB (ANN index) → similarity search → RAG context for an LLM

lessons.title: ML for Data Engineers interview prep & cheat sheet
lessons.summary: Consolidated rapid-review: high-frequency answers and a mock interview.
lessons.concept:
Rapid-review for the ML-for-Data-Engineers track. The **Go deeper** guide has the full mock; here are the anchors.

### Foundations
- **ML types** → supervised (classification/regression, needs labels) · unsupervised (clustering) · reinforcement.
- **EDA** → profile before modeling: shape, missing, distributions, outliers, correlations, target balance.
- **Preprocessing** → impute · encode · scale; fit transformers on TRAIN only (leakage); trees need no scaling.
- **Algorithms** → tabular → gradient boosting (XGBoost); images/text → deep learning.
- **Evaluation** → accuracy lies on imbalance → precision/recall/F1/AUC; overfit = train high, val low.

### Platform & MLOps
- **Feature store** → one definition → offline + online → prevents train/serve skew.
- **Registry** → versioned models + stages → which model is live + rollback.
- **Drift** → data/concept drift → monitor + retrain; **RAG** → embed → vector DB → retrieve.
- **Five seams**: training↔serving (feature store), training↔deployment (registry), deploy↔reality (monitoring), reruns (idempotency), late labels (delayed-label join).

Every lesson in this track also has its own '💼 Interview questions' panel and a cheat-sheet button.
keypoints: Foundations a DE must know: ML types (supervised/unsupervised), EDA (data profiling), preprocessing, algorithms, evaluation metrics.
Prevent leakage: fit transformers on TRAIN only; accuracy lies on imbalanced data → precision/recall/F1.
Tabular → gradient boosting; images/text/huge → deep learning; trees need no scaling.
Platform: feature store prevents train/serve skew; registry tracks which model is live; monitor drift → retrain.
The five seams (feature store, registry, monitoring, idempotency, delayed-label join) make production ML reliable.

quiz.q: What does a feature store prevent?
options: Train/serve skew — by computing each feature once for both training and serving
quiz.explain: One feature definition materialized to offline (training) and online (serving) stores.

quiz.q: What is data leakage?
options: Information unavailable at prediction time leaking into training → great offline, fails in prod
quiz.explain: Prevent with point-in-time-correct features and time-based splits.

exercises.prompt: Do the 10-question ML mock interview (Go-deeper guide) out loud.
exercises.hint: DE role, leakage, feature store, point-in-time, registry, batch/online, drift, retraining, RAG, the five seams.
exercises.solution: Reread any shaky topic's lesson and retry until crisp.

tracks.title: Agentic AI for Data Engineers
tracks.blurb: Build with the 2026 agentic stack — MCP, A2A, LangGraph/CrewAI/Claude Agent SDK — and apply it to real data work: self-healing pipelines, text-to-SQL, and quality agents.

lessons.title: What is an agentic system?
lessons.summary: An agent = an LLM that reasons, calls tools, observes results, and loops until a goal is met — not a one-shot chatbot.
lessons.concept:
A plain LLM call is one-shot: prompt in, text out. An **agent** wraps the LLM in a **loop** so it can *act* in the world and react to what it learns:

**Reason → Act → Observe → (repeat).** The LLM reasons about the next step, **acts** by calling a **tool** (query a database, hit an API, run code), **observes** the result, and reasons again — until the goal is met. This is the **ReAct** pattern.

**Agent vs workflow vs chatbot:**
- **Chatbot** — answers from the prompt only; no actions.
- **Workflow** — fixed, pre-defined steps you wrote; deterministic but rigid.
- **Agent** — *decides* the steps at runtime using tools; flexible, handles open-ended tasks, but needs guardrails.

**Why data engineers care (2026):** agents can investigate a pipeline failure, write and validate SQL, enforce data contracts, and even repair issues — tasks that used to need a human in the loop. The shift is from *you write every step* to *you set the goal, tools, and guardrails, and the agent figures out the steps*.

**The catch:** autonomy cuts both ways. An agent can take a wrong action confidently, so you bound it with **tools it's allowed to use, validation, and human-in-the-loop** for risky steps (covered later). Open **Go deeper** for the loop in detail and a minimal example.
lessons.example:
# the agent loop in pseudo-code
state = {goal: "find why orders_fact is stale"}
while not done(state):
    thought = llm.reason(state)        # plan next step
    action  = thought.tool_call        # e.g. query_logs("orders_fact")
    result  = tools.run(action)         # ACT
    state   = observe(state, result)    # OBSERVE -> back to reason
return state.answer
keypoints: An agent = LLM + tools + memory run in a loop: reason → act → observe → repeat.
ReAct: the model alternates reasoning with tool actions until the goal is met.
Chatbot (no actions) vs workflow (fixed steps) vs agent (decides steps at runtime).
DE value: agents investigate failures, write/validate SQL, enforce contracts, repair pipelines.
You provide the goal, tools, and guardrails; the agent chooses the steps.
Autonomy needs bounds: allowed tools, validation, and human-in-the-loop for risky actions.

quiz.q: What distinguishes an agent from a plain LLM call?
options: It runs a loop that calls tools and reacts to results until a goal is met
quiz.explain: Agents act via tools and iterate (reason→act→observe), rather than returning a single one-shot answer.

quiz.q: The ReAct pattern alternates:
quiz.explain: ReAct interleaves reasoning steps with tool actions, feeding observations back into the next reasoning step.

quiz.q: Compared with a fixed workflow, an agent:
options: decides the steps at runtime (flexible) but needs guardrails
quiz.explain: Agents choose steps dynamically, which is powerful for open-ended tasks but requires bounds and validation.

exercises.prompt: Name the four words of the agent loop.
exercises.solution: Reason → Act → Observe → (repeat) until the goal is met.

exercises.prompt: Chatbot vs workflow vs agent — one line each.
exercises.solution: Chatbot: answers, no actions. Workflow: fixed steps you wrote. Agent: picks steps at runtime using tools.

exercises.prompt: Why does autonomy require guardrails?
exercises.solution: An agent can take an incorrect action with high confidence; allowed-tools, validation, and HITL bound the blast radius.

lessons.diagram_caption: Goal → reason → act (tool) → observe → repeat until done; tools and memory feed the loop.

lessons.title: Anatomy: tools, memory & planning
lessons.summary: The four parts of every agent — LLM reasoning core, tools (function calling), memory, and planning — and how they fit.
lessons.concept:
Every agent is built from four parts. Understand each and you can reason about any framework.

**1. LLM — the reasoning core.** Decides what to do next. Quality and prompting drive everything.

**2. Tools — how the agent acts.** Functions the LLM can invoke via **function calling**: each tool has a name, description, and a typed (JSON-schema) signature. The model emits a structured call (e.g. `query_db(sql=...)`), your runtime executes it, and returns the result. For DE, tools are things like `run_sql`, `read_table_schema`, `call_api`, `run_python`, `check_freshness`. **Good tool design = good agent**: clear names/descriptions, narrow scope, validated inputs.

**3. Memory.** Two kinds: **short-term** (the conversation/context window — recent steps and observations) and **long-term** (a **vector store** the agent can search to recall facts, past runs, or docs — the RAG pattern). Memory keeps the agent coherent across many steps.

**4. Planning.** Decomposing a goal into ordered steps. Simple agents plan implicitly each loop (ReAct); advanced ones make an explicit plan, then execute and **reflect** (self-critique) to recover from mistakes.

Put together: **agent = LLM + tools + memory + planning, run in a loop.** Frameworks (next module) differ mainly in how they structure these four — but the parts are always the same. Open **Go deeper** for tool-schema examples and memory patterns.
keypoints: Four parts: LLM (reasoning), tools (action), memory (recall), planning (decompose).
Tools = typed functions via function calling: name + description + JSON-schema args.
Good tool design (clear, narrow, validated) is the biggest lever on agent quality.
Memory: short-term (context window) + long-term (vector store / RAG recall).
Planning: implicit per-step (ReAct) or explicit plan + reflection to self-correct.
Any framework is just a way to structure these same four parts in a loop.

quiz.q: How does an agent 'use a tool'?
options: The LLM emits a structured (function) call with typed args; the runtime executes it and returns the result
quiz.explain: Function calling: the model outputs a structured tool call that your runtime executes, feeding the result back.

quiz.q: Long-term agent memory is typically:
options: a vector store the agent searches to recall facts/past runs
quiz.explain: Long-term memory is usually a vector store (RAG) the agent queries; short-term memory is the context window.

quiz.q: The biggest practical lever on agent quality is often:
quiz.explain: Clear, narrowly-scoped, validated tools make the agent reliable; vague tools cause errors.

exercises.prompt: List the four parts of an agent.
exercises.solution: LLM (reasoning core), tools (action via function calling), memory (short + long term), planning (decompose + reflect).

exercises.prompt: What three things define a tool the LLM can call?
exercises.solution: A name, a description, and a typed (JSON-schema) argument signature; plus the runtime that executes it.

exercises.prompt: Short-term vs long-term memory?
exercises.solution: Short-term = the context window (recent steps/observations); long-term = a vector store the agent searches to recall older facts.

lessons.diagram_caption: LLM reasoning core + tools (act) + memory (recall) + planning (decompose), run in a loop.

lessons.title: MCP — Model Context Protocol
lessons.summary: The open standard (Anthropic → Linux Foundation) for connecting agents to tools & data — write one server, every agent can use it.
lessons.concept:
Before MCP, every agent-to-tool integration was bespoke glue code. **MCP (Model Context Protocol)** standardizes it: one protocol so any MCP-aware agent can use any MCP server. Created by **Anthropic**, donated to the **Linux Foundation** (Dec 2025), and backed by OpenAI, Google, Microsoft and AWS — it's the **dominant** agent-to-tool standard in 2026 (~97M downloads).

**Architecture.** Client-server over **JSON-RPC 2.0**. The **host** (your agent app) opens an **MCP client** session per **MCP server**; each server exposes capabilities to the agent.

**Three primitives:**
- **Tools** — functions the model can invoke (run a query, call an API).
- **Resources** — data the model can read (file contents, table rows, docs).
- **Prompts** — reusable templates that guide a workflow.

**Why it matters for DE.** You can wrap a data source once as an MCP server — e.g. a **Postgres MCP server** exposing `run_sql` + schema resources — and then *every* MCP-aware agent (Claude, others) can query it safely, with auth and scoping you control. It's the integration layer of the agentic stack: write the server once, reuse everywhere, swap the agent freely.

**Mental model:** MCP is to agents-and-tools what a **driver/standard port** is to hardware — a common interface that ends N×M custom integrations. Open **Go deeper** for a server sketch and security notes (scope tools, least privilege, audit).
keypoints: MCP standardizes agent ↔ tool/data integration — write one server, any MCP agent can use it.
Created by Anthropic, now under the Linux Foundation; backed by OpenAI, Google, Microsoft, AWS.
Client-server over JSON-RPC 2.0: the host opens an MCP client per MCP server.
Three primitives: Tools (invoke), Resources (read data), Prompts (templates).
DE win: wrap a data source once (e.g. Postgres MCP server) and reuse it across agents.
Ends N×M bespoke integrations; control auth, scope, and least privilege at the server.

quiz.q: What problem does MCP solve?
options: Standardizing how agents connect to tools and data, so one server works with any MCP agent
quiz.explain: MCP is a common protocol for agent-to-tool/data integration, ending bespoke per-pair glue code.

quiz.q: MCP's three primitives are:
quiz.explain: Tools are callable functions, Resources are readable data, Prompts are reusable templates.

quiz.q: Who created MCP and where does it live now?
options: Anthropic; donated to the Linux Foundation (backed by OpenAI, Google, Microsoft, AWS)
quiz.explain: Anthropic created MCP and donated it to the Linux Foundation; it's broadly industry-backed.

exercises.prompt: In one sentence, why is MCP a big deal for integrations?
exercises.solution: It replaces N×M custom agent-tool integrations with one standard, so any MCP agent can use any MCP server.

exercises.prompt: Name MCP's three primitives and an example of each.
exercises.solution: Tools (run_sql), Resources (a table's schema/rows), Prompts (a reusable 'investigate freshness' template).

exercises.prompt: How would you expose your warehouse to agents with MCP, safely?
exercises.solution: Build a warehouse MCP server exposing a read-only run_sql tool + schema resources, with auth, least-privilege scoping, and audit logging.

lessons.diagram_caption: Host (agent) opens an MCP client per MCP server (Tools/Resources/Prompts) over your data sources.

lessons.title: A2A & multi-agent protocols
lessons.summary: A2A lets agents delegate to each other (horizontal), while MCP connects an agent to tools (vertical) — complementary layers.
lessons.concept:
MCP connects an agent **down** to tools and data. **A2A (Agent2Agent)** connects agents **across** to each other. They solve different problems and are used together.

**A2A** — launched by **Google Cloud** with 50+ partners (now also under the Linux Foundation umbrella) — lets a **planner/orchestrator** agent **delegate subtasks** to specialist agents. Agents advertise capabilities via **Agent Cards** and communicate over HTTP/JSON, so heterogeneous agents (even from different vendors/frameworks) can collaborate.

**The two layers:**
- **MCP = vertical** (agent ↔ tool): *give one agent access to tools/data.*
- **A2A = horizontal** (agent ↔ agent): *let agents form a team and delegate.*

**Example (data platform):** an **orchestrator** agent receives 'refresh and validate the sales mart'. Via **A2A** it delegates to an *ingestion agent*, a *transform agent*, and a *quality agent*; each of those uses **MCP** to actually touch your database, lake, and APIs. A2A coordinates the team; MCP does the work.

**2026 status:** MCP, A2A and ACP now sit under **Linux Foundation** governance, creating real convergence. Frameworks vary in native support (e.g. CrewAI added A2A), so check your stack. Mental model: **MCP gives an agent hands; A2A lets agents form a crew.** Open **Go deeper** for when to add A2A (vs a single agent with many tools).
lessons.example:
# A2A: orchestrator delegates; each specialist uses MCP for real work (sketch)
orchestrator.delegate("ingest",   to=ingestion_agent)   # A2A (agent -> agent)
orchestrator.delegate("transform",to=transform_agent)
orchestrator.delegate("validate", to=quality_agent)
# inside quality_agent:
quality_agent.use(mcp.run_sql, "SELECT count(*) FROM sales_mart WHERE amount IS NULL")  # MCP (agent -> tool)
keypoints: MCP = vertical (agent ↔ tool/data); A2A = horizontal (agent ↔ agent). Complementary.
A2A (Google Cloud + partners) lets a planner delegate subtasks to specialist agents.
Agents advertise capabilities via Agent Cards; communicate over HTTP/JSON.
Typical pattern: orchestrator delegates via A2A; specialists do work via MCP tools.
2026: MCP, A2A, ACP under Linux Foundation → convergence; framework support varies.
Use A2A when you need a team of specialized agents; a single tool-rich agent may suffice for simpler tasks.

quiz.q: MCP vs A2A in one line:
options: MCP connects an agent to tools (vertical); A2A connects agents to each other (horizontal)
quiz.explain: MCP is the agent-to-tool layer; A2A is the agent-to-agent coordination layer.

quiz.q: In A2A, agents advertise their capabilities via:
quiz.explain: Agent Cards describe an agent's capabilities so others can discover and delegate to it.

quiz.q: A common combined pattern is:
options: an orchestrator delegating via A2A while specialists do work via MCP tools
quiz.explain: A2A coordinates the team of agents; each specialist uses MCP to actually touch data/tools.

exercises.prompt: Which protocol is 'vertical' and which is 'horizontal'?
exercises.solution: MCP is vertical (agent↔tool/data); A2A is horizontal (agent↔agent).

exercises.prompt: When do you reach for A2A over a single agent?
exercises.solution: When the task benefits from specialized agents collaborating (ingestion/transform/quality/repair); a single tool-rich agent suffices for simpler, narrow tasks.

exercises.prompt: Sketch how an orchestrator validates a mart using both protocols.
exercises.solution: Orchestrator delegates 'validate' to a quality agent via A2A; the quality agent runs null/row-count checks via an MCP run_sql tool against the warehouse.

lessons.diagram_caption: MCP: agent → tool (vertical). A2A: planner → specialist agents (horizontal). Use both together.

lessons.title: Agent frameworks compared (2026)
lessons.summary: LangGraph, Claude Agent SDK, CrewAI, AutoGen/AG2, LlamaIndex, Pydantic AI — what each is best at, chosen by your constraint.
lessons.concept:
You rarely build the agent loop from scratch — you pick a **framework**. The 2026 production-ready set, chosen by your **dominant constraint**:

- **LangGraph** — *control.* Models the agent as a **state machine** (nodes, edges, shared state) with retries, **human-in-the-loop**, and time-travel debugging. Best for complex, stateful, auditable workflows.
- **Claude Agent SDK** — *Anthropic-native production.* First-class **tool use, hooks, MCP, skills, and subagents**; the smoothest path to production Claude agents.
- **CrewAI** — *team velocity.* Define **roles** and **tasks** and ship a multi-agent prototype fast; intuitive abstractions; added A2A support.
- **AutoGen / AG2** — *conversation.* **Conversational** agent teams; AG2's **GroupChat** picks who speaks next — good for research/brainstorming-style multi-agent.
- **LlamaIndex agents** — *data.* RAG-grounded retrieval agents over **your** documents/data.
- **Pydantic AI** — *type safety.* Type-safe, Pythonic agents with validation built in.
- **Semantic Kernel** — *enterprise/.NET/Microsoft stacks.*

**How to choose:** don't chase hype — start from the **dominant constraint**: need fine control & auditability → LangGraph; all-in on Anthropic/MCP → Claude Agent SDK; fast multi-agent prototype → CrewAI; conversational research → AutoGen; data-retrieval-centric → LlamaIndex; strict typing → Pydantic AI. Many real systems combine them (e.g. CrewAI roles, LangGraph for the critical stateful path). Open **Go deeper** for a selection checklist.
lessons.example:
# same idea, different ergonomics
# LangGraph: explicit state graph -> control + retries + HITL
graph.add_node("plan", plan); graph.add_node("act", act); graph.add_edge("plan","act")
# CrewAI: roles + tasks -> fast multi-agent
Crew(agents=[ingestor, transformer, qa], tasks=[...]).kickoff()
# Claude Agent SDK: tools + MCP + subagents -> Anthropic-native production
keypoints: LangGraph = control: stateful graph, retries, HITL, time-travel debugging.
Claude Agent SDK = Anthropic-native production: tools, hooks, MCP, skills, subagents.
CrewAI = team velocity: role/task multi-agent prototypes fast (A2A support).
AutoGen/AG2 = conversational agent teams (GroupChat).
LlamaIndex = RAG-grounded data agents; Pydantic AI = type-safe; Semantic Kernel = .NET.
Choose by your dominant constraint, not hype; real systems often combine frameworks.

quiz.q: Which framework is best when you need fine control, retries, and human-in-the-loop over a stateful workflow?
quiz.explain: LangGraph models the agent as a controllable state machine with retries, HITL, and debugging.

quiz.q: CrewAI's main strength is:
quiz.explain: CrewAI's role/task abstractions get a multi-agent prototype running quickly.

quiz.q: The right way to choose a framework is:
options: start from your dominant constraint (control, Anthropic-native, velocity, conversation, data, typing)
quiz.explain: Match the framework to your primary constraint; many systems even combine several.

exercises.prompt: Match: control / velocity / Anthropic-native.
exercises.solution: Control = LangGraph; velocity = CrewAI; Anthropic-native production = Claude Agent SDK.

exercises.prompt: You need a RAG-grounded agent over internal docs. Pick one.
exercises.solution: LlamaIndex agents — built for retrieval-grounded agents over your data.

exercises.prompt: Why might you combine frameworks?
exercises.solution: E.g. CrewAI for fast role-based orchestration plus LangGraph for the critical stateful path needing control/HITL.

lessons.diagram_caption: Pick by constraint: control (LangGraph), Anthropic-native (Claude SDK), velocity (CrewAI), conversation, data, typing.

lessons.title: Multi-agent orchestration
lessons.summary: An orchestrator coordinating specialist agents (ingestion, transform, quality, repair) — the production pattern for agentic pipelines.
lessons.concept:
For non-trivial work, one giant agent struggles. The production pattern is a **fleet**: an **orchestrator** plus focused **specialist** agents.

**Orchestrator (planner).** Receives the goal, decomposes it, **delegates** subtasks (via **A2A**), and assembles results. It owns the plan and the control flow.

**Specialists.** Narrow agents, each great at one job and equipped with the right **MCP tools**: an *ingestion* agent, a *transform* agent, a *data-quality* agent, a *repair* agent. Narrow scope = better prompts, fewer mistakes, easier evaluation.

**Coordination patterns:**
- **Supervisor / orchestrator-worker** — central planner delegates and aggregates (most common for pipelines). LangGraph models this as a state graph; CrewAI as roles+tasks.
- **Group chat** (AutoGen/AG2) — agents converse and a selector picks who speaks next (research/debate-style).
- **Sequential / handoff** — agents pass work down a chain.

**Why multi-agent beats one mega-agent:** separation of concerns (each agent's context stays focused), reusability, independent evaluation/guardrails per agent, and parallelism. The cost is more moving parts — invest in **observability and tracing** so you can see who did what.

**DE mapping:** orchestrator → {ingestion, transform, quality, repair} specialists, each using MCP over your DB/lake/APIs. This is exactly the **agentic pipeline** of the next module. Open **Go deeper** for orchestration trade-offs.
lessons.example:
# supervisor pattern (sketch) — orchestrator delegates to specialists
plan = orchestrator.plan(goal="refresh + validate sales_mart")
for task in plan:                       # e.g. ingest -> transform -> quality -> repair
    agent = route(task)                 # pick the specialist
    result = agent.run(task)            # specialist uses its MCP tools
    orchestrator.observe(result)        # aggregate, decide next / retry / escalate
keypoints: Pattern: an orchestrator (planner) + narrow specialist agents.
Orchestrator decomposes the goal and delegates subtasks (A2A); specialists use MCP tools.
Narrow specialists = focused context, better prompts, fewer errors, easier evals/guardrails.
Coordination: supervisor/orchestrator-worker (common), group chat (AutoGen), sequential handoff.
Benefits: separation of concerns, reuse, per-agent evaluation, parallelism.
Cost: more moving parts — invest in observability/tracing across agents.

quiz.q: The most common multi-agent pattern for data pipelines is:
options: a supervisor/orchestrator that delegates to specialist workers and aggregates
quiz.explain: The orchestrator-worker (supervisor) pattern plans, delegates to specialists, and assembles results.

quiz.q: Why prefer several narrow agents over one mega-agent?
options: focused context, better prompts, fewer errors, and per-agent evals/guardrails
quiz.explain: Separation of concerns keeps each agent's context tight and makes evaluation/guardrails tractable.

quiz.q: The main cost of multi-agent systems is:
options: more moving parts — you must invest in observability/tracing
quiz.explain: Coordinating many agents adds complexity; tracing who did what is essential.

exercises.prompt: Name the four specialist agents in an agentic pipeline.
exercises.solution: Ingestion, transform, data-quality, and repair agents, coordinated by an orchestrator.

exercises.prompt: Which protocol does the orchestrator use to delegate, and which do specialists use to work?
exercises.solution: Orchestrator delegates via A2A (agent↔agent); specialists act via MCP tools (agent↔tool/data).

exercises.prompt: Give two coordination patterns and when to use each.
exercises.solution: Supervisor/orchestrator-worker for pipelines (central plan + delegate); group chat for research/debate where agents converse and a selector picks the next speaker.

lessons.diagram_caption: Orchestrator delegates (A2A) to ingestion/transform/quality/repair specialists, each using MCP over your data.

lessons.title: Self-healing & autonomous pipelines
lessons.summary: The flagship 2026 use case — agents that detect anomalies, diagnose root cause, and auto-fix or escalate.
lessons.concept:
The biggest agentic-DE trend of 2026 is the **self-healing pipeline**: instead of a human waking up to a 3 a.m. page, an **agent** detects the problem, diagnoses it, and **fixes it or escalates**.

**How it works:**
1. **Detect** — the agent (or a quality layer it watches) flags anomalies in real time: **null floods, schema drift, freshness misses, volume swings**.
2. **Diagnose** — it reads logs and **lineage** to find the root cause (e.g. an upstream column was renamed).
3. **Act** — for **safe, well-understood** fixes it auto-remediates: retry, **backfill** the affected partition, cast a changed type, **quarantine** bad records, or open a fix PR. For **ambiguous or risky** changes it **escalates to a human (HITL)** with its diagnosis attached.

**The intent layer.** Mature setups define an **intent layer** — the pipeline's purpose, consumers, and expectations (freshness, accuracy, reliability) — so the agent knows what 'healthy' means and what trade-offs are acceptable.

**Architecture.** It's the **multi-agent** pattern applied: an orchestrator coordinating detect/diagnose/repair specialists, each using **MCP** to read logs, query the warehouse, and apply fixes — bounded by **guardrails**.

**The hard part isn't detection — it's safe action.** Auto-fixing the wrong thing confidently can corrupt downstream data, so self-healing is paired tightly with validation and HITL (next lessons). Open **Go deeper** for safe-action design.
lessons.example:
# self-healing loop (sketch)
issue = monitor.detect()                 # null flood in orders.amount
root  = agent.diagnose(issue, logs, lineage)   # upstream cast changed string->null
if root.fix_is_safe:                     # known, low-risk
    agent.apply(root.fix)                # backfill partition / re-cast / quarantine
else:
    human.escalate(issue, diagnosis=root)  # HITL with full context
keypoints: Self-healing pipeline = detect → diagnose → fix/escalate, done by an agent.
Detect: null floods, schema drift, freshness misses, volume anomalies.
Diagnose: read logs + lineage to find root cause.
Act: auto-fix safe cases (retry, backfill, cast, quarantine); escalate risky ones to a human.
An intent layer encodes purpose + freshness/accuracy/reliability expectations = what 'healthy' means.
It's the multi-agent pattern + MCP tools, bounded by guardrails; safe action is the hard part.

quiz.q: A self-healing pipeline's three phases are:
quiz.explain: The agent detects anomalies, diagnoses root cause from logs/lineage, then auto-fixes or escalates.

quiz.q: What does the agent use to find an issue's root cause?
quiz.explain: Lineage shows upstream/downstream dependencies and logs show what changed — together they localize the cause.

quiz.q: When should a self-healing agent escalate instead of auto-fixing?
options: when the fix is ambiguous or risky — escalate to a human with the diagnosis
quiz.explain: Safe, known fixes can be automated; ambiguous/risky changes go to a human-in-the-loop with context.

exercises.prompt: List four anomalies a self-healing agent watches for.
exercises.solution: Null floods, schema drift, freshness misses, and volume swings.

exercises.prompt: Give two auto-fixes that are usually safe, and one case to escalate.
exercises.solution: Safe: retry a transient failure, backfill the affected partition, re-cast a changed type, quarantine bad rows. Escalate: ambiguous business-logic or destructive changes.

exercises.prompt: What does an 'intent layer' provide the agent?
exercises.solution: The pipeline's purpose, consumers, and freshness/accuracy/reliability expectations — so the agent knows what 'healthy' means and acceptable trade-offs.

lessons.diagram_caption: Agent watches ingest→transform→serve; diagnoses via logs+lineage; auto-fixes safe cases or escalates (HITL).

lessons.title: Text-to-SQL & code-gen agents
lessons.summary: Natural-language → validated SQL/pipeline code (Databricks Genie Code, Snowflake Cortex Code) — with the guardrails that make it trustworthy.
lessons.concept:
Text-to-SQL and code-generation agents turn a **plain-English question** into **runnable, validated** SQL or pipeline code. In 2026 this leapt forward: **Databricks Genie Code** (Mar 2026) and **Snowflake Cortex Code** reason through a problem, plan, write, and **validate** production-grade code — Genie more than **doubled** task success (≈32% → 77%).

**The pipeline (and where guardrails go):**
1. **Understand** the question and **read the schema/catalog** (so the SQL targets real tables/columns).
2. **Generate** candidate SQL with the LLM.
3. **Validate** — *the critical step*: dry-run/`EXPLAIN`, check the **cost/bytes scanned**, run against known **tests/expected shapes**, and verify it answers the question.
4. **Execute** and return the result **plus the SQL** (so a human can audit it).

**Why validation is non-negotiable.** An agent can produce SQL that **runs and passes a glance but is business-wrong** (wrong join grain, missing filter), and **high-confidence wrong output corrupts every downstream system**. So you never auto-trust generated SQL: validate, show the query, and for writes/expensive jobs require approval.

**Beyond SQL:** the same pattern generates dbt models, ingestion code, and transformations — plan → generate → **validate** → execute. It's a force multiplier for DEs (and a great self-service layer for analysts), *provided* the validation/guardrail layer is real. Open **Go deeper** for a validation checklist.
lessons.example:
# text-to-SQL with a validation gate (sketch)
q      = "top 5 regions by Q2 revenue"
schema = catalog.read("sales")              # ground the model in real columns
sql    = llm.generate_sql(q, schema)
plan   = warehouse.explain(sql)             # dry-run: validity + bytes/cost
assert plan.ok and plan.bytes < BUDGET and passes_tests(sql)
rows   = warehouse.run(sql)                 # execute only after validation
return rows, sql                            # always return the SQL for audit
keypoints: Text-to-SQL/code-gen: NL question → plan → generate → VALIDATE → execute.
Ground generation in the real schema/catalog so queries hit real tables/columns.
Validation is the critical step: dry-run/EXPLAIN, cost/bytes check, tests, answers-the-question.
2026 tools: Databricks Genie Code, Snowflake Cortex Code (Genie ~doubled task success).
Confidently-wrong SQL corrupts downstream — never auto-trust; show the SQL, gate writes.
Same pattern generates dbt models/ingestion/transform code — a DE force multiplier.

quiz.q: Why is the 'validate' step essential in a text-to-SQL agent?
options: generated SQL can run yet be business-wrong; high-confidence wrong output corrupts downstream
quiz.explain: Validation (dry-run, cost, tests, correctness) catches plausible-but-wrong queries before they pollute systems.

quiz.q: To target real tables/columns, the agent should first:
options: read the schema/catalog to ground generation
ask the user to type DDL
quiz.explain: Grounding in the actual schema prevents hallucinated tables/columns and improves correctness.

quiz.q: Two leading 2026 code-gen tools for the data stack are:
options: Databricks Genie Code and Snowflake Cortex Code
quiz.explain: Genie Code and Cortex Code generate and validate production-grade data code; Genie roughly doubled task success.

exercises.prompt: Give the five steps of a trustworthy text-to-SQL agent.
exercises.solution: Understand + read schema → generate SQL → validate (dry-run/cost/tests) → execute → return result + the SQL for audit.

exercises.prompt: Name three things the validation step checks.
exercises.solution: Query validity (EXPLAIN/dry-run), cost/bytes scanned within budget, and that it passes tests / answers the question.

exercises.prompt: Why always return the generated SQL to the user?
exercises.solution: So a human can audit the logic (join grain, filters) — generated SQL can be confidently wrong, and transparency enables trust and correction.

lessons.diagram_caption: Question → plan+schema → generate SQL → validate (dry-run/cost/tests) → execute + return result and SQL.

lessons.title: Data quality & contract agents
lessons.summary: Agents that continuously validate data, enforce contracts, quarantine bad records, and trigger corrective action.
lessons.concept:
Data-quality work is a natural fit for agents because it's continuous, rule-rich, and increasingly *required* — agentic pipelines feeding AI need a **higher** quality bar than dashboards.

**What a data-quality agent does:**
- **Validate** data against defined standards/expectations (ranges, null rates, uniqueness, referential integrity).
- **Enforce data contracts** — check producer changes against the **contract** (schema, SLAs) and block/flag violations (the shift-left idea, now agent-driven).
- **Quarantine** bad records to a dead-letter store instead of poisoning downstream.
- **Trigger corrective action** — alert, open a ticket/PR, or hand off to a **repair** agent when thresholds are violated.

**Why agentic (vs plain dbt tests)?** Tests are static pass/fail; an agent can **investigate** a failure (read lineage, sample bad rows, hypothesize a cause), **adapt** thresholds to seasonality, and **explain** the issue in plain language — then route it. It augments tools like dbt tests / Great Expectations / Soda rather than replacing them.

**Governance reality.** Agents can mislabel good data as bad or vice-versa, so quality agents themselves need **evals** and **human review** for consequential calls. The human role becomes **setting the policies and thresholds** the agent enforces.

**Where it sits:** the *quality* specialist in the agentic-pipeline fleet, using MCP to read tables/lineage and to quarantine/route. Open **Go deeper** for a quality-agent design.
lessons.example:
# data-quality agent loop (sketch)
for table in watched:
    report = agent.validate(table, contract=contracts[table])   # ranges, nulls, schema, SLAs
    if report.violations:
        agent.quarantine(report.bad_rows)        # don't poison downstream
        cause = agent.investigate(report, lineage)
        route(cause)                              # alert / PR / hand to repair agent
keypoints: Quality agents validate against standards, enforce contracts, quarantine bad data, trigger fixes.
Agent-driven contract enforcement = shift-left, now automated end to end.
vs static tests: agents investigate, adapt thresholds, and explain failures in plain language.
They augment dbt tests / Great Expectations / Soda, not replace them.
Agentic pipelines feeding AI need a higher quality bar than human dashboards.
Quality agents need their own evals + human review; humans set policies/thresholds.

quiz.q: A data-quality agent does all EXCEPT:
options: enforce contracts and quarantine bad records
quiz.explain: Quality agents validate, enforce contracts, quarantine, and route fixes — they don't train the model.

quiz.q: How does a quality agent improve on static dbt tests?
options: it investigates failures, adapts thresholds, and explains causes — then routes them
quiz.explain: Beyond pass/fail, an agent can diagnose, adapt, and communicate — augmenting existing test tools.

quiz.q: Why do agentic pipelines need a higher quality bar?
options: confidently-wrong data corrupts every downstream AI/system that consumes it
quiz.explain: High-confidence wrong output propagates; pipelines feeding AI demand stricter quality than human dashboards.

exercises.prompt: List four jobs of a data-quality agent.
exercises.solution: Validate against standards, enforce data contracts, quarantine bad records, and trigger corrective action (alert/PR/repair).

exercises.prompt: Does an agent replace dbt tests/Great Expectations?
exercises.solution: No — it augments them: it runs/extends those checks, then investigates, adapts, explains, and routes failures.

exercises.prompt: What's the human's role with quality agents?
exercises.solution: Setting the policies, thresholds, and contracts the agent enforces, and reviewing consequential calls (the agent can be wrong).

lessons.diagram_caption: A quality agent validates each table against its contract: clean data flows downstream, bad records are quarantined, then the agent investigates and routes the issue — humans set the policy.

lessons.title: Guardrails, evaluation & governance
lessons.summary: Make agents safe to trust: permissions, validation, evals, cost limits, and human-in-the-loop — because agents can be confidently wrong.
lessons.concept:
Autonomy without bounds is a liability. The discipline that makes agentic systems shippable is **guardrails + evaluation + governance** — and it's where the data engineer's judgment now lives.

**The core risk.** An agent can produce **high-confidence wrong output** — SQL that passes a glance but is business-wrong, a 'fix' that deletes good data. In a pipeline, that error **corrupts every downstream system**. So you never let an agent act unbounded.

**Guardrails (bound what the agent can do):**
- **Permissions / least privilege** — only the tools and data it needs (e.g. read-only by default; writes gated).
- **Validation** — check actions before executing (dry-run SQL, schema/contract checks, cost/bytes limits).
- **Cost & rate limits** — budget guards so a loop can't run up a huge bill.
- **Human-in-the-loop (HITL)** — require approval for risky/irreversible actions; the agent proposes, a human disposes.

**Evaluation.** Treat agents like software: build **evals** (test suites of tasks with known-good outcomes), measure success/accuracy, and **monitor in production** (log every action, trace multi-agent runs, alert on anomalies). 'It worked in the demo' isn't enough.

**Governance & the new human role.** Humans shift from doing every step to **defining policies, setting guardrails, designing agent collaboration, and owning the edge cases** — becoming *orchestrators of agent fleets*. You're accountable for what the fleet does. Open **Go deeper** for a guardrail checklist.
keypoints: Core risk: agents can be confidently wrong; in a pipeline that corrupts everything downstream.
Guardrails: least-privilege permissions, pre-execution validation, cost/rate limits, HITL.
Human-in-the-loop for risky/irreversible actions — agent proposes, human disposes.
Evaluate agents like software: eval suites, success/accuracy metrics, production monitoring + tracing.
Pipelines feeding AI need a higher quality/guardrail bar than human dashboards.
The human role shifts to setting policy, guardrails, and owning edge cases — orchestrating agent fleets.

quiz.q: The central reason agents need guardrails is:
options: they can be confidently wrong, and in a pipeline that corrupts downstream systems
quiz.explain: High-confidence wrong actions propagate through dependent systems, so actions must be bounded and validated.

quiz.q: Which is a guardrail for irreversible/risky actions?
quiz.explain: HITL requires human approval before risky/irreversible actions execute.

quiz.q: How should you decide an agent is good enough to trust?
options: build eval suites with known-good outcomes and monitor success/accuracy in production
quiz.explain: Treat agents like software: evals against known-good tasks plus production monitoring and tracing.

exercises.prompt: Name four guardrails for an agentic system.
exercises.solution: Least-privilege permissions, pre-execution validation (dry-run/schema/cost), cost & rate limits, and human-in-the-loop for risky actions.

exercises.prompt: Why is 'it worked in the demo' insufficient?
exercises.solution: Agents are non-deterministic and can be confidently wrong; you need eval suites with known-good outcomes plus production monitoring/tracing.

exercises.prompt: How does the data engineer's role change in an agentic world?
exercises.solution: From executing every step to defining policies/guardrails, designing agent collaboration, and owning edge cases — orchestrating and being accountable for agent fleets.

lessons.diagram_caption: Agent proposes → guardrails (permissions, validation, cost, evals) → execute if safe, else human-in-the-loop.

lessons.title: Agentic AI interview prep & cheat sheet
lessons.summary: The agentic concepts now showing up in 50LPA+ DE interviews — distilled to a one-page review with likely questions.
lessons.concept:
Agentic AI is now fair game in senior DE interviews (design rounds especially: 'design an LLM/agentic pipeline'). This is your review sheet.

**Must-know definitions:** agent = LLM + tools + memory + planning in a **reason→act→observe** loop (ReAct); agent vs workflow vs chatbot; **tools = function calling** (typed); short-term vs long-term (**vector**) memory.

**Protocols (2026):** **MCP** = agent ↔ tool/data standard (Anthropic → Linux Foundation; primitives **Tools/Resources/Prompts**; JSON-RPC client-server) — *write one server, any agent uses it*. **A2A** = agent ↔ agent delegation (Agent Cards). MCP vertical, A2A horizontal; use together.

**Frameworks:** LangGraph (control/stateful), Claude Agent SDK (Anthropic-native production), CrewAI (fast multi-agent), AutoGen/AG2 (conversational), LlamaIndex (RAG data), Pydantic AI (type-safe). Choose by **constraint**.

**Patterns:** multi-agent **orchestrator + specialists**; **self-healing pipeline** (detect→diagnose→fix/escalate); **text-to-SQL** (plan→generate→**validate**→execute); **data-quality agents** (validate/enforce/quarantine/route).

**Governance (the senior signal):** agents can be **confidently wrong** → guardrails (least privilege, validation, cost limits, **HITL**), **evals**, and monitoring. Pipelines feeding AI need a higher bar. Human role = set policy + own edge cases.

**Likely questions:** 'What is MCP and why does it matter?'; 'Design a self-healing pipeline'; 'How do you stop a text-to-SQL agent from corrupting data?'; 'MCP vs A2A?'; 'When multi-agent vs single agent?'. Answers: see the per-lesson banks + the inline Interview panels.
lessons.example:
# 60-second agentic reset
Agent?      -> LLM + tools + memory + planning, reason->act->observe (ReAct)
MCP vs A2A? -> MCP = agent<->tool (vertical); A2A = agent<->agent (horizontal)
Frameworks? -> control=LangGraph, Anthropic=Claude SDK, velocity=CrewAI
DE uses?    -> self-healing pipelines, text-to-SQL, quality agents
Safety?     -> guardrails + validation + cost limits + HITL + evals
keypoints: Agent = LLM + tools + memory + planning in a reason→act→observe loop (ReAct).
MCP = agent↔tool standard (Tools/Resources/Prompts; Anthropic→Linux Foundation); A2A = agent↔agent.
Frameworks by constraint: LangGraph (control), Claude SDK (Anthropic), CrewAI (velocity), AutoGen, LlamaIndex, Pydantic AI.
Patterns: orchestrator+specialists; self-healing (detect→diagnose→fix/escalate); text-to-SQL (validate!); quality agents.
Governance is the senior signal: confidently-wrong risk → guardrails, HITL, evals, monitoring.
Be ready to design an agentic/LLM pipeline with rate limits, retries, cost budgets, and validation.

quiz.q: Best one-line answer to 'what is MCP?'
options: an open standard for connecting agents to tools/data (write one server, any MCP agent uses it)
quiz.explain: MCP standardizes agent-to-tool/data integration; Tools/Resources/Prompts over JSON-RPC, Anthropic→Linux Foundation.

quiz.q: Top way to stop a text-to-SQL agent from corrupting data?
options: validate before execute (dry-run/cost/tests), show the SQL, gate writes with HITL
quiz.explain: Validation + transparency + HITL on writes prevent confidently-wrong queries from polluting systems.

quiz.q: When is multi-agent preferred over a single agent?
options: when specialized agents (ingest/transform/quality/repair) with focused context improve reliability
quiz.explain: Narrow specialists keep context focused and enable per-agent evals/guardrails; a single tool-rich agent suits simpler tasks.

exercises.prompt: Give the one-line agent definition.
exercises.solution: An LLM with tools, memory, and planning run in a reason→act→observe loop (ReAct).

exercises.prompt: MCP vs A2A in interview-ready form.
exercises.solution: MCP connects an agent to tools/data (vertical, Tools/Resources/Prompts); A2A connects agents to each other (horizontal, Agent Cards); use together.

exercises.prompt: Design answer: how do you make an agentic pipeline safe?
exercises.solution: Least-privilege tools, validate actions before executing (dry-run/cost), cost/rate limits, HITL for risky/irreversible steps, evals + production monitoring; humans set policy.

tracks.title: System Design & Interview Mastery
tracks.blurb: Put it all together: design end-to-end pipelines and ace the senior interview.

lessons.title: A framework for any pipeline design
lessons.summary: A repeatable structure for designing (and interviewing on) any data system: clarify requirements, then walk data characteristics, ingestion, storage, processing, serving, scale, and trade-offs.
lessons.concept:
Whether designing a real system or answering an interview question, use a **repeatable framework** so you cover the right things in order — and **think out loud**.

### Step 1: Clarify requirements (don't skip!)
Before any design, **ask questions**:

- **What's the goal / use case?** (BI dashboards? real-time alerts? ML features?)
- **Latency** — how fresh must data be? (minutes? sub-second?) — drives **batch vs streaming**.
- **Volume & throughput** — how much data, how fast? (events/sec, GB/day) — drives **scale**.
- **Consumers** — who/what reads it and how? (analysts via SQL, apps via API).
- **Constraints** — budget, existing stack, team skills, compliance.

Designing without requirements is the #1 mistake — **the requirements determine the design.**

### Step 2: Walk the pipeline (the structure)
Then design stage by stage (the data lifecycle):

1. **Data characteristics** — structured/semi/unstructured? volume? velocity?
2. **Ingestion** — batch or streaming? sources? (EL tools, Kafka/CDC)
3. **Storage** — lake/lakehouse/warehouse? format (Parquet)? partitioning?
4. **Processing** — Spark/SQL/dbt? transformations, modeling (medallion).
5. **Serving** — warehouse for BI, serving store for apps/ML, API, reverse ETL.
6. **Cross-cutting** — orchestration, quality/observability, governance, cost.

### Step 3: Address scale & trade-offs
Estimate capacity (next lesson), identify bottlenecks (the **shuffle**, hot partitions, the network), and **state trade-offs** explicitly (latency vs cost vs complexity). There's no perfect design — show you understand the **trade-offs** your choices make.

### Why it matters
This framework — **clarify requirements → walk the lifecycle (ingest/store/process/serve) → scale & trade-offs** — works for **any** data design and signals **structured thinking** in interviews. Combined with the rest of this curriculum (the tools for each stage), it's how you design real, scalable, appropriate data systems.
keypoints: Use a repeatable framework: clarify requirements → walk the lifecycle (ingest/store/process/serve) → scale & trade-offs.
Step 1 — clarify requirements first: goal/use case, latency (batch vs stream), volume/throughput, consumers, constraints (budget/stack/skills/compliance).
Designing without requirements is the #1 mistake — requirements determine the design.
Step 2 — walk stages: data characteristics → ingestion → storage → processing → serving → cross-cutting (orchestration/quality/governance/cost).
Step 3 — estimate capacity, find bottlenecks (shuffle, hot partitions, network), and state trade-offs (latency vs cost vs complexity) explicitly.
There's no perfect design — show you understand the trade-offs your choices make; the framework signals structured thinking in interviews.

quiz.q: What's the first step in designing any data system?
options: Clarify requirements (goal, latency, volume, consumers, constraints) — they determine the design
quiz.explain: Designing without requirements is the #1 mistake; latency, volume, consumers, and constraints drive every downstream choice (batch vs stream, storage, scale).

quiz.q: What structure do you walk through after clarifying requirements?
options: The data lifecycle stages: data characteristics → ingestion → storage → processing → serving → cross-cutting
quiz.explain: Designing stage by stage (ingest/store/process/serve + cross-cutting concerns) ensures complete, structured coverage of the system.

quiz.q: What should you always state explicitly in a design?
options: The trade-offs your choices make (latency vs cost vs complexity) — there's no perfect design
quiz.explain: Every design trades off latency, cost, and complexity; articulating those trade-offs shows mature, structured engineering thinking (and impresses interviewers).

exercises.prompt: Why is clarifying requirements the most important step, and what questions do you ask?
exercises.solution: Because **the requirements determine every design decision** — without them you're guessing, and the **#1 mistake** is diving into a solution before understanding the problem. Key questions: **What's the goal/use case?** (BI dashboards vs real-time alerts vs ML features — very different systems). **Latency** — how fresh must the data be (nightly? minutes? sub-second)? — this **drives batch vs streaming**, often the biggest fork. **Volume & throughput** — how much data and how fast (events/sec, GB/day)? — drives **scale and tooling**. **Consumers** — who/what reads it and how (analysts via SQL, an app via API, a model)? — drives **serving**. **Constraints** — budget, existing stack, team skills, compliance/residency? — drives **what's feasible**. Different answers yield **completely different designs** (a sub-second fraud system vs a nightly BI pipeline share almost nothing). So you **ask first, design second** — and in interviews, asking these questions signals you understand that **good design serves real requirements**, not a generic template.

exercises.prompt: Walk the framework at a high level for 'design a system to power BI dashboards from app data'.
exercises.solution: **Clarify:** BI dashboards → **latency** can be hourly/daily (not real-time), **volume** moderate (app DB scale), **consumers** = analysts via SQL/BI tool, **constraints** = existing cloud/budget. This points to **batch ELT into a warehouse/lakehouse**, not streaming. **Walk the stages:** **Ingestion** — **EL** the app's OLTP data (Fivetran/Airbyte, or **CDC** for freshness) into the warehouse (batch, scheduled). **Storage** — land raw (bronze) in the **lakehouse/warehouse**, Parquet, partitioned by date. **Processing** — **dbt/SQL** transforms raw → cleaned (silver) → **dimensional star schemas/aggregates** (gold) — the medallion model. **Serving** — analysts query the **gold tables** via the warehouse + a BI tool (dashboards). **Cross-cutting** — **orchestrate** with Airflow (schedule ingest→transform), **test** data (dbt tests) and **monitor** freshness/quality, apply **governance** (access/PII), watch **cost** (scan less, right-size compute). **Scale & trade-offs:** moderate volume → standard warehouse scales fine; **trade-off** = chose **batch** (simpler, cheaper, hourly-fresh) over streaming (unneeded latency/complexity). This structured walk — **requirements → ingest/store/process/serve → scale/trade-offs** — produces a complete, justified design.

exercises.prompt: How does this framework demonstrate 'structured thinking' in an interview?
exercises.solution: It shows the interviewer you **approach open-ended problems methodically** rather than jumping to a random tool. By **starting with clarifying questions**, you demonstrate you understand that **requirements drive design** (and you avoid solving the wrong problem) — interviewers specifically look for candidates who **ask before designing**. By **walking the lifecycle stages in order** (ingest→store→process→serve + cross-cutting), you give **complete, organized coverage** — nothing forgotten, easy to follow — instead of a scattered answer. By **estimating scale and explicitly stating trade-offs** (latency vs cost vs complexity), you show **engineering maturity** — recognizing there's **no perfect design**, only choices appropriate to the requirements, which is exactly the senior-level judgment interviews probe. And by **thinking out loud** through the framework, you let the interviewer **follow your reasoning** and guide you. So the framework turns a daunting open question into a **structured, justified, complete** design conversation — signaling the **clarity, breadth, and trade-off awareness** that distinguish strong data engineers, which is what system-design interviews are really assessing.

exercises.prompt: Why is 'there's no perfect design — show you understand the trade-offs' a key mindset?
exercises.hint: Every choice has costs; context decides.
exercises.solution: Because **every design decision trades one quality for another**, so the goal isn't a mythical 'perfect' system but the **right trade-offs for the context**. Streaming buys **low latency** but costs **complexity and money**; denormalizing buys **read speed** but costs **storage and update complexity**; stronger consistency costs **latency/availability**; more scale costs **operational burden**. There is **no design that's best on all axes** — improving one usually **worsens another**. So a strong engineer **doesn't claim a perfect answer**; they **make choices appropriate to the requirements** (latency need, volume, budget, skills) and **explicitly state the trade-offs** ('I chose batch because we only need hourly freshness, trading real-time latency for much lower complexity and cost'). This mindset matters because: (1) it produces **fit-for-purpose** designs (not over-engineered or under-built); (2) it shows **maturity** — understanding *why* a choice is right *here* and what it costs; and (3) in interviews, **articulating trade-offs is exactly what's assessed** (anyone can name tools; understanding their costs is the skill). Recognizing 'no perfect design, only trade-offs' is the core of good system design — choosing deliberately and owning the consequences.

lessons.diagram_caption: A repeatable design framework: clarify requirements → walk ingest/store/process/serve → address scale & trade-offs.

lessons.title: Choosing batch vs streaming (and cost)
lessons.summary: The most consequential design fork: when low-latency streaming is truly needed vs when simpler, cheaper batch suffices — and the cost/complexity implications.
lessons.concept:
**Batch vs streaming** is often the **biggest fork** in a data design, and the most common mistake is **choosing streaming when batch would do** — paying huge complexity/cost for latency you don't need.

### The deciding question: required latency
Ask: **how fresh must the data actually be for the use case?**

- **Daily/hourly is fine** (most BI, reporting, ML training data) → **batch**.
- **Seconds/minutes near-real-time** (live dashboards, monitoring) → **micro-batch** (Spark every few seconds).
- **Sub-second, act-on-each-event** (fraud blocking, real-time personalization, alerting) → **true streaming** (Flink/Kafka).

Let the **business requirement** decide — not 'streaming is modern'.

### The cost of streaming
Streaming buys low latency but **costs**:

- **Complexity** — out-of-order/late data (watermarks), state management, exactly-once, a 24/7 system.
- **Operational burden** — always-on, harder to debug/test, on-call.
- **Money** — continuous compute vs batch's scale-to-zero between runs.

So streaming is justified **only when low latency delivers real business value** — otherwise batch is **simpler, cheaper, and easier to operate**.

### The pragmatic middle & hybrid
**Micro-batch** (Spark Structured Streaming) gives near-real-time with batch-like simplicity — meeting many 'streaming' needs cheaply. And the **lakehouse** lets one table be written by a stream and read by BI, blurring the line (Kappa). You can also do **batch for history + a thin streaming layer** only where latency matters.

### The interview/real answer
Don't reflexively pick streaming. **Start from the latency requirement**, choose the **simplest approach that meets it**, and **state the cost/complexity trade-off**. 'We need hourly freshness, so batch — streaming's latency isn't worth its complexity/cost here' is a **stronger** answer than defaulting to Kafka+Flink for a daily report.
keypoints: Batch vs streaming is often the biggest design fork; the common mistake is choosing streaming when batch would do.
Deciding question: required latency. Daily/hourly→batch; seconds/minutes→micro-batch; sub-second act-on-each-event→true streaming.
Let the business requirement decide, not 'streaming is modern'.
Streaming costs: complexity (late data/watermarks, state, exactly-once, 24/7), operational burden, and continuous compute money.
Micro-batch (Spark) gives near-real-time with batch-like simplicity; the lakehouse blurs the line (one table written by stream, read by BI — Kappa).
Start from the latency requirement, choose the simplest approach that meets it, and state the cost/complexity trade-off.

quiz.q: What's the deciding question for batch vs streaming?
options: How fresh must the data actually be (required latency) for the use case
quiz.explain: Required latency drives the choice: daily/hourly→batch, seconds→micro-batch, sub-second act-on-each-event→true streaming. The business need decides, not fashion.

quiz.q: What's the most common batch-vs-streaming mistake?
options: Choosing streaming when batch would do — paying huge complexity/cost for latency you don't need
quiz.explain: Streaming adds real complexity, operational burden, and continuous cost; adopting it without a genuine low-latency need wastes effort — batch is simpler/cheaper for most workloads.

quiz.q: What does micro-batch offer in this trade-off?
options: Near-real-time latency with batch-like simplicity — meeting many 'streaming' needs cheaply
quiz.explain: Micro-batch (Spark Structured Streaming) processes small frequent batches, giving near-real-time freshness without full streaming complexity — a pragmatic middle ground.

exercises.prompt: For each, choose batch, micro-batch, or true streaming and justify: (a) nightly revenue report, (b) a 'last 5 minutes' ops dashboard, (c) blocking fraudulent transactions.
exercises.solution: (a) **Batch** — a nightly report has **no low-latency need**; a daily scheduled job is **simplest and cheapest**, and streaming would add complexity/cost for zero benefit. (b) **Micro-batch** — a 'last 5 minutes' dashboard needs **near-real-time** but not sub-second; **small batches every few seconds** (Spark Structured Streaming) give the needed freshness with **batch-like simplicity and exactly-once**, avoiding full streaming complexity. (c) **True streaming** — blocking a fraudulent transaction must happen in **milliseconds, acting on each event** as it occurs; only **event-at-a-time streaming** (Flink/Kafka) meets that, and the complexity/cost is **justified by the real-time business value** (preventing fraud). The **latency requirement** cleanly determines each: **no latency need → batch; near-real-time → micro-batch; act-on-each-event sub-second → streaming**. You pick the **simplest approach that meets the requirement**, not the most sophisticated.

exercises.prompt: What does streaming actually cost, beyond compute money?
exercises.solution: Streaming's costs go well beyond the continuous-compute bill: (1) **Complexity** — you must handle **out-of-order and late data** (event time, **watermarks**), **stateful** processing (windows, joins, dedup with bounded, checkpointed state), and **exactly-once** delivery — genuinely hard correctness problems that batch (processing a bounded, replayable chunk) sidesteps. (2) **Operational burden** — a streaming system runs **24/7**, so it needs **always-on** infrastructure, is **harder to test and debug** (no neat reproducible batch run), and carries **on-call** responsibility when it breaks at 3am. (3) **Money** — **continuous** compute (always running) vs batch's ability to **scale to zero** between runs, so steady-state cost is higher. (4) **Development time** — building and reasoning about a correct streaming pipeline takes longer. So streaming trades **all of this** for **low latency** — which is only worth it when **low latency delivers real business value** (fraud, live personalization, alerting). For everything else, **batch is simpler, cheaper, easier to operate and debug** — which is why 'don't stream unless you need to' is the standard guidance. Recognizing the **full cost** (not just compute) is key to choosing correctly.

exercises.prompt: Why is 'we need hourly freshness, so batch' a stronger interview answer than defaulting to Kafka+Flink?
exercises.solution: Because it demonstrates **requirement-driven design and trade-off awareness** — exactly the senior judgment interviews assess — whereas defaulting to Kafka+Flink for a daily/hourly need signals **resume-driven over-engineering**. The strong answer shows you (1) **started from the actual latency requirement** ('hourly freshness') rather than a trendy tool; (2) **chose the simplest approach that meets it** (batch), reflecting that **simpler is better when it suffices** — less complexity, cost, and operational burden; and (3) can **articulate the trade-off** ('streaming's latency isn't worth its complexity/cost here'), proving you understand what streaming *costs*, not just that it exists. Reflexively reaching for **Kafka+Flink** for a report suggests you **don't grasp the cost** of streaming or that **design should fit requirements** — you'd build an expensive, hard-to-operate 24/7 system for a need a nightly job handles. Interviewers value engineers who **right-size** solutions and **justify** them, not those who pick the most complex tool to look sophisticated. So 'batch, because the requirement only needs hourly and streaming's costs aren't justified' shows **maturity, judgment, and cost-awareness** — a far stronger signal than an unjustified streaming default.

exercises.prompt: How do micro-batch and the lakehouse blur the batch-vs-streaming line?
exercises.hint: Near-real-time simply; one table for both.
exercises.solution: They make the choice **less binary** by providing **middle grounds**. **Micro-batch** (Spark Structured Streaming) processes the stream in **small frequent batches (every few seconds)** using the **same DataFrame code as batch**, delivering **near-real-time latency with batch-like simplicity and exactly-once** — so many needs that *sound* like 'streaming' (a few-second-fresh dashboard) are met **without true event-at-a-time complexity** (Flink). You get most of streaming's freshness at much of batch's simplicity. The **lakehouse** blurs it further: because a lakehouse table is **transactional (ACID)**, **one table can be written by a stream and read by BI simultaneously** — the live updates and the analytical queries hit the **same table**, removing the need for separate 'speed' and 'batch' systems (the **Kappa** unification). Combined with the idea that **'a batch is a bounded stream'** and engines using **one API for both**, the hard line between batch and streaming softens: you choose an **execution mode (batch / micro-batch / continuous)** and a **freshness level** along a **spectrum**, often on shared infrastructure and code, rather than committing to two entirely separate architectures. This means you can **start batch and add a thin streaming/micro-batch layer only where latency matters**, getting the right freshness per use case without fully separate systems — a more flexible, pragmatic reality than 'batch OR streaming'.

lessons.diagram_caption: Let the latency requirement decide: seconds → streaming, minutes → micro-batch, hours/daily → batch (cheaper, simpler). Don't stream what a daily batch can serve.

lessons.title: Capacity & cost estimation
lessons.summary: Back-of-the-envelope sizing: estimating data volume, throughput, storage, and cost so your design is grounded in numbers, not hand-waving.
lessons.concept:
Good designs are **grounded in numbers**. **Capacity estimation** — quick back-of-the-envelope math — tells you how big the system must be, whether it fits on one machine or needs distribution, and **roughly what it costs**. Interviewers explicitly look for this.

### What to estimate
- **Data volume** — `rows × bytes/row`, or `events/day × bytes/event`, → **GB/TB/day** and **total** (× retention).
- **Throughput** — **events/sec** (ingest rate) and **queries/sec** — peak and average.
- **Storage** — daily volume × retention (and remember **compression** ~3–10× for columnar, and **replication** ×3 raises it).
- **Compute** — rough processing needs (a node handles ~X MB/s); does it fit one machine or need a cluster?
- **Cost** — storage $/TB/month + compute $/hour + **egress** + scanned-data pricing.

### How to do it (the technique)
**Round aggressively** and use **powers of 10**:

```
100M events/day × 1 KB = 100 GB/day
× 365 days × 1 year retention = ~36 TB/year (raw)
÷ ~5× columnar compression ≈ 7 TB stored
100M/day ÷ 86,400 s ≈ ~1,200 events/sec average (×~5 peak ≈ 6k/s)
```
These numbers tell you: 7 TB is **trivial for a warehouse/lake**, 6k events/sec is **easily handled by Kafka**, etc. The goal is **order-of-magnitude** correctness, not precision.

### Why numbers change the design
Estimates **drive decisions**: **GB/day** → one machine; **TB/hour** → distributed (Spark). 100 events/sec → a simple consumer; 1M/sec → partitioned Kafka + scaled consumers. **$ matters** → partition, compress, tier, right-size.

### Why it matters
Estimation turns a design from **hand-waving into engineering**: it justifies **why** you need distribution (or don't), sizes the infrastructure, and surfaces **cost**. Practice the back-of-envelope method (volume → throughput → storage → cost, rounding to powers of 10) — it's a core system-design and interview skill.
keypoints: Capacity estimation (back-of-envelope math) sizes the system, shows if it fits one machine or needs distribution, and reveals rough cost.
Estimate: data volume (rows×bytes or events/day×bytes), throughput (events/sec, queries/sec — peak & avg), storage (volume×retention), compute, and cost.
Remember compression (~3–10× for columnar reduces storage) and replication (×3 raises it).
Technique: round aggressively, use powers of 10; aim for order-of-magnitude correctness, not precision.
Numbers drive design: GB/day→one machine, TB/hour→distributed (Spark); 100/s→simple consumer, 1M/s→partitioned Kafka + scaled consumers.
Cost (storage $/TB + compute $/hr + egress + scanned-data) justifies partition/compress/tier/right-size — estimation turns hand-waving into engineering.

quiz.q: What's the goal of back-of-the-envelope capacity estimation?
options: Order-of-magnitude correctness — round aggressively (powers of 10) to size the system and reveal rough cost
quiz.explain: Estimation aims for the right order of magnitude (GB vs TB, hundreds vs millions/sec) to drive design decisions and cost — precision isn't the point; the ballpark is.

quiz.q: How do you estimate daily data volume?
options: events/day × bytes/event (or rows × bytes/row) → GB/TB/day, then × retention for total
quiz.explain: Multiply the record rate by the per-record size to get volume per day, then multiply by retention for total storage (adjusting for compression and replication).

quiz.q: Why do capacity estimates change the design?
options: They tell you if it fits one machine or needs distribution, the throughput tier, and the cost — driving tool and scale choices
quiz.explain: GB/day vs TB/hour decides single-machine vs distributed; 100/s vs 1M/s decides simple consumer vs partitioned Kafka; cost drives partition/compress/tier — numbers ground the design.

exercises.prompt: Estimate storage for 100M events/day at ~1 KB each, kept 1 year, columnar-compressed. Show the math.
exercises.solution: **Daily volume:** 100,000,000 events × 1 KB = **100,000,000 KB = ~100 GB/day** (raw). **Yearly (raw):** 100 GB/day × 365 ≈ **36,500 GB ≈ ~36 TB/year** raw. **Compressed:** columnar (Parquet) typically compresses **~3–10×**; at ~5× → 36 TB ÷ 5 ≈ **~7 TB stored** for the year. (If replicated ×3 in some stores, multiply back up — but object-storage durability is built in, so ~7 TB is the figure for a lakehouse.) **What this tells you:** **~7 TB is trivial** for a cloud warehouse/lakehouse (object storage is cheap, and these handle PB) — **no exotic infrastructure needed for storage**. The technique: **round to powers of 10** (100M, 1 KB, 365≈400), compute **volume → × retention → ÷ compression**, and read off the **order of magnitude** (single-digit TB), which is what drives the decision — not the exact figure. This grounds the design ('storage is a non-issue at 7 TB/yr') in **numbers** rather than hand-waving.

exercises.prompt: From 100M events/day, estimate ingest throughput (avg and peak) and what infrastructure handles it.
