Consistency spectrum: strong/linearizable → causal/bounded-staleness/quorum → eventual; pick by correctness needs.
DE: analytics/dashboards tolerate eventual consistency (favor availability/latency); financial/exactly-once needs strong consistency.
lessons.diagram_caption: Replicate for availability/durability; CAP forces a Consistency-vs-Availability choice during a partition (PACELC: else latency vs consistency) — pick by use case.

quiz.q: What does the CAP theorem state?
options: During a network partition, a system must choose between Consistency (may reject requests) and Availability (may serve stale data) — not both
quiz.explain: Partitions are unavoidable in distributed systems; under one you trade C (CP, reject to stay correct) vs A (AP, stay up but possibly stale).

quiz.q: How should consistency choice differ for analytics vs financial data?
options: Analytics/dashboards usually tolerate eventual consistency (favor availability/latency); financial/exactly-once needs strong consistency
quiz.explain: Match the model to correctness needs: stale-by-seconds is fine for dashboards (eventual), but money/exactly-once requires strong consistency.

exercises.prompt: Explain CAP and PACELC, and how you'd choose consistency for (a) a dashboard serving layer and (b) a financial ledger.
exercises.hint: CP vs AP under partition; latency vs consistency else; match to use case.
exercises.solution: **CAP:** in a distributed system, network **partitions** (P) are inevitable; **during** one you can't have both **Consistency** and **Availability**, so you choose: a **CP** system stays **consistent** (every read sees the latest write) but may **reject/timeout** requests it can't safely serve; an **AP** system stays **available** (always responds) but may return **stale** data. **PACELC** completes it: **if Partition → trade A vs C; Else (normal operation) → trade Latency vs Consistency** — i.e. even **without** a failure, **stronger consistency costs latency** (you wait for replicas/quorum). So the consistency-vs-availability/latency trade is **constant**, not just during failures. **(a) Dashboard serving layer → AP / eventual consistency.** Dashboards can tolerate data that's **seconds/minutes stale**, and you want **high availability and low latency** (replicas near users, fast reads). Under PACELC, prefer **latency** (don't pay for strong consistency you don't need); under a partition, prefer **availability** (serve slightly stale numbers rather than error). **(b) Financial ledger → CP / strong consistency.** Money requires **correctness** — no double-spend, no lost transaction — so use **strong/linearizable** consistency (single-leader or quorum with R+W>N, transactions). Under a partition, prefer **consistency**: **reject** a write you can't safely commit rather than risk inconsistency (accept reduced availability); under PACELC, accept **higher latency** for correctness. **The point:** don't default to 'strong everywhere' (needlessly slow/less available) or 'eventual everywhere' (wrong for money) — **choose per use case**, stating the CAP/PACELC trade-off. Articulating this (CP vs AP, latency-vs-consistency, matched to requirements) is exactly what system-design interviews probe.

lessons.title: Delivery guarantees & idempotency
lessons.summary: Distributed messaging offers at-most-once (may lose), at-least-once (may duplicate — the common default), or exactly-once (no loss/dup, via dedup/transactions); since most systems are at-least-once, designing idempotent consumers (apply-twice == apply-once) is what makes retries safe.
lessons.concept:
In distributed pipelines, messages/records can be **lost, duplicated, or reordered** by failures and retries. The **delivery guarantee** and **idempotency** determine correctness — a constant system-design and DE concern.

### The three delivery guarantees
- **At-most-once** — each message delivered **0 or 1** times → **may LOSE data** (fire-and-forget, no retries). Rarely acceptable for data.
- **At-least-once** — each message delivered **1 or more** times → **may DUPLICATE** (a retry after a failed ack re-sends). The **common default** (Kafka, Kinesis, Pub/Sub, SQS) — durable but you must handle duplicates.
- **Exactly-once** — each message effectively processed **once** — **no loss, no duplication**. The hardest to achieve; done via **deduplication**, **transactions**, or **idempotency** + checkpointing.

### Why at-least-once is the norm
Guaranteeing no loss requires **retries** (re-send until acked), and retries can **duplicate** (the original may have succeeded but the ack was lost). So robust systems default to **at-least-once** and push **deduplication** to the consumer/sink.

### Idempotency — the key technique
**Idempotency** means **applying an operation twice has the same effect as once**. If your consumer/sink is **idempotent**, **duplicates are harmless** — so **at-least-once + idempotent processing ⇒ effectively exactly-once** for the end state. Ways to be idempotent:
- **MERGE / upsert by key** — re-applying the same row is a no-op (Delta MERGE, `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT`).
- **Overwrite a partition** — re-running replaces it deterministically (idempotent batch).
- **Dedup on a unique id** — track processed ids (hash set / table / Bloom filter) and skip seen ones.
- **Idempotency keys** — the producer attaches a unique key; the sink ignores repeats (common in APIs/payments).
- **Deterministic transforms** — same input → same output.

### Exactly-once mechanisms
When you need it: **transactional writes** tied to the input offset (Kafka transactions, Flink/Dataflow checkpointed exactly-once, BigQuery Storage Write API), or **idempotent sinks** + offset tracking so a redo produces the same result. (Often 'exactly-once' = at-least-once delivery + idempotent/transactional sink.)

### Why it matters
The practical rule: **distributed delivery is usually at-least-once, so design idempotent consumers** so **retries/duplicates don't double-apply** (double-count revenue, double-charge, corrupt aggregates). 'How do you handle duplicates / ensure exactly-once?' is a standard interview question, and the answer is **idempotency (MERGE/upsert, dedup on id, overwrite partition, idempotency keys)** — not assuming the messaging layer magically prevents duplicates.
lessons.example: A streaming job consumes payments from **Kafka (at-least-once)**. A consumer crash **after processing but before committing the offset** causes the messages to be **redelivered** — duplicates. If the job naively **appended** each payment, it would **double-count revenue**. Instead it's **idempotent**: it **MERGEs by `transaction_id`** into the target (re-applying the same id is a no-op) and/or **dedups on `transaction_id`** (skip seen ids) — so the redelivery is **harmless** and the end state is **exactly-once**. For the warehouse load, it **overwrites the target partition** deterministically (re-running the day replaces it). The design answer to 'ensure exactly-once' is **at-least-once + idempotent sink (MERGE/dedup/overwrite/idempotency keys)**, optionally with **transactional** writes — not relying on the broker to never duplicate.
keypoints: Delivery guarantees: at-most-once (may lose), at-least-once (may duplicate — common default), exactly-once (no loss/dup, hardest).
At-least-once is the norm because no-loss needs retries, and retries can duplicate (ack lost after success).
Idempotency = applying twice == applying once; at-least-once + idempotent processing ⇒ effectively exactly-once end state.
Be idempotent via: MERGE/upsert by key, overwrite a partition, dedup on a unique id, idempotency keys, deterministic transforms.
Exactly-once mechanisms: transactional writes tied to offsets (Kafka txns, Flink/Dataflow checkpoints, BQ Storage Write API) or idempotent sinks + offset tracking.
Design rule: assume at-least-once, make consumers idempotent so duplicates don't double-apply (revenue, charges, aggregates).
lessons.diagram_caption: At-most-once (may lose), at-least-once (may duplicate — common default), exactly-once (via dedup/transactions); idempotent consumers make at-least-once retries safe.

quiz.q: Why is at-least-once the common default, and what must you handle?
options: Guaranteeing no loss needs retries, which can duplicate (ack lost after success) — so you must handle duplicates (idempotency)
quiz.explain: No-loss requires retries; a retry after a lost ack duplicates. So robust systems are at-least-once and consumers must dedup/be idempotent.

quiz.q: How do you make at-least-once processing effectively exactly-once?
options: Make the consumer/sink idempotent — MERGE/upsert by key, dedup on a unique id, overwrite a partition, or use idempotency keys/transactions
quiz.explain: Idempotent processing (apply-twice == apply-once) makes duplicates harmless, so at-least-once delivery yields an exactly-once end state.

exercises.prompt: A streaming payments job double-counts revenue when Kafka redelivers messages after a crash. Diagnose and fix with idempotency.
exercises.hint: At-least-once redelivery; MERGE/dedup by transaction_id; overwrite partition.
exercises.solution: **Diagnose:** Kafka (and most brokers) is **at-least-once** — a consumer crash **after processing but before committing the offset** causes the broker to **redeliver** those messages on restart (duplicates). The job **naively appends/sums** each payment, so the redelivered messages get **counted again** → **double-counted revenue**. The bug isn't the broker; it's that the consumer **isn't idempotent**. **Fix — make processing idempotent so duplicates are harmless:** (1) **Dedup / MERGE by a stable key** — each payment has a unique **`transaction_id`**; instead of appending, **MERGE/upsert by `transaction_id`** into the target (Delta `MERGE`, `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING`), so re-applying the same id is a **no-op** — the redelivery changes nothing. Or maintain a **processed-id set/table** (or a Bloom filter for scale) and **skip** ids already seen. (2) **For aggregates**, derive them from the **deduplicated** facts (so each transaction contributes once), or recompute the affected window from the idempotent base table. (3) **For the batch/warehouse load**, **overwrite the target partition** deterministically (re-running the day **replaces** it rather than adding) — idempotent by construction. (4) Combine with **exactly-once** mechanisms where available — checkpointed exactly-once (Flink/Dataflow) + a **transactional/idempotent sink** (BigQuery Storage Write API, Delta) so a redo produces the same commit. **Result:** **at-least-once delivery + idempotent sink ⇒ exactly-once end state** — redeliveries no longer double-count. The general rule (and interview answer): **assume at-least-once and design idempotent consumers** (MERGE/dedup by key, overwrite partition, idempotency keys), rather than assuming the messaging layer prevents duplicates.

lessons.title: Fault tolerance, backpressure & observability
lessons.summary: Assume everything fails: use retries with backoff and dead-letter queues for errors, queues/backpressure to absorb spikes, checkpoints + replay to recover, and observability (freshness, volume, quality, lineage + alerts) to detect problems — the operational backbone of a reliable pipeline.
lessons.concept:
Distributed systems **fail constantly** — nodes crash, networks drop, sources spike, data goes bad. **Reliability** comes from designing for failure, not hoping it won't happen. These patterns are the operational backbone of any pipeline (and a key system-design dimension).

### Retries + backoff
Transient failures (a brief network blip, a throttled API) recover on **retry**. Use **exponential backoff** (increasing waits) to avoid hammering a struggling dependency, and **cap attempts**. Pair with **idempotency** (prior lesson) so retries don't double-apply.

### Dead-letter queue (DLQ)
A **poison message** (always fails — bad schema, corrupt record) would **block** or infinitely retry. Route it, after **max attempts**, to a **dead-letter queue** for inspection/replay — so one bad record doesn't stall the pipeline.

### Backpressure & buffering
When input **spikes** faster than the consumer can process, you need to **absorb** it, not crash. A **durable queue/log** (Kafka, SQS, Pub/Sub) **buffers** the surge; the consumer **paces** itself (pulls at its rate) — **backpressure**. This **decouples** producer and consumer speeds and smooths spikes (the queue is a shock absorber).

### Checkpoints + replay
For **recovery**, periodically **checkpoint** progress (offsets + state) so a failed job **resumes from the last good state** instead of restarting. **Replay** (re-read from a retained log / seek) lets you **reprocess** after a bug fix or to backfill — which requires **retention** and **idempotent** reprocessing.

### Redundancy & graceful degradation
**Replicas** (prior lesson) survive node loss; **multi-AZ/region** survives bigger failures; **graceful degradation** (serve stale/cached data, shed load) keeps the system useful under stress.

### Observability (you can't fix what you can't see)
Monitor the **four data-quality/health signals**:
- **Freshness** — is data arriving on time? (lag / SLA).
- **Volume** — expected row/byte counts? (anomalies = missing/duplicated data).
- **Quality** — schema, nulls, ranges, uniqueness (data tests / expectations).
- **Lineage** — where data comes from / what it feeds (impact, debugging).
Plus logs/metrics/traces and **alerting** so problems are caught **before users notice**.

### Why it matters
Reliability is a **design dimension**, not an afterthought: **assume everything fails** and build **retries/DLQ** (handle errors), **queues/backpressure** (absorb spikes), **checkpoints/replay** (recover/reprocess), **redundancy** (survive failures), and **observability** (detect issues). 'How do you make this reliable / handle failures / a traffic spike / bad data?' is a standard interview probe, and these patterns are the answer — the operational maturity that separates a robust pipeline from a fragile one.
lessons.example: A streaming ingestion pipeline is made **resilient**: producers write to **Kafka** (a durable **buffer** that absorbs a **10× spike** — backpressure — while consumers pace themselves); the consumer uses **retries with exponential backoff** for transient sink errors and routes **poison messages** (bad schema) to a **dead-letter queue** after 5 attempts (so they don't block); it **checkpoints offsets+state** so a crash **resumes** from the last commit, and **retention** lets it **replay** the last hour after a bug fix (idempotently). **Observability** watches **freshness** (consumer lag vs SLA), **volume** (row counts vs expected — alert on anomalies), **quality** (schema/null/range tests), and **lineage** (impact analysis), with **alerts** paging on-call **before** dashboards go stale. When a source doubled its rate and emitted some malformed records, the **queue absorbed** the surge, the **DLQ** caught the bad records, and **freshness/volume alerts** flagged it — no data lost, no pipeline stall. That's reliability by design.
keypoints: Assume everything fails; build reliability in (retries, DLQ, queues, checkpoints, redundancy, observability) rather than hoping.
Retries with exponential backoff recover transient failures (cap attempts; pair with idempotency); DLQ sets aside poison messages.
Backpressure: a durable queue/log (Kafka/SQS/Pub/Sub) buffers spikes while the consumer paces itself — decouples producer/consumer speeds.
Checkpoints (offsets+state) resume from the last good state; replay (retained log + seek) reprocesses after fixes/backfills (needs idempotency).
Redundancy (replicas, multi-AZ/region) and graceful degradation (stale/cached, load shedding) survive failures.
Observability: monitor freshness, volume, quality, and lineage + logs/metrics/alerts to catch issues before users do.
lessons.diagram_caption: Reliability patterns: retries+backoff & DLQ (errors), queue/backpressure (spikes), checkpoints+replay (recovery), and observability (freshness/volume/quality/lineage).

quiz.q: How do you absorb a sudden 10× traffic spike without crashing the pipeline?
options: Buffer with a durable queue/log (Kafka/SQS/Pub/Sub) and let the consumer pace itself (backpressure) — decoupling producer and consumer speeds
quiz.explain: A durable queue acts as a shock absorber for spikes; the consumer pulls at its own rate (backpressure), so bursts don't overwhelm or crash it.

quiz.q: What are the key data-health signals to observe?
options: Freshness (on time?), volume (expected counts?), quality (schema/nulls/ranges), and lineage (sources/impact) — plus alerting
quiz.explain: Freshness, volume, quality, and lineage are the core data-observability signals; alerting on them catches issues before users do.

exercises.prompt: Make a streaming ingestion pipeline reliable against transient errors, poison messages, traffic spikes, crashes, and bad data. What patterns and why?
exercises.solution: Apply the reliability patterns, each targeting a failure mode: (1) **Transient errors** (network blip, throttled sink) → **retries with exponential backoff** (cap attempts), paired with **idempotent** writes so retries don't double-apply — most blips self-heal. (2) **Poison messages** (bad schema/corrupt record that always fails) → after **max attempts**, route to a **dead-letter queue** for inspection/replay, so **one bad record doesn't block or infinitely retry** the pipeline. (3) **Traffic spikes** (10× burst) → put a **durable queue/log (Kafka/SQS/Pub/Sub)** in front as a **buffer**; the consumer **paces itself** (pulls at its rate) — **backpressure** absorbs the surge instead of crashing, decoupling producer and consumer speeds. (4) **Crashes** → **checkpoint** offsets + state regularly so the job **resumes from the last good state** (no full restart, no loss); ensure **retention** so you can **replay/seek** to **reprocess** after a bug fix or backfill (idempotently). (5) **Bigger failures** → **replicas / multi-AZ** redundancy and **graceful degradation** (serve cached/stale, shed load). (6) **Bad data / detection** → **observability**: monitor **freshness** (consumer lag vs SLA), **volume** (row counts vs expected — anomaly = missing/dup data), **quality** (schema/null/range/uniqueness tests / expectations), and **lineage** (impact analysis), with **alerts** that page on-call **before** users notice. **Why:** distributed systems **fail constantly**, so reliability must be **designed in** — retries/DLQ handle errors, queues/backpressure handle spikes, checkpoints/replay handle recovery, redundancy handles outages, and observability **detects** problems early. Together they turn a fragile pipeline into one that **degrades gracefully and self-heals**, which is exactly the operational maturity system-design interviews assess when they ask 'how do you make this reliable / handle a spike / handle bad data?'

lessons.title: Worked example — event tracking & recommendations
lessons.summary: A capstone design tying batch, streaming, storage, ML, and serving together: an event-tracking pipeline feeding a recommendation system.
lessons.concept:
A **capstone** worked example that combines almost everything: an **event-tracking pipeline** that powers **product recommendations** — blending real-time and batch, storage, ML, and serving.

### The problem
*'Design a system that tracks user events (views, clicks, purchases) and uses them to power product recommendations — both real-time (react to current session) and batch (train models on history).'*

### Step 1: Clarify requirements
- **Use case:** capture events + serve **recommendations** (real-time reaction **and** trained models).
- **Latency:** **mixed** — recs should reflect the **current session** (seconds) **and** be powered by **models trained on history** (batch). → **both** streaming and batch.
- **Volume:** very high (every user interaction) — millions of events/sec at scale.
- **Consumers:** the recommendation **service** (low-latency reads) and **ML training** (batch over history).

### Step 2: Walk the pipeline
@@diagram:recommendation-pipeline

1. **Ingestion:** app emits events → **Kafka** (the event backbone — durable, high-throughput, fan-out).
2. **Real-time path (streaming):** a stream processor computes **live features** (recent activity, session signals) and writes them to a **feature store** (Redis) for **low-latency** access.
3. **Batch path:** events also **archive to the lakehouse** (raw → cleaned), where **ML models** (collaborative filtering, etc.) are **trained on full history** (Spark) on a schedule, and batch features are computed.
4. **Serving:** the **recommendation service** reads **real-time features (Redis)** + the **trained model** to generate recommendations with **low latency**; results may also be cached.
5. **Analytics:** the lakehouse also powers **BI** on engagement and **model evaluation**.

### Step 3: Scale & trade-offs
- **Scale:** millions/sec → **partitioned Kafka + scaled stream consumers**; lakehouse + Spark for big-data training; Redis for fast serving.
- **Architecture:** this is a **Lambda/Kappa-flavored** design — a **streaming path for fresh features** and a **batch path for model training over history**, sharing the Kafka event log and the lakehouse.
- **Trade-offs:** the **mixed latency requirement justifies running both paths** (real-time + batch); complexity is warranted by the recommendation use case.

### The lesson
Real systems often need **both batch and streaming**, **multiple stores** (Kafka, lakehouse, Redis), and **ML** — combined via the **framework**. This capstone shows the **whole curriculum working together**: ingestion, streaming, lakehouse, ML, serving, and polyglot storage, all **driven by requirements**.
keypoints: Capstone: an event-tracking pipeline powering recommendations — combining real-time (current session) and batch (model training on history).
Clarify: capture events + serve recs, mixed latency (seconds for session + batch-trained models → both streaming and batch), very high volume, rec-service + ML consumers.
Ingestion: app events → Kafka (durable, high-throughput event backbone with fan-out to multiple consumers).
Real-time path: stream processor computes live features → feature store (Redis) for low-latency serving; batch path: archive to lakehouse, train ML models on full history (Spark).
Serving: recommendation service reads real-time features + trained model for low-latency recs; lakehouse also powers BI and model evaluation.
A Lambda/Kappa-flavored design (streaming features + batch training sharing Kafka + lakehouse); mixed-latency requirement justifies both paths — the whole curriculum working together.

quiz.q: Why does a recommendation system need both streaming and batch paths?
options: Recs need real-time signals (current session, seconds → streaming) AND models trained on full history (batch) — mixed latency
quiz.explain: Recommendations blend low-latency reaction to the current session (streaming features) with models trained on historical data (batch) — the mixed requirement justifies both.

quiz.q: What role does Kafka play in this design?
options: The durable, high-throughput event backbone that fans out to the streaming path, the batch/lakehouse archive, and other consumers
quiz.explain: Kafka ingests the high-volume event stream durably and fans it out to multiple independent consumers (real-time features, lakehouse archive) — the central event log.

quiz.q: How does the recommendation service achieve low-latency recs?
options: Reading real-time features from a fast store (Redis) plus the pre-trained model — serving-store + model, not a slow scan
quiz.explain: Low-latency serving reads precomputed real-time features from Redis and applies the already-trained model, avoiding slow historical scans at request time.

exercises.prompt: Explain the real-time path vs batch path in this design and why both exist.
exercises.solution: **Real-time path (streaming):** as events flow through **Kafka**, a **stream processor** computes **live features** — the user's **recent activity and current-session signals** (what they just viewed/clicked) — and writes them to a **feature store (Redis)** for **low-latency** access. This lets recommendations **react to the current session within seconds** (e.g. surface items related to what you're viewing **now**). **Batch path:** events are also **archived to the lakehouse** (raw → cleaned), where **ML models** (collaborative filtering, ranking models) are **trained on the full history** (Spark) on a schedule, and **batch features** are computed over long windows. This produces the **learned model** that captures **broad patterns** ('users who bought X also bought Y') from **all** historical data — which **can't** be computed in real time. **Why both:** recommendations need **both** kinds of signal — **fresh, session-level reactivity (streaming)** *and* **deep, history-trained intelligence (batch)** — and **neither alone suffices**: streaming has the **current context** but can't train on history; batch has the **trained model** but is stale to the current session. The **serving layer combines them** (real-time features + trained model) for recs that are **both current and smart**. So the **mixed latency requirement** (seconds + history-trained) **directly produces a two-path design** — the essence of why real recommendation systems blend streaming and batch.

exercises.prompt: Why is this called a 'Lambda/Kappa-flavored' architecture, and what do the paths share?
exercises.hint: Streaming + batch over a shared log/storage.
exercises.solution: It's **Lambda/Kappa-flavored** because it runs a **streaming path** (for fresh, low-latency features) **alongside a batch path** (for model training/aggregation over full history) — the hallmark of **Lambda** (parallel speed + batch layers) — while **sharing the Kafka event log and the lakehouse**, which leans **Kappa** (one event log, **replayable** for reprocessing through the same/derived pipelines). **What the paths share:** (1) the **Kafka event log** — the **single source of events** that **both** paths consume (the stream processor for live features, an archiver/batch loader for history), with Kafka's **fan-out** letting them read independently; and (2) the **lakehouse** — the **durable archive of raw/cleaned events** that the **batch path** trains on and that enables **replay/reprocessing** (re-train or recompute features after a change) and **BI/analytics**. So rather than two **wholly separate** systems duplicating logic (classic Lambda's main drawback), they **share the event backbone (Kafka) and the storage (lakehouse)**, with the streaming path producing **fresh features** and the batch path producing the **trained model**, combined at **serving**. This shared-log, dual-path structure is the **practical real-time-ML architecture**: **stream for freshness, batch for depth, one event log + lakehouse underneath** — capturing Lambda's both-worlds benefit while sharing infrastructure (Kappa's simplification) and enabling **replay**.

exercises.prompt: Identify the polyglot stores in this design and what each is for.
exercises.solution: This design uses **multiple stores (polyglot persistence)**, each for its **best access pattern**: (1) **Kafka** — the **durable event log/streaming backbone**: high-throughput ingestion of the event firehose, buffering, ordering, and **fan-out** to multiple consumers (and replay). It's transport + buffer, not long-term analytical storage. (2) **Lakehouse (object storage + Delta/Iceberg)** — the **durable, cheap, queryable archive** of raw/cleaned events: holds **full history** for **ML training (Spark)**, **batch features**, **BI/analytics**, and **replay/reprocessing**. Optimized for **big scans over history**, not low-latency point reads. (3) **Feature store (Redis)** — the **low-latency serving layer**: holds **precomputed real-time features** keyed by user/item for **sub-millisecond reads** by the recommendation service at request time. Optimized for **fast key lookups**, not history or scans. (Plus a **warehouse/BI** layer over the lakehouse for engagement analytics and model evaluation.) Each store is **matched to its access pattern** — **Kafka** for streaming ingestion/fan-out, **lakehouse** for historical analytics/training, **Redis** for low-latency feature serving — and they're **combined** because no single store does all three well (a firehose log, big-history analytics, and sub-ms lookups have **incompatible optimizations**). This is **textbook polyglot persistence**: use the **right store for each job** and **connect them** (events flow Kafka → stream→Redis and Kafka → lakehouse → train→model), rather than forcing one store to serve ingestion, training, and low-latency serving simultaneously.

exercises.prompt: How does this capstone demonstrate the whole curriculum working together?
exercises.hint: Ingestion, streaming, lakehouse, ML, serving, polyglot, framework.
exercises.solution: It **integrates nearly every track** into one requirement-driven design, showing how the pieces combine in a real system. **Foundations/lifecycle:** events flow **source → ingest → store → process → serve** with cross-cutting concerns. **Pipelines & Streaming:** **Kafka** ingestion (durable log, fan-out, partitioning), a **stream processor** with **event-time features, watermarks, exactly-once, consumer-lag/autoscaling**. **Lakehouse & Storage:** raw/cleaned events archived in a **lakehouse (Delta/Iceberg, medallion)** for history, replay, and training. **Spark/PySpark & ML:** **Spark** trains **models on full history** (and computes batch features). **NoSQL/Caching:** a **Redis feature store** for **low-latency serving** (polyglot persistence — Kafka + lakehouse + Redis, each for its access pattern). **Data Modeling & Warehouse:** the lakehouse also powers **BI/analytics** and **model evaluation**. **Architecture:** a **Lambda/Kappa-flavored** blend of **streaming (fresh features) + batch (trained models)** sharing the event log and storage. **DataOps/Governance:** orchestration, monitoring (lag), quality, and access control wrap it. And it's all derived via the **System Design framework** — **clarify the mixed-latency requirement → walk the lifecycle (both paths) → address scale (millions/sec → partitioned Kafka + Spark + Redis) and trade-offs (both paths justified)**. So the capstone shows that real systems **combine batch and streaming, multiple stores, ML, and analytics**, **chosen and connected by the framework from requirements** — demonstrating the **entire curriculum (ingestion, streaming, lakehouse, Spark/ML, NoSQL/serving, modeling, DataOps/governance, and design) working together** to build a sophisticated, appropriate data platform. It's the synthesis the whole course builds toward.

lessons.diagram_caption: Capstone: events → Kafka → streaming features (Redis) + batch model training (lakehouse/Spark) → low-latency recommendation serving.

lessons.title: Design a data warehouse / lakehouse platform
lessons.summary: The most common DE design: clarify the requirements (BI/analytics, hourly-fresh, moderate-to-large volume), then build sources → ingestion (batch + CDC) → a medallion lakehouse (bronze→silver→gold, Delta/Iceberg) → warehouse/serving → BI/ML, with orchestration, governance, and observability — and state the batch-vs-streaming and one-copy-many-engines trade-offs.
lessons.concept:
**'Design a data platform / warehouse / lakehouse for analytics'** is the most common DE system-design prompt. Apply the **framework**: clarify → walk the lifecycle → scale & trade-offs.

### Step 1: Clarify requirements
- **Use case:** BI dashboards + ad-hoc analytics (+ maybe ML). **Consumers:** analysts (SQL/BI), data scientists.
- **Latency:** typically **hourly/daily** freshness is fine → **batch** (not streaming) unless a real-time need exists.
- **Volume:** moderate-to-large (many sources, GB–TB/day).
- **Sources:** app **OLTP DBs**, **SaaS** (CRM, ads), **events/logs**, files.
- **Constraints:** cloud, budget, team skills, governance/compliance (PII).

### Step 2: Walk the architecture
@@diagram:sd-design-lakehouse

1. **Ingestion** — **batch EL** (Fivetran/Airbyte) for SaaS; **CDC** (Debezium/Datastream) for OLTP DBs to keep them fresh without heavy queries; **Auto Loader/COPY** for files/events. Land in **bronze**.
2. **Storage — a medallion lakehouse** on object storage (S3/GCS): **bronze** (raw, immutable), **silver** (cleaned, deduped, conformed), **gold** (modeled — star schemas/marts/aggregates), in **Parquet/Delta/Iceberg**, **partitioned** by date.
3. **Processing** — **dbt/SQL** (or Spark) transforms bronze→silver→gold with **tests**; orchestrated as a DAG.
4. **Serving** — analysts query **gold** via the **warehouse/lakehouse SQL engine** (BigQuery/Snowflake/Redshift/Databricks SQL) + **BI tool**; **reverse-ETL** pushes metrics back to apps; **ML** trains on the lake.
5. **Cross-cutting** — **orchestration** (Airflow/Composer/Workflows), **governance** (Unity Catalog/Lake Formation/Dataplex — access, PII, lineage), **observability** (freshness/volume/quality/lineage), **cost** controls.

### Step 3: Scale & trade-offs
- **Scale:** the lakehouse + a serverless/elastic warehouse scales to TBs; partition/cluster for cheap queries; right-size compute.
- **Batch vs streaming:** chose **batch** (hourly) — **simpler and cheaper** than streaming; add a **thin streaming path** only if a real-time use case appears (don't over-engineer).
- **One copy, many engines:** the **lakehouse** (open Parquet/Delta) is queried by **many engines** (SQL, Spark, ML) — no data duplication, avoids warehouse lock-in; the warehouse/serving layer is for fast BI.
- **Cost:** scan/compute-driven — partition pruning, columnar, materialized views, right-sized compute.

### The interview answer
A strong response **clarifies** (BI, hourly, volume, sources), **draws** sources→ingest(batch+CDC)→**medallion lakehouse**→warehouse/serving→BI/ML with **orchestration/governance/observability**, and **states trade-offs** (batch over streaming for cost/simplicity; one open lakehouse copy queried by many engines; cost via layout). This is the canonical, requirement-driven DE platform design.
lessons.example: **'Design a platform to power BI from our app DB + Salesforce + event logs.'** **Clarify:** hourly freshness, analysts via BI, moderate volume → **batch ELT**. **Architecture:** **CDC** the app **OLTP** DB (Datastream/Debezium) + **batch EL** Salesforce (Fivetran) + **Auto Loader** the event logs → **bronze** on S3/GCS; **dbt/Spark** transforms **bronze→silver→gold** (star schemas, partitioned Parquet/Delta) with **tests**; analysts query **gold** via BigQuery/Snowflake/Databricks SQL + a BI tool; **Airflow** orchestrates; **Unity Catalog/Lake Formation** governs PII/access/lineage; **observability** watches freshness/volume/quality. **Trade-offs:** **batch** (hourly) beats streaming here (simpler/cheaper, latency not needed); the **open lakehouse** is queried by SQL/Spark/ML (one copy, no lock-in); **cost** controlled by partitioning + right-sized compute. That requirement-driven, framework-structured walk — with trade-offs — is the model answer.
keypoints: Most common DE design: clarify (BI/analytics, hourly freshness, sources, volume, governance), then walk the lifecycle.
Ingestion: batch EL for SaaS + CDC for OLTP DBs + Auto Loader/COPY for files/events → land in bronze.
Storage: a medallion lakehouse (bronze→silver→gold) on object storage in Parquet/Delta/Iceberg, partitioned by date.
Processing: dbt/SQL (or Spark) transforms with tests, orchestrated as a DAG; serving via warehouse/lakehouse SQL + BI, reverse-ETL, ML.
Cross-cutting: orchestration, governance (catalog/PII/lineage), observability (freshness/volume/quality), cost controls.
Trade-offs to state: batch over streaming (cost/simplicity); one open lakehouse copy queried by many engines (no lock-in); cost via layout.
lessons.diagram_caption: Lakehouse platform: sources → ingest (batch + CDC) → medallion lakehouse (Delta/Iceberg) → warehouse/serving → BI/ML, with orchestration, governance, and observability.

quiz.q: For a standard BI analytics platform with hourly freshness, batch or streaming, and why?
options: Batch — hourly freshness doesn't need streaming, and batch is simpler and cheaper; add a thin streaming path only if a real-time need appears
quiz.explain: Match latency to the requirement: hourly BI is fine with batch (simpler/cheaper); don't pay streaming's complexity/cost unless a real-time use case demands it.

quiz.q: Why land data in an open lakehouse (Delta/Iceberg) rather than only loading a warehouse?
options: One open copy is queried by many engines (SQL/Spark/ML) — no duplication, avoids lock-in; the warehouse/serving layer adds fast BI on top
quiz.explain: The open lakehouse decouples storage from compute so multiple engines query one copy; you add a serving layer for fast BI without duplicating or locking in data.

exercises.prompt: Design a data platform for BI from an app OLTP DB, a SaaS source, and event logs. Walk the framework with trade-offs.
exercises.hint: Clarify → ingest(batch+CDC) → medallion lakehouse → serving → cross-cutting → trade-offs.
exercises.solution: **Clarify:** Goal = BI dashboards + ad-hoc analytics for analysts (SQL/BI); **latency** = hourly/daily is fine → **batch ELT**; **volume** = moderate (app DB + SaaS + logs); **constraints** = cloud, budget, PII governance. **Architecture (walk the lifecycle):** **Ingestion** — **CDC** the **OLTP** app DB (Datastream/Debezium) to capture changes without heavy source queries; **batch EL** the **SaaS** source (Fivetran/Airbyte); **Auto Loader/COPY** the **event logs** — all landing in **bronze**. **Storage** — a **medallion lakehouse** on **S3/GCS**: **bronze** (raw/immutable), **silver** (cleaned, deduped, conformed), **gold** (modeled **star schemas / marts / aggregates**), as **partitioned Parquet/Delta/Iceberg**. **Processing** — **dbt/SQL** (or Spark) builds bronze→silver→gold with **data tests**, orchestrated as a **DAG** (Airflow/Composer). **Serving** — analysts query **gold** via the **warehouse/lakehouse SQL engine** (BigQuery/Snowflake/Databricks SQL) + a **BI tool**; optional **reverse-ETL** and **ML** on the lake. **Cross-cutting** — **orchestration**, **governance** (Unity Catalog/Lake Formation/Dataplex for access, **PII**, lineage), **observability** (freshness/volume/quality/lineage + alerts), **cost** controls. **Scale & trade-offs:** the **lakehouse + elastic warehouse** scales to TBs; **partition/cluster** + columnar keep queries cheap. **Trade-offs to state:** chose **batch** (hourly) over **streaming** — **simpler/cheaper**, latency not needed (add a thin streaming path only if a real-time use case appears); used an **open lakehouse** queried by many engines (**one copy, no lock-in**) with a serving layer for fast BI; controlled **cost** via layout + right-sized compute; **CDC** keeps the OLTP source fresh without load. This **requirement-driven, framework-structured** design — with explicit trade-offs — is the canonical answer to the most common DE design prompt.

lessons.title: Design a real-time metrics pipeline
lessons.summary: When low latency IS required: events → a durable log (Kafka/Pub/Sub) → a stream processor doing event-time windowed aggregation (with watermarks and exactly-once) → a fast serving store (OLAP/Redis) → dashboards, plus a lake archive for replay/history (Kappa) — designed for the streaming concerns: windows, watermarks, idempotency, and backpressure.
lessons.concept:
**'Design a real-time analytics / metrics / monitoring dashboard'** is the streaming counterpart to the lakehouse design — used when **low latency genuinely matters**. Apply the framework.

### Step 1: Clarify requirements
- **Use case:** **live** metrics/dashboards (per-minute counts, rolling aggregates), **alerting**.
- **Latency:** **seconds** — this is the requirement that **justifies streaming** (vs batch).
- **Volume/throughput:** high (events/sec).
- **Consumers:** dashboards (low-latency reads), alerting.
- **Correctness:** event-time accuracy (handle late/out-of-order); exactly-once for metrics.

### Step 2: Walk the architecture
@@diagram:sd-design-metrics

1. **Ingestion** — apps emit events to a **durable log** (**Kafka / Pub/Sub / Kinesis**) — partitioned, replayable, fan-out, and a **buffer** (backpressure).
2. **Stream processing** — a **stream processor** (**Flink / Spark Structured Streaming / Dataflow**) computes **event-time windowed aggregations** (per-minute counts, rolling sums), using **watermarks** for late data and **exactly-once/idempotent** writes.
3. **Serving store** — write aggregates to a **fast-read store**: an **OLAP** store (Druid/ClickHouse/Pinot) or **Redis/key-value** for **sub-second** dashboard reads (pre-aggregated).
4. **Dashboards/alerting** — read the serving store; alerts fire on thresholds.
5. **Lake archive (Kappa)** — also archive raw events to the **lakehouse** for **replay/reprocessing** (after a bug fix) and **batch history/training** — one event log feeding both.

### Step 3: Scale & trade-offs
- **Scale:** **partition** the log by key; **scale stream consumers** (autoscaling); pre-aggregate to shrink serving data; the log absorbs spikes (**backpressure**).
- **Streaming concerns:** **event-time windows + watermarks** (correct under late/out-of-order data), **exactly-once/idempotent** sink (no double-counted metrics — see idempotency lesson), **checkpointing** for recovery.
- **Trade-off:** streaming's **complexity/cost** is **justified** by the **sub-second** requirement; if minutes were acceptable, **micro-batch** would be simpler/cheaper.
- **Architecture:** **Kappa** (one streaming path + lake archive for replay) is usually cleaner than **Lambda** (separate batch+stream codebases).

### The interview answer
**Clarify** (low latency = streaming justified), **draw** events→**durable log**→**windowed stream processor**→**fast serving store**→dashboards (+ lake archive for replay), and **address the streaming concerns** (event-time windows, watermarks, exactly-once/idempotency, backpressure, partitioning/scaling). State the **trade-off** that streaming is warranted here by the latency requirement.
lessons.example: **'Design a live dashboard of per-minute active users and error rates.'** **Clarify:** seconds-fresh → **streaming justified**; high event volume; dashboards + alerts. **Architecture:** events → **Kafka** (partitioned by key, durable buffer) → **Flink/Dataflow** computing **1-minute event-time windows** (active users via approx-distinct, error counts) with **watermarks** for late events and **exactly-once** writes → a **fast serving store** (ClickHouse/Druid or Redis) for **sub-second** dashboard reads → dashboards + **threshold alerts**. Also **archive raw events to the lakehouse** for **replay** (re-run after a metric-definition fix) and history. **Scale/trade-offs:** partition Kafka + autoscale consumers; the log absorbs spikes (backpressure); **idempotent sink** so retries don't double-count; **Kappa** (one stream + archive) over Lambda. Streaming's complexity is **warranted by the seconds-latency requirement** — that justification is the key point.
keypoints: Use when low latency (seconds) is genuinely required — the requirement that justifies streaming over batch.
Architecture: events → durable log (Kafka/Pub/Sub/Kinesis) → stream processor (event-time windowed aggregation) → fast serving store → dashboards.
Streaming concerns: event-time windows + watermarks (late/out-of-order), exactly-once/idempotent sink (no double-counted metrics), checkpointing.
Serving: pre-aggregate into an OLAP store (Druid/ClickHouse/Pinot) or Redis for sub-second dashboard reads.
Archive raw events to the lakehouse for replay/reprocessing and batch history (Kappa — one event log feeds both).
Scale via partitioned log + autoscaling consumers + backpressure; state that streaming's cost/complexity is justified by the latency need.
lessons.diagram_caption: Real-time metrics: events → durable log → windowed stream aggregation (watermarks, exactly-once) → fast serving store → dashboards; lake archive for replay (Kappa).

quiz.q: What requirement justifies a streaming (vs batch) metrics pipeline?
options: A genuine low-latency need (seconds-fresh dashboards/alerts) — otherwise micro-batch/batch is simpler and cheaper
quiz.explain: Streaming's complexity/cost is warranted only when sub-second/seconds freshness is actually required; for minutes, micro-batch suffices.

quiz.q: Which streaming concerns must a real-time metrics design address?
options: Event-time windows + watermarks (late/out-of-order), exactly-once/idempotent sink (no double-counted metrics), checkpointing, and backpressure via the log
quiz.explain: Correct real-time metrics need event-time windowing, watermarks, idempotent/exactly-once writes, recovery checkpoints, and a buffering log for spikes.

exercises.prompt: Design a live per-minute metrics dashboard (active users, error rate). Walk the architecture and the streaming concerns.
exercises.hint: Log → windowed stream proc → serving store → dashboard; watermarks, exactly-once, backpressure, archive.
exercises.solution: **Clarify:** seconds-fresh dashboards + alerts → **streaming justified**; high event volume; correctness needs **event-time** accuracy and no double-counting. **Architecture:** (1) **Ingestion** — apps emit events to a **durable log** (**Kafka/Pub/Sub**), **partitioned** by key, **replayable**, acting as a **buffer** (backpressure). (2) **Stream processing** — a **stream processor** (**Flink/Dataflow/Spark Structured Streaming**) computes **1-minute event-time windows**: **active users** (often **approx-distinct/HLL** for cheap cardinality), **error rate** (counts), using **watermarks** to handle **late/out-of-order** events (with allowed lateness) and **exactly-once/idempotent** writes so a retry doesn't **double-count** metrics. (3) **Serving** — write the pre-aggregated metrics to a **fast-read store**: an **OLAP** store (**ClickHouse/Druid/Pinot**) or **Redis**, so dashboards get **sub-second** reads. (4) **Dashboards/alerts** read the serving store; alerts fire on thresholds. (5) **Lake archive (Kappa)** — also archive **raw events** to the **lakehouse** for **replay** (re-run after a metric-definition bug fix, using log/lake retention) and **batch history/training**. **Streaming concerns to address explicitly:** **event-time windows + watermarks** (correctness under lateness), **exactly-once/idempotent sink** (no double-counted metrics), **checkpointing** (recovery), **backpressure** (the log absorbs spikes), and **partitioning + autoscaling consumers** for scale; mitigate **hot-key skew**. **Trade-offs:** streaming's complexity/cost is **justified by the seconds-latency requirement** (if minutes were fine, **micro-batch** would be simpler); choose **Kappa** (one streaming path + archive) over **Lambda** (two codebases). This requirement-driven design with the streaming concerns addressed is the model real-time answer.

lessons.title: Design a CDC ingestion system
lessons.summary: To replicate an operational database into the lake/warehouse without heavy queries: read the DB's transaction log (CDC via Debezium/Datastream) → stream change events through Kafka → apply them with an idempotent MERGE (upsert by primary key) into a mirror table — with an initial snapshot then incremental, per-key ordering, delete/schema-change handling, and exactly-once via dedup.
lessons.concept:
**'How do you get data from an operational database into the warehouse/lake (freshly, without hammering the source)?'** The answer is **Change Data Capture (CDC)** — a classic DE design.

### Step 1: Clarify requirements
- **Goal:** **replicate** an **OLTP** DB (Postgres/MySQL) into the **lake/warehouse**, **fresh** (minutes), **without** heavy queries on the source.
- **Why not batch full-dumps?** Repeated `SELECT *` is **expensive**, **slow**, and **stale** — and misses deletes.
- **Correctness:** capture **inserts, updates, deletes**; preserve **per-row order**; **exactly-once** end state.

### Step 2: Walk the architecture
@@diagram:sd-design-cdc

1. **Capture** — **CDC** reads the database's **transaction log** (Postgres **WAL** / MySQL **binlog**) via **Debezium** / **Datastream** / **DMS** — capturing every change with **low source impact** (it tails the log, not the tables).
2. **Transport** — change events flow through **Kafka** (a topic per table), **partitioned by primary key** so a row's changes stay **ordered**.
3. **Apply (sink)** — a consumer **applies** the change stream to a **mirror table** in the lake/warehouse via an **idempotent MERGE / upsert by primary key**: `WHEN MATCHED AND op=delete THEN DELETE`, `WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE`, `WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT`.
4. **Snapshot + incremental** — an **initial snapshot** of existing rows, then **incremental** CDC for ongoing changes (the standard bootstrap).

### Step 3: Scale & trade-offs / hard parts
- **Idempotency** — the **MERGE by primary key** is **idempotent** (applying a change twice = once), so **at-least-once** redelivery is **safe** (no double-apply); **dedup** within a batch (reduce-to-latest per key) before MERGE.
- **Ordering** — partition Kafka **by primary key** so a row's I/U/D apply **in order** (out-of-order would corrupt state).
- **Deletes** — CDC captures deletes (soft/hard) — apply them (a batch dump would miss them).
- **Schema changes** — handle source **DDL** (column add/drop) — schema evolution on the sink.
- **Lakehouse fit** — the mirror is often a **Delta/Iceberg** table (MERGE-on-read / upserts); **CDF** can propagate changes downstream (Silver/Gold).

### The interview answer
**CDC** (read the **log**, not the tables) → **Kafka** (partition by PK for order) → **idempotent MERGE by PK** into a mirror; **snapshot then incremental**; handle **deletes, ordering, schema changes**, and **exactly-once via idempotent MERGE + dedup**. Emphasize **low source impact** (log-based) and **idempotency/ordering** (the hard parts) — that's what distinguishes a strong CDC design.
lessons.example: **'Replicate our Postgres orders DB into the lakehouse, fresh and without heavy queries.'** **Design:** **Debezium/Datastream** tails the Postgres **WAL** (low source impact) → emits change events (insert/update/delete) to **Kafka**, **partitioned by `order_id`** (per-key ordering) → a consumer **dedups to the latest change per key** per micro-batch, then **MERGEs into a Delta `orders` mirror** by `order_id` (`op=D`→DELETE, `op=U`→UPDATE *, else INSERT). **Bootstrap:** an **initial snapshot** of existing orders, then **incremental** CDC. **Hard parts handled:** **idempotent MERGE** (at-least-once redelivery is safe — no double-apply), **ordering via PK partitioning**, **deletes** applied (a nightly dump would miss them), **schema evolution** for source DDL. **CDF** then propagates changes to Silver/Gold incrementally. The mirror stays a **fresh, exact replica** with minimal source load — the strong CDC answer (log-based capture + idempotent/ordered MERGE).
keypoints: CDC replicates an OLTP DB into the lake/warehouse freshly without heavy source queries — by reading the transaction log, not the tables.
Capture: Debezium/Datastream/DMS tail the WAL/binlog (low source impact); transport via Kafka, partitioned by primary key for per-row ordering.
Apply: an idempotent MERGE/upsert by primary key into a mirror table (handle insert/update/delete) — so at-least-once redelivery is safe.
Bootstrap with an initial snapshot, then incremental CDC for ongoing changes.
Hard parts: idempotency (MERGE + reduce-to-latest dedup), per-key ordering, deletes, and source schema changes (evolution).
Fits the lakehouse (Delta/Iceberg MERGE; CDF propagates changes to Silver/Gold) — vs expensive, delete-missing batch full-dumps.
lessons.diagram_caption: CDC: tail the DB log (Debezium/Datastream) → stream change events (Kafka, partitioned by PK) → idempotent MERGE by primary key into a mirror; snapshot then incremental.

quiz.q: Why use log-based CDC instead of repeated batch SELECT * dumps to replicate a database?
options: CDC tails the transaction log (low source impact), captures every change including deletes, and is fresher — dumps are expensive, stale, and miss deletes
quiz.explain: Log-based CDC reads the WAL/binlog with minimal source load and captures inserts/updates/deletes continuously; batch dumps are heavy, stale, and miss deletes.

quiz.q: How do you apply CDC change events correctly into a mirror table?
options: An idempotent MERGE/upsert by primary key (apply insert/update/delete), with per-key ordering (partition by PK) and dedup — so redelivery is safe
quiz.explain: Apply changes with a MERGE by PK (idempotent), partition by PK for ordering, and dedup to latest — so at-least-once delivery doesn't corrupt or double-apply.

exercises.prompt: Design CDC replication of a Postgres OLTP database into a Delta lakehouse table, fresh and low-impact. Cover capture, transport, apply, bootstrap, and the hard parts.
exercises.hint: WAL via Debezium → Kafka by PK → idempotent MERGE; snapshot+incremental; ordering/deletes/schema/idempotency.
exercises.solution: **Clarify:** replicate Postgres **orders** into a **Delta** mirror, **minutes-fresh**, **low source impact**, capturing **I/U/D**, **exactly-once** end state. **Capture:** use **log-based CDC** — **Debezium** (or **Datastream/DMS**) tails the Postgres **WAL**, emitting a change event per row mutation (with op type and PK). This has **low source impact** (reads the log, not the tables) and is **continuous/fresh**, unlike heavy `SELECT *` dumps (expensive, stale, **miss deletes**). **Transport:** publish change events to **Kafka**, **one topic per table**, **partitioned by primary key** (`order_id`) so all changes to a given row stay **ordered** (out-of-order apply would corrupt state). **Apply (sink):** a consumer (Spark/Flink `foreachBatch`) **reduces to the latest change per key** within each micro-batch (dedup), then runs an **idempotent MERGE** into the **Delta `orders`** mirror by `order_id`: `WHEN MATCHED AND op='D' THEN DELETE`, `WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET *`, `WHEN NOT MATCHED AND op<>'D' THEN INSERT *`. Because MERGE-by-PK is **idempotent**, **at-least-once** redelivery is **safe** (no double-apply) — exactly-once end state. **Bootstrap:** take an **initial snapshot** of existing rows (Debezium snapshot or a one-time load), then switch to **incremental** CDC. **Hard parts handled:** **idempotency** (MERGE + dedup), **per-key ordering** (PK partitioning), **deletes** (applied — a dump would miss them), **schema changes** (handle source DDL via schema evolution on the Delta table), and **late/duplicate** events (dedup + MERGE). **Downstream:** enable **Change Data Feed** on the mirror to propagate changes **incrementally** to Silver/Gold (no full re-scan). **Result:** a **fresh, exact, low-impact** replica. The strong answer emphasizes **log-based capture (low impact)** and the **idempotent, ordered MERGE** (the hard parts) — which is what separates a robust CDC design from a naive append.

lessons.title: Design a data quality & observability system
lessons.summary: To keep data trustworthy: embed quality gates (rules/expectations on freshness, volume, schema, nulls/ranges/uniqueness) into pipelines — pass → publish, fail → quarantine + alert — and track the four observability signals (freshness, volume, quality, lineage) with metrics, SLAs, and on-call alerting, treating data as a product.
lessons.concept:
**'How do you ensure data quality / know when data is wrong / build trust in the data?'** is an increasingly common DE design prompt — and a real job responsibility. The answer combines **quality gates** (prevent bad data) and **observability** (detect issues fast).

### Step 1: Clarify requirements
- **Goal:** **trustworthy** data — catch bad/missing/late/duplicated data **before** it reaches dashboards/ML.
- **Scope:** which datasets, what SLAs (freshness/quality), who's on-call, what's critical.

### Step 2: The design
@@diagram:sd-design-dq

**A) Quality gates in pipelines (prevent):** embed **declarative checks/expectations** at pipeline stages:
- **Freshness** — data arrived within SLA.
- **Volume** — row/byte counts within expected bounds (anomaly = missing/dup).
- **Schema** — expected columns/types (catch drift).
- **Quality rules** — **nulls, ranges, uniqueness, referential** (e.g. `amount ≥ 0`, `id` unique, `status` in set).
On a check: **pass → publish** to the next layer; **fail → quarantine** the bad rows (or fail the run) **+ alert**. Tools: **dbt tests**, **Great Expectations**, **Glue/Lakeflow expectations**, **Soda**.

**B) Observability (detect):** monitor the **four signals** continuously:
- **Freshness, Volume, Quality, Lineage** — across all tables, with **metrics**, **anomaly detection**, **dashboards**, and **alerting** (page on-call). Tools: **Monte Carlo**, **dbt/Elementary**, **OpenLineage**, platform monitoring.

**C) Process/culture:** **data SLAs/SLOs**, **data contracts** (producers commit to a schema/quality), **incident response** (runbooks, blameless post-mortems), and **ownership** (treat **data as a product**).

### Step 3: Trade-offs
- **Fail vs quarantine** — hard-fail for **critical invariants** (stop bad data); **quarantine + alert** to keep pipelines flowing while isolating bad rows (choose deliberately).
- **Coverage vs noise** — enough checks to catch real issues, not so many that alerts are ignored (alert fatigue); start from **recommendations** + known invariants.
- **Prevention vs detection** — gates **prevent**, observability **detects**; you need **both**.

### The interview answer
**Quality gates** (declarative rules/expectations on freshness/volume/schema/quality → pass=publish, fail=quarantine+alert) **in the pipelines**, plus **observability** (monitor freshness/volume/quality/lineage with metrics + anomaly detection + alerting), wrapped in **process** (SLAs, data contracts, incident response, ownership — **data as a product**). Emphasize **both prevention and detection**, and the **fail-vs-quarantine** and **coverage-vs-noise** trade-offs.
lessons.example: **'Build data quality for our analytics platform.'** **Design:** embed **expectations** in each pipeline (dbt tests / Great Expectations / Lakeflow expectations): **freshness** (data within SLA), **volume** (row counts within bounds), **schema** (columns/types), and **quality rules** (`order_id` unique + not-null, `amount ≥ 0`, `status` in a set). On a batch: **good rows publish** to the next layer; **bad rows route to a quarantine table** (and a **hard-fail** rule stops the run if a critical invariant like row-count collapse trips) — with **alerts** to the on-call. Continuously, an **observability** layer (Monte Carlo / Elementary + OpenLineage) tracks **freshness/volume/quality/lineage** across all tables with **anomaly detection** and **dashboards/SLAs**, paging **before** users notice. **Process:** **data contracts** with producers, **runbooks**, **blameless post-mortems**, and **ownership** (data as a product). **Trade-offs:** **fail** critical invariants, **quarantine + alert** the rest; tune checks to avoid **alert fatigue**. This prevention-plus-detection design keeps data **trustworthy** — the strong answer to the data-quality prompt.
keypoints: Goal: trustworthy data — catch bad/missing/late/duplicated data before it reaches dashboards/ML, via prevention + detection.
Quality gates in pipelines: declarative rules/expectations on freshness, volume, schema, and quality (nulls/ranges/uniqueness/referential).
On a check: pass → publish; fail → quarantine bad rows (or hard-fail) + alert (dbt tests, Great Expectations, Lakeflow/Glue expectations, Soda).
Observability: continuously monitor the four signals (freshness, volume, quality, lineage) with metrics, anomaly detection, and alerting (Monte Carlo, Elementary, OpenLineage).
Process: data SLAs/SLOs, data contracts, incident response (runbooks/post-mortems), ownership — treat data as a product.
Trade-offs: fail (critical invariants) vs quarantine+alert; enough coverage without alert fatigue; you need both prevention and detection.
lessons.diagram_caption: Embed quality gates (freshness/volume/schema/quality) in pipelines — pass→publish, fail→quarantine+alert — and observe the four signals with metrics, SLAs, and lineage.

quiz.q: What's the difference between quality gates and observability in a data-quality system?
options: Gates PREVENT bad data (rules/expectations in pipelines: pass→publish, fail→quarantine/alert); observability DETECTS issues (monitor freshness/volume/quality/lineage) — you need both
quiz.explain: Quality gates stop bad data at pipeline stages; observability continuously detects anomalies across the estate. Prevention + detection together build trust.

quiz.q: What are the four data-observability signals?
options: Freshness (on time?), Volume (expected counts?), Quality (schema/nulls/ranges/uniqueness), and Lineage (sources/impact)
quiz.explain: Freshness, volume, quality, and lineage are the core signals to monitor (with alerting) so data issues are caught before users notice.

exercises.prompt: Design a data quality & observability system for an analytics platform. Cover prevention (gates), detection (observability), process, and trade-offs.
exercises.hint: Expectations in pipelines (pass/quarantine/fail+alert); four signals + anomaly detection; SLAs/contracts; fail-vs-quarantine.
exercises.solution: **Clarify:** goal = **trustworthy** data; catch bad/missing/late/duplicated data **before** dashboards/ML; define **SLAs**, critical datasets, and on-call. **A) Prevention — quality gates in pipelines:** embed **declarative checks/expectations** at each stage (using **dbt tests / Great Expectations / Lakeflow/Glue expectations / Soda**): **freshness** (data arrived within SLA), **volume** (row/byte counts within expected bounds — anomaly = missing/dup), **schema** (expected columns/types — catch drift), and **quality rules** (**nulls, ranges, uniqueness, referential**: `id` unique+not-null, `amount ≥ 0`, `status` in a set). **On a check:** **pass → publish** to the next layer; **fail → quarantine** the offending rows to a side table (keep good rows flowing) **and alert**; for **critical invariants** (e.g. row-count collapse, a key invariant), **hard-fail the run** to stop bad data propagating. **B) Detection — observability:** continuously monitor the **four signals** (**freshness, volume, quality, lineage**) across **all** tables with **metrics**, **anomaly detection**, **dashboards/SLAs**, and **alerting** that **pages on-call before users notice** (tools: **Monte Carlo, Elementary, OpenLineage**, platform monitoring). **Lineage** powers **impact analysis** (what breaks downstream) and **root-cause** (what fed a wrong number). **C) Process/culture:** **data SLAs/SLOs**, **data contracts** (producers commit to schema/quality), **incident response** (runbooks, **blameless post-mortems**), and **ownership** — treat **data as a product**. **Trade-offs:** **fail vs quarantine** — hard-fail **critical** invariants, **quarantine + alert** the rest to keep pipelines flowing (choose per dataset); **coverage vs noise** — enough checks to catch real issues without **alert fatigue** (start from recommended rules + known invariants, tune thresholds); **prevention + detection** — gates stop bad data, observability catches what slips through, and you need **both**. This design keeps data **trustworthy** end to end — the strong answer to 'how do you ensure data quality and know when data is wrong?'

lessons.title: System Design interview prep & cheat sheet
lessons.summary: Consolidated rapid-review: high-frequency answers and a mock interview.
lessons.concept:
Use the framework: clarify → high-level → deep dive → scale & trade-offs → wrap up. Latency decides batch vs streaming. Estimate with events×bytes→TB. Match storage to access pattern. Behavioral: STAR.

Every lesson in this track also has its own '💼 Interview questions' panel and a cheat-sheet button. See the Go-deeper guide for the full mock interview.
keypoints: Framework: clarify requirements → high-level → deep dive → scale/trade-offs → wrap up.
Batch vs streaming: latency decides; don't stream what daily batch serves.
Capacity: events × bytes → TB/yr; QPS = events ÷ 86,400; state assumptions.
Trade-offs: latency ↔ cost ↔ complexity; match storage to access pattern.
Behavioral: STAR; interviewers evaluate thinking, not a 'correct' answer.

quiz.q: What should you do FIRST in a system-design interview?
quiz.explain: The right design depends entirely on requirements — never assume.

quiz.q: How do you decide batch vs streaming?
options: Let the latency requirement decide — seconds→stream, minutes→micro-batch, hours→batch
quiz.explain: Streaming adds cost/complexity; don't stream what a daily batch can serve.

exercises.prompt: Do the 10-question System Design mock (Go-deeper guide) out loud, using the framework.
exercises.hint: framework, batch/stream, capacity, trade-offs, worked example, storage, scaling, real-time, process, STAR.
exercises.solution: Reread any shaky topic's lesson and retry until crisp.

tracks.summary: Tie it all together: a framework for designing data systems, choosing batch vs streaming, capacity/cost estimation, trade-offs, storage/tool selection, scaling, worked examples, and acing the data-engineering interview.

tracks.title: Interview Question Bank (50LPA+)
tracks.blurb: A research-backed bank of real questions from FAANG, Goldman Sachs and major banks — organized by round and by company (per-company playbooks for 16 top employers - Amazon, Google, Meta, Microsoft, Netflix, Databricks, Uber, Airbnb, LinkedIn, Apple, Stripe, plus banks/finance: Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan, Morgan Stanley, Capital One, Bloomberg), with detailed solutions, code, and links to the matching tutorials.

lessons.title: The 50LPA+ interview loop (how it works)
lessons.summary: The real 2026 structure at FAANG, Goldman & banks — the rounds, what each tests, and how to prepare per stage.
lessons.concept:
High-paying data-engineering interviews (50LPA+ / senior bands) follow a predictable **multi-round loop** in 2026. Knowing the map is half the battle.

**The stages:**
1. **Recruiter screen** (30-60 min) — background, motivation, salary band, basic tech fit.
2. **Technical screen** (CoderPad-style) — **SQL + a coding problem**; this is the first real gate. Goldman, Google, JPMorgan all start here.
3. **Onsite loop** (virtual, 4-5 rounds of 45-60 min):
   - **SQL round** — medium-to-hard: multi-joins, **window functions**, CTEs, optimization.
   - **Coding / DSA round** — Python data structures & algorithms (arrays, strings, trees, graphs).
   - **Data modeling round** — schema design, star vs snowflake, **SCD**, fact grain. (Meta weights this most.)
   - **System / pipeline design round** — design a warehouse, batch ETL, or streaming pipeline; **trade-off reasoning**.
   - **Behavioral round** — STAR stories; Amazon **Leadership Principles** (bring 12-15 stories), Goldman **values**.
4. Sometimes a **big-data / distributed round** (Spark, Hadoop) — common at Goldman and banks.

**2026 shifts:** design prompts now include **LLM/RAG pipelines** ('process 10K docs/day with rate limits, retries, cost budgets'), and interviewers test **architecture trade-off reasoning** and **cloud fluency** over rote recall.

Use the rounds below as a study plan: each has its own question bank, difficulty-tagged, with detailed solutions. Open **Go deeper** for the full per-round breakdown and per-company timing.
lessons.example:
# A typical Goldman / Google / JPMorgan loop, end to end
Recruiter screen      -> fit + band
Technical screen      -> 1 SQL + 1 coding (CoderPad), ~60 min      [GATE]
Onsite (same day):
  Round 1  SQL          (medium-hard: windows, CTEs)
  Round 2  Coding/DSA   (Python: arrays/strings/trees/graphs)
  Round 3  Data modeling(schema, SCD2, fact grain)
  Round 4  System design(pipeline/warehouse + trade-offs)
  Round 5  Behavioral   (STAR; LPs / company values)
  [+ Big-data/Spark round at banks]
keypoints: The loop: recruiter → technical screen (SQL+coding) → onsite (SQL, coding, modeling, design, behavioral) → offer.
The technical screen (CoderPad SQL+coding) is the first real gate — Goldman/Google/JPMorgan all use it.
Onsite weights: SQL + data modeling + system design are the core DE differentiators.
Behavioral is scored, not casual: STAR; Amazon LPs (12-15 stories); Goldman values (Partnership, Client Service, Integrity, Excellence).
2026 trend: LLM/RAG pipeline design prompts + trade-off reasoning + cloud fluency over rote recall.
Banks (Goldman, JPMorgan) add a distributed-systems / Spark round and value-fit emphasis.

quiz.q: What is usually the first real technical gate in a 2026 DE loop?
options: The CoderPad technical screen (SQL + a coding problem)
quiz.explain: Most top firms gate on a CoderPad-style screen with one SQL and one coding question before the onsite.

quiz.q: Which onsite round does Meta weight most heavily for DEs?
options: Data modeling (often via a product-sense prompt)
quiz.explain: Meta's DE loop leans hardest on data modeling, usually introduced through a product-sense scenario.

quiz.q: A 2026-specific design prompt you should prepare for is:
options: Design a pipeline to process 10K docs/day via an LLM with rate limits, retries, cost budgets
quiz.explain: Interviewers increasingly pose LLM/RAG ingestion design with operational constraints — a 2026 shift.

exercises.prompt: List the five core onsite rounds for a DE loop.
exercises.solution: SQL, coding/DSA, data modeling, system/pipeline design, and behavioral (plus a distributed/Spark round at banks).

exercises.prompt: How many behavioral stories should you bring to an Amazon loop, and why?
exercises.solution: 12-15 — Amazon embeds Leadership Principle questions in every round, including coding, so 5 stories won't cover them.

exercises.prompt: Name two 2026 shifts in what design rounds test.
exercises.solution: LLM/RAG pipeline design with operational constraints (rate limits/retries/cost), and architecture trade-off reasoning + cloud fluency over rote recall.

lessons.diagram_caption: Recruiter → technical screen (SQL+coding) → onsite loop (SQL, coding, modeling, design, behavioral) → offer.

lessons.title: SQL round — question bank
lessons.summary: Easy→hard SQL asked at Goldman, Meta, Amazon & banks — window functions, CTEs, gaps-and-islands, dedup — with full solutions.
lessons.concept:
SQL is **non-negotiable** and the most common DE gate. Expect medium-to-hard: multi-table joins, **window functions** (ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK, LAG/LEAD, running totals), **CTEs**, GROUP BY/HAVING, CASE, and **query optimization**. The hard ones are pattern-based: top-N per group, gaps-and-islands (sessionization), deduplication keeping the latest, funnels, and medians.

**A few you must be able to write cold:**

*Easy — 2nd highest salary:*
```sql
SELECT MAX(salary) FROM emp WHERE salary < (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM emp);
-- or: DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) = 2
```
*Medium — top 3 products per category:*
```sql
SELECT * FROM (
  SELECT p.*, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY sales DESC) rn
  FROM products p) t WHERE rn <= 3;
```
*Hard — dedupe keeping latest row per key:*
```sql
WITH r AS (SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY id ORDER BY updated_at DESC) rn FROM t)
SELECT * FROM r WHERE rn = 1;
```

**How to win the round:** clarify the schema, state assumptions, prefer **window functions over correlated subqueries**, handle NULLs and ties explicitly, and mention indexes/partition pruning when asked about performance. Open **Go deeper** for the full bank (easy→hard) with detailed solutions and the patterns behind them.
lessons.example:
-- Hard: month-over-month revenue growth %
WITH m AS (
  SELECT date_trunc('month', ts) mo, SUM(amount) rev
  FROM orders GROUP BY 1)
SELECT mo, rev,
       ROUND(100.0*(rev - LAG(rev) OVER (ORDER BY mo))
             / LAG(rev) OVER (ORDER BY mo), 1) AS growth_pct
FROM m ORDER BY mo;
keypoints: Master window functions: ROW_NUMBER/RANK/DENSE_RANK, LAG/LEAD, running totals, PARTITION BY.
Top-N per group = ROW_NUMBER() in a subquery filtered to rn <= N.
Dedup keeping latest = ROW_NUMBER() PARTITION BY key ORDER BY updated_at DESC, keep rn=1.
Gaps-and-islands (sessionization) = difference of row numbers / running gaps.
Prefer window functions over correlated subqueries; handle NULLs and ties explicitly.
For performance: indexes, partition pruning, avoid SELECT *, push filters down.

quiz.q: Top-N rows per group is cleanest with:
options: ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY grp ORDER BY metric DESC) filtered to rn<=N
quiz.explain: Window ROW_NUMBER partitioned by group, filtered in an outer query, is the standard top-N-per-group pattern.

quiz.q: To keep only the latest row per id you:
options: ROW_NUMBER() PARTITION BY id ORDER BY updated_at DESC and keep rn=1
quiz.explain: Rank rows within each id by recency and keep the first — deterministic dedup.

quiz.q: RANK vs DENSE_RANK differ in that:
options: RANK leaves gaps after ties, DENSE_RANK does not
quiz.explain: After tied rows, RANK skips numbers (1,1,3) while DENSE_RANK is contiguous (1,1,2).

exercises.prompt: Write 2nd-highest-salary two ways.
exercises.solution: MAX(salary) WHERE salary < (SELECT MAX...); or DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC)=2.

exercises.prompt: Give the pattern for sessionizing events with a 30-min inactivity gap.
exercises.solution: Flag a new session when ts - LAG(ts) OVER (PARTITION BY user ORDER BY ts) > 30 min; cumulative SUM of the flag = session id.

exercises.prompt: How do you compute a running 7-day total?
exercises.solution: SUM(metric) OVER (ORDER BY day ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW).

exercises.prompt: Name three ways to make a slow analytical query faster.
exercises.solution: Add/leverage indexes, partition-prune (filter on partition key), select fewer columns, pre-aggregate/materialize.

lessons.diagram_caption: A favorite SQL interview question: the order you WRITE clauses vs the order the engine EVALUATES them (FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY → LIMIT).

lessons.title: Coding & DSA round — question bank
lessons.summary: Python data-structures & algorithms as asked in CoderPad screens — plus DE-flavored problems (dedupe streams, merge sorted files), with solutions.
lessons.concept:
The coding round is Python **DSA** (CoderPad/HackerRank-style): arrays, strings, hashing, two-pointers, sliding window, stacks, trees, graphs (BFS/DFS), heaps. For DE specifically, expect **data-flavored** twists: deduplicate a stream, top-K frequent items, merge K sorted files, parse/aggregate logs, chunk a large file.

**What's tested:** clarity of thought, correct edge-case handling, and complexity (state Big-O). Talk before you type; confirm input/output; test on a small case.

*Medium — top-K frequent elements (heap, O(n log k)):*
```python
import heapq
from collections import Counter
def top_k(nums, k):
    return [x for x,_ in Counter(nums).most_common(k)]
```
*DE-flavored — merge K sorted files (streaming, low memory):*
```python
import heapq
def merge_sorted(iters):
    # heapq.merge streams without loading all into RAM
    yield from heapq.merge(*iters)
```
*Medium — sliding-window max sum of size k:*
```python
def max_window(a, k):
    s = sum(a[:k]); best = s
    for i in range(k, len(a)):
        s += a[i] - a[i-k]; best = max(best, s)
    return best
```

**How to win:** narrate your approach and complexity first, handle empty/None and duplicates, and for DE problems mention **memory** (stream, don't load everything) and **idempotency**. Full bank with solutions in **Go deeper**.
keypoints: Core: arrays/strings, hashing, two-pointers, sliding window, stack, trees, graphs (BFS/DFS), heaps.
Always state time/space complexity and handle edge cases (empty, None, duplicates, ties).
Top-K = heap or Counter.most_common; running window = add new minus old.
DE-flavored: dedupe streams, merge K sorted (heapq.merge), parse logs, chunk large files.
Mention memory: stream/iterate instead of loading everything into RAM.
Narrate approach before coding; test on a small example.

quiz.q: Top-K frequent elements is efficiently done with:
options: A hash count + a heap of size k (O(n log k))
quiz.explain: Count with a hash map, then keep a size-k heap (or Counter.most_common) — O(n log k).

quiz.q: Merging many sorted files without exhausting RAM uses:
options: Loading all into a list and sorting
A k-way merge with a heap (heapq.merge) streaming from each file
quiz.explain: A heap-based k-way merge streams one element at a time from each source — bounded memory.

quiz.q: In a DE coding round you should additionally call out:
options: Memory use and idempotency (stream, don't load all; safe reruns)
quiz.explain: DE problems reward awareness of memory footprint and idempotent/restartable processing.

exercises.prompt: Compute max sum of any size-k window in O(n).
exercises.solution: Keep a running sum; for each new element add a[i] and subtract a[i-k]; track the max.

exercises.prompt: Deduplicate a stream keeping the latest per id with bounded memory.
exercises.solution: Iterate in time order into a dict keyed by id (last write wins); O(n) time, O(distinct ids) space.

exercises.prompt: How would you find the top 10 products by sales from a 1TB file on a laptop?
exercises.solution: Stream/chunk the file (or DuckDB over Parquet), aggregate counts in a dict/heap, keep top-10 — never load 1TB into RAM.

exercises.prompt: What three things do you state before coding?
exercises.solution: Clarify input/output + constraints, outline the approach, and the time/space complexity.

lessons.title: Data modeling round — question bank
lessons.summary: Schema design, star vs snowflake, SCD Type 1/2, fact grain, late-arriving data — the round Meta weights most — with worked answers.
lessons.concept:
Data modeling separates DEs from generic coders, and **Meta weights it most** (often via a product-sense prompt: 'model the data for X'). Expect: dimensional modeling (**star vs snowflake**), choosing the **fact grain**, **slowly changing dimensions** (Type 1 overwrite vs **Type 2** history), surrogate keys, handling **late-arriving** data, and normalization trade-offs.

**The method:** clarify the business questions → pick the **grain of the fact** (one row per what?) → identify dimensions and measures → choose SCD per dimension → note keys, partitioning, and idempotent loads.

*Classic: model an e-commerce orders mart.*
- **Grain:** one row per order line item.
- **Facts:** `fct_order_items` (qty, price, discount, FKs).
- **Dims:** `dim_customer`, `dim_product`, `dim_date`, `dim_store` — **SCD Type 2** on customer & product (keep history for correct point-in-time reporting).
- **Why star:** fewer joins, BI-friendly, fast aggregation vs snowflake's normalized depth.

*SCD Type 2 mechanics:* add `valid_from`, `valid_to`, `is_current`; on change, close the old row and insert a new versioned row (via MERGE). This preserves history so 'revenue by customer tier' is correct as of each order date.

**How to win:** always **name the grain first**, justify star vs snowflake by query pattern, and pick SCD type by whether history matters. Full bank in **Go deeper**.
lessons.example:
-- SCD Type 2 upsert (close old, insert new version)
MERGE INTO dim_customer d USING staging s ON d.cust_id = s.cust_id AND d.is_current
