WHEN MATCHED AND (d.tier <> s.tier) THEN UPDATE SET d.valid_to = current_date, d.is_current = false;
-- then INSERT the new version with valid_from = current_date, is_current = true
keypoints: Always state the FACT GRAIN first (one row per …) — the most common follow-up.
Star (denormalized dims) = fewer joins, fast BI; snowflake = normalized, less redundancy, more joins.
SCD Type 1 overwrites (no history); Type 2 versions rows (valid_from/valid_to/is_current) to keep history.
Use surrogate keys; SCD2 enables correct point-in-time / as-of reporting.
Handle late-arriving data with event-time + reprocessing/backfill of the affected partitions.
Pick the model by query pattern and whether history matters — justify the trade-off.

quiz.q: The single most important thing to define for a fact table is:
quiz.explain: The grain (one row per order line, per session, etc.) determines everything else in the model.

quiz.q: You must report 'customer tier at time of purchase' historically. Which SCD?
quiz.explain: Type 2 keeps history so point-in-time attributes are correct; Type 1 would lose the past tier.

quiz.q: Star schema is preferred over snowflake mainly because:
options: Denormalized dimensions mean fewer joins and faster BI aggregation
quiz.explain: Star trades some redundancy for fewer joins and faster, simpler analytical queries.

exercises.prompt: Model a ride-sharing trips mart: grain, facts, dims.
exercises.solution: Grain = one row per completed trip; fct_trips (distance, fare, duration, FKs); dims: dim_rider, dim_driver, dim_date, dim_location (SCD2 on rider/driver attributes).

exercises.prompt: When do you choose SCD Type 1 over Type 2?
exercises.solution: Type 1 when history doesn't matter (correct a typo, current-only attribute); Type 2 when you must report attributes as-of past events.

exercises.prompt: How do you handle a trip event that arrives 2 days late?
exercises.solution: Partition by event date, and on late arrival reprocess/backfill that date's partition idempotently rather than appending to today.

exercises.prompt: Star vs snowflake for a high-concurrency BI dashboard — pick and justify.
exercises.solution: Star — denormalized dims reduce joins and speed aggregation under concurrency; snowflake's extra joins hurt dashboard latency.

lessons.diagram_caption: The star schema — a central fact table joined to denormalized dimensions — is the default answer to most modeling interview questions; know when to snowflake, when to use OBT.

lessons.title: Big data & Spark round — question bank
lessons.summary: Spark internals asked at Goldman, banks & FAANG — shuffles, skew, joins, partitioning, OOM, AQE — with precise answers.
lessons.concept:
Banks (Goldman, JPMorgan) and FAANG often add a **distributed-systems / Spark** round. They probe whether you understand **why** Spark behaves as it does, not just the API.

**The greatest hits:**
- **Narrow vs wide transformations** — narrow (map/filter) stay on-partition; wide (groupBy/join/distinct) trigger a **shuffle** (network + disk) — the main cost.
- **Data skew** — one key dominates a partition → straggler task. Fix with salting, AQE skew join, or broadcast.
- **Joins** — **broadcast** the small side (avoids shuffle) when it fits; otherwise sort-merge. Know the broadcast threshold.
- **repartition vs coalesce** — repartition shuffles to increase/balance; coalesce reduces partitions without a full shuffle.
- **Caching** — persist reused DataFrames; choose storage level; unpersist when done.
- **OOM / spills** — too-large partitions or wide aggregations; fix partition sizing, avoid collect(), increase parallelism.
- **AQE** (Adaptive Query Execution) — runtime re-optimization: coalesces shuffle partitions, handles skew, switches join strategy.

**How to win:** reason from the **shuffle** and **partitioning**, quantify (partition size ~128MB), and always name the fix for skew and the join-strategy choice. Full bank in **Go deeper**.
lessons.example:
# Broadcast the small dimension to avoid a shuffle on a big join
from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast
fct.join(broadcast(dim_small), 'product_id')   # map-side join, no shuffle of fct
# Skew fix: salt the hot key, or rely on AQE skew-join handling
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.enabled', True)
keypoints: Wide transformations (join/groupBy/distinct) cause shuffles — the dominant cost; narrow ones don't.
Skew = one fat key → straggler; fix with salting, AQE skew join, or broadcast.
Broadcast the small side to avoid a shuffle when it fits in memory; else sort-merge join.
repartition shuffles to balance/increase partitions; coalesce reduces without full shuffle.
OOM/spills come from oversized partitions or collect(); size partitions (~128MB), raise parallelism.
AQE re-optimizes at runtime: coalesces shuffle partitions, handles skew, picks join strategy.

quiz.q: Which is the dominant performance cost in Spark jobs?
options: The shuffle caused by wide transformations (join/groupBy)
quiz.explain: Shuffles move data across the network and to disk; minimizing/optimizing them is the core of Spark tuning.

quiz.q: A join where one side is small enough to fit in memory should use:
options: A broadcast (map-side) join to avoid shuffling the big side
quiz.explain: Broadcasting the small side sends it to every executor, avoiding a shuffle of the large table.

quiz.q: One key has 90% of the rows, causing a straggler task. This is:
options: Data skew — fix with salting, AQE skew join, or broadcast
quiz.explain: Skew concentrates work on one partition; salting/AQE/broadcast redistribute it.

exercises.prompt: Explain narrow vs wide transformations with examples.
exercises.solution: Narrow (map, filter) compute within a partition, no shuffle; wide (groupBy, join, distinct) repartition data across the cluster, causing a shuffle.

exercises.prompt: Your join OOMs on a 2B-row table. Walk through fixes.
exercises.solution: Check skew (salt hot keys/AQE), broadcast if one side fits, size partitions (~128MB) and raise shuffle partitions, avoid collect(), filter/project before the join.

exercises.prompt: repartition(200) vs coalesce(10) — when each?
exercises.solution: repartition to increase/rebalance partitions (full shuffle, e.g. before a heavy stage); coalesce to reduce partitions cheaply (e.g. before writing fewer output files) without a full shuffle.

exercises.prompt: What does AQE do for you at runtime?
exercises.solution: Coalesces shuffle partitions to the right number, handles skewed joins, and can switch join strategies based on actual runtime statistics.

lessons.diagram_caption: Big-data interviews often start with the Hadoop stack and how the cloud decoupled storage from compute — HDFS/YARN/MapReduce and the tools that grew around them.

lessons.title: System & pipeline design round — question bank
lessons.summary: The design round decoded — a repeatable 6-step method, plus full worked designs (e-commerce warehouse, fraud streaming, CDC, and a 2026 LLM/RAG pipeline).
lessons.concept:
The design round tests whether you can architect a **data system end-to-end** and reason about **trade-offs** — the strongest senior signal. Use a repeatable method so you never freeze.

**The 6-step method:** (1) **Clarify** requirements, scale, SLAs, batch vs stream; (2) **Estimate** volume/sec, storage, QPS, latency; (3) **High-level** architecture — draw **ingest → store → process → serve**; (4) **Deep-dive** schema, partitioning, formats, key components; (5) **Trade-offs** — bottlenecks, failure modes, cost, alternatives; (6) **Wrap** — monitoring/observability, what you'd improve.

**Worked example — e-commerce analytics warehouse:** Kafka Connect → **Bronze** (raw events on object storage) → **Silver** (dbt clean/dedupe) → **Gold** (pre-aggregated marts) on Snowflake/Databricks, separate compute for ETL vs BI, **SCD2** on customer/product dims, partition by event date, idempotent loads, freshness + volume monitors.

**2026 prompt — LLM document pipeline:** 'process 10K docs/day through an LLM with rate limits, retries, cost budgets.' Answer: queue + workers with **backoff/retry**, idempotent by doc-id, batch + cache embeddings, a **cost budget guard**, dead-letter for failures, and store vectors keyed by chunk-id (the RAG-ingestion capstone).

**How to win:** drive the conversation, **state assumptions**, draw the data flow, and always name the trade-off. Full worked designs (fraud streaming, CDC, metrics platform) in **Go deeper**.
lessons.example:
# Real-time fraud detection (worked sketch)
Txn events -> Kafka (100+ partitions) -> Flink (stateful: features + rules/model, event-time + watermarks)
          -> alerts topic -> action service ;  also -> lake (Iceberg) for training/replay
# Trade-offs: exactly-once vs latency; state size vs window; model in-stream vs lookup
keypoints: Use the 6-step method: clarify → estimate → high-level → deep-dive → trade-offs → wrap.
Always draw ingest → store → process → serve, and state your assumptions.
Batch analytics: medallion (bronze/silver/gold) + dbt + SCD2 + partitioning + idempotency.
Streaming: Kafka → Flink/Spark (event-time, watermarks, exactly-once) → serve + lake for replay.
2026 LLM pipeline: queue+workers, backoff/retry, idempotent by id, batch/cache embeddings, cost-budget guard, dead-letter.
Name trade-offs explicitly: latency vs cost, exactly-once vs throughput, normalize vs denormalize.

quiz.q: The first step of the design method is:
options: Clarify requirements, scale, SLAs, and batch-vs-stream
quiz.explain: Clarifying requirements and constraints prevents designing the wrong system; everything else follows from it.

quiz.q: For an e-commerce analytics warehouse, a standard layered design is:
options: Medallion: Bronze (raw) → Silver (clean/dedupe, dbt) → Gold (aggregated marts)
quiz.explain: The medallion architecture with dbt transforms and SCD2 dims is the canonical answer.

quiz.q: For 'process 10K docs/day via an LLM with rate limits', a key element is:
options: A queue + workers with backoff/retry, idempotency by doc-id, and a cost-budget guard
quiz.explain: Operational constraints demand retries/backoff, idempotency, batching/caching, and budget controls.

exercises.prompt: Give the 6 steps of the design method.
exercises.solution: Clarify → Estimate → High-level (ingest/store/process/serve) → Deep-dive → Trade-offs → Wrap (monitoring + improvements).

exercises.prompt: Design a CDC pipeline from an OLTP DB to the lake.
exercises.solution: Log-based CDC (Debezium) → Kafka → stream/merge into Iceberg/Delta via MERGE (captures inserts/updates/deletes) → idempotent, with snapshot + incremental; serve to warehouse.

exercises.prompt: Streaming fraud detection: name three trade-offs you'd raise.
exercises.solution: Exactly-once vs latency, state/window size vs memory, in-stream model scoring vs external feature lookup; also alert precision vs recall.

exercises.prompt: What do you cover in the 'wrap' step that juniors forget?
exercises.solution: Monitoring/observability (freshness, volume, quality, lineage), failure handling, cost, and what you'd improve at higher scale.

lessons.diagram_caption: Clarify → Estimate → High-level → Deep-dive → Trade-offs → Wrap: a repeatable design framework.

lessons.title: Behavioral & leadership round — question bank
lessons.summary: The scored behavioral round — STAR, Amazon Leadership Principles, Goldman values — with example questions and answer frameworks.
lessons.concept:
The behavioral round is **scored against a rubric**, not a casual chat — and under-preparing for it sinks strong technical candidates. Use the **STAR** method (Situation, Task, Action, Result) with **quantified** results, and map stories to each company's framework.

**Company frameworks:**
- **Amazon — Leadership Principles.** LP questions appear in *every* round, including coding. Bring **12-15 stories** spanning Ownership, Customer Obsession, Dive Deep, Bias for Action, Deliver Results, Disagree & Commit.
- **Goldman Sachs — values:** Partnership, Client Service, Integrity, Excellence. Frame stories around collaboration, client impact, and doing the right thing under pressure.
- **Meta/Google** — impact, ambiguity, collaboration, and 'what did *you* do' (not the team).

**Common prompts:** a time you owned a failure; resolved a conflict; influenced without authority; handled ambiguity; made a data-driven decision; delivered under a tight deadline; disagreed with a manager.

**STAR skeleton:** 1 sentence Situation, 1 Task, **3-4 sentences Action (your specific actions)**, 1 quantified Result + a lesson. Keep it to ~2 minutes, use 'I' not 'we', and prepare a failure story that shows growth.

**How to win:** pre-write 12-15 STAR stories, tag each to multiple principles, lead with impact, and quantify. Full prompt bank + model answers in **Go deeper**.
lessons.example:
STAR (Ownership / Deliver Results):
S: Nightly pipeline failed silently; finance got stale numbers.
T: I owned restoring trust and preventing recurrence.
A: Added idempotent reruns, freshness + volume checks with alerts, and a backfill runbook; led a blameless postmortem.
R: Cut data incidents 80% next quarter; finance sign-off moved 3h earlier. Lesson: observability is part of the deliverable.
keypoints: Behavioral is scored on a rubric — prepare as hard as for coding.
Use STAR with quantified results; speak as 'I', keep to ~2 minutes.
Amazon: 12-15 LP stories; LP questions appear in every round (even coding).
Goldman: frame around Partnership, Client Service, Integrity, Excellence.
Have a genuine failure story that shows ownership and growth.
Tag each story to several principles so a few stories cover many prompts.

quiz.q: The behavioral round at FAANG is best described as:
options: A casual chat that rarely matters
A structured, rubric-scored evaluation you must prepare for
quiz.explain: It's a structured assessment with specific criteria; under-preparing costs strong candidates offers.

quiz.q: How many behavioral stories should you bring to Amazon, and why?
options: 12-15, because LP questions appear in every round including coding
quiz.explain: Amazon embeds Leadership Principles throughout, so you need many tagged stories.

quiz.q: A STAR answer should spend most of its time on:
quiz.explain: The Action is where your contribution shows; keep S and T brief and quantify the R.

exercises.prompt: Write the STAR skeleton lengths.
exercises.solution: ~1 sentence Situation, ~1 Task, 3-4 sentences Action (your actions), 1 quantified Result + lesson; ~2 minutes total.

exercises.prompt: Map one project to three Amazon LPs.
exercises.solution: E.g. a pipeline-reliability project → Ownership (you fixed it), Dive Deep (root-caused), Deliver Results (80% fewer incidents).

exercises.prompt: Frame a story for Goldman's values.
exercises.solution: Pick a story showing partnership/collaboration and client impact done with integrity under pressure; quantify the client outcome.

exercises.prompt: Why prepare a failure story, and what must it show?
exercises.solution: Interviewers probe self-awareness; it must show you owned the failure, root-caused it, and changed your approach with a measurable improvement.

lessons.title: Company playbooks: Google, Meta, Amazon, Goldman, JPMorgan
lessons.summary: Round-by-round breakdown per company — what each weights, the format, and how to tailor your prep.
lessons.concept:
Each top employer has a recognizable **DE loop and emphasis**. Tailor your prep to the company in front of you.

**Google** — recruiter → 1-2 technical screens → onsite of **4-5 rounds (45-60 min)**: coding/DSA, SQL, data modeling, system/pipeline design, behavioral (Googleyness & leadership). Strong on coding rigor and clean problem-solving; expect GCP/BigQuery fluency.

**Meta** — recruiter → technical screen → onsite of **4 technical + 1 behavioral**, often in a day. **Data modeling carries the most weight**, usually introduced via a **product-sense** prompt; plus SQL and Python. Emphasize 'what did you do' and product impact.

**Amazon** — recruiter → online assessment/screen → onsite (the 'loop'). **Leadership Principles in every round, including coding** — bring 12-15 STAR stories. SQL + DSA + data modeling + design; Bar Raiser present.

**Goldman Sachs** — **CoderPad** (arrays/trees/recursion/graphs), SQL (medium-hard: multi-join, windows, CTEs, optimization), **distributed systems (Spark/Hadoop)**, data modeling & ETL architecture, and **values-based** behavioral (Partnership, Client Service, Integrity, Excellence).

**JPMorgan Chase** — recruiter → technical (often Python; core CS, sometimes Java/concurrency, big-data concepts) → **4-5 onsite** rounds (30-35 min each): coding (medium DSA), SQL (joins/windows/aggregations/subqueries), case studies, behavioral + 'why JPMorgan/finance'.

**How to win:** match your top prep area to the company (modeling for Meta, LPs for Amazon, Spark+values for Goldman, breadth+motivation for JPMorgan). Full per-company question lists in **Go deeper**.
lessons.example:
# Quick tailoring cheat
Google    -> coding rigor + design + GCP/BigQuery
Meta      -> DATA MODELING (product-sense) + SQL + Python
Amazon    -> 12-15 LP stories + SQL/DSA/design (Bar Raiser)
Goldman   -> CoderPad + hard SQL + Spark/distributed + VALUES
JPMorgan  -> Python/CS breadth + SQL + case study + 'why finance'
keypoints: Google: 4-5 rounds; coding rigor + system design; GCP/BigQuery fluency helps.
Meta: data modeling weighted most, via product-sense prompts; SQL + Python.
Amazon: Leadership Principles in every round — 12-15 STAR stories; Bar Raiser.
Goldman: CoderPad + medium-hard SQL + distributed systems (Spark) + values-based behavioral.
JPMorgan: Python/CS breadth + SQL + case studies + motivation ('why JPMorgan/finance').
Tailor your strongest prep to the company's known emphasis.

quiz.q: Which company embeds Leadership Principles in every round, including coding?
quiz.explain: Amazon assesses LPs throughout the loop, so you need many tagged STAR stories.

quiz.q: Meta's DE onsite weights which area most?
options: Data modeling (often via a product-sense prompt)
quiz.explain: Meta leans hardest on data modeling, framed through product scenarios.

quiz.q: Goldman Sachs technical rounds notably include:
options: CoderPad + hard SQL + distributed systems (Spark/Hadoop) + values behavioral
quiz.explain: Goldman tests CoderPad algorithms, medium-hard SQL, distributed systems, and values fit.

exercises.prompt: You have a Meta onsite next week — what do you drill most?
exercises.solution: Data modeling via product-sense prompts (grain, dims, SCD), plus SQL and Python; practice 'what did I do' impact stories.

exercises.prompt: Prep priorities for a Goldman DE loop?
exercises.solution: CoderPad DSA (arrays/trees/recursion/graphs), medium-hard SQL (windows/CTEs/optimization), Spark/distributed concepts, and values-based STAR stories.

exercises.prompt: How does JPMorgan's loop differ from a pure FAANG one?
exercises.solution: More CS/Python breadth (sometimes Java/concurrency, big-data concepts), shorter back-to-back rounds, case studies, and explicit 'why JPMorgan/finance' motivation.

lessons.title: Amazon - DE interview playbook
lessons.summary: Amazon's data-engineer loop (SQL-heavy technical screen + onsite: SQL, coding/ETL, data modeling, system design, and Leadership-Principles behavioral) with signature questions, answers, and links to the concepts.
lessons.concept:
**The loop:** recruiter -> **technical screen** (SQL + Python/ETL on a shared editor - the first gate) -> **onsite loop** (SQL deep-dive, coding/ETL design, data modeling/warehouse, system/pipeline design, and a **Leadership Principles** behavioral). Bring **12-15 STAR stories**. Below is a full, round-by-round bank with answers, code, and expected output.

## SQL round

**Q1. 2nd-highest salary per department.**
```sql
SELECT dept, salary FROM (
  SELECT dept, salary,
         DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC) rk
  FROM emp) t
WHERE rk = 2;
```
`DENSE_RANK` keeps ties sharing a place. Output:
```
dept | salary
-----+-------
Eng  | 145000
Ops  |  98000
```
-> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

**Q2. Top-3 products by revenue per category.**
```sql
SELECT category, product, revenue FROM (
  SELECT category, product, revenue,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY revenue DESC) rn
  FROM sales) t
WHERE rn <= 3;
```
`ROW_NUMBER` returns exactly 3 even on ties; use `RANK` if tied products should all appear. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

**Q3. 7-day rolling average of daily revenue.**
```sql
SELECT d, AVG(rev) OVER (ORDER BY d ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) r7
FROM daily;
```
The frame `6 PRECEDING -> CURRENT ROW` = 7 rows. Output (first rows ramp up until 7 days exist):
```
d          | r7
-----------+--------
2026-01-01 | 100.0
2026-01-02 | 110.0
...        | ...
```
-> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

**Q4. Month-over-month growth %.**
```sql
SELECT mth, rev,
  ROUND(100.0*(rev - LAG(rev) OVER (ORDER BY mth)) / LAG(rev) OVER (ORDER BY mth), 1) AS mom_pct
FROM monthly;
```
`LAG` pulls the prior month; first row is NULL (no prior). Output: `2026-02 | 12000 | 20.0`. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

**Q5. Dedup - keep the latest row per key.**
```sql
SELECT * FROM (
  SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY id ORDER BY ts DESC) rn FROM events) t
WHERE rn = 1;   -- or BigQuery: QUALIFY rn = 1
```
-> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

**Q6. Sessionize events (gap > 30 min = new session).**
```sql
WITH f AS (
  SELECT user_id, ts,
    CASE WHEN ts - LAG(ts) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts) > INTERVAL '30' MINUTE
              OR LAG(ts) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts) IS NULL
         THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS new_s
  FROM events)
SELECT user_id, ts, SUM(new_s) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts) AS session_id
FROM f;
```
Gaps-and-islands: flag new sessions, running-SUM to number them. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

**Q7. Running total (cumulative revenue).**
```sql
SELECT d, rev, SUM(rev) OVER (ORDER BY d) AS cum_rev FROM daily;
```
Output: `2026-01-01|100|100`, `2026-01-02|110|210`, ... -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

**Q8. Customers who bought in Jan but NOT Feb (anti-join).**
```sql
SELECT DISTINCT j.customer_id
FROM jan_orders j
LEFT JOIN feb_orders f ON f.customer_id = j.customer_id
WHERE f.customer_id IS NULL;   -- NOT EXISTS is equivalent
```
-> Revise: [SQL cram](#/cheat/databases-sql)

**Q9. Median order value (no MEDIAN function).**
```sql
SELECT PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY amount) AS median FROM orders;
```
If unavailable, use `ROW_NUMBER`/`COUNT` to pick the middle row(s). -> Revise: [SQL cram](#/cheat/databases-sql)

**Q10. Pivot monthly revenue to columns (portable).**
```sql
SELECT product,
  SUM(CASE WHEN mth='Jan' THEN amt END) jan,
  SUM(CASE WHEN mth='Feb' THEN amt END) feb
FROM sales GROUP BY product;
```
Conditional aggregation works on every engine. -> Revise: [SQL cram](#/cheat/databases-sql)

## Coding & ETL round (Python)

**Q1. Two Sum (indices summing to target).**
```python
def two_sum(nums, target):
    seen = {}
    for i, n in enumerate(nums):
        if target - n in seen:
            return [seen[target - n], i]
        seen[n] = i
# two_sum([2,7,11,15], 9) -> [0, 1]
```
Hash map, O(n) time / O(n) space. -> Revise: [DSA patterns](#/cheat/dsa-de)

**Q2. Group anagrams.**
```python
from collections import defaultdict
def group_anagrams(words):
    g = defaultdict(list)
    for w in words:
        g[''.join(sorted(w))].append(w)
    return list(g.values())
# ['eat','tea','tan','ate'] -> [['eat','tea','ate'],['tan']]
```
Sorted string as the key. -> Revise: [Python cram](#/cheat/python-de)

**Q3. Top-K frequent elements.**
```python
import heapq
from collections import Counter
def top_k(nums, k):
    return [x for x,_ in Counter(nums).most_common(k)]
# top_k([1,1,1,2,2,3], 2) -> [1, 2]
```
Counter + heap; O(n log k). At huge scale, Count-Min sketch + heap. -> Revise: [DSA patterns](#/cheat/dsa-de)

**Q4. Make a daily load idempotent.**
```python
# WRONG: append -> duplicates on rerun
# RIGHT: delete-then-insert the partition, or MERGE on the key
def load(day, rows, conn):
    conn.execute("DELETE FROM fact WHERE dt = %s", (day,))
    conn.executemany("INSERT INTO fact VALUES (%s,%s,%s)", rows)
```
Re-running `day` reproduces the same state, no duplicates. -> Revise: [idempotency](#/lesson/pipe-idempotency)

**Q5. Handle late / out-of-order events in batch.**
Partition by **event date** (not arrival); a backfill for a late day overwrites that partition idempotently. -> Revise: [backfill & scheduling](#/lesson/pipe-scheduling-backfill)

**Q6. Merge overlapping intervals.**
```python
def merge(iv):
    iv.sort()
    out = [iv[0]]
    for s, e in iv[1:]:
        if s <= out[-1][1]:
            out[-1][1] = max(out[-1][1], e)
        else:
            out.append([s, e])
    return out
# merge([[1,3],[2,6],[8,10]]) -> [[1,6],[8,10]]
```
-> Revise: [DSA patterns](#/cheat/dsa-de)

**Q7. Parse a large log, count by status (memory-safe).**
```python
from collections import Counter
def count_status(path):
    c = Counter()
    with open(path) as f:            # stream line-by-line, don't load all
        for line in f:
            c[line.split()[8]] += 1
    return c
# Counter({'200': 90210, '404': 331, '500': 12})
```
-> Revise: [Python cram](#/cheat/python-de)

**Q8. Retry with exponential backoff (transient failures).**
```python
import time
def with_retry(fn, tries=5):
    for i in range(tries):
        try:
            return fn()
        except TransientError:
            if i == tries - 1: raise
            time.sleep(2 ** i)       # 1,2,4,8s ...
```
Pair with idempotency so retries are safe. -> Revise: [idempotency](#/lesson/pipe-idempotency)

## Data modeling round

**Q1. Model orders analytics - state the grain first.** `fact_order_item` at grain 'one product per order line' (measures: qty, price, discount; FKs: date, customer, product) around conformed dims. -> Revise: [star schema](#/lesson/model-star-schema)

**Q2. Star vs snowflake?** Star = denormalized dims (fewer joins, faster BI); snowflake = normalized dims (less redundancy, more joins). Analytics usually favors star. -> Revise: [denormalization](#/lesson/model-denormalization)

**Q3. SCD Type 1 vs 2 vs 3.** T1 = overwrite (no history); **T2 = new row + validity dates + current flag (full history)**; T3 = prior-value column (limited). Default to T2 for dimensions that change. -> Revise: [SCD2](#/lesson/model-scd2)

**Q4. Additive vs non-additive measures.** Sums/counts are additive across all dims; **ratios (CTR) are non-additive** - store numerator & denominator, divide at query time. -> Revise: [star schema](#/lesson/model-star-schema)

**Q5. Fact table types.** Transaction (one row per event), periodic snapshot (balance per period), accumulating snapshot (a process with milestones, e.g., order->ship->deliver). -> Revise: [modeling cram](#/cheat/data-modeling)

**Q6. Why surrogate keys (not natural keys)?** Insulate the warehouse from source-key changes, enable SCD2 (same natural key, many surrogate rows), and speed joins (narrow ints). -> Revise: [star schema](#/lesson/model-star-schema)

**Q7. Many-to-many (e.g., product <-> promotion)?** A **bridge table** at the intersection grain, or a multi-valued dimension with an allocation factor. -> Revise: [modeling cram](#/cheat/data-modeling)

**Q8. Conformed dimensions - why?** A shared `dim_date`/`dim_customer` used across facts so metrics compare consistently across teams. -> Revise: [star schema](#/lesson/model-star-schema)

## System / pipeline design round

**Q1. The framework (say it out loud).** Clarify (scale, freshness, consumers) -> estimate (volume/QPS) -> ingest -> store -> transform -> serve -> cross-cutting (idempotency, DQ, late data, skew, cost, monitoring). -> Revise: [system design](#/cheat/system-design)

**Q2. Design clickstream -> daily dashboards (billions/day).** Ingest to S3/Kafka; partition by event date; Spark/Glue transform; **idempotent** loads to Redshift/lakehouse; DQ gates; orchestrate with Airflow/Step Functions. -> Revise: [system design](#/cheat/system-design)

**Q3. Dedup billions of events at scale.** Partition + `ROW_NUMBER` per key in Spark; for streaming, a keyed state store or a Bloom filter to skip seen keys. -> Revise: [shuffle & partitions](#/lesson/bd-partitions-shuffle)

**Q4. Cut a slow/expensive Redshift or Athena query.** Partition + columnar Parquet + narrow SELECT (scan fewer bytes); Redshift: DISTKEY the join column, SORTKEY the date filter. -> Revise: [Athena partitioning](#/lesson/aws-athena-partitioning)

**Q5. Batch vs streaming - how to choose?** Latency requirement drives it: minutes/hours -> batch; seconds -> streaming; need both -> Lambda-style (speed + batch reconciliation). -> Revise: [streaming cram](#/cheat/streaming)

**Q6. Guarantee data quality before publish.** Row-count/volume, not-null keys, referential, range, and anomaly checks that **block publish** + quarantine bad rows + alert. -> Revise: [DataOps cram](#/cheat/dataops)

**Q7. Design a backfill for 90 late days.** Parameterize the DAG by date; reprocess each event-date partition idempotently in dependency order; throttle to protect the warehouse. -> Revise: [backfill & scheduling](#/lesson/pipe-scheduling-backfill)

## Behavioral round (Leadership Principles)

Answer each in **STAR** (Situation, Task, Action, Result) mapped to an LP, with a **quantified** result.

**Q1. Ownership** - 'a time you took on something beyond your role.'
**Q2. Dive Deep** - 'a data bug you root-caused to the source.'
**Q3. Bias for Action** - 'a decision you made with incomplete data.'
**Q4. Customer Obsession** - 'a time you pushed back to protect the customer/data consumer.'
**Q5. Disagree and Commit** - 'a time you disagreed with your team, then committed.'
**Q6. Deliver Results** - 'a tight deadline you hit and how.'
**Q7. Earn Trust** - 'a mistake you made and how you handled it.'
Prepare 12-15 stories you can flex across these. -> Revise: [behavioral prep](#/lesson/ib-behavioral)
lessons.example: **Signature Amazon question - 'design the data model + pipeline for a new marketplace metric.'** Walk it end to end: (1) **clarify** the metric, grain, freshness, scale; (2) **model** it - `fact` at the right grain + conformed dims, SCD2 where history matters; (3) **pipeline** - ingest to S3, partition by event date, Spark transform, **idempotent** MERGE into the warehouse, DQ checks that block publish; (4) **trade-offs** - batch vs stream, cost (scan-less), late data, skew; (5) close with monitoring/alerting. Then a **behavioral** LP follow-up ('a time you had to Dive Deep to fix a data bug') in STAR with numbers. Nailing grain + idempotency + trade-off reasoning + a quantified LP story is the Amazon bar.
keypoints: Loop: SQL+Python technical screen (the gate) -> onsite SQL, coding/ETL, data modeling, system design, LP behavioral.
SQL: window functions (2nd-highest, rolling avg, MoM/YoY) and dedup/top-N are near-guaranteed.
ETL: idempotency, late/out-of-order data, retries - MERGE or delete-then-insert per partition.
Modeling: state the grain first; star schema, SCD2, conformed dimensions; Redshift dist/sort keys.
System design: clarify -> estimate -> ingest/store/transform/serve -> trade-offs; scan fewer bytes to cut cost.
Behavioral is scored: STAR mapped to Leadership Principles; prepare 12-15 quantified stories.

quiz.q: Amazon SQL round: get the 2nd-highest salary per department. Best approach?
options: DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC), keep rk=2
quiz.explain: A partitioned window rank is the clean, efficient per-group top-N; keep rk=2.

quiz.q: What most distinguishes a strong Amazon behavioral answer?
options: A STAR story mapped to a Leadership Principle with a quantified result
quiz.explain: Amazon scores behavioral against LPs; STAR + a specific, quantified outcome is the bar.

exercises.prompt: Amazon-style: write SQL for the top-3 products by revenue within each category, and say why the window approach beats alternatives.
exercises.solution:
```sql
SELECT category, product, revenue FROM (
  SELECT category, product, revenue,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY revenue DESC) rn
  FROM sales) t
WHERE rn <= 3;
```
Use `ROW_NUMBER` (or `RANK`/`DENSE_RANK` if ties should share a place) partitioned by category, ordered by revenue desc, then keep `rn <= 3`. Why it wins: it's a **single pass** the engine can do with a windowed sort, versus a **correlated subquery** (re-scans per row, O(n^2)) or self-joins (fan-out, error-prone with ties). It also generalizes to top-N per group by changing one number. Mention: `ROW_NUMBER` breaks ties arbitrarily, `RANK`/`DENSE_RANK` keep tied products - pick per the requirement. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

exercises.prompt: Amazon-style design: make a nightly S3->Redshift load safe to retry and backfill. Outline the design.
exercises.hint: Partition by event date + idempotent MERGE.
exercises.solution: Partition source data in S3 by **event date** (not load date). Each nightly run processes one date, keyed by the run's `logical_date`. Load with an **idempotent** step: stage the date's rows, then **MERGE** on the business key (update matched, insert new) - or **delete-then-insert for that partition** - so re-running the same date yields the same result, never duplicates. Track state in the orchestrator (Airflow/Step Functions) so a **failed task retries** just itself, and a **backfill** re-runs the same DAG for past dates in dependency order. Add DQ checks (row counts, not-null keys) that **block publish** on failure and route bad rows to quarantine. This gives retry-safety (idempotency), reprocessing (backfill), and trust (DQ) - the Amazon ETL bar. -> Revise: [idempotency](#/lesson/pipe-idempotency), [backfill](#/lesson/pipe-scheduling-backfill)

lessons.title: Google - DE interview playbook
lessons.summary: Google's DE loop (an online SQL+Python assessment, then a 4-5 round onsite) with a BigQuery/GCP flavor - SQL depth, coding, data modeling, and large-scale system design - plus signature questions, answers, and concept links.
lessons.concept:
**The loop:** online **coding assessment** (SQL + Python) -> **onsite** (4-5 rounds x 45-60 min): SQL, coding/DSA, data modeling, large-scale **system design**, and Googleyness/behavioral. **Flavor:** clean SQL, real CS fundamentals, and scale reasoning - often **BigQuery/Dataflow**. Full round-by-round bank below.

## SQL round

**Q1. Keep the latest row per key (BigQuery).**
```sql
SELECT * FROM events
QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY id ORDER BY ts DESC) = 1;
```
`QUALIFY` filters on a window function with no subquery. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

**Q2. Sessionize (gap > 30 min = new session).**
```sql
WITH f AS (
  SELECT uid, ts,
    IF(ts - LAG(ts) OVER (PARTITION BY uid ORDER BY ts) > INTERVAL 30 MINUTE
       OR LAG(ts) OVER (PARTITION BY uid ORDER BY ts) IS NULL, 1, 0) new_s
  FROM events)
SELECT uid, ts, SUM(new_s) OVER (PARTITION BY uid ORDER BY ts) AS session_id FROM f;
```
Gaps-and-islands. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

**Q3. Top-N search terms per day.**
```sql
SELECT day, term, cnt FROM (
  SELECT day, term, COUNT(*) cnt,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY day ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC) rn
  FROM searches GROUP BY day, term) t
WHERE rn <= 10;
```
-> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

**Q4. Running distinct users (approx, huge scale).**
`APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT(user_id)` uses HyperLogLog - KB of memory for billions of rows. Output: `~10423981`. -> Revise: [DSA patterns](#/cheat/dsa-de)

**Q5. Fill gaps with a date spine.**
```sql
SELECT d.day, COALESCE(s.rev,0) rev
FROM UNNEST(GENERATE_DATE_ARRAY('2026-01-01','2026-01-31')) AS d(day)
LEFT JOIN sales s ON s.day = d.day;
```
Zero-activity days now appear. -> Revise: [SQL cram](#/cheat/databases-sql)

**Q6. Funnel: search -> click -> purchase conversion.**
Count distinct users at each step (conditional aggregation), divide successive steps. -> Revise: [SQL cram](#/cheat/databases-sql)

**Q7. Query nested/repeated fields.**
```sql
SELECT id, tag FROM events, UNNEST(tags) AS tag;   -- ARRAY -> rows
```
-> Revise: [BigQuery partition & cluster](#/lesson/bq-partition-cluster)

## Coding / DSA round

**Q1. Merge K sorted lists (heap).**
```python
import heapq
def merge_k(lists):
    h = [(l[0], i, 0) for i, l in enumerate(lists) if l]
    heapq.heapify(h); out = []
    while h:
        val, li, ei = heapq.heappop(h)
        out.append(val)
        if ei + 1 < len(lists[li]):
            heapq.heappush(h, (lists[li][ei+1], li, ei+1))
    return out
# merge_k([[1,4],[1,3,4],[2,6]]) -> [1,1,2,3,4,4,6]
```
O(n log K). -> Revise: [merge-K / heaps](#/lesson/dsa-merge-k)

**Q2. Longest substring without repeating chars (sliding window).**
```python
def length_of_longest(s):
    seen = {}; start = ans = 0
    for i, c in enumerate(s):
        if c in seen and seen[c] >= start:
            start = seen[c] + 1
        seen[c] = i; ans = max(ans, i - start + 1)
    return ans
# "abcabcbb" -> 3
```
-> Revise: [two-pointer & window](#/lesson/dsa-two-pointer-window)

**Q3. Number of islands (BFS/DFS on a grid).** Traverse each unvisited land cell, flood-fill its component, count components. O(rows*cols). -> Revise: [DSA patterns](#/cheat/dsa-de)

**Q4. Course schedule / task order (topological sort).**
```python
from collections import deque, defaultdict
def can_finish(n, prereqs):
    g = defaultdict(list); indeg = [0]*n
    for a, b in prereqs: g[b].append(a); indeg[a]+=1
    q = deque(i for i in range(n) if indeg[i]==0); seen=0
    while q:
        u=q.popleft(); seen+=1
        for v in g[u]:
            indeg[v]-=1
            if indeg[v]==0: q.append(v)
    return seen==n     # False => a cycle exists
```
-> Revise: [topological sort](#/lesson/dsa-topo-sort)

**Q5. Two Sum.** Hash map of complements, O(n). `two_sum([3,2,4],6) -> [1,2]`. -> Revise: [DSA patterns](#/cheat/dsa-de)

**Q6. Sliding-window maximum.** Monotonic deque of indices; O(n). -> Revise: [two-pointer & window](#/lesson/dsa-two-pointer-window)

**Q7. Parse & count distinct from a delimited string.**
```python
def distinct(s):
    return len({p.strip() for p in s.split(',') if p.strip()})
# distinct("a, b, a, ,c") -> 3
```
-> Revise: [Python cram](#/cheat/python-de)

## Data modeling round

**Q1. Model an events warehouse in BigQuery.** Partition by event_date, **cluster** by user/event_type so queries prune + block-skip. -> Revise: [BigQuery partition & cluster](#/lesson/bq-partition-cluster)

**Q2. Nested (ARRAY/STRUCT) vs flat?** Nested avoids joins and fits BigQuery's columnar storage for 1:many that's always queried together; flatten when consumers need row-level joins. -> Revise: [BigQuery partition & cluster](#/lesson/bq-partition-cluster)

**Q3. Normalize vs denormalize for analytics?** Denormalize (star) for read-heavy analytics; normalize for write-heavy OLTP. -> Revise: [denormalization](#/lesson/model-denormalization)

**Q4. Grain first - what is it and why?** The grain is what one fact row represents; fixing it prevents double-counting and drives dims/measures. -> Revise: [star schema](#/lesson/model-star-schema)

**Q5. Track changing user attributes.** SCD2 - new row + validity dates + current flag. -> Revise: [SCD2](#/lesson/model-scd2)

**Q6. Additive vs non-additive.** Sums add across all dims; ratios don't - store numerator/denominator. -> Revise: [star schema](#/lesson/model-star-schema)

**Q7. Slot/cost model.** BigQuery bills bytes scanned (or slots) - partition+cluster+narrow SELECT cut both. -> Revise: [BigQuery partition & cluster](#/lesson/bq-partition-cluster)

## System / pipeline design round

**Q1. 1M click events/min, sessionize, dashboards every 5 min.** Pub/Sub -> **Dataflow** (Beam) session windows + **watermark** + allowed lateness -> 5-min aggregates -> BigQuery partitioned/clustered. -> Revise: [Dataflow windowing](#/lesson/df-windowing), [watermarks](#/lesson/stream-watermarks-late)

**Q2. Exactly-once in the stream.** Dataflow checkpointing + idempotent BigQuery sink (dedup on event id). -> Revise: [delivery semantics](#/lesson/sd-idempotency-delivery)

**Q3. Handle late/out-of-order events.** Event-time windows + watermark + allowed lateness (accumulate or discard). -> Revise: [watermarks](#/lesson/stream-watermarks-late)

**Q4. Control a BigQuery bill.** Partition + cluster, never SELECT *, `maximum_bytes_billed`, `require_partition_filter`. -> Revise: [BigQuery partition & cluster](#/lesson/bq-partition-cluster)

**Q5. Hot-key skew in aggregation.** Salt the hot key or pre-aggregate; rebalance partitions. -> Revise: [shuffle & partitions](#/lesson/bd-partitions-shuffle)

**Q6. Batch vs stream on GCP.** Dataflow unifies both (Beam); pick by latency need. -> Revise: [streaming cram](#/cheat/streaming)

**Q7. Data-quality gate.** Volume/null/referential/anomaly checks blocking publish. -> Revise: [DataOps cram](#/cheat/dataops)

## Behavioral round (Googleyness)

STAR with structured thinking + collaboration + learning:
**Q1.** Navigating ambiguity on an under-specified project.
**Q2.** Collaborating across teams / resolving conflict.
**Q3.** A failure and what you changed afterward.
**Q4.** A data-driven decision that changed direction.
**Q5.** Handling feedback you disagreed with.
**Q6.** Prioritizing under constraints.
-> Revise: [behavioral prep](#/lesson/ib-behavioral)
lessons.example: **Signature Google question - 'design a pipeline for 1M click events/min, sessionized, dashboards every 5 minutes.'** Clarify: exactly-once or approximate? how late can events be? Then: **Pub/Sub** ingest -> **Dataflow** (Beam) with **session windows** keyed by user and an **event-time watermark** + allowed lateness -> 5-minute aggregates -> **BigQuery** sink **partitioned by date, clustered by user**. Discuss **late/out-of-order** data (watermark + accumulation), **skew** (a hot user - salt/rebalance), **exactly-once** (Dataflow + idempotent sink), and **cost** (prune with partition/cluster). Sessionization is the algorithmic core (gaps-and-islands) - show you can do it in SQL and in the streaming model. Structured clarify -> design -> trade-offs is what Google scores.
keypoints: Loop: online SQL+Python assessment -> onsite (SQL, coding/DSA, modeling, large-scale system design, Googleyness).
SQL: dedup with QUALIFY/ROW_NUMBER and sessionization (gaps-and-islands via LAG + running sum) are classics.
Coding leans real DSA: heaps (merge-K), two-pointer/sliding window, graphs - state time AND space.
Modeling/design carry a BigQuery/Dataflow flavor: partition+cluster, ARRAY/STRUCT, windowing + watermarks.
System design: clarify -> estimate -> ingest/process/serve -> trade-offs (late data, skew, exactly-once, cost).
Behavioral (Googleyness): structured thinking, collaboration, learning from failure - STAR with impact.

quiz.q: BigQuery: keep only the latest row per key in one statement. What do you use?
options: QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY id ORDER BY ts DESC) = 1
quiz.explain: QUALIFY filters on a window function without a subquery - the idiomatic BigQuery dedup.

quiz.q: Design '1M clicks/min, sessionize, serve every 5 min' on GCP - core processing choice?
options: Dataflow/Beam with event-time session windows + watermarks, sink to BigQuery
quiz.explain: Sessionization at that rate needs stream processing with event-time windows and watermarks (Dataflow), aggregated into BigQuery.

exercises.prompt: Google-style SQL: assign a session id to each user's events where a gap over 30 minutes starts a new session.
exercises.hint: LAG the ts, flag new session, running SUM.
exercises.solution:
```sql
WITH f AS (
  SELECT user_id, ts,
    CASE WHEN ts - LAG(ts) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts)
         > INTERVAL '30' MINUTE OR LAG(ts) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts) IS NULL
         THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS new_sess
  FROM events)
SELECT user_id, ts,
  SUM(new_sess) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY ts) AS session_id
FROM f;
```
This is the **gaps-and-islands** pattern: `LAG` gets the previous timestamp, the `CASE` flags a **new session** when the gap exceeds 30 min (or it's the first event), and a **running SUM of the flag** numbers the sessions per user. It's O(n log n) (the sort), single pass conceptually, and mirrors how you'd sessionize in a streaming engine with session windows. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

exercises.prompt: Google-style: merge K sorted event streams into one ordered stream efficiently. Approach + complexity.
exercises.solution: Use a **min-heap** holding the current head of each of the K streams (each entry tagged with which stream it came from). Pop the smallest, emit it, then push the **next element from that same stream**. Repeat until all streams are exhausted. **Complexity: O(n log K)** time (n total elements, each pushed/popped once through a heap of size <= K) and **O(K)** extra space. This is the in-memory version of a **k-way external merge** (how sort/merge-join works when data exceeds RAM: sort runs, then merge them with a heap). Edge cases: empty streams, ties (stable if you tie-break on stream index), and very large K (the heap stays O(K)). -> Revise: [merge-K / heaps](#/lesson/dsa-merge-k)

lessons.title: Meta - DE interview playbook
lessons.summary: Meta's DE loop (a technical phone with ~25 min SQL + ~25 min Python, then an onsite of product sense, SQL/ETL, data modeling, and behavioral) with its product-analytics flavor - signature questions, answers, and concept links.
lessons.concept:
**The loop:** recruiter -> **technical phone** (~25 min **SQL** + ~25 min **Python**) -> **onsite** (Product Sense, SQL/ETL, **Data Modeling** [weighted], Behavioral). **Flavor:** product-analytics SQL and turning a fuzzy product question into metrics + a schema. Full bank below.

## SQL round

**Q1. DAU / WAU / MAU.**
```sql
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT IF(d = CURRENT_DATE, uid, NULL)) dau,
       COUNT(DISTINCT IF(d > CURRENT_DATE-7, uid, NULL)) wau,
       COUNT(DISTINCT IF(d > CURRENT_DATE-30, uid, NULL)) mau
FROM activity;
```
-> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

**Q2. Stickiness = DAU/MAU.** Divide Q1's dau by mau. Output: `0.42`. -> Revise: [SQL cram](#/cheat/databases-sql)

**Q3. Day-1 retention (cohort).**
```sql
WITH f AS (SELECT uid, MIN(DATE(ts)) d0 FROM act GROUP BY uid)
SELECT f.d0,
  COUNT(DISTINCT a.uid)*1.0/COUNT(DISTINCT f.uid) AS d1
FROM f LEFT JOIN act a ON a.uid=f.uid AND DATE(a.ts)=f.d0+INTERVAL '1' DAY
GROUP BY f.d0;
```
-> Revise: [SQL cram](#/cheat/databases-sql)

**Q4. Funnel conversion (view -> like -> share).** Conditional aggregation per step, divide. -> Revise: [SQL cram](#/cheat/databases-sql)

**Q5. Top-N creators by engagement per country.** Partitioned `ROW_NUMBER`, filter <= N. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

**Q6. 7-day rolling active users.** Count distinct over a windowed date spine. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

**Q7. Distinct at scale.** `APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT` (HLL) when exact isn't required. -> Revise: [DSA patterns](#/cheat/dsa-de)

## Python round

**Q1. Vectorized clean + count (no iterrows).**
```python
df['city'] = df['city'].str.strip().replace({'New Yrok':'New York'})
counts = df.groupby('city').size().sort_values(ascending=False)
```
-> Revise: [pandas merge/reshape](#/lesson/pd-merge-reshape)

**Q2. Parse a messy events file, aggregate.**
```python
from collections import Counter
c = Counter()
with open('events.log') as f:
    for line in f:
        c[line.split('\t')[2]] += 1   # stream, memory-safe
```
-> Revise: [Python cram](#/cheat/python-de)

**Q3. Flatten nested JSON.** `pd.json_normalize(records)` -> a flat dataframe. -> Revise: [pandas merge/reshape](#/lesson/pd-merge-reshape)

**Q4. Dedup keeping latest per key (pandas).**
```python
latest = (df.sort_values('ts')
            .drop_duplicates('user_id', keep='last'))
```
-> Revise: [pandas merge/reshape](#/lesson/pd-merge-reshape)

**Q5. Join two dataframes + fill missing.** `df.merge(dim, on='k', how='left').fillna({'seg':'unknown'})`. -> Revise: [pandas merge/reshape](#/lesson/pd-merge-reshape)

**Q6. Count distinct from a semicolon string.** `len({p.strip() for p in s.split(';') if p.strip()})`. -> Revise: [Python cram](#/cheat/python-de)

## Data modeling round (weighted)

**Q1. Design the fact/dim schema for a Reactions feature.** Grain = one reaction event; fact_reaction (FKs: user, content, date; measure: reaction_flag) + conformed dims. -> Revise: [star schema](#/lesson/model-star-schema)

**Q2. Additive vs non-additive.** CTR/engagement-rate are non-additive - store numerator & denominator. -> Revise: [star schema](#/lesson/model-star-schema)

**Q3. SCD2 on user attributes.** New row + validity + current flag. -> Revise: [SCD2](#/lesson/model-scd2)

**Q4. Conformed dimensions.** Shared dim_user/dim_date so metrics agree across features. -> Revise: [star schema](#/lesson/model-star-schema)

**Q5. Event schema for a feed.** transaction fact at event grain; append-only, event-sourced. -> Revise: [modeling cram](#/cheat/data-modeling)

**Q6. Denormalize a serving table.** Pre-aggregate for dashboard latency; keep the normalized fact as truth. -> Revise: [denormalization](#/lesson/model-denormalization)

## Product sense round

**Q1. How would you measure the success of [feature]?** North-star metric + guardrails; define events -> fact/dim -> SQL. -> Revise: [behavioral prep](#/lesson/ib-behavioral)

**Q2. A metric dropped 10% - how do you investigate?** Segment by dimension, check instrumentation/logging, seasonality, and a data bug vs real change. -> Revise: [DataOps cram](#/cheat/dataops)

**Q3. Pick a north-star for [product].** Tie to durable user value (e.g., time-to-value), not a vanity count. -> Revise: [behavioral prep](#/lesson/ib-behavioral)

**Q4. What tables/metrics would you build for [feature]?** Translate to a schema + the SQL to compute them. -> Revise: [star schema](#/lesson/model-star-schema)

## Behavioral round

STAR, emphasize ownership + quantified impact:
**Q1.** Owned an ambiguous problem end to end.
**Q2.** Influenced without authority.
**Q3.** A conflict with a stakeholder and the outcome.
**Q4.** Why Meta / this team.
-> Revise: [behavioral prep](#/lesson/ib-behavioral)
lessons.example: **Signature Meta question - 'design the data model and metrics for a new Stories feature.'** Start with **product sense**: what does success mean? (e.g., daily Stories creators, view-through rate, guardrail: report rate). Define the **events** (story_created, story_viewed) and the **grain** (one view event). Model a **fact_story_view** (measures: view flag, watch_ms; FKs: viewer, story, date) + dims (user with **SCD2** for attributes, story, date). Note **CTR is non-additive** - store views & impressions, divide at query time. Then write the **SQL** for DAU creators and view-through, using `COUNT(DISTINCT)` (HLL at scale). Meta scores your ability to go **fuzzy product question -> metrics -> schema -> SQL** cleanly, with grain and additivity correct.
keypoints: Loop: technical phone (~25 min SQL + ~25 min Python) -> onsite (product sense, SQL/ETL, data modeling [heavy], behavioral).
SQL: product analytics - DAU/WAU/MAU, stickiness, retention/cohorts, funnels; COUNT(DISTINCT)/HLL at scale.
Python: clean, vectorized data wrangling (pandas/dict/Counter); avoid row loops.
Data modeling is weighted: nail the grain, star schema, SCD2, and additive vs non-additive measures.
Product sense: turn a fuzzy feature into a north-star metric + guardrails + a schema + the SQL to compute it.
Behavioral: ownership and quantified impact in STAR.

quiz.q: Meta asks for 'stickiness'. How is it defined?
options: DAU / MAU (daily active over monthly active)
quiz.explain: Stickiness = DAU/MAU - the fraction of monthly users who come back daily.

quiz.q: Why is click-through rate (CTR) a non-additive measure in a fact table?
options: It's a ratio - you can't sum ratios across rows; store numerator & denominator and divide at query time
quiz.explain: Ratios don't add; keep clicks and impressions as additive measures and compute CTR at query time.

exercises.prompt: Meta-style: compute Day-1 retention (users active on their signup day who return the next day). Sketch the SQL.
exercises.hint: First-active cohort + a join to next-day activity.
exercises.solution:
```sql
WITH first_day AS (
  SELECT user_id, MIN(DATE(ts)) AS d0 FROM activity GROUP BY user_id)
SELECT f.d0,
  COUNT(DISTINCT a.user_id) * 1.0 / COUNT(DISTINCT f.user_id) AS day1_retention
FROM first_day f
LEFT JOIN activity a
  ON a.user_id = f.user_id AND DATE(a.ts) = f.d0 + INTERVAL '1' DAY
GROUP BY f.d0;
```
Define each user's **cohort** (their first-active date `d0`), then **left-join** to activity exactly one day later; the ratio of distinct returners to the cohort size is **Day-1 retention** by cohort. Use a LEFT join so non-returners count in the denominator but not the numerator, and `COUNT(DISTINCT)` to avoid double-counting multiple events. Generalize to Day-N by changing the interval. -> Revise: [SQL cram](#/cheat/databases-sql)

exercises.prompt: Meta-style modeling: design the fact/dim schema for measuring a 'Save post' feature. State grain, measures, dims, and one modeling subtlety.
exercises.hint: Grain = one save event; watch non-additive metrics.
exercises.solution: **Grain:** one **save event** (a user saves a post). **fact_post_save**: measures = `save_flag` (1, additive -> counts), maybe `is_first_save`; **FKs** = user_key, post_key, date_key. **Dims:** `dim_user` (SCD2 - segment/country change over time), `dim_post` (author, type, topic), `dim_date`. **Metrics** built on it: daily distinct savers (`COUNT(DISTINCT user)`), saves per post, save-through rate = saves / impressions. **Subtlety:** **save-through rate is non-additive** - store saves and impressions as separate additive measures (impressions may live in a different fact at a different grain) and compute the ratio at query time; never pre-store the ratio. Also decide whether an **unsave** is a new event (append, event-sourced) or a delete (mutation) - event-sourcing keeps history and is friendlier to a warehouse. Conform `dim_user`/`dim_date` with other product facts so metrics compare across features. -> Revise: [star schema](#/lesson/model-star-schema), [SCD2](#/lesson/model-scd2)

lessons.title: Microsoft - DE interview playbook
lessons.summary: Microsoft's DE loop (online assessment/technical screen, then a 4-5 round 'as-appropriate' onsite) with an Azure flavor - SQL, coding/DSA, data modeling, Azure system design, and growth-mindset behavioral - with signature questions, answers, and concept links.
lessons.concept:
**The loop:** recruiter -> **online/technical screen** (SQL + coding) -> **onsite** (4-5 rounds): SQL, coding/DSA, data modeling, **Azure system design** (Synapse, Data Factory, ADLS, Event Hubs, Databricks), and growth-mindset behavioral. Full bank below.

## SQL round

**Q1. Nth-highest value.** `DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY x DESC)`, filter = N. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

**Q2. Find missing IDs in a sequence.** Anti-join to a numbers/`generate_series` table, or `LAG` gap detection. -> Revise: [SQL cram](#/cheat/databases-sql)

**Q3. Pivot months to columns (no PIVOT).**
```sql
SELECT product,
  SUM(CASE WHEN mth='Jan' THEN amt END) jan,
  SUM(CASE WHEN mth='Feb' THEN amt END) feb
FROM sales GROUP BY product;
```
-> Revise: [SQL cram](#/cheat/databases-sql)

**Q4. Running total & moving average.** `SUM()/AVG() OVER (ORDER BY d ROWS ...)`. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

**Q5. Dedup latest per key.** `ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY k ORDER BY ts DESC)=1`. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

**Q6. Second-highest per group with ties.** Use `DENSE_RANK` so tied values share the rank. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

## Coding / DSA round

**Q1. Reverse a linked list.**
```python
def reverse(head):
    prev = None
    while head:
        head.next, prev, head = prev, head, head.next
    return prev
```
O(n)/O(1). -> Revise: [DSA patterns](#/cheat/dsa-de)

**Q2. Validate a BST.** DFS with (low, high) bounds. -> Revise: [DSA patterns](#/cheat/dsa-de)

**Q3. Two Sum.** Hash map, O(n). `two_sum([2,7,11,15],9) -> [0,1]`. -> Revise: [DSA patterns](#/cheat/dsa-de)

**Q4. Detect a cycle in task dependencies.** Topological sort (Kahn's); cycle if not all nodes emitted. -> Revise: [topological sort](#/lesson/dsa-topo-sort)

**Q5. Merge intervals.** Sort by start; merge overlaps. `[[1,3],[2,6]] -> [[1,6]]`. -> Revise: [DSA patterns](#/cheat/dsa-de)

**Q6. Sliding-window longest with constraint.** Two pointers, O(n). -> Revise: [two-pointer & window](#/lesson/dsa-two-pointer-window)

## Data modeling round

**Q1. Star schema on Synapse.** Fact at line-item grain + conformed dims; on dedicated SQL pools pick **HASH** distribution on the big join key, REPLICATE small dims, ROUND_ROBIN staging. -> Revise: [star schema](#/lesson/model-star-schema)

**Q2. SCD2 for changing attributes.** New row + validity + current flag. -> Revise: [SCD2](#/lesson/model-scd2)

**Q3. Choose a distribution key.** HASH on the frequent join column to co-locate rows and minimize data movement/skew. -> Revise: [star schema](#/lesson/model-star-schema)

**Q4. Additive vs non-additive measures.** Store ratio components separately. -> Revise: [star schema](#/lesson/model-star-schema)

**Q5. Normalize vs denormalize.** Denormalize for BI reads; normalize OLTP. -> Revise: [denormalization](#/lesson/model-denormalization)

**Q6. Columnstore indexes.** Clustered columnstore for large fact scans (compression + batch mode). -> Revise: [modeling cram](#/cheat/data-modeling)

## System design round (Azure)

**Q1. End-to-end Azure analytics pipeline.** Data Factory ingest -> ADLS Gen2 (date-partitioned) -> Azure Databricks/Synapse Spark transform -> Synapse serve / Power BI. -> Revise: [cloud patterns](#/cheat/cloud)

**Q2. Make an ADF copy re-runnable.** Parameterize by date window; upsert/overwrite that partition. -> Revise: [idempotency](#/lesson/pipe-idempotency)

**Q3. Incremental extraction.** High-watermark on modified_at; advance only on success. -> Revise: [scheduling & backfill](#/lesson/pipe-scheduling-backfill)

**Q4. Streaming on Azure.** Event Hubs -> Databricks Structured Streaming -> Delta, exactly-once via checkpoint. -> Revise: [streaming cram](#/cheat/streaming)

**Q5. Cut Synapse cost.** Right-size pools, result-set caching, partition/columnstore, avoid data movement (good distribution). -> Revise: [system design](#/cheat/system-design)

**Q6. Data-quality gate.** Validation activity blocking publish + alerting. -> Revise: [DataOps cram](#/cheat/dataops)

## Behavioral round (growth mindset)

STAR, emphasize learning + collaboration:
**Q1.** Learned from a failure.
**Q2.** Took and applied hard feedback.
**Q3.** Collaborated across teams.
**Q4.** Why Microsoft.
-> Revise: [behavioral prep](#/lesson/ib-behavioral)
lessons.example: **Signature Microsoft question - 'design an Azure pipeline from operational DB to a Power BI dashboard.'** Clarify volume/freshness. Then: **Data Factory** extracts (watermark column for incremental) -> **ADLS Gen2** raw (partitioned by date) -> **Azure Databricks/Synapse Spark** cleans and builds a **star schema** -> **Synapse dedicated SQL pool** (HASH-distributed fact, replicated dims) -> **Power BI**. Cover **incremental** extraction (high-watermark), **idempotent** partition overwrites, distribution choice to avoid data movement/skew, and monitoring/alerting. Close with a growth-mindset behavioral in STAR. Microsoft rewards clean fundamentals + Azure fluency + collaborative framing.
keypoints: Loop: online/technical screen (SQL+coding) -> onsite (SQL, DSA, modeling, Azure system design, growth-mindset behavioral).
SQL: window ranks, LAG/gaps, PIVOT/conditional aggregation.
DSA is real CS: arrays/strings/trees/graphs; topological sort for dependency cycles; state time & space.
Azure stack: Data Factory (orchestrate/ingest), ADLS Gen2 (lake), Synapse (warehouse; HASH/ROUND_ROBIN/REPLICATE distribution), Databricks, Event Hubs.
Modeling: star schema + SCD2; Synapse distribution choice avoids data movement and skew.
Behavioral: growth mindset - learning from failure, feedback, collaboration - in STAR.

quiz.q: On a Synapse dedicated SQL pool, how should a large fact table be distributed to speed up joins?
options: HASH-distribute on the common join key (REPLICATE small dims)
quiz.explain: HASH on the join key co-locates matching rows (minimizes data movement); small dims are REPLICATEd, staging uses ROUND_ROBIN.

quiz.q: Which Azure service is the natural choice to orchestrate and ingest batch data?
quiz.explain: Data Factory orchestrates and copies/ingests batch data; Event Hubs is for streaming, ADLS is storage, Power BI is BI.

exercises.prompt: Microsoft-style: write SQL to pivot monthly revenue (rows -> one column per month) without the PIVOT keyword.
exercises.solution:
```sql
SELECT product,
  SUM(CASE WHEN month='Jan' THEN amt END) AS jan,
  SUM(CASE WHEN month='Feb' THEN amt END) AS feb,
  SUM(CASE WHEN month='Mar' THEN amt END) AS mar
FROM sales
GROUP BY product;
```
**Conditional aggregation** is the portable pivot: `SUM(CASE WHEN month=X THEN amt END)` puts each month's total in its own column, grouped by product. It works on every engine (unlike the T-SQL `PIVOT` operator), extends to counts/averages, and lets you add computed columns (e.g., a quarter total) easily. Mention the trade-off: it needs one CASE per output column, so a truly dynamic set of months requires dynamic SQL. -> Revise: [SQL cram](#/cheat/databases-sql)

exercises.prompt: Microsoft-style design: an ADF pipeline copies an on-prem table to ADLS nightly. Make it incremental AND safe to retry.
exercises.solution: **Incremental:** keep a **high-watermark** (e.g., `MAX(modified_at)` from the last successful run stored in a control table). Each run copies only rows with `modified_at > last_watermark`, then advances the watermark on success - so you never re-scan the whole source. **Safe to retry / idempotent:** land data into a **date-partitioned** path in ADLS (e.g., `/raw/orders/dt=2026-07-01/`) and **overwrite that partition** on each run (or MERGE into the curated Delta/Synapse table on the business key). A failed-and-retried run for the same window reproduces the same partition rather than appending duplicates. Add a **watermark-in-transaction** pattern (only advance the watermark after the write commits) so a crash mid-write is re-done, not skipped. Parameterize the date window so the same pipeline **backfills** past days. -> Revise: [idempotency](#/lesson/pipe-idempotency), [backfill](#/lesson/pipe-scheduling-backfill)

lessons.title: Netflix - DE interview playbook
lessons.summary: Netflix's senior-heavy DE loop (recruiter, technical screen, then an onsite of strong SQL, dimensional modeling, big-data pipeline design, and a rigorous culture/behavioral) with its Iceberg/Spark/Presto stack flavor - signature questions, answers, and concept links.
lessons.concept:
**The loop:** recruiter -> **technical screen** (SQL + Python/data) -> **onsite**: advanced SQL, **Kimball data modeling**, big-data **pipeline/system design** at scale, and a rigorous **culture** round. Stack: S3 + Iceberg + Spark + Presto/Trino + Kafka/Flink. Full bank below.

## SQL round (advanced)

**Q1. Top-10 titles by watch time per country.** Partitioned `ROW_NUMBER` over per-title aggregates, filter <= 10. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

**Q2. 28-day rolling active viewers.** Window frame over a date spine. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

**Q3. Completion rate per title (non-additive).** Store completes & starts; divide at query time. -> Revise: [SQL cram](#/cheat/databases-sql)

**Q4. Sessionize plays (gap-based).** LAG + running SUM. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

**Q5. Dedup heartbeat events to latest.** `ROW_NUMBER ... =1`. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

**Q6. Cohort retention by signup month.** Cohort CTE + join to later activity. -> Revise: [SQL cram](#/cheat/databases-sql)

## Coding / Python round

**Q1. Top-K titles (heap).** `Counter` + `most_common(k)` or size-K heap; O(n log K). -> Revise: [merge-K / heaps](#/lesson/dsa-merge-k)

**Q2. Parse a large events file, memory-safe.** Stream line-by-line with a Counter. -> Revise: [Python cram](#/cheat/python-de)

**Q3. Merge K sorted streams.** Min-heap of heads. -> Revise: [merge-K / heaps](#/lesson/dsa-merge-k)

**Q4. Vectorized pandas aggregation.** `groupby(...).agg(...)`, avoid iterrows. -> Revise: [pandas merge/reshape](#/lesson/pd-merge-reshape)

**Q5. Sliding-window unique count.** Two pointers + hash. -> Revise: [two-pointer & window](#/lesson/dsa-two-pointer-window)

## Data modeling round (Kimball)

**Q1. Model 'plays/streams'.** Grain = one play event; measures watch_ms, completed; FKs member/title/device/date; SCD2 on member/title. -> Revise: [star schema](#/lesson/model-star-schema)

**Q2. SCD2 details.** Validity dates + current flag + surrogate keys. -> Revise: [SCD2](#/lesson/model-scd2)

**Q3. Denormalize a serving layer.** Pre-aggregate to Druid/Iceberg for read latency; keep the fact as truth. -> Revise: [denormalization](#/lesson/model-denormalization)

**Q4. Additive vs non-additive.** watch_ms additive; completion rate not. -> Revise: [star schema](#/lesson/model-star-schema)

**Q5. Partition strategy for the fact.** Partition Iceberg by event date -> pruning + idempotent per-day backfill. -> Revise: [lakehouse partitioning](#/lesson/lake-partitioning)

## System / pipeline design round (scale)

**Q1. 'Plays per title per day' for 200M+ members.** Kafka -> Iceberg on S3 (date-partitioned) -> Spark daily aggregates (Flink if near-real-time) -> Presto/Druid serving. -> Revise: [Spark shuffle & skew](#/lesson/bd-partitions-shuffle)

**Q2. Skew on blockbuster titles.** Salt hot keys / AQE skew-join / broadcast small side. -> Revise: [shuffle & partitions](#/lesson/bd-partitions-shuffle)

**Q3. Late/out-of-order events.** Reprocess the event-date partition idempotently; watermark in streaming. -> Revise: [late data](#/lesson/stream-watermarks-late)

**Q4. Schema evolution.** Iceberg handles add/rename safely. -> Revise: [lakehouse partitioning](#/lesson/lake-partitioning)

**Q5. Backfill 90 days.** Parameterized DAG, per-date idempotent reprocess. -> Revise: [scheduling & backfill](#/lesson/pipe-scheduling-backfill)

## Culture / behavioral round

STAR with real trade-offs + judgment (Netflix values candor + ownership):
**Q1.** Disagreed with a decision - what you did.
**Q2.** Gave difficult feedback.
**Q3.** Owned an ambiguous project end to end.
**Q4.** A judgment call with incomplete data.
-> Revise: [behavioral prep](#/lesson/ib-behavioral)
lessons.example: **Signature Netflix question - 'design the data model + pipeline for viewing analytics at 200M+ members.'** Start with **grain** ('one play/heartbeat event') and the **fact + conformed dims** (member SCD2, title, device, date), noting additive (watch_ms, plays) vs non-additive (completion rate) measures. Then the **pipeline**: Kafka ingest -> **Iceberg on S3** partitioned by event date -> **Spark** daily aggregates (Flink if near-real-time) -> Presto/Trino/Druid serving. Discuss **late/out-of-order** events, **skew** on blockbuster titles (salting / AQE), **schema evolution** (Iceberg handles it), **backfills** (reprocess a date partition idempotently), and cost. Close with a **culture** answer showing senior judgment and candor. Netflix hires for autonomy - reason about trade-offs like an owner.
keypoints: Senior-heavy loop: technical screen -> onsite (advanced SQL, Kimball modeling, big-data pipeline design, rigorous culture round).
Stack flavor: S3 + Iceberg + Spark + Presto/Trino + Kafka/Flink (+ Druid serving).
SQL: partitioned top-N per group, rolling windows over a date spine.
Modeling: grain-first star schema, SCD2, additive vs non-additive; denormalize a serving layer for latency.
Design at scale: late events, skew (hot titles -> salt/AQE), schema evolution, idempotent backfills.
Culture round is real: judgment, candor, ownership - STAR with trade-offs and quantified impact.

quiz.q: A Netflix Spark aggregation is slow because a few blockbuster titles dominate one partition. Best first fix?
options: Address skew: salt the hot keys / enable AQE skew-join / broadcast the small side
quiz.explain: A long-tail task = data skew; salting, AQE skew-join handling, or broadcasting the small side rebalances the work.

quiz.q: Why store the viewing fact in Iceberg partitioned by event date?
options: Partition pruning + schema evolution + idempotent per-date backfills
It removes the need for modeling
quiz.explain: Date partitioning prunes scans and lets you reprocess a single day idempotently; Iceberg adds safe schema evolution and snapshots.

exercises.prompt: Netflix-style SQL: top 10 titles by watch time per country for last month. Write it and note the tie-handling choice.
exercises.hint: PARTITION BY country, rank by watch time.
exercises.solution:
```sql
SELECT country, title, watch_ms FROM (
  SELECT country, title, SUM(watch_ms) AS watch_ms,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY country ORDER BY SUM(watch_ms) DESC) rn
  FROM plays
  WHERE play_date >= DATE_TRUNC('month', CURRENT_DATE) - INTERVAL '1' MONTH
    AND play_date <  DATE_TRUNC('month', CURRENT_DATE)
  GROUP BY country, title) t
WHERE rn <= 10;
```
Aggregate watch time per (country, title), then a **partitioned window rank** ordered by watch time desc, keep `rn <= 10`. **Tie handling:** `ROW_NUMBER` gives exactly 10 rows even if #10 and #11 tie (arbitrary winner); use `RANK`/`DENSE_RANK` if tied titles should both appear (may return >10). Filtering the month in the WHERE keeps the window over just last month's data. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

exercises.prompt: Netflix-style: your daily Spark aggregation over viewing events runs 5x slower some days. Walk through diagnosis and fixes.
exercises.solution: **Diagnose:** open the Spark UI - a stage with a **long-tail task** (one task processing far more rows/bytes) signals **data skew** (e.g., a viral title or a null join key). Also check for a **small-files** read problem (thousands of tiny input files -> scheduling overhead) and **spill** (shuffle bytes > memory). **Fixes:** (1) **Skew** - enable **AQE** skew-join, or **salt** the hot key (add a random suffix, aggregate, then re-aggregate), or **broadcast** the small side to avoid the shuffle entirely; (2) **small files** - compact inputs / increase `maxPartitionBytes`, and `OPTIMIZE`/compaction on the Iceberg/Delta source; (3) **partitioning** - partition the source by **event date** so the job prunes to the days it needs, and repartition before wide aggregations to control task count; (4) **spill** - raise executor memory or reduce partition size. Verify by comparing task-time distribution before/after. The root cause on 'some days' is usually a **skewed hot key** on those days. -> Revise: [Spark shuffle & skew](#/lesson/bd-partitions-shuffle), [partitioning](#/lesson/lake-partitioning)

lessons.title: Databricks - DE interview playbook
lessons.summary: Databricks' DE loop (technical screen with SQL + Spark, then an onsite of Spark internals, Delta Lake, lakehouse/medallion design, SQL, and behavioral) - the deepest Spark-performance and Delta questions of any company, with answers, code, and concept links.
lessons.concept:
**The loop:** recruiter -> **technical screen** (SQL + PySpark) -> **onsite**: Spark internals & tuning, Delta Lake, lakehouse/medallion design, SQL, behavioral. The deepest Spark/Delta questions of any company. Full bank below.

## Spark internals round

**Q1. What triggers a shuffle?** Wide transforms (groupBy, join, repartition, distinct) redistribute by key across the network. -> Revise: [shuffle & partitions](#/lesson/bd-partitions-shuffle)

**Q2. Fix a skewed join.** Broadcast the small side, enable AQE skew-join, or salt the hot key. -> Revise: [shuffle & partitions](#/lesson/bd-partitions-shuffle)

**Q3. Narrow vs wide + lazy eval.** Narrow (map/filter) = no shuffle; wide = shuffle; transforms lazy, actions trigger the DAG. -> Revise: [Spark cram](#/cheat/big-data-spark)

**Q4. RDD vs DataFrame.** DataFrame uses Catalyst + Tungsten (optimized, columnar); RDD is low-level. -> Revise: [Spark cram](#/cheat/big-data-spark)

**Q5. Broadcast join threshold.**
```python
from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast
big.join(broadcast(small), "k")     # avoids shuffling `big`
```
-> Revise: [shuffle & partitions](#/lesson/bd-partitions-shuffle)

**Q6. Partition sizing.** Target ~128-256 MB; AQE coalesces shuffle partitions. -> Revise: [shuffle & partitions](#/lesson/bd-partitions-shuffle)

## Delta Lake round

**Q1. What makes Delta ACID?** Parquet + transaction log (`_delta_log`). -> Revise: [Delta MERGE](#/lesson/dbx-delta-merge)

**Q2. Upsert / CDC.**
```sql
MERGE INTO tgt t USING src s ON t.id=s.id
WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET *
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT *;
```
-> Revise: [Delta MERGE](#/lesson/dbx-delta-merge)

**Q3. Small-files problem.** `OPTIMIZE` (+ `ZORDER`); tune write partitioning. -> Revise: [Delta MERGE](#/lesson/dbx-delta-merge)

**Q4. Time travel.** `SELECT * FROM t VERSION AS OF 12;` / `DESCRIBE HISTORY`. -> Revise: [Delta MERGE](#/lesson/dbx-delta-merge)

**Q5. VACUUM caution.** Purges old files; too-short retention breaks time travel/readers. -> Revise: [Delta MERGE](#/lesson/dbx-delta-merge)

**Q6. Full sync (delete removed rows).** `WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE THEN DELETE`. -> Revise: [Delta MERGE](#/lesson/dbx-delta-merge)

## Lakehouse / pipeline design round

**Q1. Design a medallion pipeline.** Bronze (Auto Loader, append) -> Silver (clean/dedup, MERGE) -> Gold (aggregated serving). -> Revise: [lakehouse partitioning](#/lesson/lake-partitioning)

**Q2. Exactly-once structured streaming.** Checkpoint + idempotent Delta sink; watermark bounds late-data state. -> Revise: [streaming watermarks](#/lesson/dbx-ss-watermark)

**Q3. Auto Loader.** `cloudFiles` with schema location + checkpoint = incremental idempotent ingest. -> Revise: [idempotency](#/lesson/pipe-idempotency)

**Q4. Partitioning choice.** Low-cardinality date, not high-cardinality ids (avoids tiny files). -> Revise: [lakehouse partitioning](#/lesson/lake-partitioning)

**Q5. DLT / expectations.** Declarative pipelines with data-quality expectations. -> Revise: [DataOps cram](#/cheat/dataops)

## SQL & behavioral round

**Q1. Dedup latest per key.** `QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY id ORDER BY ts DESC)=1`. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

**Q2. Rolling aggregate.** Window frame `ROWS BETWEEN N PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW`. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

**Q3. Behavioral.** A hard data bug you root-caused; owned a migration - STAR + impact. -> Revise: [behavioral prep](#/lesson/ib-behavioral)
lessons.example: **Signature Databricks question - 'design a medallion lakehouse for streaming IoT/event data and make it exactly-once.'** **Bronze:** ingest continuously with **Auto Loader** (`cloudFiles`, schema location + checkpoint), append raw. **Silver:** stream/batch clean, dedup, and **MERGE** into a conformed Delta table on the business key (idempotent). **Gold:** aggregate for serving; `OPTIMIZE`+`ZORDER` on filter columns. **Exactly-once:** structured streaming **checkpoint** + transactional Delta sink means a restart replays from the checkpoint without duplicates; a **watermark** bounds state and drops too-late events. Discuss partitioning by date (low cardinality), small-file compaction, AQE for skew, and Photon for speed. This tests the full Spark+Delta+lakehouse stack Databricks is built on.
keypoints: Loop: SQL+Spark technical screen -> onsite (Spark internals/tuning, Delta Lake, medallion/lakehouse design, SQL, behavioral).
Spark: shuffles = wide transforms; lazy eval + actions; fix skew via broadcast / AQE / salting.
Delta = Parquet + transaction log -> ACID, MERGE upserts, time travel, OPTIMIZE/ZORDER, VACUUM.
Medallion: bronze (raw, Auto Loader) -> silver (clean, MERGE) -> gold (aggregated/serving).
Exactly-once streaming = checkpoint + idempotent Delta sink; watermark bounds late-data state.
Tuning: partition by low-cardinality date, compact small files, Photon, right-size clusters.

quiz.q: In Spark, which operation forces a shuffle?
quiz.explain: Wide transformations (groupBy, join, repartition, distinct) redistribute data across partitions by key - that's the shuffle; narrow ops (map/filter/select) don't.

quiz.q: How does Delta Lake achieve exactly-once in Structured Streaming?
options: Checkpoint + idempotent/transactional Delta sink (restart replays without duplicates)
quiz.explain: The streaming checkpoint tracks progress and the transactional Delta sink commits atomically, so a restart resumes exactly-once.

exercises.prompt: Databricks-style: a nightly PySpark join of a huge fact with a small dim spills and runs for hours. Diagnose and fix.
exercises.solution: **Diagnose** in the Spark UI: a large **shuffle** on the join stage and possible **spill** (shuffle write > memory), maybe a **long-tail task** (skew). **Fixes:** (1) If the dim is small (< a few hundred MB), **broadcast** it - `df.join(broadcast(dim), 'k')` or rely on `spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold` - which turns the shuffle join into a broadcast hash join (no fact shuffle). (2) If broadcast isn't possible and there's **skew** on the join key, enable **AQE** (`spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=true`, skew-join on) or **salt** the hot keys (append a random bucket to the key on both sides, join, then drop the salt). (3) Tune **shuffle partitions** (AQE coalesces them automatically) and ensure the fact is **partition-pruned** (filter on the partition/date column). (4) Enable **Photon**. Verify by re-checking task-time distribution and shuffle bytes. Usually **broadcasting the dim** alone fixes the classic 'big fact + small dim' case. -> Revise: [shuffle & partitions](#/lesson/bd-partitions-shuffle), [Delta MERGE](#/lesson/dbx-delta-merge)

exercises.prompt: Databricks-style: write a Delta MERGE that upserts a daily batch and explain why MERGE makes the load idempotent.
exercises.solution:
```sql
MERGE INTO silver_orders t
USING staged_orders s
  ON t.order_id = s.order_id
WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET *
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT *;
```
MERGE upserts on the **business key** (`order_id`): existing rows are **updated**, new rows **inserted**. This makes the daily load **idempotent** - re-running the same batch (after a failure/retry) updates the same rows to the same values and inserts nothing new, so there are **no duplicates**, unlike a blind `INSERT`/append. Delta's **transaction log** commits the MERGE atomically (all-or-nothing), so a crash mid-merge leaves the table in its prior consistent state to be safely retried. For a full sync you can add `WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE THEN DELETE` to remove rows absent from the batch. Follow with `OPTIMIZE` to compact the files MERGE produces. -> Revise: [Delta MERGE](#/lesson/dbx-delta-merge), [idempotency](#/lesson/pipe-idempotency)

lessons.title: Uber - DE interview playbook
