lessons.summary: Uber's DE loop (technical screen with SQL + Python, then an onsite of advanced SQL, marketplace data modeling, real-time pipeline/system design, coding, and behavioral) with its streaming-heavy Kafka/Flink/Hive/Presto stack - signature questions, answers, and concept links.
lessons.concept:
**The loop:** recruiter -> **technical screen** (SQL + Python/coding) -> **onsite**: advanced **SQL**, **marketplace data modeling**, **real-time pipeline/system design**, coding, and behavioral. **Emphasis:** real-time/streaming, marketplace metrics (trips, surge, ETA), and scale on a **Kafka + Flink + Hive/HDFS + Presto** stack.

### SQL round
**Q: For each rider, time between consecutive trips.** `LAG(trip_ts) OVER (PARTITION BY rider ORDER BY trip_ts)`, subtract. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

**Q: Sessionize a driver's online periods (gap > 15 min = new session).** Gaps-and-islands: LAG + flag + running SUM. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

### Data modeling round
**Q: Model the 'trips' fact for a two-sided marketplace.** Grain = 'one completed trip'; measures (fare, distance, duration, surge_multiplier); FKs (rider, driver, city, date); conform dims; SCD2 on driver/rider attributes. -> Revise: [star schema](#/lesson/model-star-schema), [SCD2](#/lesson/model-scd2)

### Real-time / system design round
**Q: Design real-time surge pricing / live supply-demand per city.** Ingest location/request events via **Kafka**; process with **Flink** using event-time **windows + watermarks** keyed by geo-hash; emit per-cell supply/demand every few seconds; serve from a low-latency store; handle **late events** and **exactly-once**. -> Revise: [streaming windows & watermarks](#/lesson/stream-watermarks-late), [delivery semantics](#/lesson/sd-idempotency-delivery)

**Q: Design 'trips per city per hour' for both dashboards and ML.** Lambda-ish: Kafka -> Flink (speed layer, near-real-time) + Spark/Hive batch (accurate reprocess) on HDFS/S3 partitioned by date/hour; serve via Presto. Discuss skew (hot cities), backfills, schema evolution. -> Revise: [Spark shuffle & skew](#/lesson/bd-partitions-shuffle), [streaming cram](#/cheat/streaming)

### Coding & behavioral
**Q coding: top-K destinations in a stream / merge sorted trip logs.** Heap for top-K; k-way merge for sorted streams. -> Revise: [DSA patterns](#/cheat/dsa-de)
**Q behavioral: 'a time you delivered under pressure / owned an incident.'** STAR + impact. -> Revise: [behavioral prep](#/lesson/ib-behavioral)
lessons.example: **Signature Uber question - 'design real-time supply/demand (surge) per city.'** Clarify latency (seconds) and accuracy. Then: rider-request + driver-location events -> **Kafka** (partitioned by city/geo-hash) -> **Flink** job keyed by geo-cell with **event-time tumbling/sliding windows + watermark** computing supply, demand, and the surge multiplier every few seconds -> push to a fast serving store (Redis/Pinot) for the pricing service. Cover **late/out-of-order** GPS events (watermark + allowed lateness), **exactly-once** (Flink checkpoints + idempotent sink), **skew** (hot cities - key by finer geo-cells or salt), and a **batch reconciliation** (Spark/Hive) to correct the speed layer. This tests streaming event-time semantics, which Uber weights heavily.
keypoints: Loop: SQL+Python technical screen -> onsite (advanced SQL, marketplace modeling, real-time system design, coding, behavioral).
Stack: Kafka + Flink (streaming), Hive/HDFS/S3 + Spark (batch), Presto (query), Pinot/Redis (serving).
SQL: LAG for inter-event gaps, sessionization (gaps-and-islands), rolling windows.
Modeling: trips fact at trip grain, conformed dims, SCD2; two-sided marketplace metrics.
Real-time design: event-time windows + watermarks, exactly-once (checkpoints + idempotent sink), late data, skew on hot cities.
Often a Lambda-style split: Flink speed layer + Spark/Hive batch reconciliation.

quiz.q: Uber real-time design: which handles out-of-order GPS events correctly in the streaming layer?
options: Event-time windows with watermarks (+ allowed lateness)
quiz.explain: Event-time windows + watermarks order events by when they happened and bound how long to wait for late ones - essential for correct real-time surge.

quiz.q: Why key the surge stream by geo-hash / city cell?
options: To parallelize and compute supply/demand per local area (and manage skew on hot cells)
quiz.explain: Surge is local; keying by geo-cell parallelizes the computation per area, and finer cells / salting handle hot-city skew.

exercises.prompt: Uber-style SQL: for each rider, compute the gap (in minutes) between consecutive trips and flag gaps over 30 days.
exercises.solution:
```sql
SELECT rider_id, trip_ts,
  DATE_DIFF('minute', LAG(trip_ts) OVER (PARTITION BY rider_id ORDER BY trip_ts), trip_ts) AS gap_min,
  CASE WHEN LAG(trip_ts) OVER (PARTITION BY rider_id ORDER BY trip_ts)
            < trip_ts - INTERVAL '30' DAY THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS churned_back
FROM trips;
```
`LAG` fetches each rider's **previous** trip timestamp (partitioned by rider, ordered by time); the diff is the inter-trip gap, and the CASE flags a **reactivation** after a 30-day gap. The first trip per rider has a NULL LAG (gap undefined) - handle explicitly if needed. This pattern (previous-event via LAG) underlies retention, reactivation, and sessionization metrics. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

exercises.prompt: Uber-style design: 'trips per city per hour' must power both a live ops dashboard (seconds) and accurate finance reports. Outline the architecture and why.
exercises.solution: Use a **Lambda-style** split because the two consumers have different needs. **Speed layer:** trip events -> **Kafka** (partitioned by city) -> **Flink** with **event-time hourly windows + watermark**, emitting near-real-time counts to a fast store (Pinot/Redis) for the **live dashboard** - low latency, tolerates small inaccuracy from very late events. **Batch layer:** the same Kafka events land on **HDFS/S3 partitioned by date/hour**; a **Spark/Hive** job recomputes the *authoritative* hourly counts (all late data included, dedup, idempotent per partition) for **finance** - accurate, reproducible, backfillable. Serving reads speed for 'now' and batch for 'settled' hours. Address **skew** (hot cities -> finer keys/salt), **exactly-once** (Flink checkpoints + idempotent sink; batch MERGE/overwrite per partition), **schema evolution**, and **backfills** (re-run a date partition). The 'why': you can't get both seconds-latency and perfect accuracy from one path, so you combine a fast-but-approximate stream with a slow-but-correct batch. -> Revise: [streaming](#/cheat/streaming), [delivery semantics](#/lesson/sd-idempotency-delivery)

lessons.title: Airbnb - DE interview playbook
lessons.summary: Airbnb's DE loop (SQL-heavy technical screen, then an onsite of advanced SQL, dimensional modeling, data quality/metrics, pipeline/Airflow design, and behavioral) - reflecting its Airflow + metrics-layer + data-quality culture, with questions, answers, and concept links.
lessons.concept:
**The loop:** recruiter -> **technical screen** (SQL-heavy + Python) -> **onsite**: advanced **SQL**, **dimensional modeling**, **data quality & metrics consistency**, **pipeline/Airflow design**, and behavioral. **Emphasis:** SQL depth, clean dimensional modeling, and a strong **data-quality / single-source-of-truth metrics** culture (Airbnb created **Airflow** and the **Minerva** metrics layer).

### SQL round (advanced)
**Q: Booking funnel conversion (search -> view -> book).** Cohort/funnel with conditional aggregation or window steps; ratios per stage. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

**Q: Occupancy rate per listing per month, filling zero-booking months.** Join to a **date spine**, `COALESCE` to 0, then aggregate. -> Revise: [SQL cram](#/cheat/databases-sql)

### Data modeling round
**Q: Design the 'bookings' star schema.** Grain = 'one booking'; measures (nights, price, fees); dims (guest, host, listing, date); **SCD2** on listing/host attributes (price tier, location changes). Emphasize **conformed dimensions** so every team's metrics agree. -> Revise: [star schema](#/lesson/model-star-schema), [SCD2](#/lesson/model-scd2)

**Q: Why a central metrics layer?** So 'active listing' or 'booking' is defined **once** and reused - avoids conflicting numbers across dashboards. -> Revise: [modeling cram](#/cheat/data-modeling)

### Data quality & pipeline (Airflow) round
**Q: Design a reliable daily pipeline in Airflow.** DAG with idempotent, **date-partitioned** tasks; upstream **sensors**; **data-quality checks** (row counts, null keys, ranges) that block publish; retries + alerting; backfillable. -> Revise: [idempotency](#/lesson/pipe-idempotency), [scheduling & backfill](#/lesson/pipe-scheduling-backfill)

**Q: A daily metric silently dropped 20%. How do you catch it before stakeholders?** Automated **anomaly/threshold checks** + freshness + volume tests in the pipeline (fail the DAG, alert, quarantine). -> Revise: [DataOps cram](#/cheat/dataops)

### Behavioral
**Q: 'A time you improved data trust / handled ambiguous requirements / worked cross-functionally.'** Airbnb values collaboration + being a 'host' - STAR with impact on data trust. -> Revise: [behavioral prep](#/lesson/ib-behavioral)
lessons.example: **Signature Airbnb question - 'design the bookings data model + a reliable Airflow pipeline, and guarantee the numbers are trustworthy.'** **Model:** `fact_booking` at booking grain (nights, price, fees, status) + conformed dims (guest, host, listing with **SCD2**, date); define measures once so all teams agree. **Pipeline:** an **Airflow** DAG - extract (incremental high-watermark) -> land date-partitioned -> transform to the star -> **idempotent** MERGE/partition-overwrite -> **data-quality gate** (row counts, not-null keys, referential checks, anomaly thresholds) that **blocks publish** on failure -> refresh the metrics layer. Add **sensors** for upstream readiness, **retries**, **alerting**, and **backfill** by re-running dates. Emphasize **data trust**: tests + a single metric definition prevent conflicting dashboards. This blends modeling, orchestration, and quality - Airbnb's core DE bar.
keypoints: Loop: SQL-heavy technical screen -> onsite (advanced SQL, dimensional modeling, data quality/metrics, Airflow pipeline design, behavioral).
SQL: funnels/cohorts, occupancy over a date spine, window analytics - depth matters.
Modeling: booking-grain star schema, SCD2, and conformed dimensions for consistent metrics.
Metrics layer (Minerva-style): define each metric once = single source of truth, no conflicting numbers.
Airflow pipelines: idempotent date-partitioned tasks, sensors, retries, backfills.
Data quality is first-class: row/null/range/anomaly checks that block publish + alert.

quiz.q: Airbnb stresses a central metrics layer. What problem does it solve?
options: One canonical definition per metric so dashboards don't disagree
quiz.explain: A metrics layer defines 'booking'/'active listing' once and reuses it - eliminating conflicting numbers across teams.

quiz.q: Best way to compute monthly occupancy including months with zero bookings?
options: LEFT JOIN a date/listing spine and COALESCE missing counts to 0
quiz.explain: Zero-activity months don't exist in the bookings table; join a complete spine and COALESCE to 0 so they're represented.

exercises.prompt: Airbnb-style SQL: compute search->book conversion rate per day (bookings / searches, same day).
exercises.hint: Aggregate each event type per day, then divide.
exercises.solution:
```sql
WITH s AS (SELECT DATE(ts) d, COUNT(*) searches FROM search_events GROUP BY DATE(ts)),
     b AS (SELECT DATE(ts) d, COUNT(*) bookings FROM booking_events GROUP BY DATE(ts))
SELECT s.d,
       s.searches,
       COALESCE(b.bookings,0) AS bookings,
       ROUND(COALESCE(b.bookings,0)*1.0 / NULLIF(s.searches,0), 4) AS conv_rate
FROM s LEFT JOIN b ON s.d = b.d
ORDER BY s.d;
```
Aggregate each funnel stage per day in its own CTE, **LEFT JOIN** on date (so days with searches but zero bookings still appear), `COALESCE` bookings to 0, and divide with `NULLIF(searches,0)` to avoid divide-by-zero. Multiply by 1.0 to force float division. For a multi-step funnel (search->view->book) extend with more CTEs or conditional aggregation over a unified event table; watch **attribution** (same-day vs windowed) and define it explicitly. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window), [SQL cram](#/cheat/databases-sql)

exercises.prompt: Airbnb-style: design data-quality checks for a daily bookings pipeline so bad data never reaches dashboards.
exercises.hint: Volume, nulls, referential, anomaly + block publish.
exercises.solution: Add a **quality gate** as a task between transform and publish that fails the DAG (and alerts) on any breach: (1) **Volume/freshness** - today's row count within an expected band vs a trailing average, and data is present for the run date (catches upstream drops/late data). (2) **Not-null / uniqueness** - primary keys (`booking_id`) non-null and unique; required fields present. (3) **Referential integrity** - every `listing_id`/`guest_id` exists in its dimension (no orphan facts). (4) **Range/domain** - `nights > 0`, `price >= 0`, status in an allowed set. (5) **Anomaly** - key metrics (daily bookings, revenue) within a threshold of the trailing trend (catches the silent 20% drop). On failure: **block publish**, route bad rows to a **quarantine** table, alert on-call, and keep yesterday's good data live. Make checks **idempotent** and **backfill-aware** (evaluate per date partition). Frameworks: Great Expectations / dbt tests / Airflow sensors + a validation operator. The goal is **data trust** - stakeholders never see numbers that failed a check. -> Revise: [DataOps cram](#/cheat/dataops), [idempotency](#/lesson/pipe-idempotency)

lessons.title: LinkedIn - DE interview playbook
lessons.summary: LinkedIn's DE loop (technical screen with SQL + coding, then a CS-heavy onsite of SQL, DSA coding, data modeling, streaming/pipeline system design, and values-based behavioral) reflecting its Kafka-origin streaming stack - questions, answers, code, and concept links.
lessons.concept:
**The loop:** recruiter -> **technical screen** (SQL + a coding problem) -> **onsite**: **SQL**, **coding/DSA** (LinkedIn is CS-heavy), **data modeling**, **streaming/pipeline system design**, and a **values-based** behavioral ('members first'). **Emphasis:** strong CS fundamentals and streaming/scale - LinkedIn **invented Kafka** and runs Samza/Brooklin/Pinot/Venice.

### SQL round
**Q: 2nd-degree connections / mutual connections.** Self-join the connections graph, exclude 1st-degree and self; count mutuals. -> Revise: [SQL cram](#/cheat/databases-sql)

**Q: Most-viewed profiles per week, top 5.** Partitioned window rank over weekly aggregates. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

### Coding / DSA round
**Q: Course/skill prerequisite ordering; detect impossible schedules.** **Topological sort** (Kahn's); a cycle = impossible. -> Revise: [topological sort](#/lesson/dsa-topo-sort)

**Q: Longest substring / sliding-window and merge-K problems.** Two pointers / deque; heap for K-way merge. -> Revise: [two-pointer & window](#/lesson/dsa-two-pointer-window), [merge-K](#/lesson/dsa-merge-k)

### Data modeling round
**Q: Model the 'who viewed your profile' feed.** Fact at 'one profile-view event' grain + dims (viewer, viewee, date); aggregate for the feed; SCD2 on member attributes. -> Revise: [star schema](#/lesson/model-star-schema), [SCD2](#/lesson/model-scd2)

### Streaming / system design round
**Q: Design a real-time activity feed / notifications at LinkedIn scale.** Producers -> **Kafka** topics (partitioned by member) -> stream processors (Samza/Flink) fan-out/aggregate -> serving store (Pinot for analytics, key-value for feed); **CDC** from source DBs via Brooklin/Debezium into Kafka. Cover ordering per key, exactly-once, late data, and backpressure. -> Revise: [streaming cram](#/cheat/streaming), [CDC design](#/lesson/sd-design-cdc)

### Behavioral (values)
**Q: 'A time you put members/users first / collaborated / owned a hard problem.'** STAR aligned to LinkedIn values (members first, relationships matter, act like an owner) with impact. -> Revise: [behavioral prep](#/lesson/ib-behavioral)
lessons.example: **Signature LinkedIn question - 'design a real-time system for who-viewed-your-profile / an activity feed at scale.'** Clarify volume (billions of events) and latency. Then: view/activity events -> **Kafka** topics **partitioned by member id** (preserves per-member ordering) -> a stream processor (**Samza/Flink**) aggregates counts and fans out to followers -> serve the feed from a low-latency KV store and analytics from **Pinot**; ingest source-DB changes via **CDC** (Brooklin/Debezium) into Kafka so derived views stay fresh. Discuss **partitioning/ordering** (per-key order via partition key), **exactly-once** (idempotent producers + transactional processing), **late/out-of-order** events, **backpressure**, and **hot members** (skew). This plays directly to LinkedIn's Kafka-native heritage - show you understand log-based streaming and CDC. -> Revise: [CDC design](#/lesson/sd-design-cdc)
keypoints: Loop: SQL+coding technical screen -> CS-heavy onsite (SQL, DSA coding, modeling, streaming system design, values behavioral).
DSA is real: topological sort (prereqs/cycles), sliding window, heaps/merge-K, graphs - state complexity.
SQL: graph-style self-joins (mutual/2nd-degree connections), partitioned top-N.
Streaming heritage: Kafka (invented here), Samza/Flink, Brooklin CDC, Pinot serving.
System design: partition by key for ordering, exactly-once, CDC into Kafka, handle hot-member skew + backpressure.
Behavioral: STAR aligned to 'members first' / ownership values.

quiz.q: LinkedIn coding: given course prerequisites, output a valid order or detect it's impossible. Which algorithm?
options: Topological sort (Kahn's/DFS); a cycle means impossible
quiz.explain: Prerequisite ordering is a DAG problem: topological sort yields a valid order, and a detected cycle means no valid schedule exists.

quiz.q: Why partition a Kafka activity topic by member id?
options: To preserve per-member event ordering and parallelize by member
quiz.explain: Kafka guarantees order within a partition; keying by member id keeps each member's events ordered while parallelizing across members.

exercises.prompt: LinkedIn-style coding: return a valid order to take courses given prerequisite pairs, or detect a cycle. Describe the algorithm + complexity.
exercises.solution: Model courses as a **directed graph** (edge prereq -> course). **Kahn's algorithm:** compute each node's **in-degree**; push all in-degree-0 nodes to a queue; repeatedly pop a node, append it to the order, and decrement its neighbors' in-degrees, pushing any that hit 0. If the final order contains **all N nodes**, it's a valid topological order; if fewer (some nodes never reached 0), a **cycle** exists -> impossible schedule. **Complexity: O(V + E)** time, O(V + E) space (adjacency list + in-degree map + queue). DFS with a recursion stack + colors (white/gray/black) is the equivalent alternative and detects a **back edge** = cycle. Edge cases: disconnected graphs (multiple in-degree-0 starts), duplicate edges, self-loops (immediate cycle). This exact pattern powers **DAG scheduling** in Airflow/dbt/Spark. -> Revise: [topological sort](#/lesson/dsa-topo-sort)

exercises.prompt: LinkedIn-style SQL: find 2nd-degree connections of a member (friends-of-friends, excluding direct connections and self).
exercises.solution:
```sql
SELECT DISTINCT c2.connected_id AS second_degree
FROM connections c1
JOIN connections c2 ON c1.connected_id = c2.member_id
WHERE c1.member_id = :me
  AND c2.connected_id <> :me                                   -- not myself
  AND c2.connected_id NOT IN (                                 -- not already a 1st-degree
      SELECT connected_id FROM connections WHERE member_id = :me);
```
Start from my direct connections (`c1` where `member_id = me`), then hop again (`c2`) to *their* connections; those `c2.connected_id` are friends-of-friends. Exclude **myself** and anyone who is **already a 1st-degree** connection so only genuine 2nd-degree members remain; `DISTINCT` dedups people reachable via multiple mutual friends. To also rank by **mutual-connection count** (a 'People You May Know' signal), `GROUP BY second_degree` and `COUNT(*)`. Assumes the connections table stores each edge (often both directions); if stored one-way, union the reverse. -> Revise: [SQL cram](#/cheat/databases-sql)

lessons.title: Apple - DE interview playbook
lessons.summary: Apple's team-dependent DE loop (technical screen, then an 'as-appropriate' onsite of SQL, Spark/coding, data modeling, pragmatic system design, and behavioral) with a privacy/quality bent - signature questions, answers, and concept links.
lessons.concept:
**The loop:** recruiter -> **technical screen** (SQL + coding) -> **onsite** (team-dependent, 4-6 rounds): **SQL**, **Spark/Python coding**, **data modeling**, **pragmatic pipeline/system design**, and behavioral. Apple is secretive and **team-specific**, so breadth + fundamentals matter; teams care about **data quality and privacy**. **Emphasis:** solid SQL, Spark at scale, clean modeling, and practical design.

### SQL round
**Q: Top-N per group and running totals.** Partitioned window rank; `SUM() OVER (ORDER BY ...)` for cumulative. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

**Q: Day-over-day change and 7-day moving average of a metric.** `LAG` + a windowed frame. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

### Spark / coding round
**Q: Deduplicate a massive dataset keeping the latest per key in Spark.** `Window.partitionBy(key).orderBy(desc(ts))` + `row_number()==1`; mind **skew** and shuffle. -> Revise: [shuffle & partitions](#/lesson/bd-partitions-shuffle)

**Q: Join a huge fact with a small dim efficiently.** Broadcast the small side; watch skew. -> Revise: [Spark cram](#/cheat/big-data-spark)

### Data modeling round
**Q: Model device/usage analytics with privacy in mind.** Fact at event grain + conformed dims; **aggregate/anonymize** (drop or hash PII, roll up to cohorts) so the serving layer holds no raw identifiers; SCD2 where attributes change. -> Revise: [star schema](#/lesson/model-star-schema), [SCD2](#/lesson/model-scd2)

### System / pipeline design round
**Q: Design a large-scale batch pipeline for device telemetry.** Ingest to a lake partitioned by date; Spark transform; **idempotent** partition loads; DQ + privacy filters; serve aggregates. Discuss scale, skew, backfills, and minimizing retained PII. -> Revise: [idempotency](#/lesson/pipe-idempotency), [system design](#/cheat/system-design)

### Behavioral
**Q: 'A time you dove deep to fix a quality issue / collaborated across secretive teams / raised the bar.'** STAR; Apple values **excellence, attention to detail, and discretion**. -> Revise: [behavioral prep](#/lesson/ib-behavioral)
lessons.example: **Signature Apple question - 'design a privacy-aware analytics pipeline for device telemetry at scale.'** Clarify what insight is needed vs what raw data must be retained. Then: ingest telemetry to a **date-partitioned lake**; a **Spark** job cleans, **anonymizes/aggregates** early (hash or drop identifiers, roll up to cohort level) so downstream tables hold **no raw PII**; **idempotent** per-date loads; **data-quality** + privacy checks gate publish; serve aggregates to analysts. Discuss **scale** (billions of events), **skew** (hot device types - salt/broadcast), **backfills** (reprocess a date), and **data minimization** (retain the least PII for the shortest time). Apple rewards strong fundamentals plus visible care for **quality and privacy**.
keypoints: Loop is team-dependent: technical screen -> onsite (SQL, Spark/coding, modeling, pragmatic design, behavioral); breadth + fundamentals matter.
SQL: top-N per group, running totals, LAG deltas, moving averages.
Spark: dedup-latest via window row_number; broadcast small dims; manage skew/shuffle.
Modeling with privacy: anonymize/aggregate early, hash or drop PII, SCD2 for changing attributes.
Design: date-partitioned lake, idempotent loads, DQ + privacy gates, data minimization.
Behavioral: excellence, attention to detail, discretion - STAR with quantified impact.

quiz.q: Apple Spark round: keep only the latest record per key in a huge dataset. Idiomatic approach?
options: collect() to the driver and dedup in Python
quiz.explain: A partitioned window ranked by descending timestamp keeps the latest per key at scale; be mindful of skew/shuffle.

quiz.q: What's a privacy-aware modeling choice for device analytics?
options: Anonymize/aggregate early (hash or drop PII, roll up to cohorts) so serving tables hold no raw identifiers
Put PII in the fact grain
quiz.explain: Data minimization: transform to anonymized/aggregated form early so downstream tables carry no raw PII.

exercises.prompt: Apple-style Spark (PySpark): dedup events keeping the latest row per user_id, and note the scale gotcha.
exercises.solution:
```python
from pyspark.sql import Window, functions as F
w = Window.partitionBy('user_id').orderBy(F.col('event_ts').desc())
latest = (events
    .withColumn('rn', F.row_number().over(w))
    .filter(F.col('rn') == 1)
    .drop('rn'))
```
Partition by the key, order by **descending timestamp**, keep `row_number()==1` = the latest row per `user_id`. **Scale gotcha:** this is a **wide** operation - it shuffles all rows by `user_id`, so a **skewed** key (one user with a huge share of events) creates a long-tail task. Mitigations: enable **AQE** skew handling, **salt** the hot key, or pre-aggregate; and ensure the input is **partition-pruned** (filter to the needed dates) so you don't shuffle the whole history. If you only need the latest *value of a column* (not the whole row), a `groupBy(key).agg(F.max_by(...))` can avoid a full sort. -> Revise: [shuffle & partitions](#/lesson/bd-partitions-shuffle)

exercises.prompt: Apple-style: design a telemetry pipeline that yields useful analytics while minimizing retained PII. Outline it.
exercises.solution: **Principle: data minimization** - collect and keep the least identifying data for the shortest time that still answers the questions. **Ingest:** raw telemetry lands in a short-retention, access-controlled **date-partitioned** raw zone. **Transform (Spark):** as early as possible, **strip or hash** direct identifiers, **coarsen** quasi-identifiers (bucket ages, truncate geo, generalize device models), and **aggregate to cohorts** (counts/rates per segment) so the analytics tables contain **no raw PII**; consider differential-privacy noise for sensitive aggregates. **Load:** **idempotent** per-date writes to the curated/gold layer; **DQ + privacy checks** (no forbidden fields, k-anonymity thresholds) that **block publish** on breach. **Retention:** auto-expire the raw zone (lifecycle policy); keep only aggregates long-term. **Access:** least-privilege on raw, broad on aggregates. This delivers analytics value while keeping identifiable data scarce, short-lived, and locked down - the Apple bar for quality + privacy. -> Revise: [idempotency](#/lesson/pipe-idempotency), [system design](#/cheat/system-design)

lessons.title: Stripe - DE interview playbook
lessons.summary: Stripe's DE loop (practical technical screen, then an onsite of SQL, real-world coding, financial data modeling, correctness-obsessed system design, and operating-principles behavioral) where idempotency, exactly-once, and reconciliation are the whole game - questions, answers, code, and concept links.
lessons.concept:
**The loop:** recruiter -> **technical screen** (SQL + practical coding) -> **onsite**: **SQL**, **real-world coding** (parse/transform messy data, work with an API), **financial data modeling**, **correctness-first system design**, and an **operating-principles** behavioral. **Emphasis:** Stripe handles money, so **idempotency, exactly-once, reconciliation, and correctness** dominate - a duplicate or lost record is a real financial bug.

### SQL round
**Q: Daily net revenue = charges - refunds - disputes.** Aggregate signed amounts by day; careful with currency and timezones. -> Revise: [SQL cram](#/cheat/databases-sql)

**Q: Running balance per merchant account (ledger).** `SUM(amount) OVER (PARTITION BY account ORDER BY ts)` - a running total over an ordered ledger. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

### Coding round (practical)
**Q: Process a payments webhook/file with retries; don't double-count.** Dedup on an **idempotency key** (event id); upsert; treat delivery as **at-least-once** so your consumer must be idempotent. -> Revise: [idempotency](#/lesson/pipe-idempotency)

### Financial data modeling round
**Q: Model a double-entry ledger / payments fact.** Event-sourced, **append-only** entries (never mutate money records); fact at 'one ledger entry' grain; dims (account, merchant, currency, date); derive balances by aggregation. **Immutability + idempotency keys** guarantee auditability. -> Revise: [star schema](#/lesson/model-star-schema)

**Q: Handle a correction?** Post a **reversing entry**, don't edit history (keeps the audit trail). -> Revise: [SCD2 / history](#/lesson/model-scd2)

### System design round (correctness-first)
**Q: Design a pipeline ingesting payment events into an analytics warehouse with exactly-once accuracy.** At-least-once delivery + **idempotent** sink (dedup on event id / MERGE) = effectively exactly-once; **reconcile** against source totals daily (control totals) and alert on drift; partition by date; backfill safely. -> Revise: [delivery semantics](#/lesson/sd-idempotency-delivery), [CDC design](#/lesson/sd-design-cdc)

### Behavioral (operating principles)
**Q: 'A time you caught a subtle correctness bug / raised the quality bar / acted like an owner.'** Stripe prizes **rigor and user-first thinking** - STAR with a concrete correctness/impact story. -> Revise: [behavioral prep](#/lesson/ib-behavioral)
lessons.example: **Signature Stripe question - 'design a pipeline that ingests payment events into the warehouse with exactly-once accuracy and is auditable.'** Delivery is **at-least-once** (webhooks/queues retry), so the consumer must be **idempotent**: dedup on the **event/idempotency key** and **MERGE** into an **append-only, event-sourced** ledger table (never mutate money rows - post reversing entries for corrections). Partition by date; make each load **reproducible** and **backfillable**. Add a **daily reconciliation**: compare warehouse control totals (count and sum of amounts per day/currency) against the source-of-truth API/ledger and **alert on any drift** - because a single duplicated or dropped charge is a financial bug. Discuss currency/timezone handling, late events, and CDC from source DBs. The whole answer is about **correctness, idempotency, immutability, and reconciliation** - Stripe's core DE values.
keypoints: Loop: practical SQL+coding screen -> onsite (SQL, real-world coding, financial modeling, correctness-first design, operating-principles behavioral).
Money means correctness: idempotency, exactly-once, and reconciliation dominate every round.
SQL: signed-amount aggregation (net revenue), running balances over an ordered ledger, currency/timezone care.
Modeling: append-only, event-sourced double-entry ledger; corrections via reversing entries (never mutate history).
Design: at-least-once delivery + idempotent/dedup sink = effectively exactly-once; daily control-total reconciliation + alerting.
Behavioral: rigor, ownership, user-first - STAR with a concrete correctness story.

quiz.q: A payments webhook may deliver the same event more than once. How do you avoid double-counting revenue?
options: Dedup on the idempotency/event key and upsert (idempotent consumer)
quiz.explain: Delivery is at-least-once, so the consumer must be idempotent: dedup on the event/idempotency key and upsert so re-delivery is a no-op.

quiz.q: How should a financial ledger handle a correction to a past entry?
options: Post a reversing entry and a new correct entry (append-only, preserve history)
quiz.explain: Money records are immutable/auditable; corrections are new reversing + adjusting entries, never edits to history.

exercises.prompt: Stripe-style: write SQL for daily net revenue (charges minus refunds minus disputes) per currency.
exercises.hint: Signed amounts, group by day + currency.
exercises.solution:
```sql
SELECT DATE(created_at) AS d, currency,
  SUM(CASE type
        WHEN 'charge'  THEN amount
        WHEN 'refund'  THEN -amount
        WHEN 'dispute' THEN -amount
        ELSE 0 END) AS net_revenue
FROM transactions
WHERE status = 'succeeded'
GROUP BY DATE(created_at), currency
ORDER BY d, currency;
```
Model each transaction as a **signed contribution** (charges positive; refunds and disputes negative) and sum per **day + currency** - never mix currencies in one total. Filter to **settled** transactions (`status='succeeded'`) so pending/failed rows don't inflate revenue. Gotchas Stripe will probe: **timezone** (`DATE(created_at)` should use a defined zone, e.g. UTC, consistently), **amount units** (store minor units / integer cents to avoid float errors on money), and **partial refunds** (refund `amount` may be less than the charge). For reporting you'd often reconcile this against the ledger's control totals. -> Revise: [SQL cram](#/cheat/databases-sql), [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

exercises.prompt: Stripe-style design: ingest payment events into the warehouse so totals are exactly-once and auditable. Describe the design + the check that proves it.
exercises.solution: **Delivery reality:** webhooks/queues are **at-least-once**, so assume duplicates and retries. **Idempotent sink:** each event carries a unique **event/idempotency key**; **MERGE** on that key into an **append-only, event-sourced** ledger table (insert if new, no-op if seen) - re-delivery changes nothing, giving **effectively exactly-once**. Never mutate money rows; corrections are **reversing entries**. **Reproducibility:** partition by date; each load is **idempotent** and **backfillable** (re-running a date reproduces identical results). **Ordering/late data:** key ordering where it matters; accept late events and reprocess the affected partition. **The proof = daily reconciliation:** compute **control totals** (row count and `SUM(amount)` per day and currency) in the warehouse and compare to the **source of truth** (Stripe's ledger/API); **any drift alerts** on-call and blocks downstream reporting. Optionally CDC source DBs into the stream for freshness. The reconciliation is what *demonstrates* correctness - in payments, 'probably right' isn't acceptable. -> Revise: [delivery semantics](#/lesson/sd-idempotency-delivery), [idempotency](#/lesson/pipe-idempotency)

lessons.title: Goldman Sachs - DE interview playbook
lessons.summary: Goldman Sachs' DE loop (HackerRank OA, then a DSA phone screen, then a 3-5 round onsite: SQL, Python coding, Spark/big-data internals, data modeling/architecture, and values-based behavioral) with signature questions, answers, code, and concept links.
lessons.concept:
**The loop:** **HackerRank OA** (CS coding + MCQs ~1h; a ~30-min ML section for DE) -> **technical phone** (DSA: arrays, trees, recursion, graph traversal) -> **onsite** (3-5 rounds x 45 min): **SQL**, **Python coding**, **Spark/big-data internals**, **data modeling/architecture**, and **1-2 behavioral** on GS values. **Emphasis:** strong CS fundamentals, Spark internals, and value-fit (Partnership, Client Service, Integrity, Excellence).

### Coding / DSA round
**Q: Two Sum; Trapping Rain Water ('Snowpack').** Two Sum = hash map (O(n)); Trapping Rain Water = two pointers tracking left/right max (O(n), O(1) space). -> Revise: [two-pointer & window](#/lesson/dsa-two-pointer-window)

**Q: Tree/graph traversal, recursion.** Know BFS/DFS, recursion vs iteration, and complexity. -> Revise: [DSA patterns](#/cheat/dsa-de)

### SQL & data modeling round
**Q: Translate a business requirement into a schema, then query it.** Normalize (3NF) for integrity, add indexes on join/filter columns, then write the SQL (often a window). Discuss normalize vs denormalize trade-offs. -> Revise: [normalization](#/lesson/model-normalization), [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

### Spark / big-data internals round (signature at GS)
**Q: RDD vs DataFrame; why PySpark; Spark on the JVM.** DataFrames use the Catalyst optimizer + Tungsten (faster, columnar) vs low-level RDDs; PySpark drives the JVM engine via Py4J. -> Revise: [Spark cram](#/cheat/big-data-spark)

**Q: How would you partition a large dataset and tune a slow Spark job?** Partition by a low-cardinality key (date), right-size partitions, fix skew (broadcast/AQE/salt), avoid wide shuffles, cache reused data. -> Revise: [shuffle & partitions](#/lesson/bd-partitions-shuffle)

**Q: Parquet vs Avro?** **Parquet = columnar** (great for analytics/column pruning); **Avro = row-based** (great for streaming/write-heavy, schema evolution). -> Revise: [Spark cram](#/cheat/big-data-spark)

### System design / architecture round
**Q: Design a data pipeline / distributed processing system for risk or trade data.** Clarify scale/latency; ingest -> partitioned lake -> Spark transform -> idempotent load -> serve; discuss distributed-systems trade-offs, fault tolerance, and reprocessing. -> Revise: [system design](#/cheat/system-design), [idempotency](#/lesson/pipe-idempotency)

### Behavioral (values)
**Q: 'Why Goldman? What's different about GS? Why leave your current role?'** Tie to GS **values** (Partnership, Client Service, Integrity, Excellence) with STAR + impact. -> Revise: [behavioral prep](#/lesson/ib-behavioral)
lessons.example: **Signature Goldman question - the Spark-internals + architecture combo.** After a DSA screen (e.g., Trapping Rain Water via two pointers), the onsite drills **Spark internals**: RDD vs DataFrame (Catalyst/Tungsten), why PySpark talks to a JVM engine, Parquet (columnar) vs Avro (row/streaming), and how you'd **partition a huge dataset and tune a slow job** (fix skew with broadcast/AQE/salt, right-size partitions, avoid wide shuffles). Then a **data-architecture** prompt: design a pipeline for risk/trade data - ingest to a date-partitioned lake, Spark transform, **idempotent** loads, fault tolerance, reprocessing. Close with a **values** behavioral ('why GS'). GS rewards deep systems knowledge plus clear value-fit - show you understand the engine, not just the API.
keypoints: Loop: HackerRank OA -> DSA phone screen -> onsite (SQL, Python coding, Spark internals, modeling/architecture, values behavioral).
DSA is real: Two Sum (hash), Trapping Rain Water (two pointers), trees/graphs, recursion - state complexity.
Spark internals are a GS signature: RDD vs DataFrame, PySpark/JVM, Parquet vs Avro, partitioning + tuning + skew.
SQL/modeling: business requirement -> normalized schema + indexes -> window SQL; normalize vs denormalize.
Architecture: distributed pipeline design with fault tolerance, idempotent reprocessing, partitioning.
Behavioral: GS values (Partnership, Client Service, Integrity, Excellence) + 'why GS' in STAR.

quiz.q: Goldman big-data round: Parquet vs Avro - which fits analytical column pruning best?
options: Parquet (columnar) for analytics; Avro (row) for streaming/write-heavy + schema evolution
quiz.explain: Parquet is columnar (reads only needed columns - ideal for analytics); Avro is row-based, better for write/streaming and schema evolution.

quiz.q: Why is a Spark DataFrame usually faster than a raw RDD?
options: The Catalyst optimizer + Tungsten (columnar, code-gen) optimize the plan; RDDs are unoptimized low-level ops
RDDs don't run on the JVM
quiz.explain: DataFrames go through Catalyst query optimization and Tungsten execution; RDDs are opaque to the optimizer.

exercises.prompt: Goldman-style DSA: solve Trapping Rain Water (max water trapped between bars) in O(n) time, O(1) space. Explain the approach.
exercises.solution:
```python
def trap(height):
    l, r = 0, len(height) - 1
    lmax = rmax = water = 0
    while l < r:
        if height[l] < height[r]:
            lmax = max(lmax, height[l])
            water += lmax - height[l]
            l += 1
        else:
            rmax = max(rmax, height[r])
            water += rmax - height[r]
            r -= 1
    return water
```
Water above each bar = `min(maxLeft, maxRight) - height[i]`. The **two-pointer** trick: move the side with the **smaller** bar inward, because that side's `min(lmax, rmax)` is bounded by the smaller running max, so you can safely add `lmax - height[l]` (or the mirror) without knowing the far side exactly. This is **O(n) time, O(1) space** - beating the O(n)-space prefix/suffix-max arrays. Edge cases: empty/short arrays return 0; equal heights trap nothing. GS likes this because it rewards the insight over brute force. -> Revise: [two-pointer & window](#/lesson/dsa-two-pointer-window)

exercises.prompt: Goldman-style: a Spark job on trade data is slow and spills. Walk through how you'd diagnose and tune it.
exercises.solution: **Diagnose** via the Spark UI: look for a **shuffle-heavy stage**, a **long-tail task** (skew), and **spill** (shuffle bytes > executor memory). **Tune:** (1) **Reduce shuffles** - prune columns early (Parquet), push down filters, broadcast small dimension joins (`broadcast()`), avoid unnecessary `repartition`/`distinct`. (2) **Fix skew** - enable **AQE** (adaptive skew-join + partition coalescing) or **salt** the hot key. (3) **Right-size partitions** - target ~128-256 MB per partition; tune `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` (AQE coalesces automatically). (4) **Partition the source** by date so the job prunes to needed data; compact small files. (5) **Cache** data reused across actions; enable off-heap/Tungsten. (6) **File format** - read Parquet (columnar) not CSV/JSON. Verify by comparing task-time distribution and shuffle/spill metrics before vs after. The usual GS-favored answer centers on **skew + broadcast + partition sizing**. -> Revise: [shuffle & partitions](#/lesson/bd-partitions-shuffle), [Spark cram](#/cheat/big-data-spark)

lessons.title: JPMorgan Chase - DE interview playbook
lessons.summary: JPMorgan's DE loop (recruiter, a long technical round on Java/Python + Big Data + AWS with hands-on PySpark, then an exec/manager round) - broad and practical, with signature questions, answers, code, and concept links.
lessons.concept:
**The loop:** recruiter -> **technical interview** (~1h45: core Java/concurrency and/or Python, Big Data concepts, **hands-on PySpark**, SQL) -> **executive/manager round** (~30 min, fit + 'why JPMorgan'). **Emphasis:** broad practical fundamentals across Python/Java, Spark, SQL, testing, and the **AWS/Databricks** stack JPMorgan runs.

### Python / coding round
**Q: hash functions, list comprehension, *args/**kwargs, OOP.** Know Python fundamentals cold - mutability, comprehensions, variadic args, classes/inheritance. -> Revise: [Python cram](#/cheat/python-de)

**Q: PySpark hands-on - transform a dataset.** Read -> filter -> join (broadcast small side) -> groupBy/agg -> write partitioned; explain lazy eval + actions. -> Revise: [Spark shuffle & partitions](#/lesson/bd-partitions-shuffle)

### SQL round
**Q: joins; UNION vs UNION ALL; stored procedures.** UNION dedups (sorts - costlier), UNION ALL keeps all (faster); know INNER/LEFT/anti-joins and when to use a stored proc. -> Revise: [SQL cram](#/cheat/databases-sql)

### Big data / architecture round
**Q: data lake vs data warehouse?** Lake = raw, schema-on-read, cheap object storage; warehouse = curated, schema-on-write, optimized for BI; lakehouse merges them. -> Revise: [lakehouse cram](#/cheat/lakehouse)

**Q: Spark architecture, DataFrame, file formats.** Driver + executors + cluster manager; DataFrame + Catalyst; Parquet columnar. -> Revise: [Spark cram](#/cheat/big-data-spark)

**Q: AWS + orchestration.** S3 (lake), Redshift (warehouse), Airflow/NiFi (orchestrate/ingest), Databricks/Delta + Unity Catalog. Design incremental, idempotent loads. -> Revise: [AWS cram](#/cheat/platform-aws-track), [idempotency](#/lesson/pipe-idempotency)

### Testing round
**Q: types of testing for pipelines?** Unit (a transform), integration (end-to-end DAG), regression (outputs don't drift), and **mocking** external systems; plus data-quality tests. -> Revise: [DataOps cram](#/cheat/dataops)

### Behavioral / manager
**Q: 'Why JPMorgan? Walk me through a project.'** STAR + impact; show ownership and communication with the manager. -> Revise: [behavioral prep](#/lesson/ib-behavioral)
lessons.example: **Signature JPMorgan question - a hands-on PySpark + AWS pipeline discussion.** You'll write **PySpark** to transform a dataset (read Parquet from **S3** -> filter -> **broadcast** join a small dim -> `groupBy().agg()` -> write **partitioned** by date) while explaining lazy evaluation and actions, then discuss the **architecture**: lake (S3) vs warehouse (Redshift), orchestration (Airflow/NiFi), and Databricks/Delta + Unity Catalog. Expect **fundamentals** interleaved - Python (list comprehension, *args/**kwargs, OOP), SQL (UNION vs UNION ALL, joins, stored procs), and **testing** (unit/integration/regression/mock). Make loads **incremental + idempotent**. Then a short **manager** round on 'why JPMorgan'. JPMorgan rewards broad, practical competence over exotic algorithms.
keypoints: Loop: recruiter -> one long technical round (Java/Python + Big Data + PySpark + SQL) -> exec/manager round.
Python fundamentals: hashing, comprehensions, *args/**kwargs, OOP; sometimes core Java + concurrency.
SQL: joins, UNION vs UNION ALL (dedup+sort vs keep-all), stored procedures.
Big data: data lake vs warehouse, Spark architecture/DataFrame, Parquet; AWS (S3/Redshift) + Airflow/NiFi + Databricks/Unity Catalog.
Testing is asked explicitly: unit, integration, regression, mocking + data-quality tests.
Behavioral/manager: 'why JPMorgan' + a clear project walkthrough; practical breadth over trick questions.

quiz.q: JPMorgan SQL: difference between UNION and UNION ALL?
options: UNION removes duplicates (sorts, costlier); UNION ALL keeps all rows (faster)
UNION only works on one table
quiz.explain: UNION dedups (implying a sort/hash), UNION ALL concatenates everything and is faster when you don't need dedup.

quiz.q: Data lake vs data warehouse - the core distinction?
options: Lake = raw, schema-on-read, cheap storage; warehouse = curated, schema-on-write, BI-optimized (lakehouse merges them)
quiz.explain: Lake = raw/schema-on-read on object storage; warehouse = curated/schema-on-write for analytics; a lakehouse combines both.

exercises.prompt: JPMorgan-style PySpark: read Parquet from S3, keep US orders over $100, join a small customer dim, and write daily-partitioned output. Write it and note one performance choice.
exercises.hint: Broadcast the small dim; partition on write.
exercises.solution:
```python
from pyspark.sql import functions as F
from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast

orders = spark.read.parquet('s3://lake/orders/')
cust   = spark.read.parquet('s3://lake/dim_customer/')   # small

res = (orders
    .filter((F.col('country') == 'US') & (F.col('amount') > 100))
    .join(broadcast(cust), 'customer_id')                # broadcast small dim
    .groupBy('order_date', 'segment')
    .agg(F.sum('amount').alias('revenue'),
         F.countDistinct('customer_id').alias('customers')))

(res.write.mode('overwrite')
    .partitionBy('order_date')                           # prune-friendly output
    .parquet('s3://lake/gold/daily_rev/'))
```
**Performance choice:** `broadcast(cust)` turns the join into a **broadcast hash join**, sending the small dim to every executor and **avoiding a shuffle** of the large `orders` table. Also: filter **before** the join (less data), and `partitionBy('order_date')` on write so downstream reads prune by date. Remember transformations are **lazy** - nothing runs until the `write` action triggers the DAG. Idempotency: `mode('overwrite')` on a date-partitioned path (or dynamic partition overwrite) makes reruns safe. -> Revise: [shuffle & partitions](#/lesson/bd-partitions-shuffle), [AWS cram](#/cheat/platform-aws-track)

exercises.prompt: JPMorgan-style: what tests would you write for a daily ETL pipeline, and what does each catch?
exercises.solution: **Unit tests** - test a single transform function in isolation (given input rows, assert the output) - catches logic bugs early and runs fast. **Integration tests** - run the whole DAG end-to-end on sample data (source -> transform -> sink) - catches wiring/config/schema-mismatch issues between steps. **Regression tests** - compare today's output (or aggregates) against a known-good baseline - catches unintended drift when code or dependencies change. **Mocking** - replace external systems (S3, DBs, APIs) with fakes/stubs so tests are deterministic, fast, and don't hit prod - catches contract issues without side effects. **Data-quality tests** (Great Expectations/dbt tests) - row counts, not-null keys, uniqueness, referential integrity, value ranges - catch **bad data** (vs bad code) and should **block publish**. Together: unit+integration+regression verify the **code**, mocks isolate it, and DQ tests verify the **data**. Run them in CI and in the pipeline itself. -> Revise: [DataOps cram](#/cheat/dataops), [idempotency](#/lesson/pipe-idempotency)

lessons.title: Morgan Stanley - DE interview playbook
lessons.summary: Morgan Stanley's DE loop (recruiter, a DE-fundamentals technical phone, then 3-5 back-to-back finals: SQL, Python coding, ETL/system design, and behavioral + hiring manager) with a finance-domain flavor - signature questions, answers, code, and concept links.
lessons.concept:
**The loop:** recruiter -> **technical phone** (DE fundamentals) -> **final round** (3-5 back-to-back x 45 min): **SQL**, **Python coding**, **ETL/pipeline & system design**, **data modeling**, **behavioral**, and a **hiring-manager/senior-leader** chat. **Emphasis:** rock-solid SQL, Spark batch/stream, ETL pseudocode, warehousing - plus light **finance-domain** fluency (P&L, trade lifecycle, positions, risk, market data).

### SQL round
**Q: window analytics on trades/positions (running P&L, rank, moving avg).** Window frames + `LAG`/`SUM() OVER`. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

### Python coding round
**Q: write clean Python + pseudocode for an ETL step.** Parse/clean, dedup, transform; explain structure before coding. -> Revise: [pandas](#/lesson/pd-merge-reshape), [Python cram](#/cheat/python-de)

### Big data / ETL design round
**Q: batch vs stream processing in Spark - when each?** Batch = high-throughput periodic (daily P&L); stream = low-latency continuous (live market data) via Structured Streaming. -> Revise: [Spark cram](#/cheat/big-data-spark), [streaming](#/cheat/streaming)

**Q: what is a staging layer and why use it?** A landing/staging zone isolates raw ingest from transformations - enables validation, reprocessing, idempotent loads, and decoupling source from warehouse. -> Revise: [pipelines cram](#/cheat/pipelines), [idempotency](#/lesson/pipe-idempotency)

**Q: pseudocode an ETL pipeline in Python.** Extract (incremental high-watermark) -> validate -> transform -> load (idempotent MERGE/partition overwrite) -> DQ checks. -> Revise: [scheduling & backfill](#/lesson/pipe-scheduling-backfill)

### Data modeling / warehouse round
**Q: design a warehouse for trades/positions.** Star schema: `fact_trade` at trade grain + dims (instrument, account, counterparty, date); SCD2 on instrument/account; conform dims for firm-wide reporting. -> Revise: [star schema](#/lesson/model-star-schema), [SCD2](#/lesson/model-scd2)

### Behavioral / hiring manager
**Q: 'Why Morgan Stanley? A hard problem you owned?'** STAR + impact; a little finance-domain interest helps. -> Revise: [behavioral prep](#/lesson/ib-behavioral)
lessons.example: **Signature Morgan Stanley question - 'pseudocode an ETL pipeline for daily trade data, then design its warehouse.'** Pseudocode: **extract** incrementally (high-watermark on trade timestamp) into a **staging** layer, **validate** (schema, not-null keys, ranges), **transform** (clean, dedup, enrich with reference/market data), **load** idempotently (MERGE on trade id / partition overwrite), then **DQ checks** that block publish. Then **model** the warehouse: `fact_trade` at trade grain (measures: quantity, price, P&L) + conformed dims (instrument, account, counterparty, date) with **SCD2** on changing attributes. Discuss **batch vs stream** (daily P&L batch; live market-data stream) and the **staging layer's** role. A little **finance fluency** (P&L, trade lifecycle, positions, risk) signals domain fit. MS rewards clean fundamentals + structured thinking.
keypoints: Loop: recruiter -> DE-fundamentals phone -> 3-5 back-to-back finals (SQL, Python, ETL/design, modeling, behavioral, hiring manager).
SQL is non-negotiable: window analytics (running P&L, rank, moving avg), joins.
Spark: batch vs stream (daily P&L vs live market data); Structured Streaming basics.
Pipelines: the staging layer's role (isolate raw, validate, reprocess, idempotent); pseudocode ETL in Python.
Modeling: trades/positions star schema, SCD2, conformed dims, warehouse architecture.
Behavioral + light finance-domain fluency (P&L, trade lifecycle, positions, risk, market data).

quiz.q: Morgan Stanley: why include a staging layer in an ETL pipeline?
options: To isolate raw ingest from transforms - enabling validation, idempotent reprocessing, and decoupling source from warehouse
To store the final BI tables
quiz.explain: Staging lands raw data so you can validate, reprocess/backfill idempotently, and decouple source systems from the curated warehouse.

quiz.q: Batch vs stream in Spark - which fits live market-data with low latency?
quiz.explain: Streaming handles continuous low-latency data (live market feeds); batch suits high-throughput periodic work like daily P&L.

exercises.prompt: Morgan Stanley-style SQL: compute each account's running (cumulative) P&L ordered by trade date.
exercises.solution:
```sql
SELECT account_id, trade_date, pnl,
  SUM(pnl) OVER (PARTITION BY account_id ORDER BY trade_date
                 ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS running_pnl
FROM trades
ORDER BY account_id, trade_date;
```
A **running total** is a window `SUM` partitioned by account, ordered by date, with the frame `UNBOUNDED PRECEDING -> CURRENT ROW` (the default for `ORDER BY` without an explicit frame, but stating it is safer and clearer). This yields cumulative P&L per account over time in a single pass - no self-join. Watch: ties on `trade_date` (add a tiebreaker like trade_id to the ORDER BY for determinism), and reset semantics (the `PARTITION BY account_id` restarts the sum per account). To get period-to-date, add a partition on the period (e.g., month). -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

exercises.prompt: Morgan Stanley-style: pseudocode an idempotent daily ETL for trade data (extract -> ... -> load) and say why each step matters.
exercises.hint: Staging, validate, transform, idempotent load, DQ.
exercises.solution:
```
for run_date in dates_to_process:            # parameterized -> backfillable
  # 1. EXTRACT (incremental)
  raw = source.query(where modified_at > last_watermark(run_date))
  staging.overwrite(partition=run_date, raw)  # land raw first (isolate + reprocess)

  # 2. VALIDATE (gate)
  assert checks(staging[run_date]): schema, not-null trade_id, price>=0, fx present
  else: quarantine + alert; STOP publish

  # 3. TRANSFORM
  clean = dedup(staging[run_date], key=trade_id)
  enriched = clean.join(dim_instrument).join(dim_account)   # enrich w/ reference data

  # 4. LOAD (idempotent)
  warehouse.MERGE(fact_trade, enriched, on=trade_id)        # upsert -> rerun-safe

  # 5. POST-LOAD DQ + advance watermark only on success
  assert reconciliation(counts, sums) within tolerance
  set_watermark(run_date)
```
**Why each matters:** parameterizing by `run_date` makes the job **backfillable**; the **staging** layer isolates raw data so you can validate and reprocess without re-hitting the source; **validation before load** stops bad data reaching the warehouse; **dedup + MERGE on trade_id** makes reruns **idempotent** (no duplicate trades - critical for P&L); **advancing the watermark only on success** means a crash re-does, never skips; **reconciliation** proves counts/sums match the source. This structure (staging + validate + idempotent load + DQ) is exactly what MS probes. -> Revise: [idempotency](#/lesson/pipe-idempotency), [scheduling & backfill](#/lesson/pipe-scheduling-backfill)

lessons.title: Capital One - DE interview playbook
lessons.summary: Capital One's DE loop (CodeSignal gateway assessment, recruiter, then a back-to-back 'Power Day' of coding, a business case study, and behavioral/job-fit) - AWS-heavy with a signature business-case round - with questions, answers, code, and concept links.
lessons.concept:
**The loop:** **CodeSignal OA** (a strict gateway - aim ~700+) -> recruiter -> **Power Day** (3-4 back-to-back): **technical coding** (SQL + Python/ETL), a **business case study**, and **behavioral/job-fit**. **Emphasis:** clean SQL + Python data transformation on **AWS** (Capital One is all-in on AWS), clarifying vague requirements, and translating data into **business value**.

### Coding assessment / technical round
**Q: SQL - top-3 highest transactions per customer last month.** Partitioned window rank, filter <= 3, date-bounded. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

**Q: SQL - rolling average with window functions.** `AVG(x) OVER (ORDER BY d ROWS BETWEEN N PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)`. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

**Q: build an ETL function in Python.** Parse/clean, transform, load; make it idempotent and testable. -> Revise: [Python cram](#/cheat/python-de), [idempotency](#/lesson/pipe-idempotency)

### AWS / system design round
**Q: design a pipeline for customer transactions on AWS.** First **ask clarifying questions** - 'optimizing for latency or cost? historical reprocessing needed? analytical or operational downstream?' Then: S3 (lake) -> Glue/EMR Spark transform -> Redshift/Athena serve; partition by date; idempotent loads; orchestrate with Step Functions/Airflow. -> Revise: [AWS cram](#/cheat/platform-aws-track), [system design](#/cheat/system-design)

### Business case study round (signature)
**Q: given a chart/dataset, what's the insight and why does it matter to the business?** Not a coding round - **read the chart, compute ROI/impact, and structure a verbal argument** tying the data to the bottom line (e.g., which customer segment to target and the expected lift). Narrate your reasoning. -> Revise: [behavioral prep](#/lesson/ib-behavioral)

### Behavioral / job fit
**Q: 'Tell me about a time you...' + why Capital One.** STAR; Capital One weights collaboration, ownership, and communicating insight clearly. -> Revise: [behavioral prep](#/lesson/ib-behavioral)
lessons.example: **Signature Capital One question - the business case study.** Unlike a pure coding round, you're handed a **chart or dataset** and asked what it means for the business: read it, **quantify** (compute ROI, conversion lift, cost impact), and **structure a verbal argument** ('this segment has 3x LTV but we spend equally across segments; reallocating budget yields ~X% more revenue'). They're testing whether you can turn data into a **business decision** and communicate it. Pair this with the technical rounds - SQL (top-3 transactions per customer, rolling averages) and a **Python ETL** function - and the AWS design round where you **clarify first** ('latency vs cost? reprocessing? analytical vs operational?'). Capital One hires DEs who are both technically clean and **business-fluent**.
keypoints: Loop: CodeSignal gateway (~700+) -> recruiter -> Power Day (coding + business case study + behavioral).
SQL: top-N per customer (partitioned rank), rolling averages (window frame), date-bounded queries.
Python: build a clean, idempotent, testable ETL function.
AWS-heavy: S3 + Glue/EMR + Redshift/Athena + Step Functions; clarify latency-vs-cost, reprocessing, analytical-vs-operational.
Signature case study: read charts, compute ROI/impact, argue why the insight matters to the business.
Behavioral/job-fit: ownership, collaboration, and communicating insight clearly.

quiz.q: Capital One's case study round primarily tests what?
options: Turning data into a quantified business argument (ROI/impact) and communicating it
quiz.explain: The case study is a business-logic round: read charts, compute impact/ROI, and structure why the insight matters to the bottom line.

quiz.q: Given a vague 'build a pipeline for customer transactions' prompt, what should you do first?
options: Ask clarifying questions: latency vs cost, historical reprocessing, analytical vs operational
quiz.explain: Capital One explicitly values clarifying vague requirements before designing - it shapes the whole architecture.

exercises.prompt: Capital One-style SQL: find the top 3 highest transactions for each customer in the last month.
exercises.hint: PARTITION BY customer, rank by amount desc, filter <=3, bound the date.
exercises.solution:
```sql
SELECT customer_id, txn_id, amount, txn_date FROM (
  SELECT customer_id, txn_id, amount, txn_date,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY amount DESC) rn
  FROM transactions
  WHERE txn_date >= DATE_TRUNC('month', CURRENT_DATE) - INTERVAL '1' MONTH
    AND txn_date <  DATE_TRUNC('month', CURRENT_DATE)) t
WHERE rn <= 3
ORDER BY customer_id, amount DESC;
```
Bound the window to **last month** in the WHERE first (so the ranking only sees relevant rows), then `ROW_NUMBER()` **partitioned by customer**, ordered by amount desc, keep `rn <= 3`. **Tie choice:** `ROW_NUMBER` returns exactly 3 even if amounts tie; use `RANK`/`DENSE_RANK` if tied transactions should all appear. This 'top-N per group' pattern is a Capital One staple - and generalizes to top-N products, merchants, etc. by changing the partition key. -> Revise: [window functions](#/lesson/sql-window)

exercises.prompt: Capital One-style: you're shown that Segment A converts at 8% (spend $50/customer) and Segment B at 2% (spend $50/customer). Structure the business argument.
exercises.solution: **Structure: observation -> quantification -> recommendation -> caveats.** (1) **Observation:** equal spend ($50/customer) but 4x conversion gap (8% vs 2%). (2) **Quantify ROI:** if a conversion is worth $V, Segment A returns 0.08V per $50 vs Segment B's 0.02V - **A's cost-per-acquisition is 1/4 of B's** ($625 vs $2,500 per conversion at $50 spend). (3) **Recommendation:** **reallocate budget toward Segment A** (or test raising A's spend) until marginal returns equalize; e.g., shifting $X from B to A yields ~3x more conversions on that dollar. (4) **Caveats:** is Segment A **saturated** (diminishing returns / limited size)? Is B **strategically valuable** (higher LTV, cross-sell, retention) despite low conversion? Are the rates **statistically significant** (sample size)? Any **attribution** confounds? The point Capital One wants: **tie the data to a dollar decision, recommend clearly, and show you know the assumptions that could flip it.** -> Revise: [behavioral prep](#/lesson/ib-behavioral)

lessons.title: Bloomberg - DE interview playbook
lessons.summary: Bloomberg's DE loop (a CoderPad technical phone, optional OA, then a 3-5 round onsite of practical DE+coding, Python/Pandas data manipulation, system design, and behavioral) - the most selective, production-code-bar DE loop - with questions, answers, code, and concept links.
lessons.concept:
**The loop:** **CoderPad technical phone** (45-60 min, one strong problem + follow-ups) -> optional OA -> **onsite** (3-5 rounds, ~1h, two engineers each): **practical DE + coding**, **Python/Pandas** data manipulation, **system design** (team-dependent), and a **senior-manager behavioral**. **Emphasis:** a **production-code bar** (algorithms above LeetCode-medium), real data wrangling, and financial-data domain (tick/market/reference data, taxonomies, security graphs). Reputedly the **most selective** DE loop.

### Practical DE + coding round
**Q: count distinct values from a semicolon-separated string.** Split, put in a set, count - watch empties/whitespace/case.
```python
def distinct_count(s):
    return len({p.strip() for p in s.split(';') if p.strip()})
```
-> Revise: [Python cram](#/cheat/python-de)

**Q: model/traverse a taxonomy or security graph.** Graphs + BFS/DFS; topological order for dependencies. -> Revise: [topological sort](#/lesson/dsa-topo-sort), [DSA patterns](#/cheat/dsa-de)

### Python / Pandas round
**Q: clean a column typo and count records; dataframe transforms.** Vectorized pandas: `str.replace`/`map`, `groupby().size()`, dedup, handle nulls - avoid `iterrows`.
```python
df['city'] = df['city'].str.strip().replace({'New Yrok':'New York'})
counts = df.groupby('city').size().sort_values(ascending=False)
```
-> Revise: [pandas merge/reshape](#/lesson/pd-merge-reshape)

### Algorithms round (above medium)
**Q: sliding-window / heap / merge problems, tuned for production quality.** Clean code, edge cases, correct complexity - not just a passing hack. -> Revise: [two-pointer & window](#/lesson/dsa-two-pointer-window), [merge-K](#/lesson/dsa-merge-k)

### System design round (team-dependent)
**Q: design ingestion for high-volume market/tick data.** Clarify throughput/latency; partition by symbol/time; idempotent writes; serve fast reads; handle late/corrections. -> Revise: [system design](#/cheat/system-design), [partitioning](#/lesson/sd-partitioning)

### Behavioral (senior manager)
**Q: 'Why Bloomberg, this role, and the finance/data industry? A challenging project?'** STAR + genuine interest in the domain. -> Revise: [behavioral prep](#/lesson/ib-behavioral)
lessons.example: **Signature Bloomberg question - the practical Python/Pandas + coding bar.** Expect a **CoderPad** problem you must write to a **production standard** (clean, edge-cased, correct complexity) - e.g., 'count distinct values from a semicolon-separated string' (split -> set -> count, handling empties/whitespace/case), followed by **dataframe** tasks (fix a column typo, dedup, `groupby().size()` counts) done **vectorized**, not with row loops. Other rounds probe **databases/taxonomies/graphs** (BFS/DFS, topological order) and, team-dependent, **system design** for high-volume **market/tick data** (partition by symbol/time, idempotent writes, corrections/late data). The bar is high (reputedly ~8% pass) and the code quality matters as much as the answer. Close with a **behavioral** showing real interest in the finance/data domain.
keypoints: Loop: CoderPad phone (one strong problem + follow-ups) -> optional OA -> onsite (practical DE+coding, Python/Pandas, system design, senior-manager behavioral).
Production-code bar: clean, edge-cased solutions; algorithms pitched above LeetCode-medium.
Practical data wrangling: parse strings (distinct from semicolon-separated), clean columns, groupby counts - vectorized pandas.
CS: graphs/taxonomies (BFS/DFS, topological sort), sliding window, heaps/merge.
System design (team-dependent): high-volume market/tick data - partition by symbol/time, idempotent, corrections.
Behavioral: genuine interest in Bloomberg + the finance/data domain; most selective DE loop.

quiz.q: Bloomberg values production-quality code. For a pandas cleaning task, what's the right approach?
options: Vectorized ops (str methods, map/replace, groupby) - avoid row loops
Convert to a Python list and loop
Use a for-loop with .loc per row
quiz.explain: Vectorized pandas is faster and cleaner; iterrows/row loops are slow and frowned upon at Bloomberg's bar.

quiz.q: Count distinct tags in 'a; b; a; ;c ' correctly - what must you handle?
options: Strip whitespace and drop empty entries before de-duping into a set
quiz.explain: Splitting on ';' yields whitespace and empty pieces; strip and filter them, then use a set - here distinct = {a, b, c} = 3.

exercises.prompt: Bloomberg-style: count the number of distinct non-empty values in a semicolon-separated string, robust to whitespace and case. Write it and note the edge cases.
exercises.hint: split -> strip -> filter empties -> normalize case -> set.

exercises.prompt: Bloomberg-style pandas: given a dataframe of trades with a misspelled city column, fix the typo, drop null symbols, and return record counts per city (descending).
exercises.solution:
```python
import pandas as pd

def clean_and_count(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.Series:
    out = df.copy()
    out['city'] = out['city'].str.strip().replace({'New Yrok': 'New York',
                                                    'Londin': 'London'})
    out = out.dropna(subset=['symbol'])                 # drop rows missing symbol
    return (out.groupby('city').size()
               .sort_values(ascending=False))
```
All **vectorized**: `.str.strip()` + `.replace({...})` fix the typos across the whole column at once; `dropna(subset=['symbol'])` removes rows with a null symbol; `groupby('city').size()` counts records per city and `sort_values(ascending=False)` orders them. **Why this over a loop:** `iterrows()`/per-row `.loc` is orders of magnitude slower and error-prone - Bloomberg grades for vectorized, readable code. Extend with `.value_counts()` (a shortcut for groupby-size on one column), and be ready to discuss **NaN handling** (does a null city become its own group? use `dropna` or `fillna` deliberately) and **case/whitespace** normalization before grouping. -> Revise: [pandas merge/reshape](#/lesson/pd-merge-reshape)

lessons.title: Interview bank prep & cheat sheet
lessons.summary: A consolidated study plan and one-page cheat sheet across all rounds — your final review before the loop.
lessons.concept:
This is your **final-review** sheet across the whole bank. Pair it with the per-round lessons above.

**4-week plan (compress as needed):** Week 1 — SQL (windows, CTEs, top-N, dedup, gaps-and-islands). Week 2 — Python DSA + DE-flavored problems. Week 3 — data modeling (grain, star/snowflake, SCD2) + Spark internals. Week 4 — system design (the 6-step method + 5 worked designs) and behavioral (12-15 STAR stories). Throughout: 2-3 mock interviews.

**The one-page cheat sheet:**
- **SQL:** ROW_NUMBER/RANK/DENSE_RANK, LAG/LEAD, running totals; top-N = ROW_NUMBER in subquery; dedup = rn=1 by recency; sessionize = gaps-and-islands.
- **Coding:** state Big-O + edge cases; top-K = heap/Counter; merge sorted = heapq.merge; stream, don't load.
- **Modeling:** name the **grain** first; star for BI; SCD2 for history (valid_from/valid_to/is_current).
- **Spark:** shuffles are the cost; broadcast small joins; fix skew (salt/AQE); ~128MB partitions; AQE on.
- **Design:** clarify → estimate → ingest/store/process/serve → deep-dive → **trade-offs** → wrap (monitoring).
- **Behavioral:** STAR, 'I' not 'we', quantify; 12-15 stories; map to LPs/values.

**On the day:** think aloud, state assumptions, write tests, name trade-offs, and ask clarifying questions before solving. Open **Go deeper** for the printable version.
lessons.example:
# 60-second pre-round reset
SQL?       -> window functions + CTEs; clarify schema/NULLs/ties
Coding?    -> approach + Big-O first; edge cases; test small
Modeling?  -> grain -> dims -> SCD -> keys/partitioning
Design?    -> 6 steps; draw it; name trade-offs
Behavioral?-> STAR, quantify, 'I', ~2 min
keypoints: Study by round: SQL → coding → modeling+Spark → design+behavioral, with mocks throughout.
SQL: window functions, top-N, dedup, gaps-and-islands.
Modeling: state the grain first; star for BI; SCD2 for history.
Spark: minimize shuffles, broadcast small joins, fix skew, size partitions, AQE.
Design: 6-step method, always name trade-offs and monitoring.
Behavioral: 12-15 quantified STAR stories mapped to LPs/values.

quiz.q: The fastest single habit that lifts scores across SQL, coding and design is:
options: Thinking aloud: clarify, state assumptions, and name trade-offs
quiz.explain: Communicating your reasoning, assumptions, and trade-offs is what interviewers actually score.

quiz.q: Before writing a data model, you first:
options: State the fact grain (what one row represents)
quiz.explain: The grain anchors the whole model and is the most common follow-up.

quiz.q: In Spark questions, reason first from:
quiz.explain: Shuffles (from wide transforms) and partition sizing/skew drive Spark performance.

exercises.prompt: Give a 4-week prep split.
exercises.solution: Wk1 SQL; Wk2 Python DSA; Wk3 modeling + Spark; Wk4 system design + behavioral; mocks throughout.

exercises.prompt: Name the one-liner for top-N per group and dedup-latest.
exercises.solution: Top-N: ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY grp ORDER BY metric DESC) <= N. Dedup: keep rn=1 by ORDER BY updated_at DESC.

exercises.prompt: What do you always do before solving any technical question?
exercises.solution: Ask clarifying questions, state assumptions and constraints, and outline the approach (and Big-O) before coding.

tracks.title: Capstone Projects
tracks.summary: End-to-end pipelines that combine the tools — from API ingestion to a published report.

lessons.title: Capstone: API ingestion to a queryable lake
lessons.summary: Combine Requests, Pydantic, Polars, fsspec/S3 and DuckDB into one batch ELT pipeline.
lessons.concept:
This capstone wires together five libraries into the **most common data-engineering pipeline shape**: pull from an API, validate, transform, store, and analyze.

**The stages:**
- **Extract** — pull records from a REST API with **Requests**: a `Session` with a timeout, automatic **retries** with backoff, and **pagination** (loop until no next cursor). Pull **incrementally** with an `updated_since` watermark.
- **Validate** — run every record through a **Pydantic** model at the boundary. Good rows become typed objects; bad rows go to a **quarantine** with their error details, so one malformed record never poisons the batch.
- **Transform** — load the clean records into **Polars** and reshape them with a lazy pipeline (filter, derive columns, group/aggregate) that runs multithreaded.
- **Store** — write the result as **partitioned Parquet to object storage** with **fsspec** (`s3://...`), so the data is columnar, compressed, and partition-pruned for cheap reads. Make writes **idempotent** (overwrite the partition for the run's date).
- **Analyze** — query the lake in place with **DuckDB** SQL — no load step, full pushdown — and hand results to pandas/Polars for a report.

**Why this shape.** Each stage has one job and a clear contract: ingestion is robust to flaky networks; validation enforces a schema; transformation is fast and declarative; storage is query-optimized; analysis is SQL over files. The same skeleton scales from a laptop script to an orchestrated production job (the next capstone adds Prefect + a quality gate). Open **Go deeper** for the full, runnable pipeline.

**What to watch for.** The failure modes *are* the lesson: an API that rate-limits (honor 429 + backoff), a schema that drifts (Pydantic catches it at the boundary), a partition written twice (idempotency), and a join that fans out (validate keys). Handle those four and you have a pipeline that survives contact with real, messy, production data.
lessons.example:
# the pipeline in five lines of intent (full code in Go deeper)
records   = extract_api(since=last_run)        # Requests: paginated + retries
clean,bad = validate(records, model=Order)     # Pydantic: typed or quarantined
df        = transform(clean)                   # Polars: lazy filter/derive/aggregate
write_lake(df, 's3://lake/orders', dt=today)   # fsspec: partitioned Parquet
report    = duckdb.sql("SELECT region, sum(amount) FROM 's3://lake/orders/**/*.parquet' GROUP BY region").df()
keypoints: The canonical pipeline: extract → validate → transform → store → analyze.
Extract with Requests: Session + timeout + retries/backoff + pagination + incremental watermark.
Validate each record with a Pydantic model; quarantine bad rows with their errors.
Transform with a lazy Polars pipeline (multithreaded, optimized).
Store as partitioned Parquet on S3 via fsspec; make writes idempotent per run date.
Analyze with DuckDB SQL directly over the Parquet lake — no load step.

quiz.q: Why validate with Pydantic BEFORE writing to the lake?
options: To enforce a schema at the boundary so bad data is quarantined, not written downstream
quiz.explain: Validating at ingestion stops malformed/drifted records from corrupting the lake; bad rows are set aside with reasons.

quiz.q: What makes the 'store' step's writes idempotent?
options: Overwriting the run-date partition so a re-run replaces, not duplicates, the data
quiz.explain: Writing/overwriting a date partition means a retried run produces the same result instead of double-loading.

quiz.q: Why query the lake with DuckDB instead of loading it all into pandas?
options: It runs SQL over Parquet in place with pushdown, out-of-core — no full load into RAM
quiz.explain: DuckDB streams Parquet with projection/predicate pushdown, so it analyzes more than fits in memory without a load step.

exercises.prompt: Which library handles each stage: extract, validate, transform, store, analyze?
exercises.solution: Extract=Requests, Validate=Pydantic, Transform=Polars, Store=fsspec/Parquet on S3, Analyze=DuckDB.

exercises.prompt: Where do records that fail validation go, and why not just drop them?
exercises.solution: To a quarantine/dead-letter store with their error details, so you can debug schema drift and reprocess — dropping silently hides data-quality problems.

exercises.prompt: How do you pull only new data each run?
exercises.solution: Track the last run's max updated_at and pass updated_since=<watermark> to the API, so each run fetches only changed records.

exercises.prompt: Why partition the Parquet output by date?
exercises.solution: So downstream queries filtered on date read only the relevant partitions (pruning), making reads fast and cheap.

lessons.diagram_caption: Extract (Requests) → Validate (Pydantic) → Transform (Polars) → Store (Parquet/S3) → Analyze (DuckDB)

lessons.title: Capstone: files to a published report
lessons.summary: Turn raw files into a stakeholder deliverable with DuckDB, seaborn/matplotlib, and openpyxl.
lessons.concept:
This capstone is the **analyst-to-engineer workflow**: start from raw files and end with a polished report a stakeholder can open. It combines DuckDB, seaborn/matplotlib, and openpyxl.

**The stages:**
- **Read** — point **DuckDB** at the raw files (`'data/*.parquet'` or CSVs) — no load step.
- **Transform** — do the heavy lifting in **SQL**: joins across files, **window functions** (running totals, rank, month-over-month with `lag`), aggregations, and `QUALIFY` for top-N. Complex reshaping that would be awkward in pandas becomes a readable query, and the result comes back as a DataFrame (`.df()`/`.pl()`).
- **Visualize** — use **seaborn/matplotlib** for the figures: distributions to spot outliers, a bar chart of revenue by segment, a line chart of the monthly trend, a correlation heatmap. Save them as PNGs (`dpi=150`, `bbox_inches='tight'`).
