- **Deliver** — assemble a formatted **Excel workbook** with **openpyxl**: a summary sheet (bold header, number formats, a `SUM` formula, frozen header), a data sheet, and the charts embedded. Business users get the spreadsheet they expect.

**Why this matters.** A huge part of data engineering is the **last mile** — turning correct data into something people actually consume. Doing the math in DuckDB keeps it fast and reproducible; doing the presentation in seaborn + openpyxl meets stakeholders where they are. The whole thing is a short, rerunnable script you can schedule. Open **Go deeper** for the full build.

**What to watch for.** Keep the heavy aggregation in DuckDB — don't pull millions of rows into pandas just to summarize them. Label axes and units honestly, choose the chart that fits the question, and make the whole script **rerunnable** so the report regenerates on demand. The goal is a deliverable a stakeholder trusts and that you can put on a schedule.
lessons.example:
import duckdb, seaborn as sns, matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# 1) transform in SQL (window function for month-over-month)
monthly = duckdb.sql("""
  WITH m AS (SELECT date_trunc('month', dt) mo, sum(amount) rev FROM 'sales/*.parquet' GROUP BY 1)
  SELECT mo, rev, rev - lag(rev) OVER (ORDER BY mo) AS delta FROM m ORDER BY mo
""").df()
# 2) chart it
ax = sns.lineplot(data=monthly, x='mo', y='rev'); ax.figure.savefig('trend.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
# 3) (Go deeper) write a formatted Excel workbook with openpyxl + the chart
keypoints: The last-mile workflow: read files → transform in SQL → visualize → deliver an Excel report.
DuckDB reads raw Parquet/CSV in place and does joins, window functions, and aggregations as SQL.
Window functions (lag, running totals, rank) and QUALIFY express analytics cleanly; results return as DataFrames.
seaborn/matplotlib produce the figures (distributions, bars, trends, heatmaps), saved as crisp PNGs.
openpyxl assembles a formatted, multi-sheet Excel workbook with formulas and embedded charts.
Doing math in DuckDB keeps it fast/reproducible; presenting in Excel meets stakeholders where they are.

quiz.q: Why do the transformations in DuckDB SQL rather than pandas here?
options: Joins, window functions, and aggregations over files are fast, readable, and reproducible in SQL
quiz.explain: DuckDB runs complex analytics directly over the files with pushdown and returns a DataFrame — concise and fast.

quiz.q: Which library produces the final stakeholder-facing Excel workbook?
quiz.explain: openpyxl writes formatted .xlsx with headers, formulas, and embedded charts — the deliverable business users open.

quiz.q: What's a clean way to compute month-over-month change in this pipeline?
options: A window function: lag(rev) OVER (ORDER BY month)
quiz.explain: lag() over an ordered set gives the previous month's value to subtract — a one-line window expression in DuckDB.

exercises.prompt: Map each stage to its library: read/transform, visualize, deliver.
exercises.solution: Read/transform = DuckDB (SQL over files), Visualize = seaborn/matplotlib (PNGs), Deliver = openpyxl (Excel).

exercises.prompt: Write the DuckDB SQL idea for monthly revenue with month-over-month delta.
exercises.solution:
WITH m AS (SELECT date_trunc('month',dt) mo, sum(amount) rev FROM 'sales/*.parquet' GROUP BY 1)
SELECT mo, rev, rev - lag(rev) OVER (ORDER BY mo) AS delta FROM m;

exercises.prompt: How do you save a seaborn chart crisply for a report?
exercises.solution: ax.figure.savefig('chart.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')

exercises.prompt: Name two openpyxl touches that make an Excel report look professional.
exercises.solution: A bold/colored header row (Font/PatternFill), number formats (e.g. '#,##0.00'), a SUM formula, frozen header (freeze_panes), and an embedded chart.

lessons.diagram_caption: Files → DuckDB SQL (joins, windows) → seaborn/matplotlib charts → openpyxl Excel report

lessons.title: Capstone: an orchestrated, quality-gated pipeline
lessons.summary: Wrap extract/validate/transform/load in a Prefect flow with retries, a data-quality gate, and a schedule.
lessons.concept:
This capstone turns a script into a **production pipeline**: scheduled, observable, resilient, and guarded by data-quality checks. It combines Prefect, a quality gate (Great Expectations or Pydantic), Polars, Parquet, and stdlib config.

**The design:**
- **Orchestrate with Prefect** — each stage is a `@task`; a `@flow` wires them together. Tasks get **automatic retries with backoff** (transient API/DB failures heal themselves), **logging**, and **caching**. The flow runs on a **schedule** (a 3am cron deployment) and its runs, logs, and failures are visible in the **UI**.
- **Configure from the environment** — read the DB URL, API token, and run date from env vars (stdlib `os.getenv` or a Pydantic `BaseSettings`), so the same code runs in dev/staging/prod and secrets never live in code.
- **Gate on data quality** — after transforming, run a **Great Expectations** checkpoint (or Pydantic validation): if it fails, **stop the flow and quarantine** the batch instead of publishing bad data. This is the difference between a pipeline that fails loudly and one that silently ships garbage.
- **Load idempotently** — write the run-date partition so a retry replaces rather than duplicates.

**Why it matters.** Correct logic isn't enough in production — you need **observability** (see and debug runs), **resilience** (retries, idempotency), and **trust** (quality gates). Prefect supplies the first two from a couple of decorators; the quality gate supplies the third. Open **Go deeper** for the full orchestrated flow with a failing-gate path.

**What to watch for.** Three things separate a real pipeline from a fancy cron job: **idempotency** (a retry must not double-load), **graceful failure** (retries with backoff for transient errors), and **trust** (the quality gate stops silent drift). Get those right and you have something you can let run unattended overnight and believe the next morning.
keypoints: A production pipeline needs observability, resilience, and trust — not just correct logic.
Prefect: @task/@flow with automatic retries, logging, caching, schedules, and a UI for runs/failures.
Configure from the environment (os.getenv / Pydantic BaseSettings) so secrets stay out of code.
Add a data-quality gate (Great Expectations / Pydantic): fail and quarantine instead of publishing bad data.
Load idempotently by overwriting the run-date partition so retries don't duplicate.
Schedule it (cron deployment); the UI gives the visibility that turns a script into an operable job.

quiz.q: What does adding a Great Expectations (or Pydantic) gate before load achieve?
options: It stops bad/drifted data from being published — failing loudly and quarantining the batch
quiz.explain: A quality gate validates the batch and halts the pipeline on failure, so bad data never reaches downstream consumers.

quiz.q: How does Prefect make the pipeline resilient to a flaky API?
options: @task(retries=..., retry_delay_seconds=...) retries transient failures with backoff
quiz.explain: Task-level retries re-run on failure after a delay, healing transient network/5xx errors without custom code.

quiz.q: Why read config and secrets from environment variables?
options: So the same artifact runs across dev/staging/prod and secrets never live in source
quiz.explain: Twelve-factor config keeps secrets out of the repo and lets you change behavior per environment by swapping values.

exercises.prompt: Make the extract task retry 3 times with a 30s delay in Prefect.
exercises.solution:
@task(retries=3, retry_delay_seconds=30)
def extract(since): ...

exercises.prompt: Where in the flow does the quality gate go, and what happens on failure?
exercises.hint: Between transform and load; raise to stop.
exercises.solution: After transform and before load; if validation fails, raise an exception so the flow stops and the batch is quarantined — load never runs.

exercises.prompt: How do you schedule the flow to run nightly at 3am?
exercises.hint: A deployment with a cron schedule.
exercises.solution: etl.serve(name='nightly', cron='0 3 * * *')  # workers pull and run it

exercises.prompt: Why overwrite the run-date partition instead of appending on load?
exercises.solution: So a retried or re-run flow replaces that date's data rather than duplicating it — the load is idempotent.

lessons.diagram_caption: Prefect flow: extract → transform → quality-gate → load, with retries, schedule & UI

lessons.title: Capstone: synthetic data & engine benchmark
lessons.summary: Generate data with Faker, then benchmark pandas vs Polars vs DuckDB to know when to use which.
lessons.concept:
This capstone builds **intuition for choosing an engine** by generating a large dataset and timing the same job across pandas, Polars, and DuckDB. It combines Faker, the stdlib, and the three DataFrame/SQL engines.

**The steps:**
- **Generate** — use **Faker** (seeded, with `unique` keys) to build a few million realistic rows, written once to **Parquet** so every engine reads the same input.
- **Define one job** — a representative aggregation: filter, group by a key, and compute sums/means/distinct counts (the shape of most analytics).
- **Benchmark fairly** — time each engine with the stdlib (`time.perf_counter` or `timeit`), running a **warm-up** first, repeating, and measuring **peak memory**. Read the same Parquet each time; don't include data generation in the timing.
- **Interpret** — typically: **pandas** is fine in-memory but slowest and most memory-hungry; **Polars** (lazy) is much faster and lighter via multithreading + pushdown; **DuckDB** is comparably fast and shines **out-of-core** when data exceeds RAM. Bigger-than-memory inputs make pandas OOM while Polars-streaming and DuckDB keep going.

**Why it matters.** "Which tool?" is a constant question, and hand-wavy answers lead to slow pipelines. Measuring on *your* data shape, with **honest benchmarking** (warm cache, representative size, repeated runs, memory tracked), turns the choice into evidence. Open **Go deeper** for the full benchmark harness and a results table.

**What to watch for.** Unfair benchmarks mislead more than they help: don't time cold caches, toy-sized data, or I/O you didn't mean to include; do run a warm-up, repeat the measurement, and track **memory**, not just wall-clock. The deliverable is a small, honest results table that tells you — for *your* data shape — when pandas, Polars, or DuckDB is the right call.
lessons.example:
import time, faker, polars as pl, duckdb, pandas as pd

# generate once -> Parquet (so every engine reads identical input)
build_synthetic('bench.parquet', rows=5_000_000)     # Faker, seeded

def timed(fn, *a):
    fn(*a)                                            # warm-up
    t = time.perf_counter(); fn(*a); return time.perf_counter() - t

timed(lambda: pd.read_parquet('bench.parquet').groupby('region')['amount'].sum())
timed(lambda: pl.scan_parquet('bench.parquet').group_by('region').agg(pl.col('amount').sum()).collect())
timed(lambda: duckdb.sql("SELECT region, sum(amount) FROM 'bench.parquet' GROUP BY region").df())
keypoints: Build engine-selection intuition by benchmarking the same job on pandas, Polars, and DuckDB.
Generate realistic volume with Faker (seeded, unique keys), written once to Parquet for a fair input.
Benchmark honestly: warm up, repeat, time with perf_counter/timeit, and track peak memory.
pandas: simple in-memory but slowest/heaviest; Polars: much faster/lighter (lazy, multicore, pushdown).
DuckDB: comparably fast and excels out-of-core; larger-than-RAM data OOMs pandas but not Polars-stream/DuckDB.
Measure on your own data shape — evidence beats hand-wavy 'which tool?' answers.

quiz.q: Why generate the dataset once to Parquet before benchmarking?
options: So every engine reads identical input and data generation isn't counted in the timing
quiz.explain: A fixed Parquet input makes the comparison fair — you measure each engine's processing, not data creation.

quiz.q: What honest-benchmarking practice avoids misleading numbers?
options: Warm up, repeat runs, and track memory — and use representative data size
quiz.explain: Warm caches, repeated runs, representative size, and memory tracking give trustworthy, reproducible comparisons.

quiz.q: When does the gap between pandas and Polars/DuckDB matter most?
options: When data approaches or exceeds RAM — pandas OOMs while Polars-streaming/DuckDB stay out-of-core
quiz.explain: Out-of-core engines keep working past memory limits where eager pandas fails, and they're faster via parallelism/pushdown.

exercises.prompt: Generate 5M reproducible synthetic rows and write them to Parquet.
exercises.solution: Faker.seed(0); build rows with fake.* in batches; pl.DataFrame(rows).write_parquet('bench.parquet')

exercises.prompt: Time a pandas vs Polars vs DuckDB group-by on the same Parquet.
exercises.solution:
pandas: pd.read_parquet(...).groupby('k')['v'].sum()
polars: pl.scan_parquet(...).group_by('k').agg(pl.col('v').sum()).collect()
duckdb: duckdb.sql("SELECT k, sum(v) FROM 'f.parquet' GROUP BY k").df()

exercises.prompt: Why include a warm-up run before timing?
exercises.solution: The first run pays one-time costs (file caching, lazy imports, query compilation); timing a warmed run measures steady-state performance.

exercises.prompt: Data is 50 GB on a 16 GB laptop. Which engines still work and why?
exercises.solution: Polars with collect(streaming=True) and DuckDB stream the data out-of-core; eager pandas tries to load it all and OOMs.

lessons.diagram_caption: Faker → Parquet → time the same job on pandas vs Polars vs DuckDB (warm, repeated, memory-tracked)

lessons.title: Capstone: a real-time streaming pipeline
lessons.summary: Process events the moment they arrive — Kafka, a stream processor with windows, and a live serving sink.
lessons.concept:
This capstone builds a **real-time** pipeline — processing events the moment they arrive instead of in nightly batches. It applies the Streaming track end to end.

**The stages:**
- **Produce** — apps emit events (clicks, orders, IoT readings) to **Kafka**, a durable, **partitioned** log. Partitioning by key gives ordering per key and parallelism; **consumer groups** scale and load-balance readers.
- **Process** — a stream engine (**Spark Structured Streaming**, Flink, or Bytewax) consumes the topic and computes **event-time windowed** aggregations (revenue per region per minute) using **watermarks** to handle **late, out-of-order** events.
- **Serve & store** — results land in a fast **serving store** (Postgres/Redis) that powers a **live dashboard**, *and* append to the **lake** (Parquet/Delta) for history and reprocessing.
- **Guarantee correctness** — **exactly-once** comes from **checkpointing** the processor's offsets/state plus an **idempotent sink** (upsert by key+window). **Backpressure** is handled by the engine; you size partitions and parallelism to keep up.

**Why it matters.** The batch ELT capstone answered "what happened yesterday?"; this answers "what's happening *now*?" — fraud alerts, live metrics, real-time recommendations. The hard parts are **time** (event vs processing time, watermarks), **state** (windows, joins), and **delivery guarantees** — exactly what stream processing exists to manage. Open **Go deeper** for the full runnable pipeline.

**What to watch for.** Streaming's hard problems are all here: pick the **window** that fits (tumbling for periodic metrics, sliding for moving averages, session for user activity); set the **watermark** to your real lateness tolerance (too tight drops data, too loose holds state forever); and keep the **sink idempotent** so a replay after failure can't double-count. Get those three right and the dashboard stays both fresh *and* correct.
lessons.example:
# the streaming job in intent (full code in Go deeper)
events = read_kafka('orders')                       # durable, partitioned source
agg = (events
   .withWatermark('ts', '10 minutes')               # tolerate late data
   .groupBy(window('ts', '1 minute'), 'region')
   .agg(sum('amount').alias('revenue')))            # windowed aggregation
write_stream(agg, sink='postgres', checkpoint='/ckpt',
             output_mode='update')                  # exactly-once via checkpoint + upsert
keypoints: A streaming pipeline processes events continuously, with low latency — not in batches.
Kafka is the durable, partitioned backbone; consumer groups scale and balance readers.
A stream processor computes event-time windowed aggregations using watermarks for late data.
Results serve a live dashboard (Postgres/Redis) and append to the lake for history.
Exactly-once = checkpointing offsets/state + an idempotent (upsert) sink.
The hard parts are time (event vs processing), state (windows/joins), and delivery guarantees.

quiz.q: Why use event-time windows with a watermark instead of processing-time windows?
options: Events arrive late/out of order; event-time + watermark produce correct windowed results anyway
quiz.explain: Grouping by when events actually happened (event time), with a watermark to bound lateness, gives correct aggregates despite delays.

quiz.q: How does the pipeline achieve exactly-once results?
options: Checkpointing the processor's offsets/state plus an idempotent (upsert) sink
quiz.explain: Checkpointing lets the job resume without reprocessing, and an upsert sink makes re-delivered records harmless.

quiz.q: What does partitioning a Kafka topic by key give you?
options: Per-key ordering and parallelism across consumers
quiz.explain: Records with the same key go to the same partition (ordered), and multiple partitions let a consumer group process in parallel.

exercises.prompt: Which component provides the durable, replayable event source?
exercises.solution: Kafka — a partitioned, durable log; consumers track offsets and can replay from any point.

exercises.prompt: How do you tolerate events that arrive 5 minutes late in a 1-minute window?
exercises.solution: Set a watermark (e.g. withWatermark('ts','10 minutes')) so the engine keeps window state long enough to include late events, then evicts it.

exercises.prompt: Where do results go, and why two destinations?
exercises.solution: A fast serving store (Postgres/Redis) powers the live dashboard; the lake (Parquet/Delta) keeps full history for reprocessing and batch analytics.

exercises.prompt: Contrast this capstone with the batch api-to-lake one in a sentence.
exercises.solution: Same extract→transform→store→serve goal, but continuous and low-latency (answers 'what's happening now?') instead of scheduled batches ('what happened yesterday?').

lessons.diagram_caption: Producers → Kafka → stream processor (windows + watermark) → serving store + lake → dashboard

lessons.title: Capstone: analytics engineering with dbt
lessons.summary: Build a tested, documented warehouse in layers with dbt — sources to staging to marts.
lessons.concept:
This capstone builds a **tested, documented warehouse** with **dbt** — the analytics-engineering workflow that turns SQL into software. It applies the Modeling, Pipelines, and CI/CD tracks together.

**The layered build:**
- **Sources** — declare the raw tables (`sources.yml`) so dbt tracks freshness and lineage.
- **Staging** — one model per source table: light cleanup (rename, cast types, standardize), 1:1 with the source. The clean base everything builds on.
- **Intermediate** — joins and business logic, broken into readable steps.
- **Marts** — the consumption layer: a **star schema** of **fact** and **dimension** models that BI tools query.

**What makes it engineering:**
- **Tests** — `not_null`, `unique`, `relationships` (FK integrity), `accepted_values`, plus custom tests, run on every build.
- **Docs & lineage** — column descriptions + an auto-generated DAG showing how every model depends on others.
- **Incremental models** — process only new/changed rows instead of rebuilding huge tables.
- **Snapshots** — capture **SCD2** history of slowly changing dimensions.
- **Refs** — `{{ ref('stg_orders') }}` builds the dependency graph so dbt runs models in the right order.

It runs on **DuckDB** locally (free, fast dev) and **Snowflake/BigQuery** in prod, shipped through the **CI/CD** you just learned (PR → `dbt build` on a CI schema → merge → `dbt run` on prod). dbt is how modern teams make transformations **versioned, tested, and documented**. Open **Go deeper** to build the project.

**What to watch for.** Keep **staging thin** — rename/cast only, no business logic — so the base stays stable, and push logic into intermediate/marts. Make every model **testable** and every column **documented**. Reach for **incremental** models only where rebuild cost demands it (they add complexity), and run `dbt build` (not just `run`) so tests gate the result. The payoff is a warehouse a whole team can change safely.
lessons.example:
-- models/staging/stg_orders.sql  (one model per source, light cleanup)
select order_id, customer_id, cast(amount as double) as amount,
       lower(status) as status, order_date
from {{ source('shop', 'orders') }}
where amount is not null

-- models/marts/fct_orders.sql  (the consumption layer, built from staging via ref)
select o.order_id, o.amount, o.order_date, c.region
from {{ ref('stg_orders') }} o
join {{ ref('stg_customers') }} c using (customer_id)
-- schema.yml adds tests: unique+not_null on order_id, relationships on customer_id
keypoints: dbt is analytics engineering: SQL transformations made versioned, tested, and documented software.
Build in layers: sources → staging (1:1 cleanup) → intermediate (logic) → marts (star schema for BI).
ref()/source() build the dependency DAG so models run in the correct order with full lineage.
Tests (not_null, unique, relationships, accepted_values) run on every build and gate merges.
Incremental models process only new rows; snapshots capture SCD2 history of changing dimensions.
Runs on DuckDB locally and Snowflake/BigQuery in prod, shipped via Git + CI/CD.

quiz.q: What is the purpose of dbt's staging layer?
options: One model per source with light cleanup (rename/cast/standardize), 1:1 with the source
quiz.explain: Staging models normalize each raw source into a clean, consistent base that all downstream models build on.

quiz.q: How does dbt know the order to run models in?
options: From ref()/source() calls, which build a dependency DAG
quiz.explain: Each {{ ref('model') }} declares a dependency; dbt compiles these into a DAG and runs models in topological order.

quiz.q: Which dbt feature avoids rebuilding a huge fact table every run?
quiz.explain: Incremental models insert/merge only the new rows since the last run instead of a full rebuild.

exercises.prompt: What are the four dbt layers from raw to BI?
exercises.solution: Sources (raw) → staging (1:1 cleanup) → intermediate (joins/logic) → marts (fact + dimension star schema).

exercises.prompt: Add tests asserting order_id is unique and not null, and customer_id references customers.
exercises.solution: In schema.yml under the model's order_id column: tests: [unique, not_null]; under customer_id: relationships: {to: ref('stg_customers'), field: customer_id}.

exercises.prompt: Why reference upstream models with {{ ref('stg_orders') }} instead of the table name?
exercises.solution: ref() makes dbt track the dependency (correct run order + lineage) and resolves the right schema per environment (dev/CI/prod).

exercises.prompt: How do you keep history of a dimension whose attributes change over time?
exercises.solution: A dbt snapshot captures SCD2 history — each change becomes a new versioned row with valid-from/valid-to, so you can query the dimension as of any date.

lessons.diagram_caption: dbt layers: sources → staging → intermediate → marts (fact + dim), tested & documented

lessons.title: Capstone: a medallion lakehouse
lessons.summary: Turn a raw lake into a reliable lakehouse with bronze/silver/gold layers on Delta/Iceberg.
lessons.concept:
This capstone organizes a data lake into the **medallion architecture** — **bronze → silver → gold** — on a **table format** (Delta Lake or Apache Iceberg) that brings **ACID transactions** to plain files. It applies the Lakehouse track end to end.

**The three layers:**
- **Bronze (raw)** — data ingested **as-is**, append-only, schema-on-read. A faithful, replayable copy of the source; you never lose the original.
- **Silver (cleaned)** — deduplicated, conformed, typed, and joined into clean entities. Built with **MERGE/upsert** so updates and CDC apply correctly. This is the trustworthy layer most pipelines read.
- **Gold (curated)** — business-level aggregates and marts (star schemas, KPIs) optimized for consumption by BI and ML.

**What the table format gives you:**
- **ACID transactions** — concurrent writers don't corrupt the table; readers see consistent snapshots.
- **MERGE** — upserts and CDC apply (insert/update/delete) in one atomic operation.
- **Time travel** — query or roll back to any past version (`VERSION AS OF`) for audit and recovery.
- **Schema evolution** — add/rename columns safely.
- **OPTIMIZE / compaction + Z-order** — fix the small-files problem and cluster data for fast skipping.

**Why it matters.** A raw lake is cheap but chaotic; the medallion pattern + a table format make it a **lakehouse** — cheap object storage with warehouse reliability (transactions, quality, performance). It's the architecture under most modern data platforms. Open **Go deeper** to build all three layers.

**What to watch for.** Never mutate **bronze** — it's your replayable source of truth, so every fix happens downstream. Make the silver **MERGE** idempotent on a stable key. **Partition** gold by what BI actually filters on (usually date), and schedule **OPTIMIZE/compaction** so streaming and CDC writes don't leave thousands of tiny files. Treat **time travel** as both your audit log and your undo button.
lessons.example:
# silver: upsert cleaned records into a Delta table (idempotent, handles updates)
MERGE INTO silver.orders t
USING bronze_clean s
ON t.order_id = s.order_id
WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET *
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT *;

# time travel for audit / rollback
SELECT * FROM silver.orders VERSION AS OF 42;
OPTIMIZE silver.orders ZORDER BY (region);   # compact + cluster for skipping
keypoints: The medallion architecture layers a lake into bronze (raw) → silver (clean) → gold (curated).
Bronze is append-only raw (replayable); silver is deduplicated/typed/conformed via MERGE; gold is business marts.
A table format (Delta/Iceberg) adds ACID transactions, so concurrent writes don't corrupt the table.
MERGE applies upserts and CDC (insert/update/delete) atomically; time travel queries/rolls back any version.
Schema evolution and OPTIMIZE/Z-order keep the table flexible and fast (no small-files problem).
The result is a lakehouse: cheap object storage with warehouse-grade reliability and performance.

quiz.q: What does the silver layer do that bronze doesn't?
options: Cleans, deduplicates, types, and conforms the data (via MERGE) into trustworthy entities
quiz.explain: Bronze keeps raw data as-is; silver is the cleaned, conformed layer most consumers actually read.

quiz.q: What does a table format like Delta/Iceberg add to files in a lake?
options: ACID transactions, MERGE/upserts, time travel, and schema evolution
quiz.explain: Table formats add a transaction log over Parquet, giving ACID guarantees, atomic MERGE, versioning/time travel, and safe schema changes.

quiz.q: How do you apply inserts, updates, AND deletes from CDC into a silver table atomically?
quiz.explain: MERGE matches on a key and applies insert/update/delete in one atomic transaction — ideal for upserts and CDC.

exercises.prompt: Name the three medallion layers and what each holds.
exercises.solution: Bronze = raw, append-only source copy; Silver = cleaned/deduplicated/typed entities (via MERGE); Gold = business aggregates/marts for consumption.

exercises.prompt: Write the idea of an upsert into a Delta silver table.
exercises.solution: MERGE INTO silver.t USING updates s ON t.id = s.id WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET * WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT *;

exercises.prompt: You published a bad batch to silver an hour ago. How do you recover?
exercises.solution: Use time travel: read the previous good version (VERSION AS OF / TIMESTAMP AS OF) and RESTORE the table to it — the transaction log kept every version.

exercises.prompt: Your silver table has thousands of tiny files and slow scans. Fix it.
exercises.solution: Run OPTIMIZE to compact small files into right-sized ones, and ZORDER BY the common filter column so min/max stats enable data skipping.

lessons.diagram_caption: Bronze (raw) → Silver (clean, MERGE) → Gold (curated marts) on an ACID table format

lessons.title: Capstone: change data capture to the lake
lessons.summary: Keep a lake in sync with a source database in near-real-time using log-based CDC.
lessons.concept:
This capstone keeps a lake **continuously in sync with a source database** — no nightly full reloads — using **Change Data Capture (CDC)**. It ties together Pipelines, Streaming, and the Lakehouse.

**Two kinds of CDC:**
- **Query-based** — poll `WHERE updated_at > <watermark>`. Simple, but it **misses hard deletes** and adds load to the source.
- **Log-based** *(the real thing)* — a tool like **Debezium** reads the database's **write-ahead log** (the transaction log), capturing **every insert, update, and delete** with almost no load on the source.

**The pipeline:**
- **Capture** — Debezium tails the WAL and emits a change event per row operation (op = c/u/d, before/after images).
- **Transport** — events flow through **Kafka** (durable, ordered per key).
- **Apply** — a processor **MERGEs** the changes into the lake's **silver** table: inserts add rows, updates overwrite, **deletes** remove (or tombstone). It must handle **ordering** (apply changes per key in commit order) and be **idempotent** (replays don't corrupt state).

**Why it matters.** CDC turns slow, source-hammering batch reloads into **near-real-time replication**, so analytics sees operational data within seconds — without impacting the production database. Handling **deletes**, **out-of-order** events, and **schema changes** is what makes it engineering. Open **Go deeper** for the full CDC flow.

**What to watch for.** Three things break naive CDC: **deletes** (you must handle the `d` op, not just upserts), **ordering** (apply changes per key in commit order using the log sequence number), and **source schema changes** (plan for columns being added or dropped). Seed the target with an initial **snapshot**, then stream the log from that point — Debezium does both — and an idempotent MERGE makes replays safe.
lessons.example:
# a Debezium change event (simplified) and how it's applied
# {op:'u', before:{id:1, amt:10}, after:{id:1, amt:25}, ts_ms:...}
MERGE INTO silver.orders t
USING cdc_batch s
ON t.id = s.id
WHEN MATCHED AND s.op = 'd' THEN DELETE
WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET *
WHEN NOT MATCHED AND s.op <> 'd' THEN INSERT *;
# apply per key in commit order; idempotent on replay
keypoints: CDC keeps a lake in sync with a source DB without full reloads — near-real-time replication.
Query-based CDC polls updated_at (simple, but misses hard deletes); log-based reads the WAL.
Log-based CDC (Debezium) captures every insert/update/delete with minimal source load.
Change events flow through Kafka (durable, ordered per key) to the processor.
A MERGE applies inserts/updates/DELETES into the silver layer; handle ordering + idempotency.
CDC enables fresh analytics on operational data without hammering the production database.

quiz.q: Why is log-based CDC better than polling updated_at?
options: It captures every change including hard DELETES, with minimal load on the source
quiz.explain: Reading the write-ahead log captures all operations (including deletes) cheaply; updated_at polling misses deletes and loads the source.

quiz.q: How are CDC changes applied to the lake's silver table?
options: A MERGE that inserts/updates/deletes by key
quiz.explain: A MERGE matches on the primary key and applies the right operation (insert/update/delete) atomically.

quiz.q: Two correctness concerns when applying a stream of changes are…
options: Ordering (apply per key in commit order) and idempotency (replays don't corrupt state)
quiz.explain: Changes must apply in the right order per key, and re-delivered events must not double-apply — ordering + idempotency.

exercises.prompt: Which CDC method captures hard deletes, and how?
exercises.solution: Log-based CDC (e.g. Debezium) reads the database's write-ahead log, which records deletes — query-based updated_at polling cannot see them.

exercises.prompt: Write the MERGE idea that applies a delete from a CDC batch.
exercises.hint: WHEN MATCHED AND op='d' THEN DELETE.
exercises.solution: MERGE INTO silver.t USING cdc s ON t.id=s.id WHEN MATCHED AND s.op='d' THEN DELETE WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET * WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT *;

exercises.prompt: Why must the apply step be idempotent?
exercises.solution: Kafka may redeliver events and the job may retry; idempotent application (keyed MERGE) means reprocessing the same change leaves the table unchanged.

exercises.prompt: What's the business benefit of CDC over a nightly full reload?
exercises.solution: Analytics sees operational changes in near-real-time, and the source DB isn't hammered by repeated full extracts.

lessons.diagram_caption: Source DB WAL → Debezium (CDC) → Kafka → MERGE (insert/update/delete) into the silver lake

lessons.title: Capstone: feature store to served model
lessons.summary: The whole ML track in one project — ingest, features, training, registry, serving, and monitoring.
lessons.concept:
This capstone ties the entire ML track into **one end-to-end system**: from raw data to a monitored, served, self-retraining model. It's the project that turns 'I understand the pieces' into 'I can build production ML.'

**The full pipeline:**
- **Ingest** — reliable pipelines land clean training data in the lake (your core DE skills).
- **Features** — a **feature pipeline** computes point-in-time-correct features and materializes them to a **feature store** (offline for training, online for serving) — killing train/serve skew.
- **Train** — a reproducible **training pipeline** pulls historical features, splits (no leakage), fits the model, evaluates, and logs everything to **MLflow**.
- **Register** — if it beats the incumbent, the model is versioned in the **registry** and promoted Staging → Production after validation.
- **Serve** — **batch** scoring writes a predictions table *and/or* an **online** API scores live requests, both loading the **Production** model and the **online** features.
- **Monitor** — log predictions + features, join labels when they arrive, compute **drift**, and alert.
- **Retrain** — drift/performance/schedule triggers the training pipeline again, closing the loop.

**Why it's the capstone.** Each ML lesson covered one stage; this builds the **whole system** the way a real team does, and exposes the seams between stages: the feature store is the contract between training and serving; the registry is the contract between training and deployment; the monitoring loop is what keeps it alive. Get the contracts right and the system runs itself. Open **Go deeper** for the complete, runnable pipeline — orchestrated, tracked, and monitored end to end.

**What to watch for.** The **seams** are where real systems break: run the **same feature code** offline and online (the store, never two implementations); make training **reproducible** (version data + code + config) so a registered model is trustworthy; gate **promotion** on a shadow/A-B test, not offline metrics alone; make batch loads **idempotent**; and build the **delayed-label** join so you can measure real performance. Get the two contracts (feature store, registry) and the monitoring loop right, and the system quietly runs — and refreshes — itself.
lessons.example:
# the end-to-end ML system in intent (full code in Go deeper)
features = build_and_materialize(raw)        # feature pipeline -> feature store (offline+online)
train_df = store.get_historical_features(labels, FEATURES)   # point-in-time correct
model, metrics = train(train_df)             # split + fit + MLflow log
if metrics.auc > prod_auc(): register_and_promote(model)     # registry: Staging->Production
batch_score(store, 'models:/churn/Production')               # serving (batch)
monitor(predictions, features); trigger_retrain_if_drift()   # MLOps loop
keypoints: This capstone builds the whole ML system end to end: ingest → features → train → register → serve → monitor → retrain.
A feature pipeline materializes point-in-time features to a store (offline for training, online for serving) — no skew.
The training pipeline pulls historical features, splits without leakage, fits, evaluates, and logs to MLflow.
The registry versions the model and gates Staging → Production promotion; serving loads the Production version.
Serving runs batch (predictions table) and/or online (live API), using online features + the Production model.
Monitoring logs predictions+features, joins delayed labels, detects drift, and triggers retraining — closing the loop.

quiz.q: What is the feature store the contract between, in this system?
options: Training and serving — the same feature definitions feed both, preventing skew
quiz.explain: Defining features once and serving them to both training (offline) and inference (online) makes the store the consistency contract that eliminates train/serve skew.

quiz.q: How does serving always get the approved model without a redeploy?
options: It loads the registry's Production stage — promotion/rollback is a registry change
quiz.explain: Serving references models:/.../Production, so promoting a new version (or rolling back) is a registry transition the service picks up automatically.

quiz.q: What closes the loop and keeps the deployed model healthy over time?
quiz.explain: Models decay; the monitoring + retraining loop detects drift/performance drops and refreshes the model, keeping it accurate.

exercises.prompt: List the seven stages of the end-to-end ML pipeline.
exercises.solution: Ingest → feature pipeline/store → train (MLflow) → register/promote → serve (batch/online) → monitor (drift) → retrain.

exercises.prompt: Which two 'contracts' hold the system together, and between whom?
exercises.solution: The feature store is the contract between training and serving (consistent features); the model registry is the contract between training and deployment (which model is live).

exercises.prompt: How is train/serve skew prevented in this build?
exercises.solution: Features are defined once and materialized to both the offline store (training, point-in-time correct) and the online store (serving), so both use identical logic.

exercises.prompt: What triggers a retrain, and what runs when it does?
exercises.solution: A schedule, a drift threshold, or a performance drop triggers the training pipeline (pull features → split → train → evaluate → register/promote), then monitoring resumes.

lessons.diagram_caption: Ingest → feature store → train (MLflow) → registry → serve (batch/online) → monitor → retrain

lessons.title: Capstone: RAG ingestion pipeline (docs → vectors)
lessons.summary: Turn documents into a searchable vector index for LLMs — the #1 new data-engineering workload of 2025-26.
lessons.concept:
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) made **document ingestion** a core DE responsibility: you build the offline pipeline that turns messy documents into a clean, governed vector index an LLM can search. It splits into two workflows.

**Offline indexing (the DE job):**
- **Load & parse** — pull source files (PDFs, HTML, docs), extract clean text plus **metadata** (title, source URI, timestamp, access tags). Preserve the raw source for audit.
- **Chunk** — split text into retrievable pieces. The 2026 benchmark default is **recursive ~512-token chunks with 10-20% overlap**; factoid Q&A favors 256-512, multi-hop 512-1024. Chunk size is the single most impactful knob — getting the bracket wrong degrades precision 15-30%.
- **Embed** — run each chunk through an embedding model (self-hosted BGE/GTE for sensitive data; a hosted model for convenience). **Redact/tokenize PII before embedding.**
- **Store** — write vectors + metadata to a vector store (Qdrant self-hosted, Pinecone managed), keeping a **stable doc-id and chunk-id** so you can update/delete content deterministically, and a **source URI** for citations and audit.

**Online retrieval (you build the data path):** query → embed → **hybrid search** (dense + sparse, fused with reciprocal-rank-fusion) → **rerank** (Cohere/cross-encoder) to pass fewer, better chunks → LLM. Rerankers add 50-200ms but cut LLM tokens, often paying for themselves.

**Why this is a DE pipeline, not ML.** It's ingestion, validation, transformation, storage, and incremental updates — your existing skills. The new parts are chunking strategy, embeddings, hybrid retrieval, and **retrieval-native access control** (every chunk carries authorization so answers respect permissions). Treat it like any pipeline: idempotent upserts by chunk-id, schema/metadata contracts, observability on freshness and recall.
lessons.example:
# offline indexing (sketch) — full code in Go deeper
docs   = load_and_parse(sources)                 # text + metadata + source_uri
chunks = recursive_chunk(docs, size=512, overlap=0.15)
chunks = redact_pii(chunks)                       # before embedding!
vecs   = embed(chunks, model='bge-large')
store.upsert(vecs, ids=[c.chunk_id for c in chunks])  # idempotent by chunk_id

# online retrieval
hits   = store.hybrid_search(embed(query), dense+sparse, k=20)
top    = rerank(query, hits)[:5]                  # fewer, better chunks
answer = llm(prompt(query, top))                  # cite source_uri
keypoints: RAG ingestion is a DE pipeline: load/parse → chunk → embed → store, plus incremental updates.
Chunk size is the #1 knob: recursive ~512 tokens, 10-20% overlap is the 2026 default.
Store stable doc-id + chunk-id + source URI → deterministic updates, citations, audit.
Redact/tokenize PII BEFORE embedding; enforce retrieval-native access control.
Retrieve with hybrid (dense+sparse) search + RRF, then rerank to pass fewer, better chunks.
Metadata enrichment lifts answer accuracy more than swapping embedding models.

quiz.q: Why store a stable chunk-id with each vector?
options: So you can deterministically update/delete that chunk and cite its source on re-ingest
quiz.explain: Stable ids make upserts idempotent and let you update/delete specific content and trace answers to a source.

quiz.q: What is the 2026 benchmark default chunking strategy?
quiz.explain: Recursive ~512-token chunking with modest overlap is the validated general-purpose default; semantic chunking adds cost/fragments.

quiz.q: Why redact PII before the embedding step?
options: Once text is embedded the sensitive content is baked into vectors and hard to control — redact at the source
quiz.explain: Embeddings encode the original text; PII must be removed/tokenized before embedding so it never enters the index.

exercises.prompt: Name the four offline-indexing stages in order.
exercises.solution: Load & parse → chunk → embed → store (with metadata + stable ids).

exercises.prompt: Your factoid chatbot gives vague answers. What chunking change do you try first?
exercises.solution: Shrink chunks toward 256-512 tokens (factoid range) and add metadata; oversized chunks dilute retrieval precision.

exercises.prompt: Why add a reranker if it costs latency?
exercises.solution: It passes fewer, more relevant chunks to the LLM, cutting token cost and improving answer quality — often a net win at scale.

exercises.prompt: How do you make sure a user only gets answers from documents they're allowed to see?
exercises.solution: Attach access tags to each chunk's metadata and filter retrieval by the user's permissions (retrieval-native access control).

lessons.diagram_caption: Offline: load → chunk → embed → vector store. Online: query → embed → hybrid search → rerank → LLM.

lessons.title: Capstone: an open Iceberg lakehouse
lessons.summary: Build a multi-engine open lakehouse on Apache Iceberg + a REST catalog — the settled 2026 architecture.
lessons.concept:
By 2026 the table-format question is settled: **Apache Iceberg won**, and the **lakehouse** (warehouse performance on data-lake economics) is the default architecture. This capstone builds the open, multi-engine version and contrasts it with the Delta medallion capstone.

**The three layers:**
- **Object storage** — Parquet **data files** on S3/GCS/ADLS. One cheap copy of the data.
- **Iceberg table format** — a metadata layer (snapshots, manifests, column stats) over those files giving **ACID** commits, **time travel**, **hidden partitioning** (no more partition columns leaking into queries), and safe **schema evolution**. Engines read the metadata to skip files.
- **REST catalog** — the single source of truth that tracks the current table state with server-side **compare-and-swap** (essential for safe concurrent writes on object storage). In 2026 the open choices are **Apache Polaris** (TLP Feb 2026) and **Apache Gravitino** (1.x), plus cloud catalogs (e.g. Google's *Lakehouse for Apache Iceberg*).

**Why open + REST is the big idea.** Every major engine speaks the REST catalog protocol, so the lakehouse is genuinely **multi-engine over one copy of data**: Spark for ETL, Flink for streaming ingest, Trino/Dremio for interactive SQL, DuckDB for ad-hoc — all pointed at the same catalog, no copies, no lock-in. Pick a REST-compatible catalog and you can swap implementations later without touching engine config.

**Operate it like a pro:** compact small files (Flink/streaming creates many), expire old snapshots to control metadata growth and storage cost, maintain stats for data skipping, and govern through the catalog (Polaris/Gravitino increasingly add lineage + access control as catalogs bend toward AI/agentic workloads). The lesson: **storage = Parquet, reliability = Iceberg, coordination = REST catalog, compute = any engine.**
lessons.example:
# one copy of data, many engines — all via the REST catalog
# Spark ETL writes an Iceberg table
spark.sql("CREATE TABLE cat.db.orders (id bigint, ts timestamp, amt double) USING iceberg")
spark.sql("INSERT INTO cat.db.orders SELECT * FROM staging")

# Trino queries the SAME table (REST catalog) — no copy
# SELECT region, sum(amt) FROM cat.db.orders WHERE ts > now() - interval '1' day

# time travel + maintenance
# SELECT * FROM cat.db.orders FOR VERSION AS OF 42        -- audit a past snapshot
# CALL cat.system.rewrite_data_files('db.orders')          -- compaction
# CALL cat.system.expire_snapshots('db.orders', now()-7d)  -- control metadata/cost
keypoints: 2026 reality: Iceberg won; lakehouse = warehouse reliability on cheap object storage.
Three layers: Parquet data files · Iceberg metadata (ACID, time travel, schema evolution) · REST catalog.
REST catalog (Polaris/Gravitino/cloud) = single source of truth with compare-and-swap for safe concurrent writes.
Open + REST = one copy of data, many engines (Spark, Flink, Trino, DuckDB) — no lock-in, swap engines freely.
Operate it: compact small files, expire old snapshots, keep stats for data skipping.
Contrast: Delta (Databricks-centric) vs Iceberg (open, multi-engine) — both give ACID over object storage.

quiz.q: What does the REST catalog provide that raw Parquet-on-S3 cannot?
options: A single source of truth with compare-and-swap so concurrent writers don't corrupt the table
quiz.explain: A proper catalog with server-side compare-and-swap is what makes concurrent commits safe on object storage.

quiz.q: Why is the REST protocol a big deal for Iceberg?
options: Any engine that speaks REST can use any compliant catalog → multi-engine, swap implementations without changing engine config
quiz.explain: The standardized REST protocol makes the ecosystem genuinely multi-engine and avoids catalog lock-in.

quiz.q: Streaming ingest created millions of tiny files and queries slowed. What maintenance fixes it?
quiz.explain: Compacting small files and expiring stale snapshots restores read performance and controls metadata/storage growth.

exercises.prompt: Name the three layers of an open Iceberg lakehouse.
exercises.solution: Object storage (Parquet data files) → Iceberg table format (metadata: snapshots/manifests/stats) → REST catalog (source of truth).

exercises.prompt: How can Spark, Trino, and DuckDB all query the same table without copies?
exercises.solution: They all point at the same REST catalog and read the Iceberg metadata/Parquet — one copy of data, many engines.

exercises.prompt: List two routine maintenance jobs for a production Iceberg table.
exercises.solution: Compact small data files (rewrite_data_files) and expire old snapshots; also refresh column stats for data skipping.

exercises.prompt: When would you pick Delta over Iceberg, and vice-versa?
exercises.solution: Delta if you're Databricks-centric and want its tight integration; Iceberg for an open, multi-engine, no-lock-in lakehouse via a REST catalog.

lessons.diagram_caption: Many engines → REST catalog (compare-and-swap) → Iceberg table format → Parquet on object storage (one copy).

lessons.title: Capstone: data contracts & shift-left quality
lessons.summary: Stop breaking changes at the source: enforce schema + SLA contracts in CI before bad data ships.
lessons.concept:
In 2026 the industry standard is **shift-left**: catch data problems at the commit/ingestion point, not after dashboards break. The mechanism is a **data contract** — a machine-readable agreement between a data **producer** and its **consumers**.

**What a contract specifies:** the schema and types, semantics (what each field means), **SLAs** (freshness, volume, allowed null rates), and the **owner**. The 2026 settled format is **ODCS** (Open Data Contract Standard) written as YAML and kept **in the repo** next to the code.

**How enforcement actually works (the key insight):** most 'contract tools' only document — they don't enforce. Real enforcement runs the contract in **CI**: when a producer opens a change, `datacontract-cli` (ODCS) or `buf` (Protobuf) validates the new schema/data against the contract and **blocks the merge** if it breaks consumers. Compliant changes flow through; breaking changes fail the build before they ship. That's the whole point — the cost of a violation drops from a production incident to a red CI check.

**The three building blocks of shift-left:**
1. **Contracts** — define what 'good' means and who owns it (ODCS YAML in repo).
2. **Quality gates** — enforce expectations where data is created/transformed (dbt tests, Great Expectations, Soda at runtime; datacontract-cli/buf at registration time in CI).
3. **Feedback loops** — detect, triage, trace impact, improve.

**Start simple:** YAML contracts in your repo + validation functions in CI. You can migrate to a heavier platform later — the contract definitions transfer. Pair this capstone with the data-quality/governance track: contracts are how you make 'shift-left' real instead of a slogan.
lessons.example:
# orders.contract.yaml  (ODCS, lives in the repo)
schema:
  - field: order_id   type: string   required: true   unique: true
  - field: amount     type: decimal  required: true   min: 0
  - field: created_at type: timestamp required: true
sla:
  freshness: 1h        # data no older than 1 hour
  max_null_rate: {amount: 0.0}
owner: orders-team

# CI step (blocks the merge on violation)
# $ datacontract test orders.contract.yaml --server prod
# FAIL: column 'amount' nullable in proposed schema -> breaks consumers  ✗ build red
keypoints: Shift-left = catch data issues at commit/ingest, not after consumers break.
A data contract pins schema, types, semantics, SLAs (freshness/volume), and owner.
2026 format is ODCS (YAML) kept in the repo; enforce with datacontract-cli or buf in CI.
Enforcement (not documentation) is the point: breaking changes fail the build before shipping.
Three blocks: contracts + quality gates (dbt/GE/Soda) + feedback loops.
Start with YAML + CI checks; migrate to a platform later — definitions transfer.

quiz.q: What makes a data contract actually useful (vs decorative)?
options: It's enforced in CI so breaking changes fail the build before reaching consumers
quiz.explain: Most tools only document; value comes from automated enforcement that blocks violations before they ship.

quiz.q: What does 'shift-left' mean for data quality?
options: Catch problems at the producer/commit/ingest stage instead of after downstream breakage
quiz.explain: Shift-left pushes enforcement to where data is created, so issues are caught early and cheaply.

quiz.q: A producer makes 'amount' nullable. With contracts in CI, what happens?
options: Consumers crash in prod next week
The CI check fails the merge and the change is blocked until fixed
quiz.explain: The contract test detects the breaking change at the PR and blocks it — turning an incident into a red build.

exercises.prompt: List four things a data contract should specify.
exercises.solution: Schema/types, semantics, SLAs (freshness/volume/null rate), and the owner.

exercises.prompt: Where do you enforce the contract, and with what?
exercises.solution: In CI at commit/registration time, with datacontract-cli (ODCS) or buf (Protobuf), plus runtime gates (dbt tests/GE/Soda).

exercises.prompt: Why keep the contract in the repo next to the code?
exercises.solution: So it's version-controlled, reviewed in PRs, and runs in CI with the change — making enforcement automatic.

exercises.prompt: How do you start cheaply without buying a platform?
exercises.solution: Write ODCS YAML contracts in the repo + CI validation functions; migrate to a platform later since definitions transfer.

lessons.diagram_caption: Producer → contract (schema/SLA/owner) validated in CI → compliant passes to consumers; breaking change blocked.

lessons.title: Capstone: real-time analytics serving
lessons.summary: Serve sub-second, high-concurrency analytics straight from streaming events with a real-time OLAP store.
lessons.concept:
Batch warehouses answer 'what happened yesterday'. **User-facing, real-time analytics** (live dashboards, in-product metrics, anomaly alerts) need **sub-second** queries on **fresh** data at **high concurrency** — a different system: a **real-time OLAP** store.

**The pipeline:** event sources (apps, clickstream, IoT) → **Kafka** (durable log) → optional **stream processing** (Flink to enrich/aggregate) → **real-time OLAP** (ClickHouse / Apache Pinot / Apache Druid) → user-facing dashboard. The OLAP store ingests the stream continuously and serves queries with **columnar storage + pre-aggregation + indexes**, delivering millisecond latency with **ingestion-to-query under one second**.

**Choosing the engine (2026):**
- **ClickHouse** — a single binary, simplest to operate; great general OLAP and can consolidate observability/warehouse-style work too, controlling cost by collapsing systems. Ingests best via micro-batch/Kafka connectors. Pick it for flexible queries + simple ops.
- **Apache Pinot** — built for **user-facing analytics**: star-tree indexing + pre-aggregation give ultra-low latency at **high QPS**, single-digit ms even under heavy concurrent load. Pick it when SLA is sub-second freshness at scale. (Multi-component: Controller/Broker/Server + ZooKeeper.)
- **Apache Druid** — close to Pinot; strong time-series/real-time, also multi-component.

**The trade-off** is operational: Pinot/Druid are distributed multi-service systems (real, ongoing ops tax); ClickHouse is one binary (simpler) but its streaming ingest is micro-batch, not true per-event. **Match the engine to the SLA:** ultra-low latency + high concurrency + guaranteed sub-second freshness → Pinot/Druid; general real-time OLAP with simpler ops and broader query patterns → ClickHouse.
lessons.example:
-- continuous ingest from Kafka into a real-time OLAP table, then serve sub-second queries
-- (ClickHouse example)
CREATE TABLE events_queue (...) ENGINE = Kafka SETTINGS kafka_broker_list='...', kafka_topic_list='events';
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW events_mv TO events AS SELECT * FROM events_queue;  -- stream -> table

-- user-facing dashboard query: must return in milliseconds at high QPS
SELECT toStartOfMinute(ts) AS m, count() AS c
FROM events WHERE ts > now() - INTERVAL 15 MINUTE GROUP BY m ORDER BY m;
keypoints: Real-time analytics = sub-second queries on fresh data at high concurrency — needs a real-time OLAP store, not a batch warehouse.
Pipeline: sources → Kafka → (Flink enrich) → ClickHouse/Pinot/Druid → user-facing dashboard.
Speed comes from columnar storage + pre-aggregation + indexes (e.g. Pinot star-tree); ingestion-to-query < 1s.
ClickHouse: single binary, simplest ops, great general OLAP; ingest via micro-batch/Kafka.
Pinot/Druid: ultra-low latency at high QPS for user-facing analytics, but multi-component ops tax.
Choose by SLA: sub-second + high concurrency → Pinot/Druid; flexible queries + simple ops → ClickHouse.

quiz.q: Why not just point a live dashboard at a batch cloud warehouse?
options: Batch warehouses aren't built for sub-second, high-QPS, fresh-data serving — that's a real-time OLAP job
quiz.explain: User-facing analytics need millisecond latency at high concurrency on fresh data; that's what real-time OLAP stores are designed for.

quiz.q: What gives Apache Pinot its edge for user-facing analytics?
options: Star-tree indexing + pre-aggregation → single-digit-ms latency at high concurrency
quiz.explain: Pinot's pre-aggregation and star-tree indexes deliver ultra-low latency under high QPS — ideal for product-facing dashboards.

quiz.q: What's the main argument for ClickHouse over Pinot/Druid?
options: Single binary → much simpler operations, and one engine can cover several workloads
quiz.explain: ClickHouse is a single binary that's far simpler to operate and can consolidate multiple workloads, lowering the ops/cost tax.

exercises.prompt: Sketch the real-time analytics pipeline from event to dashboard.
exercises.solution: Event sources → Kafka → (Flink enrich/aggregate) → real-time OLAP (ClickHouse/Pinot/Druid) → user-facing dashboard.

exercises.prompt: Where does sub-second latency come from in these stores?
exercises.solution: Columnar storage + pre-aggregation + indexes (e.g. Pinot star-tree), so queries scan/aggregate minimal data.

exercises.prompt: Your SLA is single-digit-ms at very high concurrency for an in-product dashboard. Which engine?
exercises.solution: Pinot (or Druid) — built for ultra-low latency at high QPS — accepting the multi-component ops cost.

exercises.prompt: You want one simpler system for mixed real-time + general OLAP. Which, and the trade-off?
exercises.solution: ClickHouse — simplest ops and consolidates workloads; trade-off is micro-batch (not true per-event) streaming ingest.

lessons.diagram_caption: Sources → Kafka → (Flink) → real-time OLAP (ClickHouse/Pinot/Druid) → sub-second, high-QPS dashboard.

lessons.title: Capstone: data observability & FinOps
lessons.summary: Make the platform trustworthy and cost-aware: monitor the 5 data-health pillars and attribute spend per pipeline.
lessons.concept:
A gold-standard platform isn't just pipelines — it's pipelines you can **trust** and **afford**. This capstone adds the two operational planes that separate a hobby project from a production system: **data observability** and **FinOps**.

**Data observability** continuously monitors the health of data flowing through ingest → transform → serve across **five pillars**:
- **Freshness** — is data arriving on time? (SLA: orders no older than 1h)
- **Volume** — did row counts swing abnormally? (a 90% drop = upstream break)
- **Schema** — did columns/types change unexpectedly? (drift detection)
- **Distribution / quality** — are values in valid ranges, null rates sane?
- **Lineage** — what's upstream/downstream, so you can trace **impact** and root cause fast.
When a pillar breaks, you **alert** against an **SLO** and use lineage to find blast radius. Tools: dbt tests / Great Expectations / Soda for quality checks, a catalog for lineage, and freshness/volume monitors on tables.

**FinOps** makes cost a first-class signal. Cloud data bills sprawl (idle warehouses, unpruned scans, cross-region egress, tiny-file compaction, runaway streaming). You **tag** resources, attribute **per-pipeline / per-team spend**, and surface a **cost dashboard** that finds waste — then act (auto-suspend idle compute, partition to scan less, right-size, kill zombie jobs). Cost is just another observable metric with an owner.

**Why pair them.** Reliability and cost are the two questions leadership always asks: *Can I trust this number?* and *Why is the bill so high?* A platform that answers both — automatically, with alerts and dashboards — is what 'gold standard, operable' actually means, and it's exactly the maturity 50LPA+ interviewers probe.
lessons.example:
# observability: a freshness + volume + schema check (dbt/Soda-style intent)
checks for orders:
  - freshness(created_at) < 1h            # else alert: stale
  - row_count between 10000 and 500000     # else alert: volume anomaly
  - schema matches orders.contract.yaml    # else alert: schema drift
  - missing_count(amount) = 0              # quality

# FinOps: attribute spend, find waste
# SELECT pipeline_tag, sum(credits) cost FROM warehouse_usage
# GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY cost DESC;   -- top spenders -> right-size / auto-suspend / prune
keypoints: Production = trustworthy + affordable; add an observability plane and a FinOps plane over the pipeline.
Five observability pillars: freshness, volume, schema, distribution/quality, lineage.
Alert against SLOs; use lineage to trace impact and root-cause fast.
Quality via dbt tests/Great Expectations/Soda; lineage via the catalog.
FinOps: tag resources, attribute per-pipeline/team spend, dashboard the waste.
Act on cost: auto-suspend idle compute, partition/prune to scan less, right-size, kill zombie jobs.

quiz.q: Which set are the data-observability pillars?
quiz.explain: Observability tracks data health: freshness, volume, schema, distribution/quality, and lineage.

quiz.q: Why is lineage part of observability?
options: It lets you trace impact (downstream) and root cause (upstream) when something breaks
quiz.explain: Lineage maps dependencies so you can find blast radius and the source of an issue quickly.

quiz.q: A core FinOps move when a warehouse bill spikes from idle clusters?
options: Auto-suspend idle compute and attribute spend per pipeline to find waste
quiz.explain: Auto-suspending idle compute and attributing per-pipeline cost surfaces and removes waste.

exercises.prompt: List the five observability pillars.
exercises.solution: Freshness, volume, schema, distribution/quality, and lineage.

exercises.prompt: Row counts dropped 95% overnight. Which pillar fired, and how do you find the cause?
exercises.solution: Volume anomaly; use lineage to trace upstream to the broken source/job (root cause) and downstream impact.

exercises.prompt: Name three FinOps actions to cut a sprawling cloud data bill.
exercises.solution: Auto-suspend idle compute, partition/prune so queries scan less, and right-size/kill zombie jobs (plus reduce cross-region egress).

exercises.prompt: Why pair observability with FinOps in one capstone?
exercises.solution: They answer 'can I trust this number?' and 'why is the bill so high?' — the two questions that define an operable, gold-standard platform.

lessons.diagram_caption: Observability plane (freshness/volume/schema/quality/lineage) + FinOps cost plane over ingest→transform→serve.

lessons.title: Capstone: a big-bank data platform (your stack, end to end)
lessons.summary: The flagship showcase — DataStage ETL, GCS, PySpark/Databricks, streaming, Greenplum, dbt, contracts and CI/CD combined into one runnable bank pipeline across batch and streaming.
lessons.concept:
This capstone is the **complete, runnable platform** a senior data engineer at a bank actually owns — combining the full enterprise stack into one governed pipeline across **batch and streaming**. It mirrors a real architecture and produces gold marts you can demo as your own work.

**The architecture (Lambda style):**
- **Sources** — core banking, cards, market data, CRM.
- **DataStage ETL → GCS landing → Bronze** — extract, standardize, validate, **route bad rows to a reject link**, land immutable raw with ingestion metadata.
- **Databricks / PySpark → Silver** — dedupe, type, validate, flag reversals, conform; build an **SCD2 customer dimension**; run **referential-integrity** quality checks.
- **Streaming (Kafka + Structured Streaming) → fraud/AML** — score card transactions in real time (high amount + high-risk geography) and raise alerts.
- **Data contracts** — an ODCS-style **CI gate** that blocks bad data before it reaches consumers.
- **Greenplum + dbt → Gold** — `fact_transactions`, **daily reconciliation** (control totals), **regulatory exposure**, and **fraud summary** marts, with tests.
- **CI/CD** — Git → tests → contract gate → dbt build → deploy; nothing ships red.

**Why it's the gold standard for a bank:** it's a single **golden source** with **lineage, data quality, reconciliation, and controls** — exactly what regulators now expect (they're intolerant of fragmented architectures and manual reconciliation). Real-time stops fraud *now*; batch produces trustworthy regulatory and reconciled numbers at EOD.

**It runs locally** with faithful stand-ins (DuckDB for Greenplum, file source for Kafka, local dir for GCS, Parquet for Delta) and documents how each maps to the real tool — so you can both **run it** and **speak to production**. The full project (runnable code, CI workflow, docker-compose) is in `capstone-projects/15-bank-data-platform/`.
lessons.example:
# the pipeline, one command:
python run.py
# 1 generate -> 2 DataStage ETL (bronze) -> 3 PySpark medallion (silver, SCD2, quality)
# -> 4 streaming fraud/AML -> 5 data-contract gate -> 6 load Greenplum/DuckDB
# -> 7 dbt build (reconciliation, regulatory, fraud marts + tests) -> 8 report
keypoints: Combines DataStage ETL + GCS + PySpark/Databricks + Kafka streaming + Greenplum + dbt + contracts + CI/CD.
Lambda architecture: batch for reconciliation/regulatory (EOD) + speed for fraud/AML (real-time).
Handles real mess: bad numerics/reject link, duplicates (dedupe), reversals, orphan FKs, SCD2.
Governance: data contracts as a CI gate, quality checks, reconciliation, lineage — a regulated golden source.
Gold marts: fact_transactions, daily_reconciliation, regulatory_exposure, fraud_summary (dbt + tests).
Runs locally with faithful stand-ins; every stand-in maps to the real tool (DuckDB→Greenplum, file→Kafka, Parquet→Delta).
lessons.diagram_caption: Sources → DataStage→GCS→Databricks (batch) + Kafka→fraud (stream) → Greenplum/dbt gold, governed + CI/CD.

quiz.q: Why does a bank platform use a Lambda (batch + speed) architecture?
options: Real-time stops fraud/AML now; batch produces trustworthy reconciliation & regulatory numbers at EOD
quiz.explain: Fraud needs sub-second decisions (speed layer); reconciliation/regulatory need trustworthy, complete EOD data (batch layer).

quiz.q: In this platform, where are bad/unparseable source rows handled?
options: Routed to a reject dataset in the DataStage ETL stage (and the contract gate blocks bad silver)
quiz.explain: DataStage routes invalid rows to a reject link; the data-contract CI gate then blocks any bad silver from reaching consumers.

quiz.q: What proves the PySpark dedup worked, in the gold layer tests?
options: The dbt unique test on fact_transactions.txn_id passes
quiz.explain: silver dedupes by txn_id; the dbt unique test on fact_transactions.txn_id passing confirms there are no duplicates downstream.

exercises.prompt: List the 8 stages of the bank pipeline in order.
exercises.solution: generate → DataStage ETL (bronze) → PySpark medallion (silver/SCD2/quality) → streaming fraud → contract gate → load warehouse → dbt gold marts → report.

exercises.prompt: Map three local stand-ins to their real bank tools.
exercises.solution: DuckDB → Greenplum; file source → Kafka; local dir → GCS; Parquet → Delta/Databricks. The code paths are structured to swap them.

exercises.prompt: Name the four gold marts and who consumes them.
exercises.solution: fact_transactions (analytics), gold_daily_reconciliation (finance/risk control totals), gold_regulatory_exposure (regulators), gold_fraud_summary (financial crime).

lessons.title: Capstone projects interview prep & cheat sheet
lessons.summary: Consolidated rapid-review: high-frequency answers and a mock interview.
lessons.concept:
Your portfolio is your strongest asset. Present each project: problem → architecture → decisions/trade-offs → reliability → results. Defend with idempotency, raw-then-modeled, quality gates, resilience.

Every lesson in this track also has its own '💼 Interview questions' panel and a cheat-sheet button. See the Go-deeper guide for the full mock interview.
keypoints: Present a project: problem → architecture → decisions/trade-offs → reliability → results.
Defend every project with idempotency, raw-then-modeled layers, and quality gates.
Know why you chose each tool/format and the alternatives + trade-offs.
Be ready for 'how does it handle failure?' and 'how would you scale it?'.
The nine capstones together demonstrate the full modern DE stack.

quiz.q: How should you present a project in an interview?
options: Problem & requirements → architecture → key decisions/trade-offs → reliability → results
quiz.explain: Structure shows engineering judgment, not just that it works.

quiz.q: What makes a pipeline safe to rerun/backfill?
options: Idempotency — partition overwrite or upsert by key so reruns don't duplicate
quiz.explain: Never blind-INSERT; overwrite the partition or merge by key.

exercises.prompt: Pick 2-3 capstones and rehearse the 5-step presentation + per-project mock questions out loud.
exercises.hint: problem, architecture, decisions, reliability, results; failure handling; scaling.
exercises.solution: Be able to defend each design choice and its trade-offs.

tracks.title: DSA for Data Engineering
tracks.blurb: The data structures & algorithms that actually matter for data engineering — complexity at scale, hashing/dedup, sorting & external merge, heaps/top-K, probabilistic structures (Bloom/HLL/Count-Min), storage-engine trees (B-tree/LSM), graphs/DAGs for pipeline scheduling, and the SQL/coding patterns DE interviews ask.

lessons.title: Big-O & complexity for data workloads
lessons.summary: Big-O describes how cost grows with data size; at scale the exponent dominates, so choosing algorithms (especially join strategies) by complexity — and minding memory (spill) and network (shuffle) — is what keeps pipelines fast.
lessons.concept:
**Big-O notation** describes how an algorithm's **time/space grows with input size n** — and in data engineering, where n is **billions of rows**, the difference between complexities is the difference between **seconds and never finishing**.

### The complexity ladder (with data examples)
| Complexity | Grows | DE example |
|---|---|---|
| **O(1)** | constant | hash-map lookup, array index |
| **O(log n)** | logarithmic | binary search, **B-tree index** lookup |
| **O(n)** | linear | **full scan**, filter, map |
| **O(n log n)** | linearithmic | **sort**, sort-merge join |
| **O(n + m)** | linear (two inputs) | **hash join** (build map of one side, probe with the other) |
| **O(n × m)** | quadratic | **nested-loop join** (compare every pair — avoid!) |

### Why it matters at scale
At **small n**, complexity barely matters. At **billions of rows**, it's everything: an **O(n²)** nested-loop join on a billion rows is **10¹⁸ comparisons** — fatal — while a **hash join (O(n+m))** finishes. So a data engineer **picks algorithms by complexity**, especially **join strategy** (hash vs sort-merge vs broadcast vs nested-loop) — the single most impactful algorithmic choice in query performance.

### Beyond time: the real distributed bottlenecks
In distributed data systems, the classic time-complexity isn't the whole story — the **real** bottlenecks are:
- **Memory** — does it fit, or does it **spill** to disk (slow)? (space complexity matters).
- **Network / shuffle** — moving data between nodes is the dominant cost; algorithms that **minimize data movement** (broadcast a small side, pre-aggregate) win.
- **I/O** — bytes read (columnar/pruning reduce it).
So 'fast' means **good complexity AND low memory/shuffle/IO**.

### Amortized & average vs worst case
Hash operations are **O(1) average** (worst-case O(n) on collisions); dynamic arrays append in **amortized O(1)**. Know the **average** case you'll actually hit, and the **worst** case that can bite (hash collisions, skew).

### Why it matters
Big-O is the **lens** for reasoning about whether something scales. The headline DE applications: **choose join algorithms by complexity** (never O(n²) at scale), **prefer O(1) hashing** for dedup/group-by, and remember that **memory and network** often dominate the textbook time complexity in distributed systems.
lessons.example: Joining a 1-billion-row fact to a 1-billion-row dimension: a **nested-loop join** is **O(n×m) ≈ 10¹⁸** comparisons — it will never finish. A **hash join** builds a hash map of one side and probes with the other — **O(n+m) ≈ 2×10⁹** — done in minutes. If one side is **small**, a **broadcast** join ships it to every node and avoids the shuffle entirely. The algorithm choice — driven by **complexity** (and **data movement**) — is the difference between a query that completes and one that doesn't.
keypoints: Big-O describes how time/space grow with n; at billions of rows the complexity class decides feasibility.
Ladder: O(1) hash lookup, O(log n) index/binary search, O(n) scan, O(n log n) sort/sort-merge join, O(n+m) hash join, O(n×m) nested-loop (avoid).
Join strategy (hash vs sort-merge vs broadcast vs nested-loop) is the highest-impact algorithmic choice in query performance.
In distributed systems the real bottlenecks are memory (spill), network (shuffle), and I/O — minimize data movement.
Hashing is O(1) average (O(n) worst on collisions); dynamic-array append is amortized O(1) — know average vs worst case.
Think 'good complexity AND low memory/shuffle/IO' — not just textbook time.
lessons.diagram_caption: Complexity ladder for data work — O(1) hashing to O(n²) nested-loop join; at scale the class decides feasibility, and memory/shuffle/IO are the real bottlenecks.

quiz.q: Why does Big-O matter so much in data engineering specifically?
options: n is often billions of rows, so the complexity class decides whether a job finishes in seconds or never (e.g. O(n²) joins are fatal)
quiz.explain: At massive n, the complexity class dominates — an O(n²) nested-loop join on a billion rows is infeasible, while O(n+m) hash join finishes.

quiz.q: Besides time complexity, what are the dominant bottlenecks in distributed data systems?
options: Memory (spill), network (shuffle / data movement), and I/O — algorithms that minimize data movement win
quiz.explain: Distributed performance is usually bound by memory/spill, network shuffle, and I/O — not raw CPU time — so minimizing data movement is key.

exercises.prompt: Explain why join algorithm choice is the most impactful complexity decision, comparing nested-loop, hash, sort-merge, and broadcast.
exercises.hint: O(n×m) vs O(n+m) vs O(n log n) vs shipping a small side.
exercises.solution: Joins dominate query cost, and their **complexity varies enormously**, so the strategy is the highest-impact choice. **Nested-loop join** compares **every pair** → **O(n×m)** — at a billion rows each, ~10¹⁸ comparisons, **infeasible**; only acceptable for tiny inputs. **Hash join** builds a **hash map** of one side (the smaller 'build' side) then **probes** it with the other ('probe' side) → **O(n+m)** with O(1) lookups — the workhorse for equality joins, vastly faster, provided the build side fits in memory (else it spills/partitions). **Sort-merge join** **sorts both sides** (O(n log n)) then merges in a linear pass — good when inputs are **already sorted** or too big to hash, and the basis of many distributed joins. **Broadcast join** ships a **small** side to **every node** so each does a **local hash join** with **no shuffle** of the large side — the cheapest when one side is small (eliminates the dominant network cost). So picking by **complexity and data movement**: never nested-loop at scale; **broadcast** if one side is small; **hash** for general equality joins that fit; **sort-merge** for very large/sorted inputs. This single decision — made by the optimizer but guided by your stats/hints/layout — is usually the difference between a fast query and a stuck one.

exercises.prompt: A query is O(n) in theory but still slow on a cluster. What real-world factors (beyond time complexity) would you investigate?
exercises.solution: Even O(n) work can be slow if the **distributed realities** are bad. Investigate: (1) **Shuffle / data movement** — does the query **reshuffle** large data across the network (a wide transformation, a non-co-located join, a repartition)? Network is the dominant cost; reduce it (broadcast small sides, pre-aggregate with Combine/`GROUP BY` before shuffle, partition/cluster so joins are local). (2) **Skew** — is one key/partition **far larger** (a hot key), so one task does most of the work while others idle? That turns 'O(n) overall' into 'O(n) on one node' — mitigate by salting/handling the hot key. (3) **Memory / spill** — do partitions exceed memory and **spill to disk** (slow), or does a hash build side not fit? Increase memory/partitions or change the algorithm. (4) **I/O** — is it scanning **too much** (no column pruning, no partition filter, row format, tiny files)? Read less (columnar, prune, compact). (5) **Small files / scheduling overhead** — many tiny tasks. So beyond Big-O time, the practical bottlenecks are **shuffle, skew, spill, and I/O** — exactly what the Spark-UI/query-plan tuning lessons address. 'O(n)' is necessary but not sufficient; on a cluster you also minimize **data movement and memory pressure**.

lessons.title: Hash maps & sets: dedup, group-by, joins
lessons.summary: Hash tables give ~O(1) insert/lookup and are the single most-used structure in data work — the basis of GROUP BY, DISTINCT/deduplication, frequency counts, and hash joins (build a map of one side, probe with the other).
lessons.concept:
A **hash table** (hash map / hash set) stores key→value (or just keys) and gives **~O(1) average** insert, lookup, and delete by computing **hash(key) → bucket**. It's the **workhorse** of data engineering — most aggregations and joins are hashing under the hood.

### How it works (briefly)
`hash(key)` maps a key to a **bucket**; the value is stored there. Lookups recompute the hash and check the bucket — **O(1) average**. **Collisions** (two keys → same bucket) are handled (chaining/open addressing); worst case is **O(n)** if many collide, but good hash functions keep it ~O(1). **Sets** are the same minus values (membership only).

### The core DE applications
